$$m \;=\; \cos\phi \cos\lambda \;\le\; \frac{1}{2}$$

with the maximum **m = 0.5** occurring at **$\phi = \lambda = 45¬∞$**. This is the "**magic 45¬∞ orientation**" ‚Äî slip is easiest here, and the macroscopic yield strength is at its **minimum** of $\sigma_y = 2 \tau_{CRSS}$.

## 3.2 Worked Example (KTU Board Style)

> **Problem:** A copper single crystal (FCC, $\tau_{CRSS} = 0.7$ MPa) is pulled in tension. The slip plane normal makes an angle $\phi = 60¬∞$ with the tensile axis, and the slip direction makes an angle $\lambda = 30¬∞$. Calculate:
> (a) The Schmid factor.
> (b) The resolved shear stress when a stress of 5 MPa is applied.
> (c) Will slip occur at 5 MPa? If not, find the stress at which slip begins.

### Solution

**(a) Schmid Factor:**

$$m \;=\; \cos\phi \cos\lambda$$

$$m \;=\; \cos 60¬∞ \cdot \cos 30¬∞$$

$$m \;=\; (0.500) \cdot (0.866)$$

$$\boxed{m \;=\; 0.433}$$

**(b) Resolved Shear Stress at œÉ = 5 MPa:**

$$\tau_R \;=\; m \cdot \sigma \;=\; 0.433 \times 5$$

$$\boxed{\tau_R \;=\; 2.165 \; \text{MPa}}$$

**(c) Slip Initiation Check:**

Required: $\tau_R \ge \tau_{CRSS}$ for slip.

Compare:

$$\tau_R \;=\; 2.165 \; \text{MPa} \quad \text{vs.} \quad \tau_{CRSS} \;=\; 0.7 \; \text{MPa}$$

Since $2.165 \ge 0.7$, **slip WILL occur** at 5 MPa.

(If the problem had asked for the critical stress to *initiate* slip, it would be:)

$$\sigma_y \;=\; \frac{\tau_{CRSS}}{m} \;=\; \frac{0.7}{0.433}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_y \;\approx\; 1.617 \; \text{MPa}}$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix ‚Äî Slip vs. Twinning (Definitive KTU Comparison Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This table appears verbatim (or near-verbatim) in almost every KTU Board exam answer for this module. Memorize it.**

| S.No. | Feature | **Slip** | **Twinning** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Atomic motion** | One part translates bodily over the other (uniform block shift) | Progressive, coordinated shearing; displacement proportional to distance from twin plane |
| 2 | **Displacement per plane** | Many atomic spacings (integer multiples of **b**) | Fraction of an atomic spacing per plane |
| 3 | **Orientation change** | No change in crystal orientation within either block | Twinned region is a **mirror image** of the parent |
| 4 | **Time / rate** | Slow, gradual, mediated by dislocation glide | Sudden, often accompanied by audible **"twin click"** |
| 5 | **Stress-strain curve** | Smooth, continuous | **Serrated** (jerky, saw-tooth) |
| 6 | **Critical stress** | $\tau_R \ge \tau_{CRSS}$ | Requires high stress, especially at low T or high strain rate |
| 7 | **Temperature dependence** | Favored at **high T** (thermally activated) | Favored at **low T** and **high strain rate** |
| 8 | **Crystal structure preference** | Dominant in FCC, BCC; HCP at high T | Dominant in HCP (Mg, Zn, Ti), BCC at low T |
| 9 | **Slip/Twin planes** | Slip planes (e.g., {111} FCC) | Twin planes (e.g., {10-12} in HCP, {112} in BCC) |
| 10 | **Surface step / relief** | Straight, parallel lines (slip lines) | Lens-shaped or lenticular bands, often terminating within the grain |
| 11 | **Role of dislocations** | Slip is the motion of dislocations | Twinning is *not* dislocation motion; it is a coordinated atomic shuffle |
| 12 | **Effect on work hardening** | Strong contributor (dislocation entanglement) | Moderate ‚Äî reorients crystal to a "hard" orientation for further slip |
| 13 | **Direction of shear** | Same direction throughout the slipped region | Shear direction **reverses** across the twin plane |
| 14 | **Volume change** | Negligible | Negligible (lattice type is unchanged) |

## 3.4 Derivation of Twinning Shear

For a simple twin, consider the **twinning shear strain** $\gamma_{twin}$:

$$\gamma_{twin} \;=\; \frac{s}{t}$$

where:
- $s$ = total shear displacement at the outer boundary of the twin.
- $t$ = thickness of the twinned region (measured perpendicular to the twin plane).

For HCP magnesium on the {10-12} twin system, the theoretical twinning shear is approximately:

$$\gamma_{twin} \;=\; \frac{(3 - \gamma^2)}{\sqrt{3} \cdot \gamma}$$

where $\gamma = c/a$ is the axial ratio. For Mg ($\gamma \approx 1.624$):

$$\gamma_{twin} \;\approx\; 0.129$$

This is small ‚Äî consistent with the observation that twinning is a *low-strain* mode per event, but can accumulate to large macroscopic strains over many twin lamellae.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Schematic Block Diagram ‚Äî Slip Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Single Crystal Under Tensile Stress sigma] --> A1[Resolve Force F on Slip Plane]
    A1 --> A2[Component of Stress along Slip Direction S]
    A2 --> A3{Compare tau_R with tau_CRSS}
    A3 -- tau_R less than tau_CRSS --> A4[Elastic Deformation Only - No Slip]
    A3 -- tau_R equals tau_CRSS --> A5[CRITICAL CONDITION - Slip Onset]
    A3 -- tau_R greater than tau_CRSS --> A6[PLASTIC SLIP - Dislocation Glide]
    A6 --> A7[Surface Step Formation of magnitude n times Burgers Vector b]
    A6 --> A8[Work Hardening due to Dislocation Entanglement]
    A6 --> A9[Slip Lines Visible on Polished Surface]
```

## 4.2 Schematic Block Diagram ‚Äî Twinning Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    B0[Parent Lattice Under Shear] --> B1[Identify Twin Plane hkl in Crystal]
    B1 --> B2[Atoms Above Twin Plane Begin Homogeneous Shear]
    B2 --> B3[Each Atomic Layer Shifts Proportionally to Distance from Twin Plane]
    B3 --> B4[Atoms Reach Mirror Symmetry Position]
    B4 --> B5[TWIN REGION FORMED as Mirror Image of Parent]
    B5 --> B6[Lenticular Lens Shaped Band Visible under Microscope]
    B5 --> B7[Acoustic Emission Click and Serrated Stress Strain Curve]
    B5 --> B8[Crystal Orientation Reoriented for Harder Slip on Retwinning]
```

## 4.3 Comparative Flow ‚Äî Slip vs. Twinning Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C0[Apply Stress to Crystal] --> C1{Number of Active Slip Systems}
    C1 -- 5 or more systems available --> C2[SLIP Dominates]
    C1 -- fewer than 5 systems available --> C3{Check Temperature and Strain Rate}
    C3 -- Low T or High Strain Rate --> C4[TWINNING Dominates]
    C3 -- High T and Low Strain Rate --> C5[SLIP may still occur]
    C2 --> C6[Dislocation Glide on Close Packed Planes]
    C4 --> C7[Coordinated Mirror Shear across Twin Plane]
    C6 --> C8[Result: Ductile, Smooth Deformation]
    C7 --> C9[Result: Brittle Tendency, Sudden Shear]
```

## 4.4 Schmid Factor Geometry ‚Äî Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    D0[Tensile Axis] --> D1[Angle phi with Slip Plane Normal N]
    D0 --> D2[Angle lambda with Slip Direction S]
    D1 --> D3[Compute m equals cos phi times cos lambda]
    D2 --> D3
    D3 --> D4{Is m equal to 0.5 at 45 degrees}
    D4 -- YES --> D5[Maximum Schmid Factor - Easiest Slip]
    D4 -- NO --> D6[Lower Schmid Factor - Slip Harder to Activate]
    D5 --> D7[Minimum Yield Strength sigma_y equals 2 times tau_CRSS]
    D6 --> D8[Higher Yield Strength Required]
```
