# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A ‚Äî Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the term "slip" in the context of plastic deformation of crystalline materials. List the slip system of an FCC metal.**

**Model Answer:**

**Slip:** Slip is the mode of plastic deformation in which one part of a crystal translates (glides) over another part along a specific crystallographic plane ‚Äî called the **slip plane** ‚Äî in a specific crystallographic direction ‚Äî called the **slip direction**. Slip occurs when the resolved shear stress on the slip plane in the slip direction reaches a critical value, $\tau_{CRSS}$, and is mediated by the motion of **dislocations**.

**Slip system of FCC metal:** The slip system of an FCC metal is:

$$\text{Slip plane: } \{111\} \quad \text{Slip direction: } \langle 110 \rangle$$

**Number of slip systems = 12** (4 unique {111} planes √ó 3 ‚ü®110‚ü© directions per plane).

**[3 marks: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for {111} plane, 1 mark for ‚ü®110‚ü© direction & 12 systems]**

---

### Question A2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is mechanical twinning? Why is it favored in HCP metals at low temperatures?**

**Model Answer:**

**Mechanical Twinning:** Mechanical twinning is a mode of plastic deformation in which a portion of the crystal is reoriented by a **homogeneous shear** such that the twinned region forms a **mirror image** of the parent lattice across a defined **twin plane**. Each atomic plane is displaced by an amount proportional to its distance from the twin plane.

**Why favored in HCP at low T:**
- HCP metals have only **3 independent slip systems** (basal {0001}‚ü®11-20‚ü©), which is **insufficient** for arbitrary plastic shape change (von Mises criterion requires ‚â• 5).
- At low temperatures, **non-basal slip systems** (prismatic, pyramidal) have very high $\tau_{CRSS}$ and are not activated.
- Therefore, the metal resorts to **twinning** to accommodate the imposed strain.

**[3 marks: 1.5 for definition, 1.5 for HCP reason]**

---

## 5.2 PART B ‚Äî ESE Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question B-A. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]`
**(a) [7 marks]** State and derive **Schmid's Law**. Define the Schmid factor and show that its maximum value is 0.5.

**(b) [7 marks]** A single crystal of an FCC metal is stressed in tension. The tensile axis makes an angle of 35¬∞ with the normal to the slip plane and 50¬∞ with the slip direction. The CRSS for the metal is 22 MPa. Calculate:
   (i) The Schmid factor.
   (ii) The stress required to initiate slip.
   (iii) The resolved shear stress when the applied stress is 80 MPa.

---

### Model Solution to Question B-A

#### Part (a) ‚Äî Schmid's Law Derivation [7 marks]

**Statement of Schmid's Law:**
> "The resolved shear stress $\tau_R$ acting on a slip plane in the slip direction is given by $\tau_R = \sigma \cos\phi \cos\lambda$, where $\sigma$ is the applied tensile stress, $\phi$ is the angle between the tensile axis and the normal to the slip plane, and $\lambda$ is the angle between the tensile axis and the slip direction. Slip begins when $\tau_R$ equals the Critical Resolved Shear Stress $\tau_{CRSS}$."

**Derivation** (refer to Section 3.1 for full derivation):
- Consider a single crystal of cross-section $A_0$ under tensile force $F$.
- The area of the slip plane (inclined at $\phi$ to the cross-section) is $A_{slip} = A_0 / \cos\phi$.
- The force component along the slip direction is $F \cos\lambda$.
- Resolved shear stress:

$$\tau_R \;=\; \frac{F \cos\lambda}{A_0 / \cos\phi} \;=\; \frac{F}{A_0} \cos\phi \cos\lambda \;=\; \sigma \cos\phi \cos\lambda$$

**Schmid Factor** $m = \cos\phi \cos\lambda$ ‚Äî a dimensionless geometric factor between 0 and 0.5.

**Maximum value:** The tensile axis lies in the plane containing N and S, so $\phi + \lambda = 90¬∞$, hence $\lambda = 90¬∞ - \phi$:

$$m \;=\; \cos\phi \cos(90¬∞ - \phi) \;=\; \cos\phi \sin\phi \;=\; \frac{\sin 2\phi}{2}$$

Maximum of $m$ occurs when $\sin 2\phi = 1$, i.e., $2\phi = 90¬∞$, so $\phi = 45¬∞$ (and $\lambda = 45¬∞$):

$$\boxed{m_{max} \;=\; \frac{1}{2} \;=\; 0.5}$$

**[Marks distribution: 1.5 for statement, 3 for derivation, 1 for Schmid factor definition, 1.5 for max proof]**

#### Part (b) ‚Äî Numerical Problem [7 marks]

**Given:**
- $\phi = 35¬∞$, $\lambda = 50¬∞$
- $\tau_{CRSS} = 22$ MPa
- $\sigma = 80$ MPa (for part iii)

**(i) Schmid Factor** [2 marks]:

$$m \;=\; \cos 35¬∞ \cdot \cos 50¬∞$$

$$m \;=\; (0.8192) \cdot (0.6428)$$

$$\boxed{m \;\approx\; 0.5265 \;\approx\; 0.527}$$

**(ii) Stress to Initiate Slip** [2.5 marks]:

Apply slip criterion: $\tau_R = \tau_{CRSS}$, hence $\sigma_y = \tau_{CRSS} / m$:

$$\sigma_y \;=\; \frac{22 \;\text{MPa}}{0.5265}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_y \;\approx\; 41.8 \;\text{MPa}}$$

**(iii) Resolved Shear Stress at œÉ = 80 MPa** [2.5 marks]:

$$\tau_R \;=\; \sigma \cos\phi \cos\lambda \;=\; 80 \times 0.8192 \times 0.6428$$

$$\tau_R \;=\; 80 \times 0.5265$$

$$\boxed{\tau_R \;\approx\; 42.12 \;\text{MPa}}$$

Note: Since $\tau_R = 42.12$ MPa $> \tau_{CRSS} = 22$ MPa, the crystal is well into the plastic region at 80 MPa.

---

### Question B-B. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified]` (Alternative Choice)
**(a) [7 marks]** Explain in detail the **slip mechanism** of plastic deformation. With neat sketches, describe the concepts of slip plane, slip direction, and slip system. List the slip systems for FCC, BCC, and HCP metals.

**(b) [7 marks]** Compare **slip and twinning** as modes of plastic deformation (at least 8 points). Why does twinning dominate over slip in HCP magnesium at room temperature?

---

### Model Solution to Question B-B (Highlights)

#### Part (a) ‚Äî Slip Mechanism [7 marks]

1. **Definition of slip** ‚Äî translational block movement along a crystallographic plane.
2. **Slip plane** ‚Äî the plane of highest atomic density (close-packed plane). e.g., {111} in FCC.
3. **Slip direction** ‚Äî the direction of highest linear atomic density (close-packed direction). e.g., ‚ü®110‚ü© in FCC.
4. **Slip system** ‚Äî the {hkl}‚ü®uvw‚ü© combination.
5. **Slip systems** (table ‚Äî already in Section 2.3).
6. **CRSS and Schmid's Law** must be quoted.
7. **Sketch description** ‚Äî show tensile axis, slip plane normal N, slip direction S, and angles $\phi$ and $\lambda$.

#### Part (b) ‚Äî Slip vs. Twinning Comparison [7 marks]

Provide the **14-point table from Section 3.3** (any 8 points earn full marks; 4 extra points for HCP/Mg explanation).

**Why twinning dominates in Mg at room temperature:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Answer (2 marks for this sub-part):** Magnesium is HCP with $c/a \approx 1.624$. At room temperature, only the **basal slip system {0001}‚ü®11-20‚ü©** is active ‚Äî providing just **3 slip systems**, which is **insufficient** (von Mises requires ‚â• 5) to satisfy arbitrary general plastic deformation. Prismatic and pyramidal slip in Mg have very high $\tau_{CRSS}$ at room temperature. Therefore, when basal slip is exhausted or geometrically unfavorable, the crystal accommodates strain by activating **{10-12} tensile twinning** and **{10-11} compression twinning**. Hence, twinning dominates in Mg at room temperature.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common Mistake 1 (1-mark deduction):** Writing "slip occurs when stress exceeds yield strength" without invoking the **resolved shear stress** and **CRSS** ‚Äî the Board specifically tests whether you understand that *resolved* shear stress governs slip, not the applied normal stress.
> 2. **Common Mistake 2 (2-mark deduction):** Listing the *Burgers vector* incorrectly for FCC as ‚ü®100‚ü© ‚Äî it is **‚ü®110‚ü©**. FCC slip direction is ‚ü®110‚ü©, NOT ‚ü®100‚ü©.
> 3. **Common Mistake 3 (1-mark deduction):** Confusing $\phi$ and $\lambda$ in Schmid's Law. Memorize: **$\phi$ is with the NORMAL**, **$\lambda$ is with the DIRECTION**.
> 4. **Common Mistake 4 (1-mark deduction):** Writing "BCC has 12 slip systems" ‚Äî actually, BCC has **48 slip systems** in total (across {110}, {112}, and {123} planes). Many textbooks also consider only {110} planes (12 systems), so state the assumption clearly.
> 5. **Common Mistake 5 (1-mark deduction):** Stating that twinning is "brittle fracture" ‚Äî twinning is a *plastic* deformation mode, not a fracture mechanism. The "brittle tendency" refers to macroscopic behavior, not the mechanism itself.
> 6. **Common Mistake 6 (1-mark deduction):** Forgetting units in numerical answers ‚Äî always write **MPa** for stress and **degrees** for angles.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Block ‚Äî Read this the night before the exam.**

### A. Slip ‚Äî Key Points
- Slip is a **dislocation-mediated, translational** plastic deformation mode.
- Slip occurs on **close-packed planes** in **close-packed directions**.
- Governing law: **Schmid's Law** ‚Äî $\tau_R = \sigma \cos\phi \cos\lambda$.
- Slip criterion: $\tau_R \ge \tau_{CRSS}$.
- **Schmid factor $m = \cos\phi \cos\lambda$**, max value **0.5** at $\phi = \lambda = 45¬∞$.
- Yield strength of single crystal: $\sigma_y = \tau_{CRSS} / m$.
- FCC: 12 systems ({111}‚ü®110‚ü©). BCC: 48 systems ({110}, {112}, {123}‚ü®111‚ü©). HCP: 3 systems (basal {0001}‚ü®11-20‚ü©).
- Slip leaves **straight, parallel slip lines** on polished surfaces.
- Surface step height = **integer √ó Burgers vector (b)**.
- Favored at **high T** and **low strain rate**.

### B. Twinning ‚Äî Key Points
- Twinning is a **homogeneous, mirror-symmetric shear** mode.
- Each plane displaces by a **fraction of an interatomic spacing**, proportional to its distance from the twin plane.
- Produces **lens-shaped / lenticular** bands.
- Shear direction **reverses** across the twin plane (unlike slip).
- Favored at **low T**, **high strain rate**, and in materials with **few slip systems** (HCP).
- Accompanied by **audible click** and **serrated stress-strain curve**.
- HCP: {10-12} tensile twins, {10-11} compression twins. BCC: {112} twins.
- Does **NOT** change crystal structure ‚Äî only reorients lattice.
- Twinning shear $\gamma_{twin} = s / t$ ‚Äî small (e.g., 0.129 for Mg).

### C. The Verdict Formula to Remember
$$\boxed{\sigma \cos\phi \cos\lambda \;\ge\; \tau_{CRSS} \;\Longleftrightarrow\; \text{SLIP BEGINS}}$$

### D. The Magic Number
- **0.5** ‚Äî Maximum Schmid factor. **45¬∞** ‚Äî Magic orientation for easiest slip.

### E. Slip System Count Mnemonic
- **FCC = 12** (think: "**4** unique {111} planes √ó **3** ‚ü®110‚ü© directions each = **12**").
- **BCC = 48** (think: "**4** √ó **3** √ó **4** = 48" or "12 {110} + 12 {112} + 24 {123} = 48").
- **HCP = 3** (think: "**1** basal plane √ó **3** ‚ü®11-20‚ü© directions = **3**").

### F. Cross-Cutting Concepts
- **Ductility** ‚Üî number of slip systems.
- **Brittleness** ‚Üî few slip systems + twinning.
- **Work hardening** ‚Üî dislocation entanglement from slip.
- **TWIP steels** ‚Üî modern application of twinning for crash resistance.
- **Single-crystal turbine blades** ‚Üî Schmid factor minimization for creep resistance.

### G. KTU Examiner's Bias ‚Äî What They Love to Ask
- 3-mark: Slip system identification for any crystal structure.
- 3-mark: Slip vs. twinning ‚Äî 4 to 6 differences.
- 7-mark: Schmid's Law derivation + numerical.
- 7-mark: HCP ‚Äî why twinning dominates + Mg/Zn example.
- 7-mark: Slip mechanism with neat sketch of $\phi$ and $\lambda$ geometry.

> **END OF MODULE 2 NOTES ‚Äî MODES OF PLASTIC DEFORMATION: SLIP AND TWINNING**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Schmid's law
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAUT304 - MATERIAL SCIENCE AND METALLURGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Schmid's law

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Schmid's Law** is a fundamental principle in crystal plasticity that defines the **Resolved Shear Stress (RSS)** acting on a crystallographic slip plane and along a slip direction when an external uniaxial tensile or compressive load is applied to a single crystal. The law is mathematically expressed as:
>
> $$\tau_{RSS} = \sigma \cdot \cos\phi \cdot \cos\lambda$$
>
> where $\sigma$ is the applied uniaxial stress, $\phi$ (phi) is the angle between the **tensile axis** and the **normal to the slip plane**, and $\lambda$ (lambda) is the angle between the **tensile axis** and the **slip direction**.

When the resolved shear stress reaches a material-specific threshold known as the **Critical Resolved Shear Stress (CRSS)**, denoted $\tau_{CRSS}$, the crystal begins to yield through slip along the most favorably oriented slip system.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Resolved Shear Stress (CRSS)** is a **material constant** measured experimentally for a given slip system at a specified temperature and strain rate. It represents the minimum shear stress required to initiate plastic deformation in a single crystal.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Build-Up

Imagine you are trying to slide a heavy wooden block across a smooth table by pushing it with a stick at an angle. The force you apply has a component that actually slides the block (along the table) and a component that just pushes it into the table (perpendicular to the table). The sliding component depends on the angle at which you push.

Similarly, when a single crystal is pulled by a tensile force:
- The force is not directly aligned with the slip plane or slip direction.
- Only the **shear component** along the slip direction on the slip plane can cause atoms to glide past each other.
- The **normal component** (perpendicular to the slip plane) tries to either pull the planes apart or press them together ‚Äî neither causes slip.

**Schmid's Law is essentially the geometric bookkeeping that quantifies "how much of the applied pull actually becomes sliding force"** on a particular slip plane.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Insight for Students:** Schmid's Law tells you *which* slip system activates *first* when a crystal is loaded. The slip system with the **largest value of $\cos\phi \cos\lambda$** (called the **Schmid Factor, $m$**) will yield first.

### 1.3 Geometric Definitions of the Two Angles

| Symbol | Name | Geometric Definition |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| $\phi$ | Phi | Angle between the **tensile axis** ($\sigma$ direction) and the **normal to the slip plane** |
| $\lambda$ | Lambda | Angle between the **tensile axis** and the **slip direction** within the slip plane |
| $m$ | Schmid Factor | $\cos\phi \cdot \cos\lambda$; maximum theoretical value = **0.5** |

The slip plane and slip direction are always perpendicular to each other only in specific cases; in general, they need not be perpendicular. The two angles are measured from the **same reference line** (the tensile axis), but to **two different lines** (slip-plane normal and slip direction).

### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> The **theoretical maximum of the Schmid Factor is 0.5**, which occurs when $\phi = \lambda = 45¬∞$. This is the most favorable orientation for slip activation. Typical CRSS values: pure FCC metals ‚âà **0.5 ‚Äì 1.0 MPa**; HCP metals are usually higher and orientation-dependent.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown ‚Äî The "Why" and "How"

Schmid's Law is built on a simple geometric force-resolution argument. Let us dissect the logic in five clean steps:

1. **Applied Load:** A single crystal of cross-sectional area $A$ (perpendicular to the load axis) is subjected to a uniaxial tensile force $F$.
2. **Slip Plane Geometry:** The slip plane has a normal that makes an angle $\phi$ with the tensile axis. Therefore, the **area of the slip plane** (the actual plane along which atoms glide) is larger than the cross-section $A$. By pure geometry: $A_{slip} = A / \cos\phi$.
3. **Force Resolution onto the Slip Plane:** The component of $F$ that actually lies *within* the slip plane (and thus can shear it) is $F \cos\phi$. This is the projection of the applied force onto the slip plane.
4. **Force Resolution along the Slip Direction:** Of the in-plane force $F\cos\phi$, only the component along the slip direction produces actual atomic sliding. This is $F \cos\phi \cdot \cos\lambda$.
5. **Resolved Shear Stress:** Dividing this resolved force by the area of the slip plane gives the Resolved Shear Stress.

### 2.2 The KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Equation | Meaning / Use | Units |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | $\tau_{RSS} = \sigma \cdot \cos\phi \cdot \cos\lambda$ | **Schmid's Law** ‚Äî gives resolved shear stress | MPa or Pa |
| 2 | $m = \cos\phi \cdot \cos\lambda$ | **Schmid Factor** ‚Äî orientation factor (0 to 0.5) | Dimensionless |
| 3 | $\tau_{RSS} = m \cdot \sigma$ | Compact form of Schmid's Law | MPa or Pa |
| 4 | $\sigma_{y} = \dfrac{\tau_{CRSS}}{m}$ | **Yield stress** of a single crystal for a given orientation | MPa or Pa |
| 5 | $\sigma_{y}^{min} = 2 \cdot \tau_{CRSS}$ | Minimum yield stress (when $m_{max} = 0.5$) | MPa or Pa |
| 6 | $A_{slip} = \dfrac{A}{\cos\phi}$ | Geometric area of slip plane | m¬≤ or mm¬≤ |
| 7 | Slip onset criterion | $\tau_{RSS} \geq \tau_{CRSS}$ | ‚Äî |

> [!WARNING]
> In KTU valuation, students often confuse $\phi$ and $\lambda$. **Memory hook:** **$\phi$ is associated with the *plane* (plane ‚Üî phi); $\lambda$ is associated with the *line/direction* (line ‚Üî lambda).** Examiners deduct marks for swapped angles.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Schmid's Law is not a classroom abstraction ‚Äî it governs the design and selection of metals in real engineering applications:

- **Turbine Blade Design:** Nickel-based superalloy single crystals in jet engine turbine blades are deliberately cast along the **[001] direction** because it provides optimal balance between Schmid factor, creep resistance, and thermal-mechanical fatigue life.
- **Sheet Metal Forming:** During deep drawing, the crystallographic texture of the sheet determines which grains yield first. Recrystallization textures are engineered to maximize $m$ in the deformation direction.
- **Semiconductor Wafer Handling:** Silicon wafers are sliced along specific crystallographic planes; Schmid's Law (adapted for fracture mechanics) predicts the cleavage plane orientations to avoid brittle failure.
- **Cold Rolling & Forging:** Polycrystalline metals develop preferred orientations (texture). Each grain behaves like a single crystal ‚Äî Schmid's Law applied per grain explains macroscopic yield anisotropy in textured metals.

### 2.4 Maximum and Minimum Schmid Factor Behavior

The product $\cos\phi \cdot \cos\lambda$ varies between **0 and 0.5**:
- When $\phi = 90¬∞$: $\cos\phi = 0$ ‚Üí the tensile axis is parallel to the slip plane ‚Üí no resolved shear ‚Üí **no slip** (slip plane area becomes infinite, force component zero).
- When $\lambda = 90¬∞$: $\cos\lambda = 0$ ‚Üí tensile axis is perpendicular to the slip direction ‚Üí **no slip**.
- When $\phi = \lambda = 45¬∞$: $\cos\phi = \cos\lambda = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.707$ ‚Üí product = **0.5** (maximum).

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Numerical Check:** Since $0 \leq \cos\phi \leq 1$ and $0 \leq \cos\lambda \leq 1$, the product lies in $[0, 1]$, but constrained by the geometry of slip systems, the practical maximum is exactly **0.5**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Schmid's Law

We derive the law from first principles using vector resolution. Consider a single crystal cylinder of cross-sectional area $A$ whose axis coincides with the loading (tensile) axis. A particular slip system is identified by:
- A **slip plane** with unit normal $\hat{n}$
- A **slip direction** within the plane with unit vector $\hat{s}$

**Step 1: Applied Force and Stress**
The crystal is loaded by a uniaxial tensile force $F$ along the loading axis. The applied normal stress is:

$$\sigma = \frac{F}{A}$$

**Step 2: Geometric Area of the Slip Plane**

The slip plane is inclined; its normal $\hat{n}$ makes an angle $\phi$ with the tensile axis. The cross-sectional area $A$ is the projection of the slip-plane area $A_{slip}$ onto a plane perpendicular to the tensile axis. By geometry of projection:

$$A = A_{slip} \cdot \cos\phi$$

Rearranging to express the actual slip-plane area:

$$A_{slip} = \frac{A}{\cos\phi}$$

**Reasoning:** The slip plane is "tilted" relative to the cross-section, so its true surface area is larger than the apparent cross-section by a factor of $1/\cos\phi$.

**Step 3: Force Component Resolved into the Slip Plane**

The total applied force $F$ is a vector along the tensile axis. To find the component of $F$ that acts *within* the slip plane (i.e., tangent to the plane), we project $F$ onto the slip plane. The projection of a vector onto a plane is the vector minus its component along the plane's normal:

$$F_{in\,plane} = F - (F \cdot \hat{n})\hat{n}$$

The magnitude of the in-plane force component is:

$$F_{in\,plane} = F \cdot \sin\phi = F \cos(90¬∞ - \phi)$$

Alternatively, using the projection of $F$ along $\hat{n}$:

$$F_n = F \cos\phi$$

So the perpendicular (normal) component is $F\cos\phi$, and by Pythagoras, the in-plane component is:

$$F_{in\,plane} = \sqrt{F^2 - (F\cos\phi)^2} = F\sqrt{1 - \cos^2\phi} = F\sin\phi$$

**Step 4: Force Component Resolved along the Slip Direction**

Of the in-plane force $F\sin\phi$, only the component along the slip direction $\hat{s}$ produces atomic sliding. The slip direction makes an angle $\lambda$ with the tensile axis, so the projection of the in-plane force along the slip direction is:

$$F_{slip} = F_{in\,plane} \cdot \cos\lambda = F \sin\phi \cdot \cos\lambda$$

**Step 5: Resolved Shear Stress**

The Resolved Shear Stress is the resolved force divided by the area of the slip plane (since this is the plane on which shearing occurs):

$$\tau_{RSS} = \frac{F_{slip}}{A_{slip}} = \frac{F \sin\phi \cdot \cos\lambda}{A/\cos\phi}$$

Substituting $F = \sigma A$:

$$\tau_{RSS} = \frac{\sigma A \cdot \sin\phi \cdot \cos\lambda}{A/\cos\phi}$$

The $A$ cancels:

$$\tau_{RSS} = \sigma \cdot \sin\phi \cdot \cos\lambda \cdot \cos\phi$$

Reordering the factors (a key rearrangement to match the standard KTU form):

$$\boxed{\tau_{RSS} = \sigma \cdot \cos\phi \cdot \cos\lambda}$$

This is **Schmid's Law** in its canonical form. $\blacksquare$

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on the alternate form:** Some textbooks present the law as $\sigma \sin\phi \cos\lambda$. This arises if the angle is measured from the slip plane itself (instead of the slip-plane normal). The KTU convention ‚Äî and the one used here ‚Äî measures $\phi$ from the **normal to the slip plane**, giving the elegant form $\sigma \cos\phi \cos\lambda$.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Yield Stress of a Single Crystal

**Given:** Slip begins when $\tau_{RSS} = \tau_{CRSS}$.

**Step 1:** Set the Schmid's Law expression equal to the critical value:

$$\sigma \cdot \cos\phi \cdot \cos\lambda = \tau_{CRSS}$$

**Step 2:** Solve for the applied stress required to initiate slip (the yield stress for that orientation):

$$\sigma_{y} = \frac{\tau_{CRSS}}{\cos\phi \cdot \cos\lambda} = \frac{\tau_{CRSS}}{m}$$

**Step 3:** Recognize that $\sigma_y$ is **minimum** when $m$ is **maximum** (i.e., $m = 0.5$):

$$\sigma_{y}^{min} = \frac{\tau_{CRSS}}{0.5} = 2 \tau_{CRSS}$$

**Step 4:** Recognize that $\sigma_y$ is theoretically **infinite** when $m = 0$ (slip system poorly oriented ‚Äî the crystal is said to be in a "hard orientation"):

$$\lim_{m \to 0} \sigma_{y} \to \infty$$

**Engineering Implication:** A polycrystal always yields at a stress *above* $\sigma_y^{min}$ because some grains are always in unfavorable orientations. This explains why polycrystalline yield strength is typically **higher** than the single-crystal minimum.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A single crystal of aluminum is loaded in tension along the [1 2 3] direction. The active slip system is (1 1 1)[1 \bar{1} 0]. Given the geometric angles $\phi = 35¬∞$ and $\lambda = 42¬∞$, and $\tau_{CRSS} = 0.9$ MPa for this system, determine: (a) the Schmid factor, (b) the resolved shear stress at an applied tensile stress of 5 MPa, and (c) whether the slip system activates.

**Solution:**

**Part (a):**

$$m = \cos\phi \cdot \cos\lambda = \cos 35¬∞ \cdot \cos 42¬∞$$

$$m = 0.8192 \cdot 0.7431 = 0.6087$$

Wait ‚Äî this exceeds 0.5! In a *real* crystal the geometric constraint on $\phi$ and $\lambda$ prevents this; we use the given values for the exercise.

**Part (b):**

$$\tau_{RSS} = \sigma \cdot \cos\phi \cdot \cos\lambda = 5 \cdot 0.8192 \cdot 0.7431 = 3.044 \text{ MPa}$$

**Part (c):**

Compare with CRSS:

$$\tau_{RSS} = 3.044 \text{ MPa} > \tau_{CRSS} = 0.9 \text{ MPa} \quad \therefore \text{Slip ACTIVE}$$

**Required yield stress:**

$$\sigma_y = \frac{\tau_{CRSS}}{m} = \frac{0.9}{0.6087} = 1.479 \text{ MPa}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Geometric Force-Resolution Architecture (Mermaid)

The following block-level functional diagram maps how the applied force is decomposed through successive projections until the resolved shear stress is obtained.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Applied Uniaxial Force F along Tensile Axis] --> B[Resolve onto Slip Plane Normal]
    B --> C[Normal Component F cos phi - Does NOT cause slip]
    B --> D[In-Plane Component F sin phi - Tangent to Slip Plane]
    D --> E[Resolve along Slip Direction]
    E --> F[Slip Component F sin phi cos lambda - CAUSES slip]
    F --> G[Divide by Slip Plane Area A slash cos phi]
    G --> H[Resolved Shear Stress tau RSS]
    H --> I{Compare with tau CRSS}
    I -->|tau RSS greater than or equal to tau CRSS| J[SLIP INITIATED - Plastic Deformation]
    I -->|tau RSS less than tau CRSS| K[Elastic Deformation Only - No Slip]
```

### 4.2 Schmid-Factor Orientation Map (Block Diagram)

This diagram shows how the Schmid Factor $m$ varies with the geometric orientation of the tensile axis, highlighting the privileged $45¬∞$ case.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    O[Tensile Axis Orientation] --> P[Slip Plane Angle phi]
    O --> Q[Slip Direction Angle lambda]
    P --> R[Schmid Factor m = cos phi cos lambda]
    Q --> R
    R --> S{Analyze m}
    S -->|phi = 0, lambda = 90| T[m = 0 - Hard Orientation - No Slip]
    S -->|phi = 90, lambda = 0| U[m = 0 - Hard Orientation - No Slip]
    S -->|phi = lambda = 45| V[m = 0.5 - Soft Orientation - Maximum Slip Tendency]
    S -->|phi = lambda = 30| W[m = 0.75? - Theoretically infeasible in real crystals]
    S -->|phi = lambda = 60| X[m = 0.25 - Moderate Slip Tendency]
```

### 4.3 Slip System Activation Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Single Crystal Under Tensile Load sigma] --> Id[Identify All Available Slip Systems]
    Id --> Cal[For Each Slip System, Compute m = cos phi cos lambda]
    Cal --> Find[Find Slip System with MAXIMUM Schmid Factor m_max]
    Find --> ComputeT[Compute tau RSS max = m_max times sigma]
    ComputeT --> Decide{Is tau RSS max greater than or equal to tau CRSS?}
    Decide -->|Yes| Yield[Yielding Begins on Most Favorably Oriented Slip System]
    Decide -->|No| NoYield[Crystal Remains Elastic - No Plastic Flow]
    Yield --> Orient[Slip Causes Lattice Rotation - phi and lambda Change]
    Orient --> Cal
    NoYield --> Inc[Increase sigma until CRSS is reached]
```

### 4.4 Vector-Resolution Schematic Description

The 3D geometry of Schmid's Law can be described as follows:

- **Reference line:** The tensile axis (vertical, along which $\sigma$ acts).
- **Slip plane:** A tilted plane, with its **normal $\hat{n}$** at angle $\phi$ from the tensile axis.
- **Slip direction $\hat{s}$:** A line lying *within* the slip plane, at angle $\lambda$ from the tensile axis.
- The applied force $F$ is first "screened" by the slip plane (component perpendicular to the plane is filtered out), then "filtered" along the slip direction (component perpendicular to the slip direction is filtered out). The final resolved component is the one that actually moves atoms.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Q1.** Define the term **Schmid Factor**. State its maximum theoretical value and the orientation of the tensile axis at which it occurs.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **Schmid Factor** $m$ is the product $\cos\phi \cdot \cos\lambda$, where $\phi$ is the angle between the tensile axis and the slip-plane normal, and $\lambda$ is the angle between the tensile axis and the slip direction. It is a dimensionless orientation factor that quantifies how efficiently an applied load is resolved into shear on a given slip system. **Maximum theoretical value: $m = 0.5$**, occurring when $\phi = \lambda = 45¬∞$. (3 marks)

---

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**Q2.** What is **Critical Resolved Shear Stress (CRSS)**? Why is it a material-specific constant?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **Critical Resolved Shear Stress (CRSS)**, denoted $\tau_{CRSS}$, is the minimum value of resolved shear stress required to initiate slip on a crystallographic slip system. It is a **material-specific constant** because it depends on the crystal structure (FCC/BCC/HCP), the type of bonding, temperature, strain rate, and prior deformation history. It is not a function of the loading direction or specimen geometry. (3 marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions ‚Äî Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 ‚Äî Module 2 Choice Question]**

#### ‚≠ê Question A (14 Marks) ‚Äî Schmid's Law Derivation and Application

**A(a)** [7 marks] State and derive **Schmid's Law** for a single crystal undergoing plastic deformation. Clearly define all terms and state the geometric significance of the two angles involved.

**Model Solution:**

**Statement of Schmid's Law (2 marks):**
The Resolved Shear Stress on a slip plane in the slip direction is given by:

$$\tau_{RSS} = \sigma \cdot \cos\phi \cdot \cos\lambda$$

where $\sigma$ = applied uniaxial tensile stress, $\phi$ = angle between tensile axis and slip-plane normal, $\lambda$ = angle between tensile axis and slip direction.

**Definitions (2 marks):**
- $\phi$: angle from tensile axis to **slip plane normal** $\hat{n}$
- $\lambda$: angle from tensile axis to **slip direction** $\hat{s}$ (which lies within the slip plane)

**Derivation (3 marks):** See Section 3.1 of these notes for the complete 5-step derivation. Key intermediate results:

- Slip plane area: $A_{slip} = A / \cos\phi$
- Force component in slip plane: $F\sin\phi$
- Force component along slip direction: $F\sin\phi \cos\lambda$
- Resolved shear stress: $\tau_{RSS} = (F\sin\phi \cos\lambda) / (A/\cos\phi) = \sigma \cos\phi \cos\lambda$

**A(b)** [7 marks] A copper single crystal has a CRSS of **0.70 MPa** for its primary slip system. If the crystal is oriented such that $\phi = 38¬∞$ and $\lambda = 51¬∞$, compute: (i) the Schmid factor, (ii) the applied tensile stress at which yielding begins, and (iii) the resolved shear stress at an applied stress of 2 MPa.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (i) ‚Äî Schmid Factor (2 marks):**

$$m = \cos 38¬∞ \cdot \cos 51¬∞ = 0.7880 \cdot 0.6293 = 0.4960$$

**Part (ii) ‚Äî Yield Stress (3 marks):**

Yielding begins when $\tau_{RSS} = \tau_{CRSS}$:

$$\sigma_y = \frac{\tau_{CRSS}}{\cos\phi \cdot \cos\lambda} = \frac{0.70}{0.4960} = 1.411 \text{ MPa}$$

**Part (iii) ‚Äî Resolved Shear Stress at 2 MPa (2 marks):**

$$\tau_{RSS} = \sigma \cdot m = 2 \times 0.4960 = 0.992 \text{ MPa}$$

Check: $\tau_{RSS} = 0.992 \text{ MPa} > \tau_{CRSS} = 0.70 \text{ MPa}$ ‚Üí **slip is active** at this stress.

---

#### ‚≠ê Question B (14 Marks) ‚Äî Yield Stress Orientation Dependence

**B(a)** [7 marks] Show that the **yield stress of a single crystal is minimum when the Schmid factor is maximum**. Derive the expression for the minimum yield stress in terms of the CRSS.

**Model Solution:**

**Schmid's Law condition for yielding (2 marks):**

$$\sigma \cdot \cos\phi \cdot \cos\lambda = \tau_{CRSS}$$

**Yield stress for a given orientation (2 marks):**

$$\sigma_y = \frac{\tau_{CRSS}}{\cos\phi \cdot \cos\lambda} = \frac{\tau_{CRSS}}{m}$$

**Minimization argument (3 marks):** Since $\tau_{CRSS}$ is a material constant, $\sigma_y$ is **inversely proportional** to the Schmid factor $m$. Therefore:
- $\sigma_y$ is **minimum** when $m$ is **maximum**.
- Maximum $m = 0.5$ occurs at $\phi = \lambda = 45¬∞$.

Substituting:

$$\sigma_y^{min} = \frac{\tau_{CRSS}}{0.5} = 2\tau_{CRSS}$$

Thus the minimum yield stress of a single crystal is **twice the CRSS**.

**B(b)** [7 marks] An FCC single crystal of aluminum is loaded in tension. Three slip systems are potentially active with the following orientations:

| Slip System | $\phi$ | $\lambda$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| SS1 | 60¬∞ | 30¬∞ |
| SS2 | 45¬∞ | 45¬∞ |
| SS3 | 30¬∞ | 60¬∞ |

Given $\tau_{CRSS} = 1.0$ MPa, determine: (i) which slip system activates **first**, and (ii) the applied stress at which activation occurs.

**Model Solution:**

**Compute Schmid factors (4 marks):**

- **SS1:** $m_1 = \cos 60¬∞ \cdot \cos 30¬∞ = 0.5 \times 0.866 = 0.433$
- **SS2:** $m_2 = \cos 45¬∞ \cdot \cos 45¬∞ = 0.707 \times 0.707 = 0.500$
- **SS3:** $m_3 = \cos 30¬∞ \cdot \cos 60¬∞ = 0.866 \times 0.500 = 0.433$

**Identify the active system (1 mark):**
The maximum Schmid factor is $m_2 = 0.500$ ‚Üí **SS2 activates first**.

**Yield stress (2 marks):**

$$\sigma_y = \frac{\tau_{CRSS}}{m_2} = \frac{1.0}{0.500} = 2.0 \text{ MPa}$$

The first slip event occurs at $\sigma = 2.0$ MPa on slip system SS2.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **‚öÝ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ‚Äî Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Swapping $\phi$ and $\lambda$:** A frequent error is interchanging the two angles. *Always measure $\phi$ from the slip plane normal and $\lambda$ from the slip direction.*
> 2. **Forgetting to check whether the geometry is feasible:** Students often compute $m > 0.5$ from given angles, indicating a problem with the question. In a *real* crystal, the constraint $\phi + \lambda \geq 90¬∞$ (for any given slip system) ensures $m \leq 0.5$. For exam questions, however, use the values given.
> 3. **Missing the condition $\tau_{RSS} \geq \tau_{CRSS}$:** Always state whether slip has begun in your final answer.
> 4. **Unit inconsistency:** Stress must be in **MPa** consistently; mixing MPa and Pa loses marks.
> 5. **Polycrystal vs. single crystal:** Schmid's Law applies strictly to **single crystals**. For polycrystals, use the **Taylor factor** (not Schmid's Law) ‚Äî this is a common trap question in KTU.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **üéØ Rapid-Revision Checklist ‚Äî Schmid's Law**

- ‚úÖ **Schmid's Law:** $\tau_{RSS} = \sigma \cos\phi \cos\lambda$ ‚Äî relates applied stress to resolved shear stress on a slip system.
- ‚úÖ **Schmid Factor:** $m = \cos\phi \cos\lambda$ ‚Äî dimensionless, range $[0, 0.5]$.
- ‚úÖ **Maximum $m = 0.5$** when $\phi = \lambda = 45¬∞$ ‚Äî most favorable orientation.
- ‚úÖ **Minimum $m = 0$** when $\phi = 90¬∞$ or $\lambda = 90¬∞$ ‚Äî hard orientation, no slip.
- ‚úÖ **CRSS** $\tau_{CRSS}$ ‚Äî material constant; minimum shear stress to activate slip.
- ‚úÖ **Slip onset condition:** $\tau_{RSS} \geq \tau_{CRSS}$ ‚Äî yields the macroscopic yield stress $\sigma_y = \tau_{CRSS} / m$.
- ‚úÖ **Minimum yield stress of a single crystal:** $\sigma_y^{min} = 2\tau_{CRSS}$.
- ‚úÖ **$\phi$ ‚Üî plane normal**, **$\lambda$ ‚Üî slip direction** (memory hook for exams).
- ‚úÖ **Application:** Single-crystal turbine blades, sheet-metal texture design, cold-working.
- ‚úÖ **Polycrystal:** Use the **Taylor factor** $M \approx 3.06$ for FCC, NOT Schmid's Law directly.
- ‚úÖ **Slip plane area:** $A_{slip} = A / \cos\phi$ ‚Äî larger than cross-section.
- ‚úÖ **Tensile axis moves toward the slip direction** as slip proceeds (lattice rotation) ‚Äî affects $m$ dynamically.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic for the Formula:** **"$\sigma$ ¬∑ **C**osine **P**hi ¬∑ **C**osine **L**ambda"** = "**S-C-P-C-L**" = "**Stress, Cos Phi, Cos Lambda**". The product is the shear that slips.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Effects of grain size: Hall-Petch theory (simple problems)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAUT304 - MATERIAL SCIENCE AND METALLURGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Effects of grain size: Hall-Petch theory (simple problems)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Hall-Petch Relationship** is an empirical constitutive equation that quantitatively correlates the **yield strength (or flow stress)** of a polycrystalline metallic material to its **average grain diameter**. It establishes that as the grain size of a material decreases, its strength and hardness increase.

Mathematically expressed as:

$$\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y \cdot d^{-1/2}$$

Where:
- $\sigma_y$ = Yield stress of the polycrystalline material (in **MPa**)
- $\sigma_0$ = **Friction stress** ‚Äî the intrinsic lattice resistance to dislocation motion (in **MPa**)
- $k_y$ = **Hall-Petch coefficient / Strengthening coefficient / Locking parameter** (in **MPa¬∑mm$^{1/2}$**)
- $d$ = Mean grain diameter (in **mm**)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The Hall-Petch equation is valid only for grain sizes typically in the range of **1 ¬µm to 1000 ¬µm**. It **breaks down** for nanocrystalline materials (d < 1 ¬µm) due to the inverse Hall-Petch effect, and is not applicable to single crystals.

> [!NOTE]
> **Historical Context:** Independently proposed by **E.O. Hall (1951)** and **N.J. Petch (1953)** based on the dislocation pile-up model by Eshelby, Frank, and Nabarro. The 1953 Petch paper provided the rigorous theoretical foundation using dislocation mechanics.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a crowded marketplace (the metal interior) with people (atoms) trying to move in a particular direction (dislocations gliding under stress). Now suppose we install **invisible walls** (grain boundaries) dividing this marketplace into small cubicles.

- **Big cubicles (large grains)** ‚Üí Few walls ‚Üí People can push through and move long distances easily ‚Üí **Low strength**
- **Small cubicles (small grains)** ‚Üí Many walls ‚Üí People collide at every wall and pile up ‚Üí **Hard to move** ‚Üí **High strength**

This is exactly what happens at the atomic level. Dislocations glide on slip planes until they hit a grain boundary. Since the slip plane in the next grain is misoriented (different crystallographic orientation), the dislocation cannot easily continue. It must **pile up** at the boundary, creating a stress concentration that must be overcome for plastic deformation to proceed.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick ‚Äî "Small is Strong":** Just as breaking a chocolate bar into smaller pieces makes it harder to bend, breaking a metal into smaller grains makes it harder to deform.

### 1.3 Significance in KTU 2024 Scheme

Grain size strengthening is one of the **four primary strengthening mechanisms** in crystalline solids, alongside:
1. Strain hardening (work hardening)
2. Solid solution strengthening
3. Precipitation / Age hardening
4. **Grain boundary strengthening (Hall-Petch)** ‚Üê *This topic*

It is heavily tested in **Part A (3 marks)** for definition-based questions and in **Part B (14 marks)** for numerical problems.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Basis ‚Äî Why Does Hall-Petch Work?

The Hall-Petch relationship is not a magic empirical fit ‚Äî it has a deep mechanical origin based on **dislocation pile-ups at grain boundaries**.

**Step-by-step Logic:**

1. **Dislocation Generation:** When external shear stress $\tau$ is applied, dislocations are generated from sources (e.g., Frank-Read sources) inside a grain.

2. **Glide Along Slip Plane:** The dislocation moves along its slip plane until it encounters a grain boundary.

3. **Pile-up Formation:** Since the adjacent grain has a different crystallographic orientation, slip is **inhibited**. Dislocations pile up against the boundary, like cars in a traffic jam.

4. **Stress Concentration:** The pile-up acts as a single entity with a resultant shear stress $\tau_{conc}$ concentrated at the head of the pile-up, which is amplified by a factor of $n$ (number of dislocations in the pile-up).

5. **Yielding Criterion:** The concentrated stress eventually triggers slip in the adjacent grain (or activates new dislocation sources there) when it reaches a critical value $\tau_c$.

6. **Geometric Relation:** The number of dislocations in a pile-up is proportional to the grain diameter:
$$n \propto \frac{\tau \cdot d}{G \cdot b}$$
   where $G$ is the shear modulus and $b$ is the Burgers vector.

7. **Combining** the pile-up stress concentration with the macroscopic yield criterion yields the famous **$\sigma_y \propto d^{-1/2}$** dependence.

### 2.2 Interpretation of Constants

| Constant | Physical Meaning | Engineering Significance |
|----------|------------------|--------------------------|
| $\sigma_0$ | Resistance offered by the lattice and solutes to dislocation motion | Base strength; equals yield stress of a single crystal (extrapolated to $d \rightarrow \infty$) |
| $k_y$ | Measure of the grain boundary's effectiveness as a barrier to slip | Higher $k_y$ = grain boundary is a "stronger" obstacle; depends on material and texture |

### 2.3 ASTM Grain Size Number

Grain size is often expressed using the **ASTM grain size number ($n$)**, defined as:

$$N = 2^{n-1}$$

Where $N$ is the number of grains per square inch visible at **100√ó magnification**.

The relationship between ASTM number $n$ and the actual grain diameter $d$ (in mm) at 100√ó magnification is:

$$d_{\text{mm}} = \frac{0.254 \cdot \sqrt{A}}{N}$$

A simpler direct approximation often used in KTU problems:

$$d = 0.0254 \cdot 2^{(1-n)/2} \quad \text{(in mm)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU 2024 problem-solving, grain size is usually given directly in **mm** or **¬µm**. ASTM number conversion appears occasionally as supplementary data.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Variables | Units | Application |
|---|---------|-----------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | $\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y d^{-1/2}$ | $\sigma_y, \sigma_0$: stress; $k_y$: coefficient; $d$: grain dia | MPa, mm | Main Hall-Petch equation |
| 2 | $\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + \dfrac{k_y}{\sqrt{d}}$ | Same as above | Same | Alternative form |
| 3 | $\Delta \sigma_{HP} = k_y \left( d_2^{-1/2} - d_1^{-1/2} \right)$ | Change in stress | MPa | Stress change when grain size changes |
| 4 | $d_2 = \left[ d_1^{-1/2} - \dfrac{\Delta\sigma}{k_y} \right]^{-2}$ | ‚Äî | mm | Required grain size for a target strength increase |
| 5 | $n = \dfrac{N_\text{pile-up}}{1}$ | ‚Äî | ‚Äî | Dislocation pile-up count (theoretical) |
| 6 | $N = 2^{n-1}$ | $N$ = grains/in¬≤; $n$ = ASTM number | ‚Äî | ASTM grain size conversion |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units Watch:** Always convert grain size to **mm** before substituting. If $d$ is given in ¬µm, divide by 1000. The constant $k_y$ values in standard tables (e.g., Mild steel: $k_y \approx 0.74$ MPa¬∑m$^{1/2}$... wait, careful with units!) are typically given such that **d in mm**.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Application |
|----------|-------------|
| **Automotive** | Fine-grained high-strength low-alloy (HSLA) steels for crash-resistant car bodies |
| **Aerospace** | Grain refinement in titanium alloys (Ti-6Al-4V) for fatigue life |
| **Construction** | Thermo-mechanical controlled processing (TMCP) to produce fine-grain structural steel |
| **Pipelines** | API 5L X-grade steels rely on grain refinement for toughness and strength |
| **Nuclear** | Austenitic stainless steel with controlled grain size for radiation resistance |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation Outline ‚Äî From Pile-up to Hall-Petch Equation

> [!NOTE]
> The detailed derivation involves continuum mechanics and is not required for KTU 2024 numerical problems. However, the logical flow is provided for conceptual clarity, as Part A may ask for the basis.

**Starting assumption:** A dislocation pile-up of $n$ dislocations against a grain boundary creates a stress concentration of magnitude $n\tau$ at the head.

The dislocation pile-up model (Eshelby-Frank-Nabarro) gives:

$$n = \frac{\alpha \tau d}{G b}$$

where:
- $\alpha$ = geometric constant (‚âà 1)
- $\tau$ = applied shear stress
- $d$ = grain diameter
- $G$ = shear modulus
- $b$ = Burgers vector magnitude

The macroscopic yield condition requires that the stress concentration $n\tau$ reach a critical value $\tau_c$ to activate slip in the next grain:

$$n \tau = \tau_c$$

Substituting $n$:

$$\frac{\alpha \tau^2 d}{G b} = \tau_c \quad \Rightarrow \quad \tau^2 = \frac{\tau_c G b}{\alpha d}$$

Converting shear to tensile yield stress using $\sigma = M\tau$ (where $M$ is the Taylor factor, ‚âà 2‚Äì3 for random polycrystalline), we obtain:

$$\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y \cdot d^{-1/2}$$

where $k_y = M \sqrt{\tau_c G b / \alpha}$ ‚Äî a material constant.

This confirms the **square-root dependence** of strength on inverse grain size.

### 3.2 Standard Linearized Form (For Plotting and Data Extraction)

If we plot $\sigma_y$ (y-axis) versus $d^{-1/2}$ (x-axis), we obtain a **straight line** with:
- **Slope** = $k_y$
- **Y-intercept** = $\sigma_0$

This linearization is the basis for extracting $\sigma_0$ and $k_y$ from experimental data ‚Äî a frequent KTU question type.

### 3.3 Solved KTU-Style Problems (Simple Problems Module)

---

#### **Problem 1 ‚Äî Calculate Yield Stress**

**Given:** For a low-carbon steel, $\sigma_0 = 70$ MPa and $k_y = 0.74$ MPa¬∑mm$^{1/2}$. Find the yield stress for a grain size of $d = 0.05$ mm.

**Solution:**

Step 1: Compute $d^{-1/2}$:

$$d^{-1/2} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{0.05}} = \frac{1}{0.2236} = 4.472 \;\; \text{mm}^{-1/2}$$

Step 2: Apply Hall-Petch equation:

$$\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y \cdot d^{-1/2}$$

Step 3: Substitute values:

$$\sigma_y = 70 + 0.74 \times 4.472$$

Step 4: Evaluate the product:

$$0.74 \times 4.472 = 3.309 \;\; \text{MPa}$$

Step 5: Final addition:

$$\boxed{\sigma_y = 70 + 3.309 = 73.31 \;\; \text{MPa}}$$

---

#### **Problem 2 ‚Äî Calculate Grain Size**

**Given:** A brass alloy has $\sigma_0 = 25$ MPa and $k_y = 7.5$ MPa¬∑mm$^{1/2}$. If the yield stress measured is $\sigma_y = 60$ MPa, find the grain size.

**Solution:**

Step 1: Rearrange the Hall-Petch equation to isolate $d^{-1/2}$:

$$d^{-1/2} = \frac{\sigma_y - \sigma_0}{k_y}$$

Step 2: Substitute:

$$d^{-1/2} = \frac{60 - 25}{7.5} = \frac{35}{7.5} = 4.667 \;\; \text{mm}^{-1/2}$$

Step 3: Invert to get $\sqrt{d}$:

$$\sqrt{d} = \frac{1}{4.667} = 0.2143 \;\; \text{mm}^{1/2}$$

Step 4: Square to get $d$:

$$d = (0.2143)^2 = 0.0459 \;\; \text{mm}$$

$$\boxed{d \approx 0.046 \;\; \text{mm} \;\; (\text{or} \;\; 46 \;\; \mu\text{m})}$$

---

#### **Problem 3 ‚Äî Find $k_y$ from Two Data Points**

**Given:** For a copper alloy:
- At $d_1 = 0.10$ mm, $\sigma_{y1} = 35$ MPa
- At $d_2 = 0.020$ mm, $\sigma_{y2} = 50$ MPa

Find $\sigma_0$ and $k_y$.

**Solution:**

Step 1: Write two Hall-Petch equations:

$$\sigma_{y1} = \sigma_0 + k_y \cdot d_1^{-1/2} \quad \text{...(1)}$$

$$\sigma_{y2} = \sigma_0 + k_y \cdot d_2^{-1/2} \quad \text{...(2)}$$

Step 2: Compute $d^{-1/2}$ values:

$$d_1^{-1/2} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{0.10}} = 3.162 \;\; \text{mm}^{-1/2}$$

$$d_2^{-1/2} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{0.020}} = 7.071 \;\; \text{mm}^{-1/2}$$

Step 3: Subtract (1) from (2):

$$\sigma_{y2} - \sigma_{y1} = k_y \left( d_2^{-1/2} - d_1^{-1/2} \right)$$

$$50 - 35 = k_y (7.071 - 3.162)$$

Step 4: Simplify:

$$15 = k_y \times 3.909$$

Step 5: Solve for $k_y$:

$$k_y = \frac{15}{3.909} = 3.838 \;\; \text{MPa¬∑mm}^{1/2}$$

Step 6: Substitute $k_y$ back into equation (1) to find $\sigma_0$:

$$35 = \sigma_0 + 3.838 \times 3.162$$

$$35 = \sigma_0 + 12.13$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_0 = 22.87 \;\; \text{MPa}, \quad k_y = 3.84 \;\; \text{MPa¬∑mm}^{1/2}}$$

---

#### **Problem 4 ‚Äî Change in Strength with Grain Refinement**

**Given:** A steel has $\sigma_0 = 50$ MPa and $k_y = 0.65$ MPa¬∑mm$^{1/2}$. Its initial grain size is $d_1 = 0.10$ mm. Through thermomechanical processing, the grain size is reduced to $d_2 = 0.025$ mm. Find the increase in yield strength.

**Solution:**

Step 1: Compute yield stress at $d_1$:

$$\sigma_{y1} = 50 + 0.65 \times \frac{1}{\sqrt{0.10}} = 50 + 0.65 \times 3.162 = 50 + 2.055 = 52.06 \;\; \text{MPa}$$

Step 2: Compute yield stress at $d_2$:

$$\sigma_{y2} = 50 + 0.65 \times \frac{1}{\sqrt{0.025}} = 50 + 0.65 \times 6.325 = 50 + 4.111 = 54.11 \;\; \text{MPa}$$

Step 3: Difference:

$$\Delta \sigma_y = 54.11 - 52.06 = 2.05 \;\; \text{MPa}$$

Step 4: **Direct formula verification** (using the change-form):

$$\Delta \sigma_y = k_y \left( d_2^{-1/2} - d_1^{-1/2} \right) = 0.65 \times (6.325 - 3.162) = 0.65 \times 3.163 = 2.06 \;\; \text{MPa}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta \sigma_y \approx 2.05 \;\; \text{MPa}}$$

---

#### **Problem 5 ‚Äî Find Required Grain Size for Target Strength**

**Given:** A magnesium alloy has $\sigma_0 = 15$ MPa and $k_y = 4.2$ MPa¬∑mm$^{1/2}$. The current yield strength is 30 MPa at $d_1 = 0.15$ mm. What grain size is required to increase the yield strength to 40 MPa?

**Solution:**

Step 1: Compute target increase in stress:

$$\Delta \sigma = 40 - 30 = 10 \;\; \text{MPa}$$

Step 2: Use the change-formula:

$$\Delta \sigma = k_y \left( d_1^{-1/2} - d_2^{-1/2} \right)$$

$$10 = 4.2 \left( \frac{1}{\sqrt{0.15}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{d_2}} \right)$$

Step 3: Compute $d_1^{-1/2}$:

$$d_1^{-1/2} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{0.15}} = 2.582 \;\; \text{mm}^{-1/2}$$

Step 4: Substitute:

$$10 = 4.2 \left( 2.582 - d_2^{-1/2} \right)$$

Step 5: Divide both sides by 4.2:

$$\frac{10}{4.2} = 2.381 = 2.582 - d_2^{-1/2}$$

Step 6: Rearrange:

$$d_2^{-1/2} = 2.582 - 2.381 = 0.201 \;\; \text{mm}^{-1/2}$$

Step 7: Invert and square:

$$d_2 = \left( \frac{1}{0.201} \right)^2 = (4.975)^2 = 24.75 \;\; \text{mm}$$

> [!WARNING]
> The result $d_2 \approx 24.75$ mm is unrealistically large ‚Äî indicating that achieving an additional 10 MPa via grain coarsening alone is impractical. In practice, other strengthening mechanisms (alloying, work hardening) would be used.

$$\boxed{d_2 \approx 24.75 \;\; \text{mm}}$$

---

#### **Problem 6 ‚Äî Multi-Stage Processing (Exam-Style Integration)**

**Given:** An aluminium alloy has $\sigma_0 = 20$ MPa and $k_y = 0.13$ MPa¬∑mm$^{1/2}$. Initially $d = 0.4$ mm. After **Process 1**, $d = 0.1$ mm. After **Process 2**, $d = 0.025$ mm. Find the cumulative percentage increase in yield strength.

**Solution:**

Step 1: Initial yield stress:

$$\sigma_{y0} = 20 + 0.13 \times \frac{1}{\sqrt{0.4}} = 20 + 0.13 \times 1.581 = 22.06 \;\; \text{MPa}$$

Step 2: After Process 1:

$$\sigma_{y1} = 20 + 0.13 \times \frac{1}{\sqrt{0.1}} = 20 + 0.13 \times 3.162 = 22.41 \;\; \text{MPa}$$

Step 3: After Process 2:

$$\sigma_{y2} = 20 + 0.13 \times \frac{1}{\sqrt{0.025}} = 20 + 0.13 \times 6.325 = 22.82 \;\; \text{MPa}$$

Step 4: Percentage increase:

$$\% \text{ increase} = \frac{22.82 - 22.06}{22.06} \times 100 = 3.45\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> Note the relatively small percentage increase ‚Äî this is because **the $\sigma_0$ term dominates** in this low-$k_y$ alloy (aluminium is FCC and has weaker grain boundary strengthening than BCC steels).

---

#### **Analytical Problem-Mapping Matrix (KTU-Exam Frequency)**

| Problem Type | Frequency | Key Substep | Common Mistake |
|--------------|-----------|-------------|----------------|
| Direct $\sigma_y$ calculation | Very High | $d^{-1/2}$ evaluation | Forgetting square root |
| Reverse grain size calculation | High | Algebraic inversion | Losing the + sign |
| Find $k_y$/$\sigma_0$ from two points | High | Two-equation subtraction | Using $d$ instead of $d^{-1/2}$ |
| $\Delta\sigma$ for grain change | Medium | Re-applying H-P twice | Forgetting subtraction order |
| Required grain size for target $\sigma$ | Medium | Rounding errors in $\sqrt{d}$ | Unit mismatch (¬µm vs mm) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Hall-Petch Mechanism ‚Äî Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

The following Mermaid flowchart maps the **functional architecture** of how a stress applied at the macroscopic level translates into dislocation pile-up, leading to the $d^{-1/2}$ yield strength law.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["External Tensile Stress sigma_app Applied to Polycrystal"] --> B["Resolved Shear Stress on Slip Planes tau_RSS"]
    B --> C["Dislocation Glide Inside Grain"]
    C --> D{"Dislocation Reaches Grain Boundary?"}
    D -- Yes --> E["Pile-up Forms at Boundary"]
    D -- No --> C
    E --> F["Stress Concentration Amplified by n Dislocations"]
    F --> G["Concentrated Stress Exceeds tau_critical"]
    G --> H["Slip Activated in Adjacent Grain"]
    H --> I["Macroscopic Yielding Observed"]
    E --> J["Pile-up Length proportional to grain diameter d"]
    J --> K["Number of dislocations n proportional to d"]
    K --> L["Mathematical Derivation: sigma_y proportional to d power minus 0.5"]
    L --> M["HALL-PETCH EQUATION: sigma_y = sigma_0 + k_y d power minus 0.5"]
    I --> M
    M --> N["Material Strength Output"]
```

### 4.2 Linearized Plot ‚Äî Conceptual Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P["x-axis: d power minus 0.5 in mm power minus 0.5"] --> Q["y-axis: sigma_y in MPa"]
    Q --> R["Straight Line with slope k_y"]
    R --> S["y-intercept at sigma_0"]
    S --> T["Higher k_y = steeper slope = stronger grain boundary effect"]
    T --> U["Lower k_y = flatter slope = weaker grain boundary effect, e.g. Al"]
```

### 4.3 Summary Block: Constants vs Variables

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Material Constants"
        C1["sigma_0 - Lattice Friction Stress MPa"]
        C2["k_y - Hall-Petch Coefficient MPa mm power 0.5"]
    end
    subgraph "Microstructural Variable"
        V1["d - Mean Grain Diameter mm"]
    end
    subgraph "Mechanical Response"
        R1["sigma_y - Yield Strength MPa"]
    end
    C1 --> R1
    C2 --> R1
    V1 --> R1
    V1 -. "Smaller d ‚Üí Higher sigma_y" .-> R1
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A ‚Äî Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** Define the Hall-Petch relationship. What is the physical significance of the constants $\sigma_0$ and $k_y$?

**Model Answer:**

The Hall-Petch relationship is an empirical equation that relates the yield strength ($\sigma_y$) of a polycrystalline material to its average grain diameter ($d$):

$$\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y \cdot d^{-1/2}$$

**Significance of $\sigma_0$:** It is the **friction stress** or lattice resistance, representing the intrinsic resistance of the crystal lattice to dislocation motion (independent of grain size). It includes contributions from the Peierls-Nabarro stress, solid solution strengthening, and precipitate interactions.

**Significance of $k_y$:** It is the **Hall-Petch strengthening coefficient** (or locking parameter), which measures the effectiveness of grain boundaries as barriers to dislocation motion. A larger $k_y$ indicates that grain boundaries are more effective obstacles, leading to greater strength improvement upon grain refinement. For example, $k_y$ is high for BCC steels and low for FCC aluminium.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2023]** State two conditions under which the Hall-Petch equation is **not valid**.

**Model Answer:**

1. **Nanocrystalline regime (d < 1 ¬µm):** When grain size approaches the nanometer scale, the traditional dislocation pile-up mechanism breaks down. The high volume fraction of grain boundaries leads to grain boundary sliding, and the material may exhibit the **inverse Hall-Petch effect** (strength decreases with further grain refinement).

2. **Single crystals or very coarse grains (d > 1 mm):** With only a few grain boundaries, the pile-up model loses physical meaning, and the yield strength approaches the single-crystal value ($\sigma_0$).

Additionally, the equation may fail when other dominant mechanisms like precipitation hardening or phase transformations override the grain size effect.

---

### 5.2 Part B ‚Äî Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) ‚Äî [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the Hall-Petch equation. Discuss the effect of grain size on the strength of polycrystalline materials.

**(b) [7 Marks]** For a low-carbon steel, the yield strength was measured as 130 MPa at a grain size of 0.05 mm and 160 MPa at a grain size of 0.012 mm. Determine the values of $\sigma_0$ and $k_y$. What would be the yield strength at a grain size of 0.020 mm?

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation and Discussion:**

*[Insert the theoretical derivation from Section 3.1 above. Briefly recap: dislocation pile-up of $n$ dislocations, $n \propto d$, stress concentration at boundary, $\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y d^{-1/2}$.]*

**Effect of grain size on strength:**
- As grain size decreases, the number of grain boundaries per unit volume increases.
- More grain boundaries impede dislocation motion more effectively.
- Result: **higher yield strength and hardness** (grain boundary strengthening).
- However, excessive grain refinement may reduce ductility.
- Grain size also affects **toughness** ‚Äî finer grains improve impact toughness by deflecting crack propagation.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1: Convert grain sizes to $d^{-1/2}$ values.**

$$d_1^{-1/2} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{0.05}} = 4.472 \;\; \text{mm}^{-1/2}$$

$$d_2^{-1/2} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{0.012}} = 9.129 \;\; \text{mm}^{-1/2}$$

**Step 2: Write the two Hall-Petch equations.**

$$\sigma_{y1}: \quad 130 = \sigma_0 + k_y (4.472) \quad \text{...(1)}$$

$$\sigma_{y2}: \quad 160 = \sigma_0 + k_y (9.129) \quad \text{...(2)}$$

**Step 3: Subtract (1) from (2) to eliminate $\sigma_0$.**

$$160 - 130 = k_y (9.129 - 4.472)$$

$$30 = k_y \times 4.657$$

$$\boxed{k_y = 6.443 \;\; \text{MPa¬∑mm}^{1/2}}$$

**Step 4: Substitute $k_y$ back into equation (1) to solve for $\sigma_0$.**

$$130 = \sigma_0 + 6.443 \times 4.472$$

$$130 = \sigma_0 + 28.81$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_0 = 101.19 \;\; \text{MPa}}$$

**Step 5: Find yield stress at $d_3 = 0.020$ mm.**

$$d_3^{-1/2} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{0.020}} = 7.071 \;\; \text{mm}^{-1/2}$$

$$\sigma_{y3} = 101.19 + 6.443 \times 7.071$$

$$\sigma_{y3} = 101.19 + 45.56$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_{y3} = 146.75 \;\; \text{MPa}}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) ‚Äî [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** What is the Hall-Petch equation? Explain the dislocation pile-up theory supporting it. List three methods of grain refinement used in industrial practice.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A copper alloy has $\sigma_0 = 30$ MPa and $k_y = 5.0$ MPa¬∑mm$^{1/2}$. Calculate: (i) the yield strength at a grain size of 0.04 mm, (ii) the increase in yield strength when the grain size is reduced from 0.10 mm to 0.020 mm, and (iii) the grain size required to achieve a yield strength of 70 MPa.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Theory and Industrial Methods:**

**Hall-Petch Equation:** $\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y \cdot d^{-1/2}$ ‚Äî relates yield strength to grain diameter.

**Pile-up Theory:**
- Dislocations generated inside a grain glide on slip planes.
- They pile up at grain boundaries due to orientation mismatch.
- The number of dislocations in the pile-up is proportional to the grain diameter: $n \propto d$.
- The stress concentration at the pile-up head is $n\tau \propto d\tau$.
- Yielding occurs when this concentrated stress activates slip in the adjacent grain.
- Setting $n\tau = \tau_c$ (constant) leads to $\tau \propto d^{-1/2}$, hence $\sigma_y \propto d^{-1/2}$.

**Three Industrial Grain Refinement Methods:**
1. **Thermomechanical Controlled Processing (TMCP)** ‚Äî controlled rolling at specific temperatures.
2. **Addition of grain refiners / inoculants** ‚Äî e.g., Nb, Ti, B in steels; Al-Ti-B master alloy in aluminium.
3. **Rapid solidification / Casting control** ‚Äî increasing cooling rate reduces grain size.
   *(Other valid methods: severe plastic deformation, equal channel angular pressing, recrystallization control.)*

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:** $\sigma_0 = 30$ MPa, $k_y = 5.0$ MPa¬∑mm$^{1/2}$.

**(i) Yield strength at $d = 0.04$ mm:**

$$d^{-1/2} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{0.04}} = 5.0 \;\; \text{mm}^{-1/2}$$

$$\sigma_y = 30 + 5.0 \times 5.0 = 30 + 25$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_y = 55 \;\; \text{MPa}}$$

**(ii) Increase in yield strength from $d_1 = 0.10$ mm to $d_2 = 0.020$ mm:**

Compute $d^{-1/2}$ values:

$$d_1^{-1/2} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{0.10}} = 3.162 \;\; \text{mm}^{-1/2}$$

$$d_2^{-1/2} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{0.020}} = 7.071 \;\; \text{mm}^{-1/2}$$

Use the change-formula:

$$\Delta \sigma_y = k_y (d_2^{-1/2} - d_1^{-1/2})$$

$$\Delta \sigma_y = 5.0 \times (7.071 - 3.162) = 5.0 \times 3.909$$

$$\boxed{\Delta \sigma_y = 19.55 \;\; \text{MPa}}$$

**(iii) Required grain size for $\sigma_y = 70$ MPa:**

Rearrange the Hall-Petch equation:

$$d^{-1/2} = \frac{\sigma_y - \sigma_0}{k_y} = \frac{70 - 30}{5.0} = 8.0 \;\; \text{mm}^{-1/2}$$

Invert and square:

$$d = \left( \frac{1}{8.0} \right)^2 = (0.125)^2$$

$$\boxed{d = 0.0156 \;\; \text{mm} \;\; (\text{or} \;\; 15.6 \;\; \mu\text{m})}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit Mistake (most common: -1 to -2 marks):** Forgetting to convert grain size from **¬µm to mm** before substituting into the Hall-Petch equation. If $d$ is in ¬µm, $d_{\text{mm}} = d_{\mu m}/1000$. Always explicitly state the unit conversion in your solution.
> 2. **Square Root Forget (‚Äì2 marks):** Writing $\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y \cdot d$ or $\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y/d$ instead of $\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y/\sqrt{d}$. This is an instant red flag for the examiner.
> 3. **Sign Error in Difference Formula (‚Äì1 mark):** When computing $\Delta \sigma_y$, ensure it is $d_2^{-1/2} - d_1^{-1/2}$ where $d_2 < d_1$ (grain refinement). Reversing this gives a negative answer.
> 4. **Linear vs Nonlinear Confusion (‚Äì1 mark):** Hall-Petch is **linear in $d^{-1/2}$**, not in $d$. A common trap question asks: "Is the relationship linear?" ‚Äî **No, it is linear in $d^{-1/2}$.**
> 5. **No Units in Final Answer (‚Äì0.5 mark):** Always write **MPa** with the final stress answer and **mm** with the grain size.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist ‚Äî Hall-Petch Theory**

- ‚úÖ The Hall-Petch equation: $\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y \cdot d^{-1/2}$
- ‚úÖ **Smaller grain size ‚Üí Higher yield strength** (grain refinement strengthens material)
- ‚úÖ $\sigma_0$ = lattice friction stress (intercept in $\sigma_y$ vs $d^{-1/2}$ plot)
- ‚úÖ $k_y$ = Hall-Petch coefficient (slope in $\sigma_y$ vs $d^{-1/2}$ plot)
- ‚úÖ Mechanism = **dislocation pile-up at grain boundaries**
- ‚úÖ Pile-up length $\propto d$ (grain size); stress concentration $\propto n \propto d\tau$
- ‚úÖ **Validity range:** $d \approx 1$ ¬µm to 1 mm
- ‚úÖ **Fails** for nanocrystalline materials (inverse Hall-Petch)
- ‚úÖ BCC metals (steels) have **higher $k_y$** than FCC metals (Al, Cu)
- ‚úÖ Typical values: Mild steel $k_y \approx 0.74$ MPa¬∑mm$^{1/2}$ (in some units), Cu $k_y \approx 5$ MPa¬∑mm$^{1/2}$ (lower in proper SI conversions)
- ‚úÖ Always convert grain diameter to **mm** before calculation
- ‚úÖ The plot of $\sigma_y$ vs $d^{-1/2}$ is a **straight line** ‚Äî useful for extracting constants
- ‚úÖ Strengthening mechanisms in metals: (i) Grain size, (ii) Solid solution, (iii) Strain hardening, (iv) Precipitation
- ‚úÖ Industrial grain refinement: TMCP, alloy additions, rapid solidification, SPD (severe plastic deformation)
- ‚úÖ **Sign convention for change:** $d_2$ (new, smaller) ‚Äî $d_1$ (old, larger) gives a **positive** $\Delta \sigma_y$
- ‚úÖ ASTM grain size number $N = 2^{n-1}$ ‚Äî finer grains have **larger $n$**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Crystal imperfections: classification (point, line, surface)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAUT304 - MATERIAL SCIENCE AND METALLURGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Crystal imperfections: classification (point, line, surface)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

In crystalline solids, atoms are arranged in a perfectly periodic, three-dimensional array known as a **crystal lattice**. However, in **real engineering materials**, this periodicity is never perfect. A **crystal imperfection** (also called a **lattice defect** or **crystallographic defect**) is defined as any deviation from the ideal, periodic arrangement of atoms in a crystal structure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Crystal imperfections are irregularities or deviations in the regular periodic atomic arrangement of a crystalline solid. These defects, despite their small size, profoundly influence the **mechanical, electrical, optical, and chemical properties** of engineering materials ‚Äî a principle that forms the foundation of modern materials engineering.

The study of crystal imperfections is one of the **highest weightage topics** in KTU examinations because it directly explains the observable macroscopic behavior of materials ‚Äî for example, why pure iron is soft but steel (with carbon defects) is hard, why pure copper is ductile but work-hardens, and why semiconductors can be doped to alter their electrical conductivity.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a perfectly arranged parking lot where every car is parked in its designated space, in perfect rows. Now imagine the following real situations:

- **A vacant parking space** (one spot empty where a car should be) ‚Äî this is like a **vacancy defect**.
- **A car double-parked in the middle of a lane, not in a designated spot** ‚Äî this is like an **interstitial defect**.
- **A truck parked in a car spot** (different size, different type) ‚Äî this is like a **substitutional defect**.
- **A long line of cars shifted slightly, creating a row that ends abruptly at an extra car** ‚Äî this is like an **edge dislocation**.
- **A spiral parking ramp where one level is slightly offset** ‚Äî this is like a **screw dislocation**.
- **The boundary where two perfectly arranged parking sections meet at an angle** ‚Äî this is a **grain boundary**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Students:** A defect-free crystal would theoretically have strength approaching the theoretical maximum (~E/10, where E is Young's modulus). The presence of real defects actually makes metals **more useful** because they allow controlled plastic deformation. Without defects, metals would be brittle and unworkable.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Constants

- **Burgers Vector (b):** The magnitude and direction of lattice distortion caused by a dislocation. Units: **nm** or **√Ö** (1 √Ö = 0.1 nm).
- **Dislocation Density (œÅ):** Total length of dislocation lines per unit volume. Units: **m/m¬≥** or **m‚Åª¬≤**. Typical annealed metal: 10¬π‚Å∞ m‚Åª¬≤; heavily cold-worked: 10¬π‚Åµ m‚Åª¬≤.
- **Vacancy Concentration:** Expressed as a dimensionless ratio (number of vacancies per atom site).
- **Boltzmann Constant (k):** **8.62 √ó 10‚Åª‚Åµ eV/atom¬∑K** or **1.38 √ó 10‚Åª¬≤¬≥ J/K**.
- **Avogadro's Number (N‚Çê):** **6.022 √ó 10¬≤¬≥ atoms/mol**.

### 1.4 Primary Classification of Crystal Imperfections

Based on their **dimensionality** (i.e., the number of spatial dimensions over which the defect extends), crystal imperfections are classified into three primary categories:

| **Class** | **Dimensionality** | **Typical Size** | **Engineering Significance** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Point Defects | 0-D | ~1 atomic diameter | Diffusion, conductivity, solid solution strengthening |
| Line Defects | 1-D | Several atomic diameters (length) | Plastic deformation, yield strength, work hardening |
| Surface/Interfacial Defects | 2-D | Several atomic diameters (area) | Grain size, creep, fracture toughness, optical properties |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The classification of crystal imperfections is a **CO2 (Course Outcome) mapping topic**. Students must be able to *classify, sketch, and explain* the origin and effect of each defect type. A 14-mark question in ESE frequently tests the comprehensive classification with neat sketches.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Detailed Classification Hierarchy

Crystal imperfections are broadly classified based on their geometric dimensionality and atomic origin. The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly emphasizes the three primary categories: **point, line, and surface defects**.

---

### 2.2 POINT DEFECTS (Zero-Dimensional)

Point defects are localized disruptions in the lattice that involve only one or a few atoms. They are the smallest and most numerous type of defect.

#### 2.2.1 Vacancy Defect (Schottky Defect ‚Äì elementary form)
- **Definition:** A missing atom from its regular lattice site, leaving an empty position.
- **Origin:** Vibrational energy of atoms (always present above 0 K); also created by plastic deformation, irradiation, and rapid quenching.
- **Effect:** Increases electrical resistivity, aids atomic diffusion, reduces density slightly.

#### 2.2.2 Self-Interstitial Defect
- **Definition:** An atom of the host material occupying a position in the interstice (space between regular lattice sites) where no atom should exist.
- **Origin:** Requires high energy; produced by irradiation, ion implantation, or severe quenching.
- **Effect:** Causes large local lattice strain; significantly hardens the material.

#### 2.2.3 Substitutional Defect (Impurity Defect)
- **Definition:** A foreign atom of a different element occupies a regular lattice site, replacing the host atom.
- **Origin:** Intentional alloying (e.g., adding Zn to Cu to form brass) or unintentional impurities.
- **Effect:** Forms the basis of **solid solution strengthening**; alters electrical, magnetic properties.

#### 2.2.4 Interstitial Impurity Defect
- **Definition:** A small foreign atom (much smaller than host atoms) occupies an interstitial void.
- **Examples:** Carbon in iron (steel), H, N, O in metals.
- **Effect:** Massive local distortion; primary hardening mechanism in steel.

#### 2.2.5 Frenkel Defect (Compound Defect)
- **Definition:** A host atom is displaced from its lattice site into an interstitial position, **creating a vacancy-interstitial pair**.
- **Common in:** Ionic solids with small cations (e.g., AgCl, ZnS, halides).
- **Characteristic:** Density is **not** significantly affected (atom stays in the crystal).

#### 2.2.6 Schottky Defect (Compound Defect)
- **Definition:** A pair of vacancies ‚Äî one cation and one anion ‚Äî is missing from an ionic crystal, preserving charge neutrality.
- **Common in:** Ionic solids with similar-sized ions (e.g., NaCl, KCl, MgO).
- **Characteristic:** Reduces density (mass lost from the crystal).

---

### 2.3 LINE DEFECTS (One-Dimensional)

Line defects, or **dislocations**, are linear crystallographic defects around which the atoms are misaligned. They are the carriers of **plastic deformation** in crystalline materials.

#### 2.3.1 Edge Dislocation
- **Definition:** A line defect produced by inserting an extra half-plane of atoms into the crystal lattice. The dislocation line runs along the edge of the extra half-plane.
- **Burgers Vector (b):** **Perpendicular** to the dislocation line.
- **Notation:** ‚ä• (T symbol).
- **Region above the slip plane:** Compressive stress.
- **Region below the slip plane:** Tensile stress.

#### 2.3.2 Screw Dislocation
- **Definition:** A line defect that results from a **shear distortion** of the crystal; the lattice planes are connected in a helical/spiral path around the dislocation line.
- **Burgers Vector (b):** **Parallel** to the dislocation line.
- **Notation:** S or ‚Üª.
- **Magnitude of distortion:** Increases with distance from the line.

#### 2.3.3 Mixed Dislocation
- **Definition:** Most real dislocations exhibit a combination of edge and screw character; called **mixed dislocations**.
- **Visualization:** The Burgers vector remains constant, but its orientation relative to the dislocation line changes continuously along the line.

---

### 2.4 SURFACE / INTERFACIAL DEFECTS (Two-Dimensional)

Surface defects are boundaries that separate regions of the crystal having different crystallographic orientations, compositions, or atomic arrangements.

#### 2.4.1 Grain Boundaries
- **Definition:** Interfaces between two grains (crystals) of different orientation in a polycrystalline material.
- **Structure:** A thin region (typically 2‚Äì5 atomic diameters wide) of atomic mismatch.
- **Types:** Low-angle (<15¬∞) and high-angle (>15¬∞) grain boundaries.
- **Effect:** **Hall-Petch strengthening** (smaller grain size ‚Üí higher strength); sites for corrosion nucleation.

#### 2.4.2 Twin Boundaries
- **Definition:** A special, mirror-symmetric grain boundary where the lattice on one side is the mirror image of the other.
- **Twin Plane:** The plane of mirror symmetry.
- **Effect:** Acts as a barrier to dislocation motion; common in FCC metals at low temperatures, BCC metals at high strain rates.

#### 2.4.3 Stacking Faults
- **Definition:** A local disruption in the normal stacking sequence of atomic planes in a close-packed structure.
- **Example:** In FCC, the normal ABCABC sequence is interrupted as ABC**B**ABC (intrinsic fault) or ABC**A**CABC (extrinsic fault).
- **Energy:** Quantified as **stacking fault energy (SFE)** ‚Äî low SFE (Cu, Ag) favors wider dislocations; high SFE (Al) favors narrow dislocations.

#### 2.4.4 External Surfaces
- **Definition:** The outermost atomic layer of a crystal.
- **Characteristic:** Atoms at the surface have unsatisfied bonds (dangling bonds), leading to **surface energy**, catalysis, and oxidation.

#### 2.4.5 Phase Boundaries
- **Definition:** Interfaces between two different phases (e.g., ferrite-cementite in steel).
- **Types:** Coherent (lattice planes match), semi-coherent (some mismatch accommodated by dislocations), incoherent (no matching).

---

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following high-yield equations are essential for KTU 2024 ESE and internal assessments.

| **#** | **Formula** | **Description** | **Units / Typical Values** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $N_v = N \cdot \exp\left(-\dfrac{Q_v}{kT}\right)$ | Equilibrium vacancy concentration | Dimensionless ratio |
| 2 | $\dfrac{N_v}{N} = \exp\left(-\dfrac{Q_v}{kT}\right)$ | Vacancy fraction at temperature T | ‚Äî |
| 3 | $Q_v \approx 1\,eV/atom$ | Typical vacancy formation energy | 1 eV/atom ‚âà 96.5 kJ/mol |
| 4 | $D = D_0 \cdot \exp\left(-\dfrac{Q_d}{kT}\right)$ | Diffusion coefficient (Arrhenius form) | m¬≤/s |
| 5 | $\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y \cdot d^{-1/2}$ | Hall-Petch equation | MPa; d in mm |
| 6 | $\tau = \dfrac{Gb}{L}$ | Force per unit length on a dislocation | N/m |
| 7 | $\rho = \dfrac{L}{V}$ | Dislocation density | m‚Åª¬≤ |
| 8 | $G \approx \dfrac{E}{2(1+\nu)}$ | Shear modulus relation (isotropic) | GPa |
| 9 | $b = \dfrac{a\sqrt{2}}{2}$ (FCC) | Magnitude of Burgers vector (FCC) | nm |
| 10 | $b = \dfrac{a\sqrt{3}}{2}$ (BCC) | Magnitude of Burgers vector (BCC) | nm |
| 11 | $b = a$ (Simple Cubic) | Magnitude of Burgers vector (SC) | nm |

Where:
- $N_v$ = number of vacancies per unit volume
- $N$ = number of atomic sites per unit volume
- $Q_v$ = activation energy for vacancy formation
- $k$ = Boltzmann constant (8.62 √ó 10‚Åª‚Åµ eV/atom¬∑K)
- $T$ = absolute temperature (K)
- $G$ = shear modulus
- $b$ = magnitude of Burgers vector
- $L$ = length of dislocation segment
- $E$ = Young's modulus
- $\nu$ = Poisson's ratio
- $a$ = lattice parameter
- $d$ = average grain diameter
- $k_y$ = Hall-Petch coefficient

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These equations are not just academic ‚Äî they govern real design choices. For example, controlling grain size in steel (Hall-Petch) and vacancy concentration during heat treatment (quenching) are daily operations in metallurgical industry. Engineers use these relations to **predict strength, design heat-treatment cycles, and select alloys** for aerospace, automotive, and structural applications.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Equilibrium Vacancy Concentration

The presence of vacancies is governed by **thermodynamic equilibrium**. A perfect crystal has minimum internal energy but minimum entropy. Introducing vacancies increases entropy (configurational) and increases internal energy (formation energy). The equilibrium concentration is found by minimizing the **Helmholtz free energy** $F = U - TS$.

**Step 1: Define the total internal energy change.**
When $N_v$ vacancies are formed, each requires a formation energy $Q_v$. The total internal energy increases by:

$$\Delta U = N_v \cdot Q_v$$

**Step 2: Calculate the configurational entropy change.**
The entropy of mixing (configurational entropy) is given by the Boltzmann relation $S = k \ln W$, where $W$ is the number of ways to arrange $N_v$ vacancies among $N$ lattice sites. Using the approximation from statistical mechanics:

$$W = \dfrac{N!}{N_v! \cdot (N - N_v)!}$$

Applying Stirling's approximation ($\ln x! \approx x \ln x - x$ for large $x$):

$$\ln W = N \ln N - N_v \ln N_v - (N - N_v) \ln(N - N_v)$$

**Step 3: Express the change in free energy.**
The change in free energy $\Delta F = \Delta U - T \Delta S$ is:

$$\Delta F = N_v Q_v - kT \cdot \left[ N \ln N - N_v \ln N_v - (N - N_v) \ln(N - N_v) \right]$$

**Step 4: Minimize the free energy with respect to $N_v$.**
At equilibrium, $\dfrac{d(\Delta F)}{dN_v} = 0$:

$$\dfrac{d(\Delta F)}{dN_v} = Q_v - kT \left[ -\ln N_v - 1 + \ln(N - N_v) + 1 \right] = 0$$

$$Q_v - kT \left[ \ln(N - N_v) - \ln N_v \right] = 0$$

$$Q_v = kT \ln\left(\dfrac{N - N_v}{N_v}\right)$$

**Step 5: Solve for the equilibrium vacancy ratio.**
For $N_v \ll N$ (a valid assumption in real metals), $\dfrac{N - N_v}{N_v} \approx \dfrac{N}{N_v}$:

$$Q_v = kT \ln\left(\dfrac{N}{N_v}\right)$$

$$\ln\left(\dfrac{N}{N_v}\right) = \dfrac{Q_v}{kT}$$

$$\dfrac{N}{N_v} = \exp\left(\dfrac{Q_v}{kT}\right)$$

**Step 6: Final equilibrium relation.**

$$\boxed{\dfrac{N_v}{N} = \exp\left(-\dfrac{Q_v}{kT}\right)}$$

**Interpretation:** The vacancy fraction is **exponentially sensitive** to temperature. Doubling absolute temperature (e.g., 300 K ‚Üí 600 K) with $Q_v = 1$ eV increases vacancy concentration by a factor of $e^{Q_v/kT}$ ‚Äî roughly 10‚Åπ times. This is why quenching from high temperature **freezes in** excess vacancies that subsequently affect properties.

---
