| Atoms displaced | 1 missing | 1 extra in void | 1 different atom | 1 host to void | 1 cation + 1 anion missing |
| Density change | Decreases slightly | No significant change | Depends on size | No change | Decreases |
| Energy required | Low (~1 eV) | High (~3‚Äì4 eV) | Moderate | Moderate‚ÄìHigh | Low‚ÄìModerate |
| Common in | All crystals | All (rare) | All alloys | Ionic crystals | Ionic crystals |
| Charge neutrality | Maintained | Maintained | Maintained | Maintained | Strictly maintained |
| KTU frequency | ‚òÖ‚òÖ‚òÖ‚òÖ‚òÖ | ‚òÖ‚òÖ‚òÖ‚òÖ | ‚òÖ‚òÖ‚òÖ‚òÖ‚òÖ | ‚òÖ‚òÖ‚òÖ | ‚òÖ‚òÖ‚òÖ |

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Line Defects (Dislocations)

| **Property** | **Edge Dislocation** | **Screw Dislocation** | **Mixed Dislocation** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Burgers vector vs. line | Perpendicular (‚ä•) | Parallel (‚Üî) | Variable angle |
| Extra half-plane | Present (above slip plane) | Absent (spiral ramp) | Partially present |
| Stress field | Compressive above, tensile below | Pure shear | Combined |
| Slip mechanism | Glide, climb (with diffusion) | Glide only | Both glide and climb |

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)

**Problem:** Calculate the equilibrium fraction of vacancies in copper at 500 ¬∞C (773 K). Given: $Q_v = 0.9$ eV/atom, $k = 8.62 \times 10^{-5}$ eV/atom¬∑K.

**Step-by-step Solution:**

$$\dfrac{N_v}{N} = \exp\left(-\dfrac{Q_v}{kT}\right)$$

**Step 1: Compute the exponent.**

$$\dfrac{Q_v}{kT} = \dfrac{0.9}{8.62 \times 10^{-5} \times 773} = \dfrac{0.9}{0.06663} = 13.51$$

**Step 2: Compute the exponential.**

$$\dfrac{N_v}{N} = \exp(-13.51) = 1.36 \times 10^{-6}$$

**Step 3: Interpretation.**
At 500 ¬∞C, copper has approximately **1.36 vacancies per million atoms**. At the melting point (1085 ¬∞C = 1358 K), this fraction increases to roughly $10^{-4}$ ‚Äî about 1 vacancy per 10,000 atoms.

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** Always include units in the exponent calculation. A common KTU valuation error is mixing k in J/K with Q in eV/atom. The conversion $1$ eV $= 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ J is often needed.

---

### 3.5 The Burgers Vector Construction (for Dislocations)

The **Burgers vector (b)** is a fundamental quantity that characterizes a dislocation. The construction procedure (FS/RH convention) is:

**Step 1:** Draw a closed loop of $n$ atomic steps around the dislocation in a perfect reference crystal. The loop closes perfectly.

**Step 2:** Repeat the **same sequence** of atomic steps around the dislocation in the **defective** crystal.

**Step 3:** The vector required to close the loop in the defective crystal is the **Burgers vector b**.

**Mathematical statement:**

$$\mathbf{b} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \mathbf{a}_i$$

For FCC metals, $\mathbf{b} = \dfrac{a}{2}\langle 110 \rangle$, magnitude $b = \dfrac{a\sqrt{2}}{2}$.

For BCC metals, $\mathbf{b} = \dfrac{a}{2}\langle 111 \rangle$, magnitude $b = \dfrac{a\sqrt{3}}{2}$.

For HCP metals, $\mathbf{b} = \dfrac{a}{3}\langle 11\bar{2}0 \rangle$, magnitude $b = a$.

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A ‚Äî Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam ‚Äì May 2024]**
**Differentiate between Frenkel and Schottky defects in ionic crystals. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| **Frenkel Defect** | **Schottky Defect** |
|---|---|
| An ion is displaced from its lattice site to an interstitial site | A pair of ions (cation + anion) is missing from the lattice |
| Density **not affected** (atom remains inside crystal) | Density **decreases** (mass lost from crystal) |
| Common when cation is much smaller than anion | Common when cation and anion are of similar size |
| Example: **AgCl, ZnS** | Example: **NaCl, KCl** |

> Both maintain **charge neutrality** of the ionic crystal. **Each carries 3 marks.**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam ‚Äì Dec 2023]**
**What is a Burgers vector? How is it oriented relative to the dislocation line for an edge and a screw dislocation?**

**Model Answer:**
The **Burgers vector (b)** is a vector that quantifies the magnitude and direction of the lattice distortion produced by a dislocation. It is determined by drawing a closed loop around the dislocation and noting the closure failure in the defective crystal.

- For an **edge dislocation**: $\mathbf{b} \perp$ dislocation line.
- For a **screw dislocation**: $\mathbf{b} \parallel$ dislocation line.
- For a **mixed dislocation**: $\mathbf{b}$ makes a variable angle with the line.

The Burgers vector is **invariant** along the dislocation line; it is a topological property of the defect. (3 marks)

---

### PART B ‚Äî Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) ‚Äî [KTU University Exam ‚Äì Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Classify crystal imperfections in detail with neat sketches. Explain any **four types of point defects** with diagrams. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What are **dislocations**? With the help of neat diagrams, explain **edge and screw dislocations**. Define **Burgers vector** and explain its significance. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a):**

**Classification of crystal imperfections** (3 marks):
Based on dimensionality, crystal imperfections are classified as:
1. **Point defects (0-D)** ‚Äì atomic scale, e.g., vacancy, interstitial
2. **Line defects (1-D)** ‚Äì linear, e.g., edge and screw dislocations
3. **Surface/Interfacial defects (2-D)** ‚Äì planar, e.g., grain boundaries, stacking faults

> Note: 3D defects (precipitates, voids) may also be mentioned.

**Four point defects explained (4 marks, 1 each):**

1. **Vacancy:** A missing host atom from a lattice site. Created by thermal vibrations, irradiation, or quenching. Schematic: ‚äÝ (box) at a regular lattice position.

2. **Self-interstitial:** A host atom forced into an interstitial void. Causes large local lattice strain due to size mismatch. Schematic: atom in the gap between regular sites.

3. **Substitutional impurity:** A foreign atom replaces a host atom on a lattice site. Example: Zn in Cu (brass). Schematic: circle of different size at a regular site.

4. **Frenkel defect:** A host atom displaced from its lattice site into an interstitial position, creating a vacancy-interstitial pair. Schematic: vacancy + nearby interstitial host atom.

> Diagrams should be drawn inside an outline of a crystal lattice with atoms as small circles.

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**Dislocations (1 mark):** Dislocations are line defects ‚Äî linear crystallographic irregularities around which the atoms are misaligned. They are the carriers of **plastic deformation** in crystalline solids.

**Edge dislocation (2.5 marks):**
- Formed by inserting an extra half-plane of atoms into the crystal.
- The dislocation line lies along the bottom edge of the extra half-plane.
- **Burgers vector ‚ä• dislocation line.**
- Above the slip plane: compressive stress.
- Below the slip plane: tensile stress.

*Sketch: 2D drawing showing extra half-plane terminating inside the crystal, labeled slip plane, dislocation line, and Burgers vector perpendicular to it.*

**Screw dislocation (2.5 marks):**
- Formed by shearing the crystal so that atomic planes form a spiral ramp around the dislocation line.
- **Burgers vector ‚à• dislocation line.**
- Pure shear stress field around the line.
- Dislocation motion is by glide on any plane containing the line.

*Sketch: 3D diagram showing spiral path of atoms, or a cube with a sheared top surface offset by one Burgers vector.*

**Burgers vector and significance (1 mark):**
The Burgers vector $\mathbf{b}$ characterizes the magnitude and direction of lattice distortion. It is **invariant** along the dislocation and determines:
- The energy of the dislocation per unit length: $E/L = \alpha G b^2$ (where $\alpha \approx 0.5$‚Äì1).
- The slip displacement produced when the dislocation moves across the crystal.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) ‚Äî [KTU University Exam ‚Äì Model Paper 2024, Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** What are **line defects**? Derive the expression for the **equilibrium vacancy concentration** in a crystal. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the various **types of surface defects** found in polycrystalline materials. Explain the **Hall‚ÄìPetch relation** and its significance. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a):**

**Line defects (2 marks):** Line defects, or dislocations, are one-dimensional crystallographic imperfections along which the atomic arrangement is disrupted. Types: edge, screw, and mixed dislocations. They govern plastic deformation, yield strength, and work hardening. Typical dislocation density in annealed metal is $10^{10}$ m‚Åª¬≤; in heavily cold-worked metal is $10^{15}$ m‚Åª¬≤.

**Derivation of equilibrium vacancy concentration (5 marks):**

*Full derivation is shown in Section 3.1 of these notes. Students must reproduce the following key steps:*

**Step 1:** Internal energy change: $\Delta U = N_v Q_v$.

**Step 2:** Configurational entropy:

$$\Delta S = k \ln\left(\dfrac{N!}{N_v!\,(N-N_v)!}\right)$$

**Step 3:** Free energy change:

$$\Delta F = N_v Q_v - kT \ln\left(\dfrac{N!}{N_v!\,(N-N_v)!}\right)$$

**Step 4:** Minimize: $\dfrac{d(\Delta F)}{dN_v} = 0$.

**Step 5:** Apply Stirling's approximation and solve:

$$\dfrac{N_v}{N} = \exp\left(-\dfrac{Q_v}{kT}\right)$$

**Step 6 (Conclusion, 0.5 mark):** The equilibrium vacancy concentration increases exponentially with temperature. It controls diffusion rates, sintering, and quench-aging phenomena.

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**Types of surface defects (3.5 marks):**

1. **Grain boundaries** ‚Äì Interfaces between two crystals (grains) of differing orientation. They are typically 2‚Äì5 atomic diameters wide, with disordered atomic packing. Classified as low-angle (<15¬∞) and high-angle (>15¬∞) boundaries.

2. **Twin boundaries** ‚Äì A special type of grain boundary where the lattice is a mirror image across the boundary plane. The twin plane is a low-energy, highly ordered interface. Common in FCC metals annealed at low temperature, and in BCC metals shocked or deformed at high strain rates.

3. **Stacking faults** ‚Äì A local interruption in the normal stacking sequence of close-packed planes. In FCC, the ABCABC stacking may be locally ABC**B**ABC (intrinsic) or ABC**A**CABC (extrinsic). Characterized by stacking fault energy (SFE).

4. **External surfaces** ‚Äì The outermost atomic layer; atoms have unsatisfied/dangling bonds, leading to surface energy and reactivity.

5. **Phase boundaries** ‚Äì Interfaces between two distinct phases (e.g., ferrite‚Äìcementite in pearlite). May be coherent, semi-coherent, or incoherent.

**Hall‚ÄìPetch relation and significance (3.5 marks):**

The **Hall‚ÄìPetch equation** relates the yield strength of a polycrystalline metal to its average grain diameter:

$$\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y \cdot d^{-1/2}$$

Where:
- $\sigma_y$ = yield strength
- $\sigma_0$ = friction stress (single-crystal yield strength)
- $k_y$ = Hall‚ÄìPetch coefficient (a constant for a given material)
- $d$ = average grain diameter

**Derivation logic:** Grain boundaries act as barriers to dislocation motion. A dislocation pile-up at a boundary produces a stress concentration. The number of dislocations in a pile-up is proportional to $d$, and the stress at the tip of the pile-up needed to activate slip in the next grain is $\tau = n\tau_0 \propto d^{1/2}$. The applied stress required to cause yielding is therefore inversely proportional to $d^{1/2}$.

**Significance:**
- **Smaller grain size ‚Üí higher yield strength** (the principle of grain refinement strengthening).
- Used in the design of high-strength structural steels (HSLA steels), aluminum alloys, and ceramic materials.
- Too small a grain size, however, can lead to grain-boundary sliding and reduced strength at high temperatures (creep regime ‚Äî the **inverse Hall‚ÄìPetch effect** below ~10 nm).

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Crystal Imperfection Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Frenkel and Schottky defects (‚Äì1 to ‚Äì2 marks):** Many students write that *both* involve vacancies and intermixing them. Remember: **Frenkel = 1 displaced ion, density unchanged. Schottky = pair of vacancies, density decreases.** A comparison table earns full marks.
>
> 2. **Wrong Burgers vector direction (‚Äì1 mark):** For an edge dislocation, $\mathbf{b}$ is **perpendicular** to the dislocation line, not parallel. Many students draw it parallel due to a screw/edge confusion.
>
> 3. **Missing the "1/2" factor in Burgers vector magnitude (‚Äì1 mark):** In FCC and BCC, the Burgers vector is $\dfrac{a}{2}\langle 110 \rangle$ and $\dfrac{a}{2}\langle 111 \rangle$ respectively. Writing just "$a$" loses a mark.
>
> 4. **Forgetting diagrams (‚Äì2 to ‚Äì3 marks):** A 14-mark question on classification **always** requires neat, labeled diagrams. A correct text answer without sketches is typically capped at 10/14.
>
> 5. **Unit errors in vacancy calculation (‚Äì1 mark):** Always express $Q_v$ in **eV/atom** when $k$ is in eV/K, or in **J/mol** when $R$ is in J/mol¬∑K. Mixing units is a frequent error.
>
> 6. **Missing "why" behind Hall‚ÄìPetch (‚Äì2 marks):** Stating the equation alone is insufficient. The examiner expects the dislocation pile-up mechanism as justification.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Save This for Last-Minute Review):**

- **Classification:** Crystal imperfections = Point (0-D) + Line (1-D) + Surface (2-D).

- **Point defects (zero-D):**
  - Vacancy ‚Üí missing atom, formed thermally, $N_v/N = \exp(-Q_v/kT)$.
  - Self-interstitial ‚Üí host atom in a void site, high formation energy.
  - Substitutional impurity ‚Üí foreign atom on lattice site, basis of alloying.
  - Interstitial impurity ‚Üí small foreign atom (C, N, H) in interstitial void, basis of steel hardening.
  - **Frenkel** = vacancy + interstitial host pair; density unchanged; e.g., AgCl.
  - **Schottky** = paired cation + anion vacancies; density decreases; e.g., NaCl.

- **Line defects (one-D) = Dislocations:**
  - **Edge dislocation** = extra half-plane; $\mathbf{b} \perp$ line.
  - **Screw dislocation** = spiral shear; $\mathbf{b} \parallel$ line.
  - **Mixed dislocation** = combination; $\mathbf{b}$ at variable angle.
  - Burgers vector is a topological invariant of the dislocation.
  - For FCC: $|\mathbf{b}| = a\sqrt{2}/2$; for BCC: $|\mathbf{b}| = a\sqrt{3}/2$.

- **Surface defects (two-D):**
  - **Grain boundaries** ‚Üí Hall‚ÄìPetch: $\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y d^{-1/2}$.
  - **Twin boundaries** ‚Üí mirror-symmetric, low energy.
  - **Stacking faults** ‚Üí interruption in ABCABC sequence; characterized by SFE.
  - **Phase boundaries** ‚Üí coherent / semi-coherent / incoherent.
  - **External surfaces** ‚Üí unsatisfied bonds, surface energy.

- **Key formulas (must memorize):**
  - $N_v/N = \exp(-Q_v/kT)$ ‚Äî **vacancy concentration**.
  - $\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y d^{-1/2}$ ‚Äî **Hall‚ÄìPetch**.
  - $|\mathbf{b}|_{FCC} = a\sqrt{2}/2$, $|\mathbf{b}|_{BCC} = a\sqrt{3}/2$ ‚Äî **Burgers magnitudes**.

- **Engineering impact:**
  - Point defects ‚Üí diffusion, doping, alloying.
  - Line defects ‚Üí plastic deformation, yield strength, work hardening.
  - Surface defects ‚Üí grain size control, creep, fracture toughness.

- **Most-tested KTU sub-topics (high weightage):**
  1. Frenkel vs Schottky comparison (3-mark direct question).
  2. Edge dislocation diagram + Burgers vector (7-mark sub-question).
  3. Equilibrium vacancy concentration derivation (5‚Äì7 marks).
  4. Hall‚ÄìPetch relation + grain boundary effects (5‚Äì7 marks).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dislocations: Burgers vector and Frank-Read source
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBAUT304 - MATERIAL SCIENCE AND METALLURGY > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Dislocations: Burgers vector and Frank-Read source # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Burgers Vector ‚Äì Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Burgers Vector ($\vec{b}$):** It is the vector that represents the magnitude and direction of the lattice distortion caused by a dislocation in a crystal. It is defined as the closure failure vector of a Burgers circuit drawn around the dislocation line. The Burgers vector is the **fundamental quantitative descriptor** of any dislocation. It tells us: - **Direction** of slip (lattice displacement) - **Magnitude** of slip (lattice distortion) - **Type** of dislocation (edge, screw, or mixed) The Burgers vector is **invariant along a dislocation line** for an individual dislocation, meaning it has the same value everywhere along its length (a key property called conservation of Burgers vector). > [!NOTE] > **Convention for Burgers Vector Direction:** > - **Edge dislocation**: $\vec{b}$ is **perpendicular** to the dislocation line. > - **Screw dislocation**: $\vec{b}$ is **parallel** to the dislocation line. > - **Mixed dislocation**: $\vec{b}$ is at an **oblique angle** to the dislocation line. ## 1.2 Frank‚ÄìRead Source ‚Äì Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Frank‚ÄìRead Source:** A Frank‚ÄìRead source is a dislocation multiplication mechanism in which a pinned segment of dislocation line, under an applied shear stress, bows out, expands into a loop, and regenerates new dislocation loops, thereby producing a vast number of dislocations from a single original line. It explains why a small number of initial dislocations can produce the large plastic strains observed in metals. Proposed by **Frank and Read (1950)**, this source is the primary mechanism by which crystals generate enough mobile dislocations to undergo macroscopic plastic deformation. The segment must be **pinned at both ends** (by obstacles such as precipitates, grain boundaries, or nodes) to act as a source. ## 1.3 Intuitive Analogies ### üçî Burgers Vector Analogy: "The Wrinkle in a Carpet" Imagine pushing a heavy carpet across a floor. Instead of dragging the whole carpet, you create a small **wrinkle (bump)** and push the wrinkle across the carpet. The wrinkle moves easily, but the entire carpet displaces. - The **wrinkle** = the dislocation line. - The **distance the carpet shifts as the wrinkle passes** = the magnitude of the **Burgers vector** ($|\vec{b}|$). - The **direction the carpet moves** = direction of $\vec{b}$. This explains why dislocations move so easily ‚Äî only a small region (the dislocation core) is distorted at any time, not the entire crystal. ### üé£ Frank‚ÄìRead Source Analogy: "The Fishing Line in the Wind" Imagine a fishing line tied between two fixed posts (the pinning points). When wind (applied stress) blows on it, the line bows outward. As the wind strengthens, the line bows more, eventually forms a loop, and the two arms meet on the far side, creating a new loop that detaches and propagates while the original segment snaps back to repeat the process. The fishing line can keep generating new loops continuously ‚Äî just like a Frank‚ÄìRead source generating dislocations. > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Significance:** The Frank‚ÄìRead source is the *engine* of plastic deformation. Without it, the few dislocations present in a real crystal could only produce a tiny amount of strain (~0.1%), far less than the 10‚Äì100% strains metals can sustain. ## 1.4 Key Physical Metrics | Metric | Standard Value | Significance | |---|---|---| | **Burgers vector magnitude for FCC** | $\frac{a}{2}\langle 110 \rangle$ | Smallest lattice translation in FCC | | **Burgers vector magnitude for BCC** | $\frac{a}{2}\langle 111 \rangle$ | Smallest lattice translation in BCC | | **Typical dislocation density (annealed)** | $10^6 - 10^8 \, \text{m}^{-2}$ | Density in well-annealed metals | | **Typical dislocation density (cold-worked)** | $10^{14} - 10^{15} \, \text{m}^{-2}$ | Density in heavily deformed metals | | **Shear modulus ($G$) for steel** | $\sim 80 \, \text{GPa}$ | Used in stress calculations | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Burgers Vector ‚Äî Theoretical Framework ### 2.1.1 Construction of the Burgers Circuit A **Burgers circuit** is a closed loop drawn atom-by-atom around a dislocation in the **real (defective) crystal**. When the same number of atomic steps are taken in a **perfect reference crystal**, the loop fails to close. The vector required to close this open circuit defines the Burgers vector $\vec{b}$. > [!NOTE] > **Right-hand convention (FS/RH):** The Burgers vector direction is defined by the **right-hand rule** with the circuit traced in a clockwise direction around the dislocation (finish-start convention). ### 2.1.2 Properties of the Burgers Vector - **Conservation:** The algebraic sum of Burgers vectors at a dislocation node equals zero (Kirchhoff's law for dislocations): $$\sum_i \vec{b_i} = 0$$ - **Independence of Path:** $\vec{b}$ is the same for any Burgers circuit enclosing the same dislocation. - **Invariant along a single line:** A continuous dislocation has a single $\vec{b}$ value. - **Lattice Translation:** $\vec{b}$ is always a lattice translation vector of the crystal (typically the shortest translation). ### 2.1.3 Dislocation Types Based on Angle Between Line and $\vec{b}$ | Type | Angle ($\theta$) between $\vec{b}$ and dislocation line | Nature | |---|---|---| | **Pure Edge** | $\theta = 90¬∞$ | Extra half-plane of atoms | | **Pure Screw** | $\theta = 0¬∞$ | Helical lattice distortion | | **Mixed** | $0¬∞ < \theta < 90¬∞$ | Combination of edge + screw | ## 2.2 Stress Field and Energy of a Dislocation ### 2.2.1 Stress Field around a Screw Dislocation For a screw dislocation along the $z$-axis with Burgers vector $\vec{b}$ parallel to $z$: $$\sigma_{\theta z} = \sigma_{z\theta} = \frac{Gb}{2\pi r}$$ where: - $G$ = shear modulus - $b = \vert \vec{b} \vert$ = magnitude of Burgers vector - $r$ = radial distance from the dislocation core ### 2.2.2 Stress Field around an Edge Dislocation For an edge dislocation along the $z$-axis with $\vec{b}$ along $x$: $$\sigma_{xx} = -\frac{Gb}{2\pi(1-\nu)} \cdot \frac{y(3x^2+y^2)}{(x^2+y^2)^2}$$ $$\sigma_{yy} = \frac{Gb}{2\pi(1-\nu)} \cdot \frac{y(x^2-y^2)}{(x^2+y^2)^2}$$ $$\sigma_{xy} = \frac{Gb}{2\pi(1-\nu)} \cdot \frac{x(x^2-y^2)}{(x^2+y^2)^2}$$ where $\nu$ is **Poisson's ratio**. ### 2.2.3 Elastic Strain Energy per Unit Length The energy stored per unit length of dislocation line is: $$E_{\text{line}} = \frac{Gb^2}{4\pi K} \ln\left(\frac{R}{r_0}\right) \quad \text{(J/m)}$$ where: - $K = 1$ for screw, $K = (1-\nu)$ for edge - $R$ = outer radius of stress field (‚âà grain size or dislocation spacing) - $r_0$ = inner cutoff radius (‚âà $\vert \vec{b} \vert$ or $b$) **Order of magnitude estimate** (using $R \approx 10^5 b$): $$E_{\text{line}} \approx \frac{Gb^2}{2} \quad \text{(J/m)}$$ This is the **characteristic dislocation energy per unit length** ‚Äî a frequently tested KTU result. ## 2.3 Force on a Dislocation (Peach‚ÄìKoehler Equation) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Peach‚ÄìKoehler Equation:** The force per unit length on a dislocation is: > $$\vec{F} = (\vec{\sigma} \cdot \vec{b}) \times \vec{\xi}$$ > where $\vec{\xi}$ is the unit vector along the dislocation line direction. For a dislocation in pure shear $\tau$ on the slip plane: $$F/L = \tau \cdot b \quad \text{(N/m)}$$ This is the **driving force** for dislocation motion (glide). ## 2.4 Frank‚ÄìRead Source ‚Äî Mechanism in Detail ### 2.4.1 Geometry Setup Consider a dislocation segment of length $L$ pinned at points A and B (e.g., by precipitate particles, nodes, or forest dislocations). When a shear stress $\tau$ is applied in the slip plane: 1. **Initial state:** Segment is straight between A and B. 2. **BowING:** Under $\tau$, the segment bows out into an arc (circle of radius $R$). 3. **Instability:** When the bowed arc becomes a **semicircle** ($R = L/2$), the configuration is unstable. 4. **Loop expansion:** Beyond this critical point, the loop expands spontaneously. 5. **Loop detachment:** When the two arms meet behind the pinning points, they annihilate (if opposite signs) or combine, leaving a closed loop and a fresh segment. 6. **Repetition:** The fresh segment immediately bows out again ‚Äî **continuous multiplication**. ### 2.4.2 Critical Stress for Frank‚ÄìRead Operation The line tension $T \approx \frac{Gb^2}{2}$ acts as a restoring force. The condition for instability is reached when: $$\tau \cdot b \cdot L = 2T = Gb^2$$ Solving for the **critical resolved shear stress (CRSS)**: $$\tau_c = \frac{Gb}{L}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Physical Meaning:** $\tau_c$ is **inversely proportional** to the source length $L$. A longer pinned segment is easier to activate. Typical values: $\tau_c \approx 10^{-4} G$ to $10^{-2} G$, which matches experimentally observed yield strengths. ### 2.4.3 Why Frank‚ÄìRead Sources Are Crucial - A freshly solidified or well-annealed metal has only $\sim 10^6$ dislocations/m¬≤. - During plastic deformation, dislocation density rises to $\sim 10^{14}$/m¬≤. - A single Frank‚ÄìRead source can multiply dislocations by orders of magnitude in a few seconds. - This explains why metals yield and flow plastically rather than fracturing brittlely. ## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master these equations for KTU 2024 ESE:** | # | Formula | Description | Typical Application | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\vert \vec{b} \vert = \frac{a}{2}\langle 110 \rangle$ | Burgers vector magnitude (FCC) | $\vert \vec{b} \vert = \frac{a\sqrt{2}}{2}$ | | 2 | $\vert \vec{b} \vert = \frac{a}{2}\langle 111 \rangle$ | Burgers vector magnitude (BCC) | $\vert \vec{b} \vert = \frac{a\sqrt{3}}{2}$ | | 3 | $E_{\text{line}} \approx \frac{Gb^2}{2}$ | Energy per unit length (dislocation) | Strain energy storage | | 4 | $F/L = \tau b$ | Force per unit length on dislocation | Peach‚ÄìKoehler in pure shear | | 5 | $\tau_c = \frac{Gb}{L}$ | Critical stress for Frank‚ÄìRead source | Yield stress estimation | | 6 | $\rho \uparrow \Rightarrow \tau_y \uparrow$ | Taylor's relation: $\tau = \tau_0 + \alpha G b \sqrt{\rho}$ | Work hardening | | 7 | $\sigma = G b \sqrt{\rho}$ | Flow stress dependence on dislocation density | Strain hardening | | 8 | $\sum_i \vec{b_i} = 0$ | Conservation at dislocation nodes | Dislocation reactions | ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Applications - **Cold working / Work hardening:** Frank‚ÄìRead sources multiply dislocations ‚Üí increased dislocation density ‚Üí increased strength (basis of cold-rolled steel, drawn wires). - **Annealing recovery:** Heat treatment reduces dislocation density by annihilation and climb. - **Single crystal silicon wafers:** Minimizing dislocations (no sources) is critical for semiconductor devices. - **Creep resistance:** High-temperature alloys (e.g., Inconel) are designed with obstacles that *pin* Frank‚ÄìRead sources, delaying multiplication. - **Precipitation hardening:** Precipitates (e.g., in Al‚ÄìCu alloys) pin dislocation segments, raising $\tau_c$ and strength. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Burgers Vector Magnitude for FCC Crystals **Given:** Face-Centered Cubic (FCC) crystal with lattice parameter $a$. The shortest lattice translation in FCC is the vector from one corner atom to the **face-centered atom** of an adjacent face. This vector lies along the $\langle 110 \rangle$ direction with magnitude $\frac{a}{2}$. **Step 1:** Identify the shortest translation vector. In an FCC unit cell, the face diagonal connects two face-centered atoms. The face diagonal length is: $$d_{\text{face}} = a\sqrt{2}$$ **Step 2:** The Burgers vector goes from a corner to the center of the face ‚Äî this is **half the face diagonal**: $$\vert \vec{b} \vert = \frac{d_{\text{face}}}{2} = \frac{a\sqrt{2}}{2}$$ **Step 3:** Express the vector notation: $$\vec{b} = \frac{a}{2}\langle 110 \rangle$$ **Step 4:** Numerical example ‚Äî for copper ($a = 0.3615 \, \text{nm}$): $$\vert \vec{b} \vert = \frac{0.3615 \times \sqrt{2}}{2} = 0.2556 \, \text{nm}$$ > [!NOTE] > This is the experimentally observed slip distance in FCC metals, confirming the **smallest lattice vector** is the active Burgers vector. ## 3.2 Derivation: Critical Stress for Frank‚ÄìRead Source **Given:** A dislocation segment of length $L$ pinned at both ends, lying in a slip plane, with Burgers vector $b$ and shear modulus $G$. **Step 1:** Identify forces acting on the bowed segment. When the dislocation bows into an arc of radius $R$, two forces balance: - **Driving force** from applied stress: $F_d = \tau b$ per unit length, pushing the dislocation outward. - **Restoring force** from line tension $T$: $F_r = T / R = \frac{Gb^2}{2R}$ per unit length, trying to straighten the line. **Step 2:** Force equilibrium at any bowed configuration: $$\tau b = \frac{T}{R} = \frac{Gb^2}{2R}$$ **Step 3:** Solve for the bowed radius $R$: $$R = \frac{Gb}{2\tau}$$ **Step 4:** Identify the critical condition. The bowed segment is a circular arc pinned at two points separated by $L$. Geometrically, the maximum radius of curvature occurs when the arc is a **semicircle**, i.e., when $R = L/2$. **Step 5:** Substitute $R = L/2$ into the equilibrium equation: $$\tau_c b = \frac{Gb^2}{2(L/2)} = \frac{Gb^2}{L}$$ **Step 6:** Solve for the critical resolved shear stress: $$\boxed{\tau_c = \frac{Gb}{L}}$$ **Step 7:** Physical interpretation. The minimum stress required to operate a Frank‚ÄìRead source of length $L$. **Larger** $L$ means **lower** $\tau_c$ (easier activation). Typical values: - $L = 1 \, \mu\text{m}$, $G = 80 \, \text{GPa}$, $b = 0.25 \, \text{nm}$: - $\tau_c = \frac{80 \times 10^9 \times 0.25 \times 10^{-9}}{1 \times 10^{-6}} = 20 \, \text{MPa}$ This matches the macroscopic yield stress of soft annealed metals ‚Äî a strong validation of the model. ## 3.3 Derivation: Dislocation Energy per Unit Length **Given:** A screw dislocation with Burgers vector $b$ along the $z$-axis. **Step 1:** Stress field of a screw dislocation (from linear elasticity): $$\tau_{\theta z} = \frac{Gb}{2\pi r}$$ **Step 2:** Elastic strain energy density in shear: $$u = \frac{\tau^2}{2G} = \frac{1}{2G}\left(\frac{Gb}{2\pi r}\right)^2 = \frac{Gb^2}{8\pi^2 r^2}$$ **Step 3:** Integrate energy density over a cylindrical volume of radius from $r_0$ (core) to $R$ (outer cutoff) and unit length: $$E_{\text{line}} = \int_{r_0}^{R} u \cdot 2\pi r \, dr$$ **Step 4:** Substitute and integrate: $$E_{\text{line}} = \int_{r_0}^{R} \frac{Gb^2}{8\pi^2 r^2} \cdot 2\pi r \, dr = \frac{Gb^2}{4\pi} \int_{r_0}^{R} \frac{dr}{r}$$ **Step 5:** Evaluate the integral: $$E_{\text{line}} = \frac{Gb^2}{4\pi} \ln\left(\frac{R}{r_0}\right)$$ **Step 6:** For a typical ratio $R/r_0 \approx 10^5$ (since $R \approx$ grain size and $r_0 \approx b$): $$\ln(10^5) \approx 11.5 \approx 2\pi$$ Substituting back: $$E_{\text{line}} \approx \frac{Gb^2}{4\pi} \cdot 2\pi = \frac{Gb^2}{2}$$ $$\boxed{E_{\text{line}} \approx \frac{Gb^2}{2} \quad \text{(J/m)}}$$ **Step 7:** Engineering implication. Every meter of dislocation line stores $\sim Gb^2/2$ joules. With $G = 80 \, \text{GPa}$ and $b = 0.25 \, \text{nm}$: $$E_{\text{line}} \approx \frac{80 \times 10^9 \times (0.25 \times 10^{-9})^2}{2} = 2.5 \times 10^{-9} \, \text{J/m}$$ A heavily cold-worked metal with $\rho = 10^{14} \, \text{m}^{-2}$ stores roughly $E_{\text{stored}} = \rho \cdot E_{\text{line}} = 250 \, \text{kJ/m}^3$ ‚Äî a significant fraction of the work done in deformation. ## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example **Problem:** A copper single crystal has a Frank‚ÄìRead source of length $L = 2 \, \mu\text{m}$ pinned between two obstacles. Given $G = 42 \, \text{GPa}$ and $b = 0.255 \, \text{nm}$, calculate: (a) The critical resolved shear stress. (b) The dislocation energy per unit length (assuming $R/r_0 = 10^4$). **Solution:** **(a) Critical resolved shear stress:** $$\tau_c = \frac{Gb}{L} = \frac{42 \times 10^9 \times 0.255 \times 10^{-9}}{2 \times 10^{-6}}$$ $$\tau_c = \frac{10.71}{2 \times 10^{-6}} = 5.355 \, \text{MPa}$$ **(b) Energy per unit length:** $$E_{\text{line}} = \frac{Gb^2}{4\pi} \ln\left(\frac{R}{r_0}\right) = \frac{42 \times 10^9 \times (0.255 \times 10^{-9})^2}{4\pi} \times \ln(10^4)$$ $$E_{\text{line}} = \frac{42 \times 10^9 \times 6.5 \times 10^{-20}}{12.566} \times 9.21$$ $$E_{\text{line}} = \frac{2.73 \times 10^{-9}}{12.566} \times 9.21 = 2.0 \times 10^{-9} \, \text{J/m}$$ # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Burgers Vector Construction (Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TB nodeA["Start: Draw a closed circuit
around the dislocation
in the REAL crystal"] nodeB["Transfer the SAME path
into a PERFECT reference crystal"] nodeC["The path FAILS to close
by a vector b"] nodeD["Vector b is the
BURGERS VECTOR"] nodeE["Edge Dislocation:
b perpendicular to line"] nodeF["Screw Dislocation:
b parallel to line"] nodeG["Mixed Dislocation:
b at oblique angle"] nodeA --> nodeB nodeB --> nodeC nodeC --> nodeD nodeD --> nodeE nodeD --> nodeF nodeD --> nodeG style nodeA fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style nodeB fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style nodeC fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style nodeD fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px style nodeE fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style nodeF fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style nodeG fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 4.2 Frank‚ÄìRead Source Mechanism (Functional Architecture Flow) ```mermaid flowchart TD stage1["STAGE 1: INITIAL
Pinned dislocation segment
length L between points A and B"] stage2["STAGE 2: BOWING
Applied shear stress tau
bows segment into an arc"] stage3["STAGE 3: SEMICIRCLE
Radius R equals L over 2
UNSTABLE CONFIGURATION"] stage4["STAGE 4: LOOP EXPANSION
Loop expands spontaneously
at constant tau"] stage5["STAGE 5: WRAP AROUND
Two arms meet
behind pinning points"] stage6["STAGE 6: ANNihilation
Opposite sign segments
cancel out"] stage7["STAGE 7: LOOP RELEASE
Closed loop detaches
and glides away"] stage8["STAGE 8: RESET
Original segment regenerates
Ready for next cycle"] stage9["STAGE 9: MULTIPLICATION
Process repeats
Dislocation density RISES"] stage1 --> stage2 stage2 --> stage3 stage3 --> stage4 stage4 --> stage5 stage5 --> stage6 stage6 --> stage7 stage7 --> stage8 stage8 --> stage9 stage8 -.-> stage1 style stage1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style stage3 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px style stage6 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style stage9 fill:#32CD32,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px ``` ## 4.3 Dislocation Type Classification (Block Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart LR root["DISLOCATION
CLASSIFICATION"] type1["EDGE
b PERPENDICULAR to line"] type2["SCREW
b PARALLEL to line"] type3["MIXED
b OBLIQUE to line"] prop1["Extra half-plane
present"] prop2["Helical distortion
of lattice"] prop3["Edge plus screw
components combined"] geom1["Slip direction
perpendicular to line"] geom2["Slip direction
parallel to line"] geom3["Slip direction
at variable angle"] root --> type1 root --> type2 root --> type3 type1 --> prop1 type2 --> prop2 type3 --> prop3 type1 --> geom1 type2 --> geom2 type3 --> geom3 style root fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px style type1 fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style type2 fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style type3 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 4.4 Stress vs Source Length Relationship (Conceptual Flow) ```mermaid flowchart TD input["INPUTS: G, b, L"] formula["Formula:
tau_c = G b divided by L"] output1["SHORT SOURCE
L small
tau_c LARGE
Hard to activate"] output2["LONG SOURCE
L large
tau_c SMALL
Easy to activate"] application["APPLICATION:
Precipitates PIN sources

INCREASE strength"] input --> formula formula --> output1 formula --> output2 output2 --> application style input fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style formula fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px style output1 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style output2 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style application fill:#FFA500,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 4.5 Energy Storage Schematic in Cold-Worked Metal ```mermaid flowchart TB anneal["ANNEALED METAL
rho equals 10^6 per m^2
Low dislocation density"] deform["APPLIED STRESS
Activates Frank Read sources"] multiply["DISLOCATION MULTIPLICATION
rho increases to 10^14 per m^2"] store["STORED ENERGY
E equals rho times Gb squared over 2
Roughly 100 to 1000 kJ per m cubed"] harden["STRAIN HARDENING
Higher yield stress
Higher dislocation density"] anneal --> deform deform --> multiply multiply --> store store --> harden harden -. "Heat treatment anneal" .-> anneal style anneal fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style multiply fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style store fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px style harden fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Burgers Vector Concept **[KTU University Exam ‚Äì Dec 2023, Model Paper Set A]** **Q: Define Burgers vector. Explain how the Burgers circuit is constructed to determine it.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **(1 mark)** Burgers vector $\vec{b}$ is the vector representing the magnitude and direction of lattice distortion produced by a dislocation. - **(1 mark)** Construction: A closed loop (Burgers circuit) of equal atomic steps is drawn around the dislocation line in the real crystal. The same sequence of steps is transferred to a perfect reference crystal; the closure failure vector defines $\vec{b}$. - **(1 mark)** For an edge dislocation $\vec{b} \perp$ dislocation line; for a screw dislocation $\vec{b} \parallel$ dislocation line. --- ### Question 2: Frank‚ÄìRead Source **[KTU University Exam ‚Äì June 2024, Model Paper]** **Q: What is a Frank‚ÄìRead source? Why is it important in plastic deformation?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **(1 mark)** A Frank‚ÄìRead source is a pinned segment of dislocation that, under applied shear stress, bows out, generates a closed dislocation loop, and regenerates itself, producing a continuous supply of new dislocations. - **(1 mark)** Importance: It is the primary mechanism of dislocation multiplication, increasing dislocation density from $\sim 10^6 \, \text{m}^{-2}$ to $\sim 10^{14} \, \text{m}^{-2}$ during plastic deformation. - **(1 mark)** The critical stress required is $\tau_c = Gb/L$, where $L$ is the pinned segment length. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each ‚Äî Internal Choice) ### Question A: Comprehensive Problem **[KTU University Exam ‚Äì Dec 2023]** **(a)** Define Burgers vector. With a neat sketch, explain how it is determined for an edge dislocation and a screw dislocation. Discuss the relationship between dislocation line direction and Burgers vector in each case. **(7 marks)** **(b)** What is a Frank‚ÄìRead source? Describe its operation with a schematic diagram. Derive the expression for the critical resolved shear stress required to operate a Frank‚ÄìRead source of length $L$. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Answer:** - **Definition (2 marks):** Burgers vector $\vec{b}$ is the vector required to close the Burgers circuit, defining the magnitude and direction of slip. It is invariant along a single dislocation and is the smallest lattice translation vector. - **Edge dislocation (2 marks):** $\vec{b}$ is perpendicular to the dislocation line. Construct Burgers circuit around the extra half-plane; closure failure in the perfect crystal gives $\vec{b}$ along the slip direction, perpendicular to the line. - **Screw dislocation (2 marks):** $\vec{b}$ is parallel to the dislocation line. The Burgers circuit around a screw shows closure failure parallel to the line direction. - **Relationship summary (1 mark):** Edge: $\vec{b} \perp \xi$; Screw: $\vec{b} \parallel \xi$; Mixed: $0 < \theta < 90¬∞$. **(b) Answer:** - **Definition (1 mark):** A Frank‚ÄìRead source is a dislocation multiplication mechanism using a pinned segment of length $L$ that, under shear stress, generates closed loops continuously. - **Operation steps (3 marks):** 1. Initial: Straight pinned segment AB. 2. Bowing: Stress bows the segment into an arc. 3. Critical semicircle: When $R = L/2$, configuration is unstable. 4. Loop expansion and detachment. 5. Original segment regenerates; cycle repeats. - **Derivation (3 marks):** - Line tension: $T = Gb^2/2$ - Force balance: $\tau b = T/R = Gb^2/(2R)$ - Critical radius: $R = L/2$ - Therefore: $\tau_c b = Gb^2/L \Rightarrow \tau_c = Gb/L$. --- ### Question B: Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam ‚Äì June 2024]** **(a)** Derive the expression for elastic strain energy per unit length of a screw dislocation. Comment on the factors affecting dislocation energy. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Discuss the role of Frank‚ÄìRead sources in work hardening. Explain how precipitation hardening increases the critical stress for Frank‚ÄìRead source operation. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Answer:** - **Stress field (2 marks):** For a screw dislocation: $\tau_{\theta z} = Gb/(2\pi r)$. - **Strain energy density (1 mark):** $u = \tau^2/(2G) = Gb^2/(8\pi^2 r^2)$. - **Integration (2 marks):** $E_{\text{line}} = \int_{r_0}^{R} u \cdot 2\pi r \, dr = (Gb^2/4\pi) \ln(R/r_0)$. - **Order of magnitude (1 mark):** $E_{\text{line}} \approx Gb^2/2$ for $R/r_0 \approx 10^5$. - **Factors (1 mark):** $G$, $b$, and $\ln(R/r_0)$ ‚Äî larger $G$ or $b$ increases energy; larger dislocation density reduces $R$ and hence energy. **(b) Answer:** - **Work hardening role (3 marks):** Frank‚ÄìRead sources multiply dislocations during plastic deformation. As $\rho$ increases, the mean free path for dislocations decreases, raising the stress needed for further slip (Taylor relation: $\tau = \tau_0 + \alpha G b \sqrt{\rho}$). - **Precipitation hardening mechanism (4 marks):** Fine, hard precipitates (e.g., $\theta''$ in Al‚ÄìCu) act as pinning points, reducing the effective Frank‚ÄìRead source length $L_{\text{eff}}$. Since $\tau_c = Gb/L$, reducing $L$ dramatically increases the critical stress. Additionally, dislocations must either cut through particles (creating antiphase boundary or chemical energy cost) or bypass them via Orowan looping, both raising the macroscopic flow stress. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Confusing Burgers vector with dislocation line direction** ‚Äî Students often draw $\vec{b}$ along the line for an edge dislocation. Remember: edge ‚Üí $\vec{b} \perp$ line; screw ‚Üí $\vec{b} \parallel$ line. > 2. **Wrong critical radius** ‚Äî The instability occurs at $R = L/2$ (semicircle), not $R = L$. Marks deducted if derivation skips this geometric condition. > 3. **Forgetting units** ‚Äî Energy is in J/m, stress in Pa, force per length in N/m. Examiners deduct 0.5 marks for missing units in final numerical answers. > 4. **Writing line tension as $Gb$** instead of $Gb^2/2$** ‚Äî this is a frequent error worth losing 1‚Äì2 marks. > 5. **Not mentioning conservation of Burgers vector** at dislocation nodes ‚Äî this is a recurring KTU sub-question worth 2 marks. > 6. **Mixing up Frank‚ÄìRead source multiplication with other mechanisms** (e.g., cross-slip, climb) ‚Äî examiners expect explicit mention that pinning is required for F‚ÄìR operation. > 7. **Skipping the logarithmic term in dislocation energy** ‚Äî using only $Gb^2/2$ without showing the full derivation loses 2 marks. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist ‚Äî KTU ESE Module 2 (Dislocations):** ### Burgers Vector Essentials - ‚úÖ Burgers vector $\vec{b}$ is the **closure failure** of a Burgers circuit. - ‚úÖ Always the **smallest lattice translation vector**: $\frac{a}{2}\langle 110 \rangle$ for FCC, $\frac{a}{2}\langle 111 \rangle$ for BCC. - ‚úÖ Edge dislocation: $\vec{b} \perp$ line; Screw dislocation: $\vec{b} \parallel$ line. - ‚úÖ **Conservation law**: $\sum \vec{b_i} = 0$ at dislocation nodes. - ‚úÖ $|\vec{b}|$ is invariant along a single dislocation line. ### Frank‚ÄìRead Source Essentials - ‚úÖ Requires a **pinned segment** of length $L$ (obstacles act as pins). - ‚úÖ Operates when applied $\tau \geq \tau_c = Gb/L$. - ‚úÖ Critical condition: bowed segment forms a **semicircle** ($R = L/2$). - ‚úÖ Generates **dislocation multiplication** ‚Äî primary source of dislocations during plastic deformation. - ‚úÖ Drives **work hardening** by increasing $\rho$; **precipitation hardening** works by reducing $L_{\text{eff}}$. ### Key Equations (Must Memorize) - ‚úÖ $\tau_c = Gb/L$ (Frank‚ÄìRead critical stress). - ‚úÖ $E_{\text{line}} = \frac{Gb^2}{4\pi}\ln(R/r_0) \approx \frac{Gb^2}{2}$ (dislocation energy). - ‚úÖ $F/L = \tau b$ (Peach‚ÄìKoehler in pure shear). - ‚úÖ $\tau = \tau_0 + \alpha Gb\sqrt{\rho}$ (Taylor hardening relation). ### High-Yield Numerical Tricks - ‚úÖ For FCC Cu: $b = 0.2556 \, \text{nm}$. - ‚úÖ For BCC Fe: $b = 0.2482 \, \text{nm}$. - ‚úÖ Always substitute $R = L/2$ when deriving critical Frank‚ÄìRead stress. - ‚úÖ Typical $\tau_c$ values are $\sim 10^{-4} G$ to $10^{-2} G$ ‚Äî verifies against experimental yield stresses.

topics.title: Correlation of dislocation density with strength and the concept of nanomaterials
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAUT304 - MATERIAL SCIENCE AND METALLURGY
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Correlation of dislocation density with strength and the concept of nanomaterials

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Dislocation Density ‚Äî Formal KTU Definition

**Dislocation density (œÅ)** is formally defined as the total length of dislocation lines per unit volume of a crystalline material. It is a scalar quantity representing the average density of linear crystallographic defects within a crystal lattice.

$$\rho = \frac{L}{V} \quad \left[ \frac{\text{m}}{\text{m}^3} = \text{m}^{-2} \right]$$

Where:
- $L$ = Total length of dislocation lines (m)
- $V$ = Volume of the crystal (m¬≥)
- Typical units: **m‚Åª¬≤** or **cm/cm¬≥** or **lines/cm¬≤**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Dislocation density directly governs the **mechanical strength** of metals. Annealed (soft) metals have œÅ ‚âà **10‚Å∂ ‚Äì 10‚Å∏** lines/m¬≤, whereas heavily cold-worked metals have œÅ ‚âà **10¬π‚Å¥ ‚Äì 10¬π‚Å∂** lines/m¬≤.

## 1.2 Nanomaterials ‚Äî Formal KTU Definition

**Nanomaterials** are defined as materials whose structural components (grains, particles, fibers, tubes, or layers) have at least one dimension measuring **less than 100 nanometers (nm)**. At this scale, materials exhibit dramatically different mechanical, optical, electrical, and magnetic properties compared to their bulk counterparts ‚Äî a phenomenon governed by **size-dependent quantum and surface effects**.

Classification based on dimensionality of confinement:

| Nanomaterial Type | Confined Dimensions | Example |
|---|---|---|
| 0-D (Nanoparticles) | All 3 | Quantum dots, metal nanoparticles |
| 1-D (Nanowires/Nanotubes) | 2 | Carbon nanotubes (CNT) |
| 2-D (Nanosheets/Films) | 1 | Graphene, MXenes |
| 3-D (Bulk Nanostructures) | 0 | Nanocrystalline metals |

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy ‚Äî The Traffic Jam Model of Dislocations

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy ‚Äî "Vehicles on a Highway":**
> Imagine a multi-lane highway representing a slip plane in a crystal. **Cars represent dislocations** moving along this plane. When traffic is light (low œÅ), cars move freely ‚Äî the metal deforms easily (soft). Now imagine a sudden traffic jam where cars pile bumper-to-bumper (high œÅ). The dislocations can no longer glide freely ‚Äî they block each other's motion. This is exactly what happens in a metal: as dislocation density increases, dislocations impede each other, and the metal becomes **stronger and harder**. This is called **dislocation strengthening** or **strain hardening**.

## 1.4 Intuitive Analogy ‚Äî Nanomaterials as "Grain-City" Reduction

Think of a metal as a city made of city blocks (grains). In a regular city (bulk material), a crack has to navigate through many large blocks. In a **nanomaterial city**, the blocks are reduced to the size of a single brick ‚Äî a crack has thousands of grain boundaries to navigate, making it nearly impossible to propagate. This is the **Hall-Petch strengthening** principle at the nano-scale.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Bailey-Hirsch Equation (Dislocation Strengthening)

The fundamental relationship linking **shear strength** to **dislocation density** is the **Bailey-Hirsch equation** (1956). It arises from the elastic interaction stress fields of dislocations.

### 2.1.1 Physical Origin

- Every dislocation is surrounded by a **stress field** extending approximately 1‚Äì10 atomic distances.
- When two dislocations approach each other, their stress fields **overlap** and **repel**.
- The stress required to force one dislocation past another increases with the square root of dislocation density.

### 2.1.2 Mathematical Form

The shear stress required to move a dislocation through a forest of others is:

$$\tau = \tau_0 + \alpha \, G \, b \, \sqrt{\rho}$$

Where:
- $\tau$ = Shear flow stress (Pa)
- $\tau_0$ = Lattice friction stress (Pa) ‚Äî intrinsic resistance of a perfect crystal
- $\alpha$ = **Strengthening constant** (typically 0.3 ‚Äì 0.5 for FCC metals, 0.4 ‚Äì 0.6 for BCC metals)
- $G$ = **Shear modulus** of the material (Pa)
- $b$ = **Magnitude of Burgers vector** (m) ‚Äî the atomic slip distance
- $\rho$ = **Dislocation density** (m‚Åª¬≤)

## 2.2 Conversion to Tensile Yield Strength

For most metals, the **Tensile Yield Strength** $\sigma_y$ relates to shear stress via the **Schmid-Boas orientation factor** for polycrystals (Taylor factor $M \approx 3.06$):

$$\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + M \cdot \alpha \, G \, b \, \sqrt{\rho}$$

Where:
- $\sigma_0$ = Friction stress in tension
- $M$ = Taylor factor (‚âà **3.06** for FCC random polycrystal)
- The product $M \cdot \alpha$ is often lumped into a single constant $k$ for engineering use.

## 2.3 The Hall-Petch Equation (Grain Boundary Strengthening)

The yield strength increases as the **inverse square root of average grain diameter**:

$$\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + \frac{k_y}{\sqrt{d}}$$

Where:
- $d$ = Average grain diameter (m)
- $k_y$ = **Hall-Petch coefficient** (Pa¬∑m^(1/2))
- $\sigma_0$ = Friction stress

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Connection:** When grain size $d$ enters the **nanometer regime** (typically below 100 nm), the Hall-Petch relationship **breaks down** and even reverses (the **inverse Hall-Petch effect**). This is a critical concept for KTU exams.

## 2.4 Inverse Hall-Petch Effect (Nanomaterials Anomaly)

When grain size is reduced below a critical value $d_c$ (typically 10‚Äì20 nm):

$$\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + \frac{k_y}{\sqrt{d}} \quad \text{(for } d > d_c\text{)}$$

$$\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y \sqrt{d} \quad \text{(for } d < d_c\text{)}$$

**Physical reasons for softening at very small grain sizes:**
1. **Grain boundary sliding** dominates over dislocation motion.
2. **Diffusion creep** (Coble creep, Nabarro-Herring creep) becomes significant.
3. **Dislocation starvation** ‚Äî grains become too small to sustain stable dislocation networks.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Formula** | **Name** | **Variables & Units** | **Engineering Use** |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\rho = L/V$ | Dislocation Density | $L$ (m), $V$ (m¬≥), $\rho$ (m‚Åª¬≤) | Quantifying deformation |
| $\tau = \tau_0 + \alpha G b \sqrt{\rho}$ | Bailey-Hirsch | $\tau, \tau_0$ (Pa), $G$ (Pa), $b$ (m), $\rho$ (m‚Åª¬≤) | Strain hardening design |
| $\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + M\alpha G b \sqrt{\rho}$ | Tensile Equivalent | Same as above, $M \approx 3.06$ | Tensile property prediction |
| $\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y / \sqrt{d}$ | Hall-Petch | $d$ (m), $k_y$ (Pa¬∑m^¬Ω) | Grain refinement for strength |
| $\sigma_y \propto \sqrt{d}$ (for $d < 10$ nm) | Inverse Hall-Petch | Same variables | Nanomaterial design |
| $\sigma_y \propto 1/L$ (Orowan) | Precipitate Strengthening | $L$ = inter-particle spacing (m) | Alloy design |
| $V/V_\text{nano} = 1 - e^{-t/\xi}$ | Surface Atom Fraction | $t$ = atom diameter, $\xi$ = particle size | Surface effect quantification |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| **Application Sector** | **Utility** |
|---|---|
| Automotive Industry | Nano-grained steel sheets for crash-resistant car bodies |
| Aerospace | CNT-reinforced aluminum for lightweight high-strength parts |
| Electronics | Nanowire transistors in next-generation semiconductors |
| Medical | Nanocrystalline titanium implants (biocompatible + strong) |
| Energy | Nanostructured electrodes in lithium-ion batteries |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Single Dislocation Stress Field to Bailey-Hirsch

### Step 1: Stress Field of a Single Edge Dislocation

The shear stress field around an isolated edge dislocation at radial distance $r$ from the core is given by classical elasticity theory:

$$\tau(r) = \frac{Gb}{2\pi(1-\nu)} \cdot \frac{1}{r}$$

Where:
- $G$ = Shear modulus
- $b$ = Burgers vector magnitude
- $\nu$ = Poisson's ratio
- $r$ = Radial distance from dislocation core

### Step 2: Average Spacing Between Dislocations

For a random network of dislocations with density $\rho$, consider a region of area $A$ and thickness $b$ (one Burgers vector layer). The number of dislocations piercing this region is $n = \rho \cdot A \cdot b$.

The **average spacing** $\ell$ between dislocations is obtained by setting $\ell^2 \cdot b \cdot \rho = 1$:

$$\ell = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\rho \cdot b}} \approx \frac{1}{\sqrt{\rho}} \quad \text{(since } b \approx 10^{-10}\text{ m is constant)}$$

### Step 3: Critical Shear Stress to Overcome Dislocation Interaction

The applied shear stress must equal the stress field of a neighboring dislocation at distance $\ell$ to force glide past it:

$$\tau_c = \frac{Gb}{2\pi(1-\nu)} \cdot \frac{1}{\ell}$$

### Step 4: Substitute Spacing Expression

Substituting $\ell = 1/\sqrt{\rho}$:

$$\tau_c = \frac{Gb}{2\pi(1-\nu)} \cdot \sqrt{\rho}$$

### Step 5: Absorb Constants into Œ±

Combining $1/[2\pi(1-\nu)]$ with other geometric and orientation-dependent constants into a single empirical constant $\alpha$:

$$\tau = \tau_0 + \alpha G b \sqrt{\rho}$$

### Step 6: Conversion to Tensile Strength

Using the Taylor relation for polycrystals $\tau = \sigma_y / M$:

$$\frac{\sigma_y}{M} = \tau_0/M + \alpha G b \sqrt{\rho}$$

Defining $\sigma_0 = M\tau_0$ and combining $M\alpha$ into a single constant:

$$\boxed{\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k \cdot G \cdot b \cdot \sqrt{\rho}}$$

This is the final KTU-required form linking tensile strength to dislocation density.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example ‚Äî KTU Style

> **Problem:** A copper alloy has been cold-worked. Given:
> - $G = 48 \times 10^9$ Pa
> - $b = 2.56 \times 10^{-10}$ m
> - $\rho = 10^{14}$ m‚Åª¬≤
> - $\sigma_0 = 25 \times 10^6$ Pa
> - $\alpha = 0.4$
> - $M = 3.06$
>
> **Calculate the new yield strength.**

### Solution

**Step 1:** Calculate the strengthening increment:

$$\Delta\sigma = M \cdot \alpha \cdot G \cdot b \cdot \sqrt{\rho}$$

**Step 2:** Evaluate $\sqrt{\rho}$:

$$\sqrt{\rho} = \sqrt{10^{14}} = 10^{7} \text{ m}^{-1}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute numerical values:

$$\Delta\sigma = 3.06 \times 0.4 \times (48 \times 10^9) \times (2.56 \times 10^{-10}) \times (10^{7})$$

**Step 4:** Multiply sequentially:

$$\Delta\sigma = 3.06 \times 0.4 \times 48 \times 2.56 \times 10^{9-10+7}$$

$$\Delta\sigma = 3.06 \times 0.4 \times 122.88 \times 10^{6}$$

$$3.06 \times 0.4 = 1.224$$

$$1.224 \times 122.88 = 150.40$$

$$\Delta\sigma \approx 150.4 \times 10^{6} \text{ Pa} = 150.4 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 5:** Add base strength:

$$\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + \Delta\sigma = 25 + 150.4 = 175.4 \text{ MPa}$$

### Final Answer

$$\boxed{\sigma_y \approx 175.4 \text{ MPa}}$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix ‚Äî Comparison of Strengthening Mechanisms

| **Mechanism** | **Governing Equation** | **Typical Magnitude** | **Operative at Nano-Scale?** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Dislocation Strengthening | $\Delta\sigma \propto \sqrt{\rho}$ | 50‚Äì300 MPa | Becomes weak (dislocation starvation) |
| Grain Boundary (Hall-Petch) | $\Delta\sigma \propto 1/\sqrt{d}$ | 100‚Äì500 MPa | Yes (until $d < 10$ nm) |
| Solid Solution | $\Delta\sigma \propto \sqrt{c}$ | 10‚Äì100 MPa | Partially |
| Precipitate (Orowan) | $\Delta\sigma \propto \ln(d) / L$ | 50‚Äì400 MPa | Yes |
| Dispersion | $\Delta\sigma \propto 1/d_p$ | Up to 500 MPa | Yes |
| Texture | Depends on $M$ factor | 10‚Äì50 MPa | Strong |

## 3.4 Derivation of the Critical Grain Size for Hall-Petch Breakdown

Setting the Hall-Petch stress equal to the Orowan stress (single dislocation bowing across a grain):

$$\frac{k_y}{\sqrt{d_c}} = \frac{Gb}{d_c}$$

Solving for $d_c$:

$$\frac{k_y \sqrt{d_c}}{1} = Gb$$

$$\sqrt{d_c} = \frac{Gb}{k_y}$$

$$d_c = \left(\frac{Gb}{k_y}\right)^2$$

**Numerical estimate for copper** ($G = 48$ GPa, $b = 2.56 \times 10^{-10}$ m, $k_y = 0.18$ MPa¬∑m^(1/2)):

$$d_c = \left(\frac{48 \times 10^9 \times 2.56 \times 10^{-10}}{0.18 \times 10^6}\right)^2$$

$$= \left(\frac{12.288}{0.18}\right)^2 = (68.27)^2 \approx 4660 \text{ nm} \approx 4.66 \text{ ¬µm}$$

This shows that for typical FCC metals, the Hall-Petch breakdown occurs around **sub-micron to tens of nanometers**, validating the relevance of nano-grain strengthening.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 ‚Äî Mechanism of Dislocation Strengthening

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Applied Shear Stress tau] --> B{Dislocation Network Density rho}
    B --> C[Low rho: 10^6 - 10^8 m^-2]
    B --> D[Medium rho: 10^10 - 10^12 m^-2]
    B --> E[High rho: 10^14 - 10^16 m^-2]
    C --> F[Easy Glide on Slip Planes]
    F --> G[Low Yield Strength: Soft Metal]
    D --> H[Dislocation Pile-ups Form]
    H --> I[Moderate Yield Strength]
    E --> J[Forest Dislocations Entangle]
    J --> K[High Yield Strength: Hard Metal]
    K --> L[Eventual Saturation at rho_max]
    L --> M[Strain Hardening Limit Reached]

    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style B fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style C fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style D fill:#FFA500,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style E fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style G fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style I fill:#FFA500,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style K fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style M fill:#8B0000,stroke:#FFF,color:#FFF
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 ‚Äî Nanomaterial Strengthening Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Bulk Material] --> B[Microcrystalline]
    B --> C[Ultrafine Grained 100 to 1000 nm]
    C --> D[Nanocrystalline less than 100 nm]
    D --> E{Critical Size d_c}
    E -->|d greater than d_c| F[Hall-Petch Strengthening]
    E -->|d less than d_c| G[Inverse Hall-Petch Softening]
    F --> F1[Dislocation Pile-up at GB]
    F1 --> F2[Stress Concentration]
    F2 --> F3[Activation of Slip]
    F3 --> F4[High Yield Strength]
    G --> G1[Grain Boundary Sliding Dominates]
    G1 --> G2[Diffusion Creep Active]
    G2 --> G3[Dislocation Starvation]
    G3 --> G4[Reduced Yield Strength]

    style A fill:#D3D3D3,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style D fill:#FF69B4,stroke:#000,color:#FFF
    style E fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style F fill:#32CD32,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style G fill:#DC143C,stroke:#000,color:#FFF
    style F4 fill:#006400,stroke:#000,color:#FFF
    style G4 fill:#8B0000,stroke:#000,color:#FFF
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 ‚Äî Block Functional Architecture of Nanomaterial Property Origin

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph NANO[Nanomaterial Property Engine]
        SE[Surface Effects]
        QE[Quantum Size Effects]
        DE[Dislocation Dynamics]
        GB[Grain Boundary Volume Fraction]
    end

    SE --> SE1[High Surface to Volume Ratio]
    SE1 --> SE2[Enhanced Reactivity]
    SE2 --> SE3[Catalysis, Sensing]

    QE --> QE1[Confinement of Electrons]
    QE1 --> QE2[Band Gap Widening]
    QE2 --> QE3[Optoelectronics]

    DE --> DE1[Dislocation Starvation]
    DE1 --> DE2[Limited Slip Systems]
    DE2 --> DE3[Anomalous Strength]

    GB --> GB1[Large GB Volume Fraction]
    GB1 --> GB2[GB Diffusion Dominant]
    GB2 --> GB3[Creep, Softening]

    NANO --> OUT[Engineering Performance]
    SE3 --> OUT
    QE3 --> OUT
    DE3 --> OUT
    GB3 --> OUT

    style NANO fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style OUT fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ‚Äî Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Similar Style]`

**Define dislocation density. How does it influence the mechanical strength of a metal?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Dislocation density $\rho$ is defined as the total length of dislocation lines per unit volume of the crystal. It is expressed as $\rho = L/V$ with units of **m‚Åª¬≤** (or lines/m¬≤).

**Influence on strength (2 marks):** According to the **Bailey-Hirsch relationship**, the shear stress required for plastic deformation increases with the square root of dislocation density:

$$\tau = \tau_0 + \alpha G b \sqrt{\rho}$$

As plastic deformation progresses, dislocations multiply and entangle, increasing $\rho$. This impedes further dislocation motion, raising the metal's yield strength and hardness ‚Äî a phenomenon known as **strain hardening** or **work hardening**.

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Similar Style]`

**What are nanomaterials? Mention any two distinct properties exhibited by them.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Nanomaterials are materials whose structural components have at least one dimension less than **100 nm**, leading to size-dependent properties not observed in bulk counterparts.

**Two distinct properties (2 marks):**

1. **High surface-to-volume ratio:** A significant fraction of atoms resides at surfaces, enhancing chemical reactivity (e.g., nano-silver as antibacterial agent).

2. **Quantum confinement effects:** Electrons are confined in low-dimensional structures, altering band gap and producing unique optical properties (e.g., quantum dots emitting different colors based on size).

3. *Optional third:* Exceptional mechanical strength (Hall-Petch enhancement) ‚Äî nano-grained metals can be 3‚Äì10√ó stronger than their coarse-grained counterparts.

---

## 5.2 Part B ‚Äî Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Predicted Pattern]`**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the Bailey-Hirsch equation showing the relationship between shear stress and dislocation density. Discuss the physical significance of each term involved.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A copper specimen with original dislocation density $10^{10}$ m‚Åª¬≤ is cold-worked, increasing its dislocation density to $10^{14}$ m‚Åª¬≤. Given $G = 48$ GPa, $b = 2.56 \times 10^{-10}$ m, $\alpha = 0.4$, $\sigma_0 = 25$ MPa, $M = 3.06$, calculate: (i) The increase in yield strength. (ii) The percentage increase in yield strength.

### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) Derivation [7 Marks]:**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** The shear stress field around an isolated edge dislocation at distance $r$ is:

$$\tau(r) = \frac{Gb}{2\pi(1-\nu)r}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** For a dislocation network of density $\rho$, the average spacing $\ell$ between dislocations is:

$$\ell = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\rho}}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** The applied shear stress must overcome the interaction stress at this characteristic spacing:

$$\tau_c = \frac{Gb}{2\pi(1-\nu)\ell} = \frac{Gb\sqrt{\rho}}{2\pi(1-\nu)}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Adding the lattice friction stress $\tau_0$ and combining constants into $\alpha$:

$$\tau = \tau_0 + \alpha G b \sqrt{\rho}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Converting to tensile strength using Taylor factor $M$:

$$\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + M\alpha G b \sqrt{\rho}$$

**Physical significance of terms (2 marks):**
- $\tau_0, \sigma_0$: Intrinsic lattice resistance (Peierls-Nabarro stress).
- $G$: Material stiffness; higher modulus means stronger dislocation interaction.
- $b$: Atomic slip distance; smaller $b$ means closer-packed slip and stronger barriers.
- $\rho$: Direct measure of deformation; higher $\rho$ = more dislocation pile-ups = harder metal.
- $\alpha$: Geometric constant capturing dislocation character and arrangement.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution [7 Marks]:**

**(i) Increase in yield strength [4 marks]:**

$$\Delta\sigma = M\alpha G b (\sqrt{\rho_2} - \sqrt{\rho_1})$$

Calculate square roots:

$$\sqrt{\rho_1} = \sqrt{10^{10}} = 10^{5} \text{ m}^{-1}$$

$$\sqrt{\rho_2} = \sqrt{10^{14}} = 10^{7} \text{ m}^{-1}$$

Substituting:

$$\Delta\sigma = 3.06 \times 0.4 \times (48 \times 10^9) \times (2.56 \times 10^{-10}) \times (10^{7} - 10^{5})$$

$$= 3.06 \times 0.4 \times 48 \times 2.56 \times 10^{6} \times (0.9999 \times 10^{7})$$

$$= 150.4 \text{ MPa} \times 9.999 \approx 1503.85 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta\sigma \approx 1503.85 \text{ MPa} \approx 1.50 \text{ GPa}}$$

**(ii) Percentage increase [3 marks]:**

Initial yield strength:

$$\sigma_{y1} = 25 + 150.4 \times 10^{5} = 25 + 0.01504 \approx 25.015 \text{ MPa}$$

Final yield strength:

$$\sigma_{y2} = 25.015 + 1503.85 = 1528.87 \text{ MPa}$$

Percentage increase:

$$\% \text{ increase} = \frac{\sigma_{y2} - \sigma_{y1}}{\sigma_{y1}} \times 100 = \frac{1503.85}{25.015} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{\% \text{ increase} \approx 6011\%}$$

---

### **Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Predicted Pattern]`**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the Hall-Petch relationship for grain boundary strengthening. Under what conditions does the inverse Hall-Petch effect occur in nanomaterials? Discuss its physical reasons.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare the mechanical behavior of conventional coarse-grained metals with nanocrystalline materials. Discuss three major applications of nanomaterials in modern engineering with specific examples.

### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) Hall-Petch Relationship [7 Marks]:**

**Statement of relationship (2 marks):**

$$\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + \frac{k_y}{\sqrt{d}}$$

Where $d$ is the average grain diameter and $k_y$ is the Hall-Petch coefficient. The yield strength increases linearly with $d^{-1/2}$.

**Physical mechanism (2 marks):** Dislocation pile-ups form against grain boundaries. The number of dislocations in a pile-up is proportional to $\sqrt{d}$. The stress concentration at the pile-up tip activates slip in the adjacent grain. Smaller grains ‚Üí shorter pile-ups ‚Üí higher applied stress needed ‚Üí stronger material.

**Inverse Hall-Petch Effect (3 marks):**

When grain size $d$ is reduced below a critical value $d_c$ (typically **10‚Äì20 nm**), the relationship inverts:

$$\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y \sqrt{d} \quad \text{(for } d < d_c\text{)}$$

**Physical reasons:**

1. **Grain boundary sliding:** At nano-scale, the large fraction of atoms at grain boundaries (up to 50%) enables GBs to slide past each other rather than transmit slip.

2. **Diffusion creep dominance:** Coble creep (GB diffusion) and Nabarro-Herring creep (lattice diffusion) mechanisms become active at small sizes.

3. **Dislocation starvation:** Grains become too small to sustain stable dislocation networks; dislocations glide to and are absorbed by GBs without multiplication.

---

**(b) Comparison and Applications [7 Marks]:**

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| **Property** | **Coarse-Grained** | **Nanocrystalline** |
|---|---|---|
| Yield Strength | Low to moderate (50‚Äì200 MPa) | Very high (500‚Äì2000 MPa) |
| Ductility | High (20‚Äì50%) | Limited (1‚Äì5%) |
| Grain Size | 10‚Äì100 ¬µm | Less than 100 nm |
| Deformation Mechanism | Dislocation glide | GB sliding, diffusion |
| Hall-Petch Regime | Always strengthening | Strengthening then softening |

**Three Applications (4 marks):**

1. **Automotive ‚Äî Nano-grained high-strength steel (AHSS):** Modern car bodies use dual-phase steels with grain sizes of 1‚Äì5 ¬µm and precipitates at the nano-scale, achieving tensile strengths above 1500 MPa. This enables weight reduction and improved crash safety.

2. **Aerospace ‚Äî Carbon nanotube (CNT) reinforced aluminum composites:** CNTs (1-D nanomaterials) embedded in Al matrices provide strength-to-weight ratios 10√ó greater than conventional Al alloys, used in aircraft structural components and spacecraft parts.

3. **Biomedical ‚Äî Nanocrystalline titanium implants:** Pure Ti with grain size below 100 nm exhibits Vickers hardness 3√ó higher than coarse Ti, improving wear resistance of hip and dental implants while maintaining biocompatibility.

4. *Optional:* Electronics ‚Äî Graphene (2-D nanomaterial) transistors with mobility exceeding silicon by an order of magnitude, enabling faster and more energy-efficient chips.

---

## 5.3 ‚öÝÔ∏è KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Unit Mismatch in Dislocation Density:** Students often write $\rho$ in m‚Åª¬≥ instead of m‚Åª¬≤. Remember ‚Äî it's length per **volume**, but the line is **1-D**, giving area‚Åª¬π in 2-D projection. Always state **m‚Åª¬≤** explicitly.
>
> 2. **Confusion Between œÑ and œÉ_y:** The Bailey-Hirsch equation gives **shear stress**, not tensile stress. Multiply by Taylor factor $M$ (‚âà3.06) for tensile equivalent. Forgetting this conversion loses 1‚Äì2 marks.
>
> 3. **Hall-Petch vs. Inverse Hall-Petch:** Do NOT apply the standard $1/\sqrt{d}$ formula blindly for grain sizes below 10 nm. Examiners specifically test this conceptual boundary.
>
> 4. **Misinterpreting $\sqrt{\rho}$:** It is NOT $\rho$ raised to half-power algebraically inside the stress; ensure correct placement in numerical problems.
>
> 5. **Nanomaterial Definition:** Writing "less than 1000 nm" instead of "less than 100 nm" ‚Äî this is a 1-mark direct deduction.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> üìå **Rapid Revision Checklist:**
>
> - **Dislocation density $\rho$** = total dislocation length per unit volume, units **m‚Åª¬≤**.
> - **Bailey-Hirsch equation:** $\tau = \tau_0 + \alpha G b \sqrt{\rho}$ ‚Äî the cornerstone relationship.
> - **Tensile form:** $\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + M\alpha G b \sqrt{\rho}$, with **Taylor factor $M \approx 3.06$**.
> - **Typical $\alpha$ values:** 0.3‚Äì0.5 for FCC, 0.4‚Äì0.6 for BCC metals.
> - **Hall-Petch equation:** $\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y / \sqrt{d}$ ‚Äî valid for $d > d_c$.
> - **Inverse Hall-Petch** occurs for $d < 10$ nm due to GB sliding, diffusion creep, and dislocation starvation.
> - **Nanomaterial definition:** at least one dimension $< 100$ nm.
> - **Classification:** 0-D (particles), 1-D (tubes/wires), 2-D (sheets/films), 3-D (bulk nanostructured).
> - **Surface-to-volume ratio** becomes extremely high in nanomaterials, dominating properties.
> - **Strengthening mechanisms magnitude order:** Solid Solution (small) < Dislocation (medium) < Grain Boundary (large).
> - **Engineering applications:** AHSS automotive, CNT-Al aerospace, nano-Ti implants, graphene electronics.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Grain boundaries: high and low angle boundaries
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBAUT304 - MATERIAL SCIENCE AND METALLURGY > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Grain boundaries: high and low angle boundaries # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is a Grain Boundary? A **grain boundary (GB)** is the internal interface separating two adjacent crystallites (grains) in a polycrystalline solid, where the crystallographic orientation of the lattice changes abruptly across the boundary plane. Grain boundaries are 2-D planar defects (interface defects) and are a direct consequence of the solidification of metals where multiple nuclei grow simultaneously and impinge upon each other with differing lattice orientations. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A grain boundary is the region of mismatch between two adjacent grains of different crystallographic orientations, typically spanning 2‚Äì5 atomic distances in width, across which the lattice orientation changes by a small angle (LAGB) or a large angle (HAGB). Mathematically, a grain boundary is characterized by **five macroscopic degrees of freedom (DOF)**: - **3 DOF** ‚Äì to define the misorientation between the two grains (axis‚Äìangle pair, e.g., [uvw] / Œ∏). - **2 DOF** ‚Äì to define the inclination of the boundary plane itself with respect to one of the grains. > [!NOTE] > **Misorientation (Œ∏):** The minimum angle of rotation required to bring the lattice of one grain into coincidence with that of its neighbour. It is the single most important parameter that classifies a GB as *low-angle* or *high-angle*. --- ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: The "Tiled Floor" Model Imagine a large hall floor tiled with square ceramic pieces. Each tile is perfectly aligned in its own little area, but each tile (grain) is rotated slightly differently from the one next to it. - Where two tiles meet almost edge-to-edge with only a tiny tilt between them, the crack line (boundary) is clean, has very few broken pieces, and stores little energy. This is a **Low-Angle Grain Boundary (LAGB)**. - Where two tiles meet at a very sharp, large tilt ‚Äî say, 45¬∞ ‚Äî the boundary line is jagged, has many chipped pieces, looks chaotic, and stores a lot of energy. This is a **High-Angle Grain Boundary (HAGB)**. > [!TIP] > **Why does this matter in engineering?** Grain boundaries are the **primary obstacles to dislocation motion**. The more grain boundaries a metal has (i.e., smaller grains), the stronger and tougher it is. This is the essence of the **Hall‚ÄìPetch relationship** (covered in a later module). Knowing whether a boundary is "low" or "high" angle dictates whether a material will deform by dislocation glide, grain boundary sliding, or twinning. --- ## 1.3 Classification ‚Äî The Critical Angle (Œ∏c) The transition between LAGB and HAGB is not arbitrary; it is governed by a **critical misorientation angle (Œ∏c)**: | Boundary Type | Misorientation Range | Dislocation Model Valid? | |---|---|---| | **Low-Angle GB (LAGB)** | **Œ∏ < 10¬∞‚Äì15¬∞** (typically 10‚Äì15¬∞) | ‚úÖ Yes ‚Äî described by arrays of discrete dislocations | | **High-Angle GB (HAGB)** | **Œ∏ > 15¬∞** (typically 10‚Äì15¬∞) | ‚ùå No ‚Äî atoms in a near-disordered, amorphous-like state | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard Critical Value:** Most textbooks and KTU valuation key accept **Œ∏c ‚âà 10¬∞‚Äì15¬∞** as the demarcation. Below 10¬∞‚Äì15¬∞, the GB can be modelled as a periodic wall of dislocations; above this, the dislocation cores overlap so much that the structure becomes indistinguishable from a disordered thin film. **Standard Reference Energy Values (for pure Cu, room temperature):** - LAGB energy: **‚âà 0.1 ‚Äì 0.5 J/m¬≤** - HAGB energy: **‚âà 0.6 ‚Äì 0.9 J/m¬≤** # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Read‚ÄìShockley Model of Low-Angle Grain Boundaries The classical theoretical treatment of LAGBs was developed by **W. T. Read and W. Shockley (1950)**. Their dislocation model states: > A low-angle grain boundary can be equivalently represented as a **vertical wall (tilted array) of evenly-spaced edge dislocations** stacked one above the other. ### 2.1.1 Geometry of a Simple Tilt Boundary Consider two crystals meeting at a small tilt angle **Œ∏** along a common tilt axis (perpendicular to the page in 2D). The boundary is composed of identical edge dislocations, each with Burgers vector **b**, separated by a distance **D**. From pure geometry, the relationship between the dislocation spacing **D**, the Burgers vector **b**, and the tilt angle **Œ∏** is: $$ D = \frac{b}{2 \sin(\theta/2)} $$ For small angles (Œ∏ < 10¬∞), $\sin(\theta/2) \approx \theta/2$ (small-angle approximation in radians), giving the simplified KTU-standard form: $$ D \approx \frac{b}{\theta} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Physical Interpretation:** As the misorientation angle **Œ∏ increases**, the dislocations get **closer together**. When they get so close that their cores overlap (around Œ∏ ‚âà 15¬∞), the dislocation model breaks down and the boundary is reclassified as a HAGB. --- ## 2.2 The Read‚ÄìShockley Energy Equation The **grain boundary energy (Œ≥)** of a low-angle tilt boundary is derived by summing the **elastic strain energy** of the dislocation array and subtracting the energy of the **overlap region** of the dislocation stress fields. The full Read‚ÄìShockley equation is: $$ \gamma = \gamma_0 \, \theta \, (A - \ln \theta) $$ where: - **Œ≥** = grain boundary energy per unit area, in **J/m¬≤** - **Œ≥‚ÇÄ** = a constant proportional to the shear modulus and Burgers vector (units: J/m¬≤) - **Œ∏** = misorientation angle in **radians** - **A** = a dimensionless constant depending on the **core energy of the dislocation** and elastic constants; for tilt boundaries, $A = 1 + \ln(b/2\pi r_0)$, where **r‚ÇÄ** is the dislocation core cutoff radius (typically **r‚ÇÄ ‚âà b**) The simplified form most often used in KTU derivations is: $$ \gamma = K \, \theta \, (A - \ln \theta) $$ | Symbol | Meaning | Typical Units | |---|---|---| | Œ≥ | Grain boundary energy | **J/m¬≤** | | Œ≥‚ÇÄ, K | Elastic-energy prefactor (depends on G, b, ŒΩ) | **J/m¬≤** | | Œ∏ | Misorientation angle (rad) | **radians** | | A | Core-energy constant (‚âà 0.5 ‚Äì 0.7 for tilt, ‚âà ‚àí0.05 for twist) | dimensionless | | b | Magnitude of Burgers vector | **m (‚âà 0.25‚Äì0.4 nm for metals)** | | r‚ÇÄ | Dislocation core cutoff radius | **m** | | D | Dislocation spacing along the wall | **m** | | G | Shear modulus | **Pa (N/m¬≤)** | | ŒΩ | Poisson's ratio | dimensionless | --- ## 2.3 Two Sub-Types of LAGB ### 2.3.1 Tilt Boundary - Rotation axis is **parallel** to the boundary plane. - Composed of a **wall of edge dislocations** stacked vertically. - Step height per dislocation = b. ### 2.3.2 Twist Boundary - Rotation axis is **perpendicular** to the boundary plane. - Composed of a **grid of two intersecting sets of screw dislocations** (a cross-hatched network). - The energy equation has a different prefactor and a smaller core constant A. > [!TIP] > **Examiner Tip:** A pure tilt boundary has only edge dislocations, while a pure twist boundary has only screw dislocations. A **mixed** boundary contains both. --- ## 2.4 High-Angle Grain Boundaries (HAGB) Above Œ∏c ‚âà 15¬∞, the dislocation cores overlap and the boundary structure can no longer be described as discrete line defects. Instead, the atoms in the boundary region are in a **disordered, glass-like (amorphous) state** with thickness ‚âà 2‚Äì3 atomic diameters. Key features: - Energy is **largely independent of Œ∏** (saturates at a high value). - Energy is roughly **30‚Äì50% of the free surface energy** of the material. - The boundary can be thought of as a thin layer of **liquid-like** or **amorphous** material. > [!NOTE] > **Special High-Angle Boundaries (Coincidence Site Lattice ‚Äì CSL):** A HAGB may still have **low energy** if the two grains share a high density of common atomic positions across the boundary. These are called **CSL boundaries**, designated by the parameter **Œ£ (Sigma)**, where 1/Œ£ = fraction of atoms that are coincident.
