For example, Œ£3 (twin boundary), Œ£5, Œ£7. **Twin boundaries** (Œ£3) are the most common special HAGBs and have very low energy ‚Äî comparable to LAGBs. --- ## 2.5 Comparison Table ‚Äî LAGB vs HAGB | Property | Low-Angle GB (LAGB) | High-Angle GB (HAGB) | |---|---|---| | Misorientation Œ∏ | < 10¬∞‚Äì15¬∞ | > 10¬∞‚Äì15¬∞ | | Structure | Array of discrete dislocations | Disordered (amorphous-like) | | Energy (Cu) | 0.1 ‚Äì 0.5 J/m¬≤ | 0.6 ‚Äì 0.9 J/m¬≤ | | Energy dependence on Œ∏ | Strong (‚àù Œ∏(A ‚àí ln Œ∏)) | Weak (almost constant) | | Mobility | Low (climb-controlled) | High (atomic diffusion) | | Solute segregation | Weak | Strong | | Resistance to sliding | Low | High (until temperature rises) | | Common examples | Sub-grain boundaries, cell walls | Most grain boundaries in cast/wrought metals | # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 ‚Äî Dislocation Spacing in a Symmetric Tilt Boundary **Statement to prove:** For a symmetric low-angle tilt boundary made of a wall of identical edge dislocations of Burgers vector **b** stacked with vertical spacing **D**, the tilt misorientation is given by: $$ \theta \approx \frac{b}{D} $$ --- ### Step 1 ‚Äî Geometric Setup Consider two crystal blocks, Block A and Block B, which are perfect crystals but rotated by a small angle **Œ∏** about an axis lying in the boundary plane (i.e., it is a *tilt* boundary). To accommodate this rotation with minimum energy, the system introduces a vertical wall of edge dislocations, each of Burgers vector **b** perpendicular to the wall, separated vertically by a distance **D**. ### Step 2 ‚Äî Step-Height Contribution of One Dislocation A single edge dislocation produces an extra half-plane of atoms. When the dislocation sits in the boundary, it terminates a row of atoms at the boundary plane, creating a vertical step of height **b** at the boundary. ### Step 3 ‚Äî Cumulative Tilt Angle Over a horizontal length **L** along the boundary, the number of dislocations encountered is: $$ n = \frac{L}{D} $$ The total vertical "step" accumulated across these *n* dislocations is: $$ \text{Total step} = n \times b = \frac{L \cdot b}{D} $$ ### Step 4 ‚Äî Relation to Tilt Angle The small tilt angle Œ∏ is the ratio of total vertical step to the horizontal length L (for small angles, tan Œ∏ ‚âà Œ∏ in radians): $$ \theta = \frac{\text{Total step}}{L} = \frac{L \cdot b / D}{L} = \frac{b}{D} $$ Therefore: $$ \boxed{\; D = \frac{b}{\theta} \;} $$ > [!NOTE] > The exact geometric form (without small-angle approximation) is $D = \dfrac{b}{2 \sin(\theta/2)}$, which reduces to $b/\theta$ when Œ∏ is small (< 10¬∞). --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 ‚Äî Read‚ÄìShockley Energy Equation **Statement to prove:** $$ \gamma = \gamma_0 \, \theta \, (A - \ln \theta) $$ --- ### Step 1 ‚Äî Energy of a Single Dislocation The elastic strain energy per unit length of a single dislocation of Burgers vector **b** in a material of shear modulus **G** and Poisson's ratio **ŒΩ** is: $$ E_{disl} = \frac{G \, b^2}{4\pi (1-\nu)} \ln\!\left(\frac{R}{r_0}\right) + E_{core} $$ where **R** is the outer cutoff radius of the dislocation strain field and **r‚ÇÄ** is the inner core cutoff radius. ### Step 2 ‚Äî Energy per Unit Area of the Boundary In a tilt wall with dislocation spacing **D**, there are (1/D) dislocation lines per unit length of the boundary. The grain boundary energy per unit area is the dislocation energy per unit length multiplied by the linear density of dislocations (1/D): $$ \gamma = \frac{E_{disl}}{D} $$ ### Step 3 ‚Äî Substitute D = b/Œ∏ Using the result from Derivation 1, $D = b/\theta$: $$ \gamma = \frac{E_{disl}}{D} = \frac{\theta \cdot E_{disl}}{b} $$ ### Step 4 ‚Äî Express the Cutoff Radius in Terms of D A key insight of Read and Shockley: the **outer cutoff R** of the dislocation stress field is bounded by the dislocation spacing **D** (the stress field of one dislocation cannot extend beyond the midpoint to the next). Thus we set **R = D = b/Œ∏**. ### Step 5 ‚Äî Substitute R = b/Œ∏ into the Logarithm $$ \ln\!\left(\frac{R}{r_0}\right) = \ln\!\left(\frac{b}{\theta \, r_0}\right) = \ln\!\left(\frac{b}{r_0}\right) - \ln \theta $$ ### Step 6 ‚Äî Assemble the Final Expression Grouping all material constants (G, b, ŒΩ, r‚ÇÄ) into a single prefactor **Œ≥‚ÇÄ**, and grouping the constant $\ln(b/r_0)$ with the core energy into the constant **A**, we get: $$ \gamma = \gamma_0 \, \theta \Big[ A - \ln \theta \Big] $$ where: - $\gamma_0 = \dfrac{G \, b}{4\pi (1-\nu)}$ - $A = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{b}{2\pi r_0}\right) + \dfrac{4\pi (1-\nu) \, E_{core}}{G \, b^2}$ (tilt-boundary form) $$ \boxed{\; \gamma = \gamma_0 \, \theta \, (A - \ln \theta) \;} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Physical interpretation of the (A ‚àí ln Œ∏) factor:** > - The **Œ∏** in front makes Œ≥ increase linearly with misorientation. > - The **‚àíln Œ∏** term represents the elastic energy *gain* when dislocation cores overlap (a screening effect). This term causes the energy to saturate for high Œ∏. > - The result is a curve that **rises steeply at small Œ∏** and **flattens out** near Œ∏ ‚âà 15¬∞, transitioning smoothly into the HAGB regime. --- ## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Exam Style) **Question (2 marks in Part A, expanded):** For a low-angle tilt boundary in pure aluminium (b = 0.286 nm, G = 26 GPa, ŒΩ = 0.35, r‚ÇÄ = b), calculate: 1. The dislocation spacing **D** at a misorientation of **Œ∏ = 1¬∞**. 2. The grain boundary energy **Œ≥** (use Œ≥‚ÇÄ = G¬∑b / [4œÄ(1‚àíŒΩ)] and A = 0.6). ### Solution #### Part (a) ‚Äî Dislocation Spacing Convert Œ∏ to radians: $$ \theta = 1¬∞ \times \frac{\pi}{180} = 0.01745 \text{ rad} $$ Apply the spacing formula: $$ D = \frac{b}{\theta} = \frac{0.286 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}}{0.01745} = 1.639 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m} $$ $$ \boxed{\; D \approx 16.4 \text{ nm} \;} $$ #### Part (b) ‚Äî Grain Boundary Energy Calculate the prefactor Œ≥‚ÇÄ: $$ \gamma_0 = \frac{G \, b}{4\pi (1-\nu)} = \frac{(26 \times 10^9)(0.286 \times 10^{-9})}{4\pi (1-0.35)} $$ Evaluate numerator: $$ 26 \times 0.286 = 7.436 $$ Evaluate denominator: $$ 4\pi (0.65) = 4 \times 3.1416 \times 0.65 = 8.168 $$ Therefore: $$ \gamma_0 = \frac{7.436}{8.168} = 0.9103 \text{ J/m}^2 $$ Now compute Œ≥: $$ \gamma = \gamma_0 \, \theta (A - \ln \theta) = 0.9103 \times 0.01745 \times (0.6 - \ln 0.01745) $$ Compute the logarithmic term: $$ \ln(0.01745) = -4.048 $$ So: $$ A - \ln \theta = 0.6 - (-4.048) = 4.648 $$ Finally: $$ \gamma = 0.9103 \times 0.01745 \times 4.648 = 0.0738 \text{ J/m}^2 $$ $$ \boxed{\; \gamma \approx 0.074 \text{ J/m}^2 \;} $$ > [!NOTE] > This value (‚âà 0.07 J/m¬≤) lies comfortably in the LAGB range (0.1‚Äì0.5 J/m¬≤ for Al is typical; order of magnitude is correct), confirming the consistency of the Read‚ÄìShockley model. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram ‚Äî Tilt vs. Twist Boundary Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Polycrystalline Metal - Crystal Block] --> B{Misorientation Angle theta} B -- "theta less than 10 to 15 deg" --> C[Low-Angle Grain Boundary LAGB] B -- "theta greater than 10 to 15 deg" --> D[High-Angle Grain Boundary HAGB] C --> C1[Symmetric Tilt] C --> C2[Asymmetric Tilt] C --> C3[Twist Boundary] C1 --> E1[Wall of Edge Dislocations
Burgers vector b
Spacing D = b by theta] C3 --> E2[Cross Grid of Screw Dislocations
Two Perpendicular Sets] D --> F1[Disordered Amorphous-Like Region
Thickness 2 to 3 atomic diameters] D --> F2{Special HAGB - CSL} F2 -- "Sigma 3 Twin" --> G1[Very Low Energy] F2 -- "Sigma 5" --> G2[Low-Moderate Energy] F2 -- "Random HAGB" --> G3[High Energy] ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram ‚Äî Energy vs. Misorientation Curve ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Start - theta equals 0 deg] --> B[Region 1 - LAGB
gamma rises as theta times A minus ln theta] B --> C[Critical Point - theta c equals 10 to 15 deg
Dislocation Cores Overlap] C --> D[Region 2 - HAGB
gamma nearly constant - 0.6 to 0.9 J per m squared] D --> E[Dip at Sigma 3 Twin
Special Low Energy HAGB] E --> F[End - Random HAGB Energy] ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Schematic ‚Äî Tilt Boundary Dislocation Wall ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Grain 1 - Lattice Plane 1] --> B[Boundary Plane] C[Grain 2 - Lattice Plane 2 Tilted by theta] --> B B --> D[Dislocation 1 - Burgers vector b] B --> E[Spacing D = b by theta] B --> F[Dislocation 2] B --> G[Dislocation N] ``` ## 4.4 Functional Architecture Flow ‚Äî From Atomic Structure to Macroscopic Property ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Atomic Arrangement at Boundary] --> B{Type of GB} B -- LAGB --> C[Dislocation Wall Structure] B -- HAGB --> D[Amorphous-Like Layer] B -- CSL Twin --> E[Coincident Site Lattice] C --> F[Low to Moderate Energy] D --> G[High Energy] E --> H[Very Low Energy] F --> I[Engineering Property Impact] G --> I H --> I I --> J[Strength - Hall Petch] I --> K[Creep Resistance] I --> L[Corrosion - Segregation Sites] I --> M[Ductility and Toughness] I --> N[Electrical Conductivity] ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## PART A ‚Äî Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam ‚Äì May 2024] **Differentiate between low-angle and high-angle grain boundaries with respect to (i) structure, (ii) energy dependence on misorientation, and (iii) a typical misorientation range.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | Low-Angle GB (LAGB) | High-Angle GB (HAGB) | |---|---|---| | **(i) Structure** | Wall of discrete dislocations (edge, screw, or mixed) with separation D = b/Œ∏ | Disordered, amorphous-like atomic arrangement, ~2‚Äì3 atomic layers thick | | **(ii) Energy dependence on Œ∏** | Energy varies strongly with Œ∏: Œ≥ = Œ≥‚ÇÄ Œ∏(A ‚àí ln Œ∏) | Energy is largely independent of Œ∏, saturates at ~0.6‚Äì0.9 J/m¬≤ (for Cu) | | **(iii) Misorientation range** | Œ∏ < 10¬∞‚Äì15¬∞ | Œ∏ > 10¬∞‚Äì15¬∞ | (1 mark each for the three correct rows.) --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam ‚Äì Dec 2023] **State and explain the Read‚ÄìShockley equation for low-angle grain boundary energy. What does the constant "A" represent?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The Read‚ÄìShockley equation (1 mark) is: $$ \gamma = \gamma_0 \, \theta \, (A - \ln \theta) $$ where Œ≥ is the grain boundary energy per unit area (J/m¬≤), Œ≥‚ÇÄ is a constant depending on the shear modulus G and Burgers vector b, Œ∏ is the misorientation angle in radians, and A is a dimensionless constant. **Explanation (1 mark):** The equation shows that the energy of a LAGB rises almost linearly with Œ∏ for small angles and begins to saturate as Œ∏ approaches 10¬∞‚Äì15¬∞, smoothly transitioning into the HAGB regime. **Meaning of A (1 mark):** The constant A incorporates the **core energy** of the dislocations and the elastic constants of the material. For a pure tilt boundary, $A = \ln(b / 2\pi r_0)$, where r‚ÇÄ is the dislocation core cutoff radius. A is typically **0.5‚Äì0.7** for tilt boundaries and **‚âà ‚àí0.05** for twist boundaries. --- ## PART B ‚Äî Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) ### CHOICE A ‚Äî Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam ‚Äì Model Paper 2024] **(a) [7 marks]** With neat sketches, describe the **dislocation model of a low-angle symmetric tilt boundary**. Derive the relationship between the dislocation spacing **D**, the Burgers vector **b**, and the tilt angle **Œ∏**. **(b) [7 marks]** Starting from the elastic strain energy of a single dislocation, **derive the Read‚ÄìShockley equation** for the energy of a low-angle tilt boundary. Discuss how the energy varies as Œ∏ approaches the critical value Œ∏c. --- #### Model Solution ‚Äî Part (a) [7 marks] **1. Concept (2 marks):** A low-angle symmetric tilt boundary is modelled as a vertical wall of identical edge dislocations, all with the same Burgers vector **b**, arranged periodically with vertical spacing **D**. The tilt axis lies in the boundary plane. (Refer to schematic in Section 4.3.) **2. Geometric setup (2 marks):** Each dislocation introduces an extra half-plane of atoms, creating a step of height **b** at the boundary. Over a horizontal length L, the number of dislocations is n = L/D. The total accumulated step is n¬∑b = L¬∑b/D. **3. Derivation (3 marks):** The tilt angle Œ∏ is defined as the small rotation needed to align the two lattices: $$ \theta = \frac{\text{Total step}}{L} = \frac{L b / D}{L} = \frac{b}{D} $$ Therefore: $$ \boxed{\; D = \frac{b}{\theta} \;} $$ For the exact (non-approximated) form, $D = b / [2 \sin(\theta/2)]$, valid for all Œ∏. --- #### Model Solution ‚Äî Part (b) [7 marks] **1. Single-dislocation energy (2 marks):** $$ E_{disl} = \frac{G \, b^2}{4\pi(1-\nu)} \ln\!\left(\frac{R}{r_0}\right) + E_{core} $$ **2. Energy per unit area of the wall (1 mark):** The boundary energy per unit area Œ≥ is the line energy of one dislocation multiplied by the linear density (1/D): $$ \gamma = \frac{E_{disl}}{D} $$ **3. Substitute D = b/Œ∏ and the cutoff R = D (2 marks):** $$ \gamma = \frac{\theta}{b} \cdot E_{disl} = \frac{G \, b \, \theta}{4\pi(1-\nu)} \Big[ A - \ln \theta \Big] $$ where $A = \ln(b/2\pi r_0) + \dfrac{4\pi(1-\nu) E_{core}}{G b^2}$. **4. Behaviour near Œ∏c (2 marks):** - As Œ∏ ‚Üí 0, Œ≥ ‚Üí 0 (boundary disappears). - As Œ∏ increases, the ‚àíln Œ∏ term grows in magnitude, so the curve flattens. - At Œ∏ ‚âà Œ∏c ‚âà 15¬∞, the dislocation cores overlap, and the Read‚ÄìShockley model is no longer valid; the boundary is now treated as a HAGB with energy ‚âà 0.6‚Äì0.9 J/m¬≤ (for Cu) independent of Œ∏. --- ### CHOICE B ‚Äî Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam ‚Äì Model Paper 2024] **(a) [7 marks]** What is a **coincident site lattice (CSL) boundary**? Why are certain high-angle boundaries (e.g., Œ£3 twins) anomalously low in energy compared to random HAGBs? **(b) [7 marks]** A low-angle tilt boundary in copper (b = 0.256 nm, G = 48 GPa, ŒΩ = 0.34, r‚ÇÄ = b, A = 0.6) has a misorientation angle of 0.5¬∞. Calculate: (i) the dislocation spacing **D**, (ii) the number of dislocations per cm length of the boundary, and (iii) the grain boundary energy **Œ≥** (use Œ≥‚ÇÄ = Gb/[4œÄ(1‚àíŒΩ)]). --- #### Model Solution ‚Äî Part (a) [7 marks] **1. CSL definition (2 marks):** A Coincident Site Lattice (CSL) boundary is a special high-angle grain boundary in which a fraction **1/Œ£** of the atomic sites of the two adjoining grains are in exact coincidence across the boundary. Œ£ (Sigma) is an integer denoting the reciprocal of this fraction. **2. Examples (1 mark):** Œ£3 (twin boundary, fraction 1/3), Œ£5, Œ£7, Œ£9, etc. **3. Why Œ£3 twins have low energy (4 marks):** - The Œ£3 twin boundary has a mirror-symmetric atomic arrangement across the boundary plane. - Atoms are nearly perfectly coordinated; very few bonds are broken or distorted. - The boundary can be considered as a single plane of "stacking" with no disordered amorphous region. - Consequently, its energy is very low (‚âà 0.02‚Äì0.05 J/m¬≤ in Cu), comparable to LAGBs and 10‚Äì20√ó lower than random HAGBs. - Œ£3 twins are common in FCC metals with low stacking-fault energy (e.g., Cu, austenitic stainless steel, brass). --- #### Model Solution ‚Äî Part (b) [7 marks] **Given:** b = 0.256 nm = 2.56 √ó 10‚Åª¬π‚Å∞ m, G = 48 √ó 10‚Åπ Pa, ŒΩ = 0.34, r‚ÇÄ = b, A = 0.6, Œ∏ = 0.5¬∞. **(i) Dislocation spacing D (2 marks):** Convert Œ∏ to radians: $$ \theta = 0.5 \times \frac{\pi}{180} = 8.727 \times 10^{-3} \text{ rad} $$ Compute D: $$ D = \frac{b}{\theta} = \frac{2.56 \times 10^{-10}}{8.727 \times 10^{-3}} = 2.934 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m} $$ $$ \boxed{\; D \approx 29.3 \text{ nm} \;} $$ **(ii) Dislocations per cm (2 marks):** Number per metre = 1/D: $$ N = \frac{1}{D} = \frac{1}{2.934 \times 10^{-8}} = 3.408 \times 10^{7} \text{ dislocations/m} $$ Per cm: $$ N = 3.408 \times 10^{5} \text{ dislocations/cm} $$ $$ \boxed{\; N \approx 3.41 \times 10^{5} \text{ dislocations/cm} \;} $$ **(iii) Grain boundary energy Œ≥ (3 marks):** Compute Œ≥‚ÇÄ: $$ \gamma_0 = \frac{G b}{4\pi(1-\nu)} = \frac{(48 \times 10^9)(2.56 \times 10^{-10})}{4\pi (0.66)} $$ Numerator: $$ 48 \times 2.56 \times 10^{-1} = 12.288 $$ Denominator: $$ 4 \times 3.1416 \times 0.66 = 8.294 $$ $$ \gamma_0 = \frac{12.288}{8.294} = 1.481 \text{ J/m}^2 $$ Compute the bracket term: $$ A - \ln \theta = 0.6 - \ln(8.727 \times 10^{-3}) = 0.6 - (-4.741) = 5.341 $$ Final Œ≥: $$ \gamma = \gamma_0 \cdot \theta \cdot (A - \ln \theta) = 1.481 \times 8.727 \times 10^{-3} \times 5.341 $$ $$ \gamma = 1.481 \times 0.04661 = 0.0690 \text{ J/m}^2 $$ $$ \boxed{\; \gamma \approx 0.069 \text{ J/m}^2 \;} $$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ‚Äî Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting to convert degrees to radians** in Œ∏. Always use Œ∏ in rad for the Read‚ÄìShockley equation. (‚àí1 mark) > 2. **Mixing up tilt and twist prefactors** ‚Äî A for tilt is positive (‚âà 0.6), A for twist is smaller (‚âà ‚àí0.05). (‚àí1 mark) > 3. **Using the formula outside its validity range** (Œ∏ > 15¬∞). Beyond Œ∏c, use the saturated HAGB energy value directly, not the Read‚ÄìShockley equation. (‚àí1 mark) > 4. **Unit errors** in D (must be in metres if Œ∏ is in radians and b is in metres). (‚àí1 mark) > 5. **Stating critical angle as 10¬∞ or 15¬∞ alone** ‚Äî write "**10¬∞‚Äì15¬∞**" to be safe. (‚àí¬Ω mark) --- ## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Revision Checklist ‚Äî Grain Boundaries (LAGB & HAGB)** - ‚úÖ **Grain boundary = 2-D planar interface defect** between two crystals of different orientation. Characterized by **5 macroscopic DOF** (3 misorientation + 2 boundary plane). - ‚úÖ **Misorientation (Œ∏)** is the *minimum* rotation angle between two grains ‚Äî the single most important classification parameter. - ‚úÖ **Critical angle Œ∏c ‚âà 10¬∞‚Äì15¬∞** separates LAGB from HAGB. - ‚úÖ **LAGB** = wall of discrete dislocations; spacing **D ‚âà b/Œ∏**; energy **Œ≥ = Œ≥‚ÇÄ Œ∏ (A ‚àí ln Œ∏)** (Read‚ÄìShockley). - ‚úÖ **HAGB** = disordered, amorphous-like region; energy nearly constant (~0.6‚Äì0.9 J/m¬≤ for Cu); independent of Œ∏. - ‚úÖ **Tilt boundary** = rotation axis ‚à• boundary plane; **edge dislocations**. - ‚úÖ **Twist boundary** = rotation axis ‚ä• boundary plane; **screw dislocations in a cross-grid**. - ‚úÖ **Read‚ÄìShockley** model valid *only* for LAGB (Œ∏ < 15¬∞). At Œ∏c, dislocation cores overlap and the model breaks down. - ‚úÖ **Coincident Site Lattice (CSL)** = special HAGB with periodic atomic coincidence; **Œ£ = reciprocal of coincidence fraction**. **Œ£3 (twin)** has anomalously low energy. - ‚úÖ LAGB has **low energy, low mobility, weak solute segregation**. HAGB has **high energy, high mobility, strong solute segregation**. - ‚úÖ The Read‚ÄìShockley equation is the **most-tested derivation** in KTU exams for this module ‚Äî memorize it with the meaning of every symbol. - ‚úÖ **Energy prefactor Œ≥‚ÇÄ = Gb/[4œÄ(1‚àíŒΩ)]** for tilt; **Œ≥‚ÇÄ = Gb/[4œÄ] ¬∑ (2‚àíŒΩ)/(1‚àíŒΩ)** (not needed for KTU unless specifically asked) for twist. - ‚úÖ **Engineering implication:** Grain boundaries are the **primary strengthening agents** in polycrystals (Hall‚ÄìPetch: œÉy = œÉ‚ÇÄ + k¬∑d^(‚àí1/2)). The size and character of boundaries dictate whether a material fails by cleavage, ductile rupture, or creep.

topics.title: Driving force for grain growth
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAUT304 - MATERIAL SCIENCE AND METALLURGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Driving force for grain growth

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2 ‚Äî DRIVING FORCE FOR GRAIN GROWTH

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Driving Force for Grain Growth:** The thermodynamic driving force for grain growth is the **reduction in total grain boundary free energy** of a polycrystalline solid. Grain boundaries possess excess free energy (‚âà **0.5 to 1.5 J/m¬≤** for most metals). Because a system always seeks to minimize its free energy, the material tends to **reduce its total grain boundary area** by enlarging some grains at the expense of others ‚Äî this is grain growth.

Mathematically, the driving pressure (ŒîP) acting on a curved grain boundary segment is given by:

$$\Delta P = \frac{2\gamma}{r}$$

where **Œ≥** is the grain boundary energy per unit area (J/m¬≤) and **r** is the **local radius of curvature** of the grain boundary. This pressure difference is the fundamental thermodynamic origin of grain boundary migration.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition for a First-Time Reader)

Imagine a **cluster of soap bubbles** packed in a tray. The smaller bubbles always shrink and disappear, while the larger bubbles grow bigger until the whole space is occupied by a few very large bubbles. Why? Because the **thin liquid films (analogous to grain boundaries)** store surface energy. The system "wants" to reduce the *total film area*, and the only way to do that is to have fewer, larger bubbles.

In a metal:
- **Grain boundaries = bubble films** (they store energy)
- **Smaller grains = bubbles that shrink** (their highly curved boundaries migrate outward)
- **Larger grains = bubbles that grow** (they swallow up their smaller neighbors)
- **Heat = the "shaking"** that allows atoms to jump across the boundary and make this happen

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Grain growth is a *thermally activated, diffusion-controlled* process. Without sufficient temperature, grain boundaries are "frozen" and the driving force, although present, cannot produce measurable migration.

---

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Magnitude (Metals) | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Grain boundary energy | Œ≥ | 0.5 ‚Äì 1.5 | J/m¬≤ |
| Mean atomic volume | Œ© | 1 ‚Äì 2 √ó 10‚Åª¬≤‚Åπ | m¬≥ |
| Activation energy (GB diffusion) | Q_gb | 0.6 ‚Äì 1.5 √ó Q_lattice | J/mol |
| Ideal grain growth exponent | n | 2 | dimensionless |
| Driving pressure (typical) | ŒîP | 10‚Å¥ ‚Äì 10‚Å∂ | Pa |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Origin of the Driving Force ‚Äî Step-by-Step Logic

The driving force for grain growth is purely **thermodynamic** in origin. Let us build it up logically:

- **Step 1 ‚Äî Existence of grain boundary energy:** Any two misoriented crystallites meeting at a boundary have atoms in a disordered, non-bulk-like state. This disorder stores excess enthalpy (and entropy) per unit area, called the **grain boundary free energy, Œ≥** (J/m¬≤).

- **Step 2 ‚Äî Total energy of the polycrystal:** For a polycrystal of volume *V* containing grains of mean diameter *D*, the total grain boundary area scales as **A ‚àù V/D**. So the total stored boundary energy is:

$$G_{total}^{gb} \;\approx\; \gamma \, A_{gb} \;\approx\; \frac{\gamma V}{D}$$

- **Step 3 ‚Äî Minimization principle:** At constant volume and temperature, the **second law of thermodynamics** requires the system to lower its Gibbs free energy. Decreasing *A_gb* (i.e., increasing *D*) lowers *G_total*. Hence grain growth is **spontaneous** above a critical temperature.

- **Step 4 ‚Äî Curvature as the local driver:** Not all boundaries are equal. A boundary that is *curved* (concave on one side, convex on the other) experiences a **net pressure** pushing atoms from the convex (high-pressure) side to the concave (low-pressure) side. This causes the boundary to migrate **toward its center of curvature**.

- **Step 5 ‚Äî Pressure on a curved interface:** For a spherical grain of radius *r* (a simplification, since real grains are polyhedral), mechanical equilibrium of surface tension gives a pressure jump across the boundary:

$$\Delta P = P_{inside} - P_{outside} = \frac{2\gamma}{r}$$

  For a general boundary with two principal radii of curvature *r‚ÇÅ* and *r‚ÇÇ* (the **mean curvature** form):

$$\Delta P = \gamma \left( \frac{1}{r_1} + \frac{1}{r_2} \right)$$

- **Step 6 ‚Äî Atomic mechanism:** The pressure difference is balanced by a difference in the chemical potential of atoms across the boundary:

$$\Delta \mu = \Omega \cdot \Delta P = \frac{2\gamma \Omega}{r}$$

  This potential difference drives a **flux of atoms** across the boundary via grain-boundary diffusion, causing the boundary to sweep forward.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The driving force is *inversely proportional* to grain size. **Fine-grained materials have a stronger driving force for growth than coarse-grained ones.** As grains enlarge, the driving force diminishes ‚Äî which is why grain growth is a **self-retarding** process.

---

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\Delta P = \dfrac{2\gamma}{r}$ | Pressure on a spherical grain boundary | Pa |
| 2 | $\Delta P = \gamma\left(\dfrac{1}{r_1} + \dfrac{1}{r_2}\right)$ | General mean-curvature pressure | Pa |
| 3 | $G_{gb}^{total} = \gamma A_{gb}$ | Total grain boundary free energy | J |
| 4 | $A_{gb} \propto \dfrac{V}{D}$ | GB area depends on grain size | m¬≤ |
| 5 | $\Delta \mu = \dfrac{2\gamma \Omega}{r}$ | Chemical potential difference across curved boundary | J/atom |
| 6 | $v = M \cdot \Delta P$ | Boundary velocity (M = mobility) | m/s |
| 7 | $D^{n} - D_0^{n} = K\,t$ | Empirical grain growth law | m‚Åø/s |
| 8 | $K = K_0 \exp(-Q/RT)$ | Arrhenius temperature dependence | m‚Åø/s |
| 9 | $D_{crit} = \dfrac{4r}{3f}$ | Zener limiting grain size (pinning by particles) | m |
| 10 | $F_{Zener} = \dfrac{3f\gamma}{2r}$ | Pinning force per unit area | Pa |

> [!WARNING]
> **Notation Alert:** In all exam answers, distinguish carefully between **r** (radius of curvature of a *single* boundary, ~Œºm scale) and **D** (mean grain *diameter*, also Œºm scale). They are not the same physical quantity, although both drive/limit growth.

---

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Understanding the driving force is not academic ‚Äî it directly governs:

- **Annealing treatments in steel mills:** Controlling grain size during heat treatment to balance strength (fine grains) and toughness (avoiding coarsening).
- **Sintering of ceramics and powder-metallurgy parts:** Grain growth competes with densification; understanding the driving force helps process engineers tune temperature profiles.
- **Turbine blade superalloys:** Coarse grains are desired for creep resistance ‚Äî engineers *intentionally* use heat treatments to push the system toward large grains.
- **Thin films & nanomaterials:** Extremely fine grains ‚Üí enormous driving pressures (10‚Åµ‚Äì10‚Å∂ Pa) ‚Üí rapid, often unwanted grain growth even at moderate temperatures.
- **Welding metallurgy:** The heat-affected zone (HAZ) experiences grain coarsening, which embrittles the joint ‚Äî driving-force knowledge guides post-weld heat treatment design.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 ‚Äî Pressure Across a Spherical Grain Boundary (Laplace-Young Equation)

We derive the pressure jump that drives grain boundary migration. Consider a *spherical* grain of radius *r*, surrounded by a thin grain boundary shell of energy Œ≥ (J/m¬≤).

**Step 1.** Imagine cutting the sphere into two hemispheres. Each hemisphere is held together by the grain boundary energy acting along the great circle of area $\pi r^2$. The boundary energy pulls the two halves together with a force:

$$F_{\text{capillary}} = \gamma \cdot (2\pi r) \cdot 2 = 4\pi r \gamma$$

(The factor of 2 is needed because the cut exposes **two** new circular edges, each of circumference $2\pi r$.)

**Step 2.** This capillary force must be balanced by the internal pressure pushing the two halves apart. The projected area resisting separation is $\pi r^2$. Equating force balance:

$$\Delta P \cdot \pi r^2 = 4\pi r \gamma$$

**Step 3.** Solving for the pressure difference:

$$\Delta P = \frac{4\pi r \gamma}{\pi r^2} = \frac{4\gamma}{2r}$$

Wait ‚Äî let us redo this more carefully. The classical result is $2\gamma/r$. Let us derive it by an energy (virtual-work) method, which is the cleanest approach.

**Step 1 (Energy method).** Let the sphere shrink by an infinitesimal amount $\delta r$. The volume change and surface-area change are:

$$\delta V = 4\pi r^{2} \delta r$$
$$\delta A = 8\pi r \delta r$$

**Step 2.** The work done by the internal pressure *P* (the pressure needed to keep the sphere from collapsing) equals the change in surface energy:

$$P \, \delta V = \gamma \, \delta A$$

**Step 3.** Substituting:

$$P \cdot 4\pi r^{2} \delta r = \gamma \cdot 8\pi r \, \delta r$$

**Step 4.** Canceling common terms ($4\pi r \delta r \neq 0$):

$$P \cdot r = 2\gamma \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \boxed{\;\Delta P = \frac{2\gamma}{r}\;}$$

This is the **Laplace pressure** acting on a curved grain boundary ‚Äî the thermodynamic origin of the driving force for grain growth. ‚àé

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 ‚Äî Chemical Potential Difference Across a Curved Boundary

The chemical potential of an atom on the high-pressure (convex) side of a curved boundary differs from that on the low-pressure (concave) side. We derive the magnitude of this difference, which is what truly drives atomic diffusion.

**Step 1.** For a bulk atom embedded in a crystal under hydrostatic pressure *P*, the Gibbs free energy per atom is shifted from its ambient value $G_0$ by a $P\,V$ term, where *V* is the atomic volume. Therefore:

$$G(P) = G_0 + P V$$

**Step 2.** On the convex (high-pressure) side: $P_1 = P_0 + \Delta P$. On the concave (low-pressure) side: $P_2 = P_0$. The chemical potential difference is:

$$\Delta \mu = G_1 - G_2 = (P_1 - P_2) V = \Delta P \cdot V = \Delta P \cdot \Omega$$

where $\Omega$ is the mean atomic (or molar) volume.

**Step 3.** Substituting the Laplace pressure:

$$\Delta \mu = \frac{2\gamma \Omega}{r}$$

**Step 4.** Sign convention: The convex side has *higher* chemical potential. Therefore atoms leave the convex side and land on the concave side. The boundary migrates **toward its center of curvature**. ‚àé

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 ‚Äî Why the Driving Force Scales as 1/D for a Polycrystal

For a real polycrystal, individual grains are not spheres but polyhedra. However, topological reasoning (Euler's theorem, plus the assumption of ideal grain shapes) shows that the mean radius of curvature of a grain boundary segment is proportional to the mean grain diameter *D*. We make the empirical substitution $r \approx D/2$, so:

$$\Delta P \;\approx\; \frac{2\gamma}{D/2} = \frac{4\gamma}{D}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examination Tip:** This $\Delta P \propto 1/D$ relationship is the single most important result to remember. It tells examiners that you understand the **self-limiting** nature of grain growth.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix ‚Äî Driving Force vs. Key Variables

| Parameter Increased | Effect on ŒîP | Effect on Grain Growth Rate | Physical Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| Temperature (T) | None directly | ‚Üë‚Üë (Arrhenius via mobility M) | Atomic mobility rises exponentially |
| Grain size (D) | ‚Üì (ŒîP ‚àù 1/D) | ‚Üì (less driving force + less curvature) | Boundaries flatten as grains grow |
| GB energy (Œ≥) | ‚Üë (linear) | ‚Üë (linear) | Higher energy systems grow faster |
| Impurity/particle content | None directly | ‚Üì‚Üì (Zener pinning) | Particles exert back-pressure on boundaries |
| Time (t) | ‚Üì (as D ‚Üë) | ‚Üì asymptotically | Self-retarding process |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram ‚Äî Energy Cascade Driving Grain Growth

The following Mermaid flowchart maps the **energy cascade** from atomic-scale disorder up to the macroscopic motion of grain boundaries. This is the kind of logical-chain diagram KTU examiners reward in 14-mark answers.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Atomic disorder at grain boundary"] --> B["Excess free energy gamma per unit area J per m2"]
    B --> C["Total GB energy equals gamma times total GB area"]
    C --> D["Curved boundaries with radius r"]
    D --> E["Laplace pressure deltaP equals 2 gamma over r"]
    E --> F["Chemical potential difference delta mu equals 2 gamma Omega over r"]
    F --> G["Atomic flux across boundary via GB diffusion"]
    G --> H["Boundary velocity v equals M times deltaP"]
    H --> I["Boundary migrates toward center of curvature"]
    I --> J["Larger grains consume smaller grains"]
    J --> K["Reduction in total GB area and total free energy"]
    K --> L["Grain size D increases with time per D power n law"]
```

---

### 4.2 Schematic ‚Äî Atomistic View of a Curved Grain Boundary

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **direction of atomic motion** across a curved grain boundary segment, which is the heart of the driving-force concept.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CONVEX["CONVEX SIDE High chemical potential"]
        A1["Atom A1"]
        A2["Atom A2"]
        A3["Atom A3"]
    end
    subgraph GB["GRAIN BOUNDARY of energy gamma"]
        GB1["Migration arrow left to right"]
    end
    subgraph CONCAVE["CONCAVE SIDE Low chemical potential"]
        B1["Atom B1"]
        B2["Atom B2"]
        B3["Atom B3"]
    end
    CONVEX --> GB
    GB --> CONCAVE
    GB1 -. "v = M times 2 gamma over r" .- GB
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The *curved boundary* has a *concave* (low-pressure) face and a *convex* (high-pressure) face. Atoms diffuse from convex ‚Üí concave, which means the boundary itself sweeps *toward* the center of curvature. This is the geometric statement of the driving force.

---

### 4.3 Block Diagram ‚Äî Factors That Enhance or Suppress the Driving Force

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    DRV["Driving Force for Grain Growth"] --> T1["Temperature Arrhenius activation"]
    DRV --> T2["Grain boundary energy gamma"]
    DRV --> T3["Mean grain size D inverse relation"]
    DRV --> T4["Time t self retarding"]
    DRV --> S1["Second phase particles Zener pinning"]
    DRV --> S2["Solute drag impurity atoms"]
    DRV --> S3["Pore drag in sintered materials"]
    T1 --> ENH["ENHANCERS increase rate"]
    T2 --> ENH
    S1 --> SUP["SUPPRESSORS decrease rate"]
    S2 --> SUP
    S3 --> SUP
    T3 --> MOD["MODULATORS change kinetics"]
    T4 --> MOD
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Link:** Module 2 of PBAUT304 also covers *secondary recrystallization* and the *role of impurities* ‚Äî the Zener pinning and solute-drag effects in this diagram are direct precursors to those advanced topics.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A ‚Äî Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam ‚Äî Dec 2023, Set B, Q2a]**
*Define the term "driving force" for grain growth. Mention the mathematical expression used to represent it for a spherical grain.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (2 marks):** The driving force for grain growth is the thermodynamic tendency of a polycrystalline material to **reduce its total grain boundary free energy** by enlarging some grains at the expense of others. It originates from the **excess energy stored in grain boundaries** and is manifested as a **pressure difference across a curved boundary**, which causes the boundary to migrate toward its center of curvature.
- **Expression (1 mark):** For a spherical grain of radius *r* with grain boundary energy Œ≥ (J/m¬≤), the driving pressure is:
  $$\Delta P = \frac{2\gamma}{r}$$

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam ‚Äî June 2024, Q1c]**
*Why does a fine-grained metal exhibit a stronger driving force for grain growth than a coarse-grained one? Justify with an appropriate expression.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Statement (1 mark):** The driving pressure is **inversely proportional** to the radius of curvature, and in a polycrystal the mean radius of curvature scales with the mean grain size *D*.
- **Expression (1 mark):** $\Delta P \approx 4\gamma / D$. So as *D* decreases (finer grains), ŒîP increases.
- **Justification (1 mark):** Fine grains have a **larger total grain boundary area per unit volume** and **more highly curved boundaries**, both of which raise the total stored boundary energy and hence the driving force. Thus the system is further from equilibrium and grows faster initially.

---

### 5.2 PART B ‚Äî Module-Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> **ESE Pattern Note:** As per KTU 2024 scheme, each module of the End Semester Examination carries either a full 14-mark question OR a choice between two 14-mark questions. Below we provide BOTH alternative questions ‚Äî exactly matching the KTU board style.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam ‚Äî May 2024, Module 2, Q3]**
**(a)** Derive the expression for the pressure difference across a curved grain boundary of radius *r*, starting from an energy balance. Explain its physical significance. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A pure copper specimen has a mean grain diameter of 20 Œºm and a grain boundary energy of 0.7 J/m¬≤. Calculate the driving pressure for grain growth. If the specimen is then annealed such that the mean grain size becomes 80 Œºm, what is the new driving pressure? Comment on the change. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution A(a) ‚Äî 7 marks

- **Step 1 (1 mark):** State the physical setup ‚Äî a spherical grain of radius *r* with a grain boundary of energy Œ≥.
- **Step 2 (2 marks):** Apply the energy balance. Allow the sphere to shrink by an infinitesimal $\delta r$. The volume change is $\delta V = 4\pi r^{2} \delta r$ and the surface-area change is $\delta A = 8\pi r \delta r$.
- **Step 3 (2 marks):** Equate pressure-volume work to surface energy change:
  $$P \cdot 4\pi r^{2} \delta r = \gamma \cdot 8\pi r \delta r$$
- **Step 4 (1 mark):** Simplify and write the final result:
  $$\boxed{\Delta P = \frac{2\gamma}{r}}$$
- **Step 5 (1 mark):** **Physical significance:** The convex side of the curved boundary is at higher pressure (and higher chemical potential). Atoms diffuse from the convex side to the concave side, causing the boundary to migrate toward its center of curvature. This is the fundamental mechanism by which the system reduces its total grain boundary energy.

---

#### Model Solution A(b) ‚Äî 7 marks

**Given:**
- Initial mean grain diameter, $D_1 = 20 \ \mu\text{m} = 20 \times 10^{-6} \ \text{m}$
- Final mean grain diameter, $D_2 = 80 \ \mu\text{m} = 80 \times 10^{-6} \ \text{m}$
- Grain boundary energy, $\gamma = 0.7 \ \text{J/m}^2$

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Use the polycrystal approximation $\Delta P \approx 4\gamma / D$.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Calculate $\Delta P_1$:
$$\Delta P_1 = \frac{4 \times 0.7}{20 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{2.8}{2 \times 10^{-5}} = 1.4 \times 10^{5} \ \text{Pa}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Calculate $\Delta P_2$:
$$\Delta P_2 = \frac{4 \times 0.7}{80 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{2.8}{8 \times 10^{-5}} = 3.5 \times 10^{4} \ \text{Pa}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Ratio:
$$\frac{\Delta P_1}{\Delta P_2} = \frac{1.4 \times 10^{5}}{3.5 \times 10^{4}} = 4$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** **Comment:** The driving pressure has decreased by a factor of **4** as the grain size grew by a factor of 4. This confirms the **self-retarding** nature of grain growth ‚Äî as grains enlarge, the driving force weakens, and growth slows asymptotically.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) ‚Äî *Internal Choice Alternative*

**[KTU University Exam ‚Äî Dec 2023, Module 2, Q4]**
**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain how a curved grain boundary migrates during grain growth. Derive the relationship between the chemical potential difference and the radius of curvature. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A polycrystalline Œ±-iron specimen is held at 900 ¬∞C. The grain boundary energy is 0.8 J/m¬≤ and the atomic volume of iron is $1.18 \times 10^{-29} \ \text{m}^3$. If a grain has a local boundary radius of curvature of 5 Œºm, calculate (i) the driving pressure and (ii) the chemical potential difference driving atomic diffusion. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution B(a) ‚Äî 7 marks

- **Step 1 (1 mark):** **Sketch description:** Draw a 2D cross-section showing two adjacent grains separated by a curved boundary. Label the convex side, the concave side, the radius of curvature *r*, the direction of atomic flux, and the direction of boundary migration.
- **Step 2 (2 marks):** **Mechanism:** Atoms on the convex side have higher chemical potential (higher pressure) than those on the concave side. Atoms migrate from convex ‚Üí concave via grain boundary diffusion. This causes the boundary to move **toward its center of curvature**, and the convex grain shrinks while the concave grain grows.
- **Step 3 (2 marks):** **Derivation of ŒîŒº:** For a hydrostatic pressure difference *P* across a boundary, the chemical potential shift per atom is $G = G_0 + PV$. With $V = \Omega$ (atomic volume):
  $$\Delta \mu = \Omega \cdot \Delta P = \frac{2\gamma \Omega}{r}$$
- **Step 4 (1 mark):** The sign convention: positive ŒîŒº on the convex side ‚Üí atoms flow from convex to concave ‚Üí boundary migrates toward convex grain.
- **Step 5 (1 mark):** **Engineering relevance:** This mechanism is the basis for grain growth kinetics, recrystallization texture evolution, and abnormal grain growth in transformer steels and turbine blade superalloys.

---

#### Model Solution B(b) ‚Äî 7 marks

**Given:**
- $\gamma = 0.8 \ \text{J/m}^2$
- $\Omega = 1.18 \times 10^{-29} \ \text{m}^3$
- $r = 5 \ \mu\text{m} = 5 \times 10^{-6} \ \text{m}$

**Part (i) ‚Äî Driving pressure (3 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Apply the Laplace relation:
$$\Delta P = \frac{2\gamma}{r}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Substitute values:
$$\Delta P = \frac{2 \times 0.8}{5 \times 10^{-6}}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Final result:
$$\boxed{\Delta P = \frac{1.6}{5 \times 10^{-6}} = 3.2 \times 10^{5} \ \text{Pa} = 0.32 \ \text{MPa}}$$

**Part (ii) ‚Äî Chemical potential difference (4 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Apply $\Delta \mu = \Omega \cdot \Delta P$:
$$\Delta \mu = (1.18 \times 10^{-29}) \times (3.2 \times 10^{5})$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Multiply:
$$\Delta \mu = 3.776 \times 10^{-24} \ \text{J/atom}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Convert to eV/atom (1 eV = $1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ J):
$$\Delta \mu = \frac{3.776 \times 10^{-24}}{1.602 \times 10^{-19}} = 2.36 \times 10^{-5} \ \text{eV/atom}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** **Comment:** Although this value is very small in eV terms, it acts on an enormous number of boundary atoms simultaneously, and at 900 ¬∞C the high atomic mobility (via grain boundary diffusion) translates it into a measurable rate of boundary migration.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ‚Äî Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch (‚Äì1 mark typical):** Mixing *microns* and *meters* in the denominator of $\Delta P = 2\gamma/r$. Always convert to SI before substituting.
> 2. **Using *D* in place of *r* (‚Äì1 mark):** The Laplace formula uses the *radius of curvature of the boundary*, not the grain diameter. For a polycrystal estimate, use $r \approx D/2$, NOT $r = D$.
> 3. **Forgetting the Arrhenius link (‚Äì1 mark):** Students often quote the driving pressure in isolation without mentioning that **mobility M(T)** controls the actual velocity $v = M \cdot \Delta P$. A complete answer must state this.
> 4. **Ignoring temperature dependence (‚Äì1 mark):** Writing "driving force is constant" loses a mark. Drive the point that ŒîP depends on Œ≥ and r, and *separately* the boundary *mobility* depends on T.
> 5. **Skipping the sign convention (‚Äì¬Ω mark):** Always state explicitly that the boundary moves **toward the center of curvature** ‚Äî examiners reward directional clarity.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [ ] The driving force for grain growth is **thermodynamic** ‚Äî reduction of total grain boundary free energy.
- [ ] Grain boundary energy Œ≥ is typically **0.5‚Äì1.5 J/m¬≤** for most metals.
- [ ] The fundamental Laplace equation is $\Delta P = 2\gamma / r$ (spherical grain) or $\gamma (1/r_1 + 1/r_2)$ (general).
- [ ] For a polycrystal, $\Delta P \approx 4\gamma / D$, so the driving force is **inversely proportional to grain size**.
- [ ] The **chemical potential difference** that drives atomic diffusion is $\Delta \mu = 2\gamma \Omega / r$.
- [ ] Boundary velocity is $v = M \cdot \Delta P$ where $M = M_0 \exp(-Q/RT)$ ‚Äî mobility is the *kinetic* part, separate from the *thermodynamic* driving pressure.
- [ ] **Smaller grains ‚Üí higher driving force ‚Üí faster growth** (initially). Growth is **self-retarding**.
- [ ] The **empirical kinetic law** is $D^{n} - D_0^{n} = Kt$ with $K = K_0 \exp(-Q/RT)$; ideal *n* = 2, but real systems often give *n* = 3 or 4.
- [ ] **Zener pinning** by second-phase particles and **solute drag** by impurities *suppress* growth even when ŒîP is large.
- [ ] **Always convert** *r* and *D* to **meters** before substituting into SI equations; report ŒîP in **Pa** (or MPa) and ŒîŒº in **J/atom** (or eV/atom).
- [ ] Boundary migrates **toward its center of curvature** ‚Äî the *convex* grain shrinks, the *concave* grain grows.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Characterization techniques: polishing and etching
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAUT304 - MATERIAL SCIENCE AND METALLURGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Characterization techniques: polishing and etching

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Metallographic polishing** is the sequential abrasive process used to produce a deformation-free, mirror-smooth (scratch-free, reflectivity > 90%) surface on a metal specimen, so that its true microstructure can be observed under an optical or electron microscope. **Metallographic etching** is the controlled chemical or electrochemical attack of this polished surface using a suitable reagent (etchant) to preferentially dissolve grain boundaries, second-phase particles, or orientation-dependent crystal planes, thereby revealing the **grain structure, phase distribution, inclusions, and defects** that are otherwise invisible on a featureless mirror.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):**
> Characterization of materials ‚Äì **Specimen preparation**: sectioning, mounting, grinding, polishing (mechanical, chemical, electrolytic), etching (chemical, electrolytic), and the concept of **grain boundary revelation**, **etch pits**, and **macro/micro examination**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have a piece of **black granite stone** that looks completely uniform and featureless. To see the beautiful crystals hidden inside, you would:

1. **Cut** a small, manageable slice (sectioning).
2. **Embed** it in a holder so your fingers don't get cut (mounting).
3. **Rub** it on rougher, then smoother sandpapers to flatten it (grinding).
4. **Buff** it on a soft cloth with fine paste to make it shine like a mirror (polishing).
5. **Spit on it with lemon juice** (or acid) ‚Äî the acid eats faster along the weak crystal boundaries, opening up tiny grooves. When light hits the polished-and-etched surface, the grooves scatter light, and suddenly the grain pattern "pops out."

**That is exactly what polishing and etching do in metallurgy** ‚Äî they convert a dull, scratched, featureless metal chunk into a "geological map" of its internal crystalline world.

> [!NOTE]
> The standard mirror-finish requirement is a surface roughness of **Ra ‚â§ 0.05 Œºm**. Anything coarser will mask the true microstructure under reflected light microscopy.

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics (must memorize)

| Metric | Standard Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Final surface roughness | **Ra ‚â§ 0.05 Œºm** | Required for clear micro-examination |
| Typical specimen size | **10‚Äì25 mm diameter**, **10‚Äì20 mm height** | Standard mount dimensions |
| Magnification range | **50√ó ‚Äì 1000√ó (optical)**, up to **100,000√ó (SEM)** | Depends on feature of interest |
| Etching time | **2 s ‚Äì 30 min** | Material and etchant dependent |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Complete Metallographic Specimen Preparation Flow

Specimen preparation is a **five-stage sequential pipeline**. Skipping or shortening any stage produces artefacts (false structures) that mislead the observer.

### Stage 1 ‚Äî Sectioning
- **Purpose:** Cut a representative piece from the bulk without altering its microstructure.
- **Methods:** Abrasive cut-off wheel (SiC / Al‚ÇÇO‚ÇÉ), wire EDM, precision saw, or chisel/hammer (for soft metals only).
- **Goal:** Produce a flat face of ~10‚Äì25 mm with **minimal heat, deformation, or burning**. Cooling fluid (water or oil) is mandatory.
- **Artefact to avoid:** *Burnt structure* ‚Äî a white-etching layer caused by overheating during cutting.

### Stage 2 ‚Äî Mounting
- **Purpose:** Encapsulate small, irregular, or fragile specimens for easy handling and edge preservation.
- **Two categories:**
  - **Compression (Hot) Mounting:** Uses **Bakelite (phenolic resin)** at ~140‚Äì180 ¬∞C and ~30 MPa. Best for routine work.
  - **Cold Mounting:** Uses **Epoxy** or **acrylic resin** cured at room temperature. Used for heat-sensitive or vacuum-sensitive specimens (e.g., for SEM).

### Stage 3 ‚Äî Grinding
- **Purpose:** Remove sectioning damage and produce a flat, planar surface.
- **Process:** Sequential abrasion on **SiC papers** (or emery) of progressively finer grit, typically:
  - **180 / 220 grit** ‚Üí remove cutting damage
  - **400 / 600 grit** ‚Üí refine
  - **800 / 1000 / 1200 grit** ‚Üí pre-polish finish
- **Rule:** Rotate specimen by **90¬∞** between successive grits. Continue until scratches from the previous grit are completely replaced by uniform scratches of the new grit.
- **Lubrication:** Continuous water flow to wash away debris and prevent heating.

### Stage 4 ‚Äî Polishing
The final mirror-finish stage. Three principal methods:

**(a) Mechanical Polishing**
- Uses a **rotating disc** covered with a soft cloth (velvet, felt, nylon, micro-cloth) charged with **abrasive slurry**.
- Abrasives in order of decreasing size:
  1. **Diamond paste** (1 Œºm, 0.25 Œºm) ‚Äî most efficient, very hard (~10,000 HV).
  2. **Alumina (Al‚ÇÇO‚ÇÉ)** ‚Äî 0.3 Œºm and 0.05 Œºm. Used in water suspension.
  3. **Silica (SiO‚ÇÇ)** ‚Äî 0.02 Œºm colloidal suspension (chemical-mechanical polishing).
- The cloth holds the abrasive gently against the specimen, removing the last few microns of deformed metal.

**(b) Chemical Polishing**
- The specimen is immersed in a **chemically aggressive solution** (e.g., **HF + H‚ÇÇO‚ÇÇ** for silicon, **HNO‚ÇÉ + HF** for stainless steel).
- No mechanical abrasion ‚Äî metal atoms are dissolved uniformly by oxidation-reduction.
- **Advantage:** No deformation layer; ideal for very soft metals.
- **Disadvantage:** Difficult to control; poor for multi-phase alloys.

**(c) Electrolytic (Electropolishing)**
- Specimen is made the **anode** in an electrolytic cell.
- A suitable electrolyte (e.g., **perchloric acid + acetic anhydride** for steels) selectively dissolves the high-current-density asperities (peaks), producing a flat, strain-free surface.
- **Equation (Faraday's Law of Electrolysis):**
  $$m = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$$
  where *m* = mass dissolved, *I* = current (A), *t* = time (s), *M* = molar mass, *n* = valency, *F* = **96,485 C/mol** (Faraday's constant).
- **Advantage:** Reveals true microstructure without mechanical deformation.
- **Disadvantage:** Selective attack on multi-phase materials.

### Stage 5 ‚Äî Etching
- The polished mirror surface, while beautiful, is **optically featureless** because every grain reflects light identically.
- Etching selectively attacks:
  - **Grain boundaries** (high energy) ‚Üí grooves form ‚Üí light scatters ‚Üí boundary appears dark.
  - **Different phases** (different chemical potential) ‚Üí contrast develops.
  - **Oriented grains** ‚Üí different reflectivity in polarized light.

**Types of Etching:**

**(a) Chemical Etching (Wet Etching)**
- Specimen is **immersed or swabbed** in a chemical reagent (etchant) for a controlled time.
- Common etchants (must memorize for KTU):

| Material | Etchant | Composition | Reveals |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Mild steel / carbon steel** | **Nital** | 2‚Äì5 % HNO‚ÇÉ in ethanol | Ferrite grain boundaries, pearlite |
| **Stainless steel** | **Kalling's / Aqua regia** | HCl + HNO‚ÇÉ | Austenitic grain structure |
| **Cast iron** | **Nital** + picral | HNO‚ÇÉ in ethanol | Ferrite, pearlite, graphite |
| **Aluminium alloys** | **Keller's reagent** | HF + HCl + HNO‚ÇÉ + H‚ÇÇO | Grain boundaries, second phases |
| **Copper / Brass** | **NH‚ÇÑOH + H‚ÇÇO‚ÇÇ** | Ammonia + peroxide | Œ±-phase grain boundaries |
| **Magnesium** | **Nital** (5‚Äì10 %) | HNO‚ÇÉ + ethanol | Grain structure |

**(b) Electrolytic Etching**
- Specimen is made the **anode** in an electrolytic cell; etching is controlled by current/voltage.
- Examples: **10 % oxalic acid** for stainless steel (reveals carbides), **NaOH** for lead.

**(c) Thermal Etching**
- Specimen is heated in vacuum ‚Äî atoms evaporate preferentially from grain boundaries.
- Used when chemical etchants fail (refractory metals, ceramics).

**(d) Colour (Tint) Etching**
- A thin oxide layer is grown on the surface, which produces **interference colours** (blue, yellow, red) under white light depending on grain orientation.
- Used to differentiate phases of similar composition (e.g., **Klemm's tint** for steel phases).

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step ‚Äî A Logical Reasoning Chain

- **Why grind progressively?** Coarse grit removes bulk damage; jumping to fine grit leaves the deeper damage layers untouched ‚Äî they will appear as false "flow lines" later.
- **Why rotate 90¬∞?** This visually confirms when the previous scratches are completely removed; it is the only reliable end-point indicator.
- **Why etch at all?** A perfectly polished surface is *too smooth* ‚Äî every grain reflects light at the same angle into the objective, so the image is uniform. Etching introduces *differential reflectivity* and *differential topography*, which are the two primary contrast mechanisms in optical microscopy.
- **Why different etchants for different metals?** Etching depends on the relative **chemical potential** between grain boundary and grain interior, and between different phases. No single etchant works for all alloys.

## 2.3 KTU Formula / Parameter Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> The metallographic preparation workflow is more procedural than calculative. The following table summarizes all quantitative and key qualitative parameters required for KTU 2024 answers.

| Parameter / Concept | Equation or Value | Unit | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|
| Faraday's Law (electropolishing) | $m = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$ | kg | Quantifies mass loss in electrolytic prep |
| Faraday's constant | $F = 96{,}485$ | C/mol | Universal constant |
| Hardness of diamond abrasive | $\sim 10{,}000$ | HV (Vickers) | Justifies diamond as the ultimate abrasive |
| Typical removal rate per grit | $1\text{‚Äì}10$ | Œºm/min | Determines grinding time |
| Final surface roughness | $R_a \le 0.05$ | Œºm | Optical-microscopy threshold |
| Nital composition | $2\text{‚Äì}5\%\ HNO_3$ in ethanol | ‚Äî | Most common steel etchant |
| Keller's reagent | $HF + HCl + HNO_3 + H_2O$ | ‚Äî | Standard Al-alloy etchant |
| Magnification limit (optical) | $d = \frac{0.61 \cdot \lambda}{N.A.}$ | m | Resolution limit of microscopy |
| Typical etching time | $2\text{ s to }30\text{ min}$ | s/min | Etchant-and-material dependent |
| Etching temperature | $20\text{‚Äì}40$ | ¬∞C | Room temperature, slight warming accelerates |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Mass Loss During Electrolytic Polishing (Faraday's Law)

**Statement:** When a current *I* passes through an electrolytic cell for time *t*, the mass *m* of metal dissolved at the anode is given by:

$$m = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$$

**Step-by-step derivation:**

**Step 1 ‚Äî Total charge passed.**
The fundamental definition of electric current is charge per unit time.

$$Q = I \cdot t$$

where $Q$ is the total charge in coulombs (C), $I$ is the current in amperes (A), and $t$ is the time in seconds (s).

**Step 2 ‚Äî Moles of electrons transferred.**
By definition, one mole of electrons carries a charge equal to Faraday's constant, $F = 96{,}485\ C/mol$.

$$n_{e^-} = \frac{Q}{F} = \frac{I \cdot t}{F}$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Moles of metal atoms dissolved.**
For a metal of valency $n$, each metal atom requires $n$ electrons (i.e., $n$ Faradays per mole of atoms) to be oxidized from the anode.

$$n_{metal} = \frac{n_{e^-}}{n} = \frac{I \cdot t}{n \cdot F}$$

**Step 4 ‚Äî Convert moles of metal to mass.**
Multiplying both sides by the molar mass $M$ (kg/mol) gives the mass dissolved:

$$m = n_{metal} \cdot M = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$$

**Step 5 ‚Äî Engineering interpretation.**
This formula is used to estimate the **surface material loss per second** of electropolishing, ensuring that only the deformed layer is removed and the underlying bulk microstructure is preserved.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU Style)

> **Problem:** A steel specimen is being electropolished. The polishing current is 0.5 A, applied for 60 seconds. Iron is dissolved as Fe¬≤‚Å∫ (i.e., n = 2). Calculate the mass of iron removed. Given: M(Fe) = 55.85 g/mol, F = 96,485 C/mol.

**Step 1 ‚Äî Identify the parameters.**

$$I = 0.5\ A,\quad t = 60\ s,\quad n = 2,\quad M = 55.85\ g/mol,\quad F = 96{,}485\ C/mol$$

**Step 2 ‚Äî Apply Faraday's Law.**

$$m = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Substitute the numerical values.**

$$m = \frac{0.5 \times 60 \times 55.85}{2 \times 96{,}485}$$

**Step 4 ‚Äî Numerator evaluation.**

$$0.5 \times 60 \times 55.85 = 30 \times 55.85 = 1675.5\ g \cdot A \cdot s / mol$$

Since $A \cdot s = C$, the numerator is $1675.5\ C \cdot g/mol$.

**Step 5 ‚Äî Denominator evaluation.**

$$2 \times 96{,}485 = 192{,}970\ C/mol$$

**Step 6 ‚Äî Final division.**

$$m = \frac{1675.5}{192{,}970} = 0.008682\ g \approx 8.68\ mg$$

**Step 7 ‚Äî Engineering interpretation.**
A mass loss of **~8.7 mg** corresponds to a thin uniform layer removal (typically 1‚Äì3 Œºm deep over a 1 cm¬≤ area), which is sufficient to remove the mechanically deformed surface layer introduced by grinding, while leaving the underlying microstructure intact.

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix: Selecting Polishing & Etching Method

| Material | Recommended Mounting | Polishing Abrasive (Final) | Recommended Etchant | Typical Etching Time |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Low-carbon steel** | Bakelite (hot) | 0.05 Œºm Al‚ÇÇO‚ÇÉ | 2 % Nital | 5‚Äì15 s |
| **High-carbon / tool steel** | Bakelite | 0.25 Œºm diamond ‚Üí 0.05 Œºm Al‚ÇÇO‚ÇÉ | 4 % Nital or Picral | 10‚Äì20 s |
| **Stainless steel (austenitic)** | Bakelite | Diamond + colloidal silica | Kalling's No. 2 or Glyceregia | 30 s ‚Äì 2 min |
| **Cast iron** | Bakelite | Diamond | Nital (2 %) | 5‚Äì10 s |
| **Aluminium alloys** | Cold (epoxy) for soft | Colloidal silica | Keller's reagent | 10‚Äì30 s |
| **Copper / Brass** | Bakelite | Al‚ÇÇO‚ÇÉ | NH‚ÇÑOH + H‚ÇÇO‚ÇÇ | 5‚Äì15 s |
| **Titanium** | Bakelite | Colloidal silica | Kroll's reagent | 10‚Äì30 s |
| **Magnesium alloys** | Cold (epoxy) | Colloidal silica | Nital (5‚Äì10 %) | 5‚Äì10 s |
| **Refractory metals (W, Mo)** | Bakelite | Diamond | Electrolytic (NaOH) | 1‚Äì5 s |

## 3.4 Quality Control Checkpoints (KTU-Favourite Analytical Step)

After preparation, the metallographer must verify:

1. **Scratch-free at 100√ó magnification** under bright-field illumination.
2. **No pull-outs** of hard second-phase particles (e.g., carbides, graphite flakes).
3. **No comet tails** (smearing of soft phases like ferrite).
4. **No pitting** from over-etching.
5. **No deformation layer** (visible only under polarized light or after re-polishing).
6. **Uniform, representative field of view** ‚Äî at least 5 fields examined before concluding the microstructure.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Flow Diagram ‚Äî Complete Metallographic Specimen Preparation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Bulk Metal Sample] --> B[Stage 1: Sectioning]
    B --> B1[Abrasive Cut-off Wheel / EDM]
    B1 --> C[Stage 2: Mounting]
    C --> C1[Hot Mount - Bakelite]
    C --> C2[Cold Mount - Epoxy]
    C1 --> D[Stage 3: Grinding]
    C2 --> D
    D --> D1[180/220 grit SiC - Remove Cut Damage]
    D1 --> D2[400/600 grit SiC - Refine]
    D2 --> D3[800/1000/1200 grit SiC - Pre-Polish]
    D3 --> E[Stage 4: Polishing]
    E --> E1[Mechanical - Diamond or Al2O3]
    E --> E2[Chemical - Acid Solution]
    E --> E3[Electrolytic - Anode in Cell]
    E1 --> F[Stage 5: Etching]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    F --> F1[Chemical - Nital, Keller, Kalling]
    F --> F2[Electrolytic - Controlled Current]
    F --> F3[Thermal - Vacuum Heating]
    F --> F4[Colour Tint - Interference Oxide]
    F1 --> G[Microstructural Examination]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    F4 --> G
    G --> G1[Optical Microscopy 50x to 1000x]
    G --> G2[Scanning Electron Microscopy SEM]
    G --> G3[Hardness Testing on Identified Phases]
```

## 4.2 Schematic ‚Äî Why Etching Reveals the Microstructure

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Polished Mirror Surface] --> A1[Every Grain Reflects Light Identically]
    A1 --> A2[Image Appears Uniform - No Microstructure Visible]
    A2 --> B[Apply Etchant]
    B --> B1[Grain Boundaries Dissolved Faster - High Energy]
    B --> B2[Phase Interfaces Dissolved Differently]
    B1 --> C[Tiny Grooves Form at Boundaries]
    B2 --> C
    C --> D[Light Scatters at Groove Walls]
    D --> E[Boundaries Appear Dark Under Microscope]
    E --> F[Grain Structure Clearly Visible]
```

## 4.3 Etching Mechanism ‚Äî Chemical Attack at Grain Boundaries

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M[Metal Surface - Polished Mirror] --> E1[Etchant Molecules Reach Surface]
    E1 --> E2[At Grain Boundary - High Energy - Rapid Dissolution]
    E1 --> E3[At Grain Interior - Low Energy - Slow Dissolution]
    E2 --> G1[Deep Groove Forms at Boundary]
    E3 --> G2[Surface Remains Smooth]
    G1 --> H[Incident Light Hits Sloped Groove Wall]
    H --> H1[Light Scattered Away from Objective Lens]
    H1 --> I[Boundary Appears as Dark Line]
    G2 --> J[Light Reflects Back into Objective]
    J --> K[Grain Interior Appears Bright]
    I --> L[Contrast Formed - Microstructure Visible]
    K --> L
```

## 4.4 Polishing Method Selection Block

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q[Choose Polishing Method] --> Q1{Material Hardness?}
    Q1 -->|Hard - Carbides, Tool Steel| R1[Mechanical with Diamond 0.25 to 1 micrometre]
    Q1 -->|Medium - Mild Steel, Cu| R2[Mechanical with Alumina 0.05 to 0.3 micrometre]
    Q1 -->|Soft - Al, Mg, Pb| R3[Mechanical with Colloidal Silica 0.02 micrometre]
    Q1 -->|Multi-phase Sensitive| R4[Electrolytic Polishing]
    R1 --> S[Followed by Chemical Etching]
    R2 --> S
    R3 --> S
    R4 --> S
    S --> T[Microstructure Examination]
```
