<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ‚Äî Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam ‚Äì May 2024 Model Paper]**
**Differentiate between mechanical polishing and electrolytic polishing of a metallographic specimen. Mention one advantage and one limitation of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Mechanical Polishing | Electrolytic Polishing |
|---|---|---|
| **Principle** | Abrasive particles (diamond/Al‚ÇÇO‚ÇÉ) on a rotating cloth physically remove material. | Specimen is the anode; an electrolytic cell dissolves material by electrochemical action. |
| **Surface layer** | Leaves a thin **deformed/Beilby layer**. | Produces a **strain-free**, true surface. |
| **Advantage** | Simple, widely applicable to most metals and alloys. | Ideal for soft metals and single-phase materials; no mechanical deformation. |
| **Limitation** | Deformed layer may mask true microstructure. | Selective attack on multi-phase alloys; not suitable for non-conducting materials. |

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam ‚Äì Dec 2023]**
**What is metallographic etching? Why is it necessary after polishing?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Metallographic etching is the controlled chemical or electrochemical attack of a polished metal surface using a suitable reagent (etchant) to reveal microstructural features.

**Necessity:** A polished surface is optically featureless because all grains reflect light identically, producing a uniform bright image. Etching introduces **differential reflectivity** and **topographic contrast** by preferentially dissolving grain boundaries and phase interfaces, so that grain structure, phases, and inclusions become visible under the microscope.

A common etchant for mild steel is **2% Nital** (2% HNO‚ÇÉ in ethanol), which attacks ferrite grain boundaries and reveals the ferrite-pearlite microstructure in a few seconds.

---

## 5.2 Part B ‚Äî Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A ‚Äî 14 Marks**
**[KTU University Exam ‚Äì Model Paper, Module 2]**

**(a)** Describe the complete procedure for the preparation of a metallographic specimen for micro-examination. Discuss sectioning, mounting, grinding, and polishing in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the principles of chemical and electrolytic etching. List the etchants used for mild steel, stainless steel, and aluminium alloys. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a) ‚Äî 7 marks

**1. Sectioning (1 mark):** A small representative piece is cut from the bulk using an abrasive cut-off wheel (SiC or Al‚ÇÇO‚ÇÉ) under continuous coolant flow. The goal is to obtain a flat specimen of ~10‚Äì25 mm with **no heat, deformation, or burning**. Wire-EDM is preferred for very hard or heat-sensitive materials.

**2. Mounting (1.5 marks):** The specimen is encapsulated for ease of handling. **Hot compression mounting** uses Bakelite (phenolic resin) at 140‚Äì180 ¬∞C and 30 MPa. **Cold mounting** uses epoxy or acrylic resin, suitable for heat-sensitive specimens (e.g., for SEM). Mounting preserves edges and brittle phases.

**3. Grinding (2 marks):** Sequential abrasion on SiC papers with progressively finer grits:
- **180‚Äì220 grit:** removes sectioning damage
- **400‚Äì600 grit:** refines the surface
- **800‚Äì1200 grit:** final pre-polish finish

The specimen is rotated by **90¬∞** between successive grits until the scratches from the previous paper are completely removed. Water lubrication removes debris and prevents heating.

**4. Polishing (2.5 marks):** The final mirror finish is achieved on a rotating disc covered with a soft cloth charged with abrasive slurry:
- **Diamond paste** (1 Œºm, 0.25 Œºm) ‚Äî most efficient, hardness ~10,000 HV.
- **Alumina (Al‚ÇÇO‚ÇÉ)** ‚Äî 0.3 Œºm and 0.05 Œºm.
- **Colloidal silica (SiO‚ÇÇ)** ‚Äî 0.02 Œºm, used for chemical-mechanical polishing of soft metals.

Three polishing methods are distinguished:
- **Mechanical** ‚Äî abrasive action.
- **Chemical** ‚Äî uniform dissolution in aggressive acid (no deformation layer).
- **Electrolytic (Electropolishing)** ‚Äî specimen as anode, governed by Faraday's Law: $m = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$.

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b) ‚Äî 7 marks

**1. Principles of Chemical Etching (3 marks):** The polished specimen is immersed in or swabbed with a chemical reagent. Etching occurs by **preferential dissolution**:
- At **grain boundaries** (high atomic disorder, high free energy) ‚Üí grooves form ‚Üí boundaries appear dark.
- At **phase interfaces** (different chemical potential) ‚Üí contrast develops between phases.
- At **oriented grains** ‚Üí different reflectivity under polarized light.

**2. Principles of Electrolytic Etching (2 marks):** The specimen is the **anode** of an electrolytic cell. A controlled current is passed; the etchant acts only at the anode, with the cathode (often stainless steel) completing the circuit. The current density and time control the depth of attack. Examples: **10% oxalic acid** for stainless steel (reveals carbides), **NaOH solution** for lead and lead alloys.

**3. Common Etchants Table (2 marks):**

| Material | Etchant | Composition |
|---|---|---|
| **Mild steel** | **Nital** | 2‚Äì5% HNO‚ÇÉ in ethanol |
| **Stainless steel** | **Kalling's No. 2** | HCl + HNO‚ÇÉ + CuCl‚ÇÇ |
| **Aluminium alloys** | **Keller's reagent** | HF + HCl + HNO‚ÇÉ + H‚ÇÇO |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not** confuse *polishing* with *etching* ‚Äî polishing gives a mirror finish; etching reveals microstructure. Examiners frequently deduct 1‚Äì2 marks for this mix-up.
> 2. **Always** mention **Nital** for mild steel and **Keller's reagent** for aluminium ‚Äî these are the most-tested etchant names.
> 3. **Do not** skip the "**90¬∞ rotation**" detail in grinding ‚Äî it is a KTU favourite 1-mark point.
> 4. In electropolishing, **always quote Faraday's Law** as the governing equation ‚Äî it is a guaranteed mark.

---

### **Question B ‚Äî 14 Marks (Alternative to Question A)**
**[KTU University Exam ‚Äì Model Paper, Module 2]**

**(a)** What is meant by mechanical polishing? Explain the role of abrasives like diamond paste and alumina, and state why colloidal silica is preferred for soft metals like aluminium. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the various types of etching used in metallography. Explain the formation of grain-boundary grooves and the dark-line contrast observed under the microscope. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a) ‚Äî 7 marks

**1. Mechanical Polishing Definition (1 mark):** Mechanical polishing is the final stage of metallographic preparation in which a mirror-like, scratch-free, deformation-minimized surface is produced by the action of fine abrasive particles held on a soft, rotating cloth against the specimen.

**2. Role of Diamond Paste (2 marks):** Diamond is the hardest known material (~10,000 HV). Diamond pastes (typically 6 Œºm, 3 Œºm, 1 Œºm, and 0.25 Œºm grades) cut through even very hard phases (e.g., carbides in tool steel) efficiently, producing a flat, planar surface with minimal subsurface damage.

**3. Role of Alumina (Al‚ÇÇO‚ÇÉ) (2 marks):** Alumina is used in water suspensions of 0.3 Œºm and 0.05 Œºm grades. It is cheaper than diamond, suitable for medium-hardness metals (mild steel, copper, brass), and gives a fine finish when used on napped cloths.

**4. Why Colloidal Silica for Aluminium (2 marks):** Aluminium is **soft and chemically reactive**. Hard abrasives like diamond smear the surface and embed themselves. **Colloidal silica (0.02 Œºm SiO‚ÇÇ)** performs **chemical-mechanical polishing** ‚Äî it chemically oxidizes the Al surface to Al‚ÇÇO‚ÇÉ, which is then gently removed by the same particles. The result is a **scratch-free, undeformed surface** ideal for soft metals.

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b) ‚Äî 7 marks

**1. Types of Etching (3 marks):**

- **Chemical (Wet) Etching** ‚Äî by acid/alkaline reagents. Most common.
- **Electrolytic Etching** ‚Äî specimen is the anode; current-controlled attack.
- **Thermal Etching** ‚Äî heating in vacuum; atoms evaporate from boundaries.
- **Colour (Tint) Etching** ‚Äî controlled oxide layer produces interference colours.
- **Ionic Etching (Cathodic Vacuum Etching)** ‚Äî used for refractory metals and ceramics.

**2. Grain-Boundary Groove Formation (2.5 marks):**
Grain boundaries are regions of atomic misfit, high stored energy, and faster chemical attack. When the etchant contacts the surface, atoms at the boundary dissolve at a higher rate than atoms within the grain. This creates a **V-shaped groove** at every boundary. The groove has sloped side walls that are not perpendicular to the incident light, so light is scattered away from the objective lens.

**3. Dark-Line Contrast (1.5 marks):**
Because the scattered light does not enter the objective lens, the boundary region appears **dark** in the bright-field image, while the flat grain interior reflects light directly into the objective and appears **bright**. This contrast mechanism (called **etch contrast** or **topographic contrast**) is the basis for revealing grain structure in optical metallography.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Revision Checklist ‚Äî Must Memorize Before KTU Exam:**

- ‚úÖ **Five stages** of metallographic preparation: **S**ectioning ‚Üí **M**ounting ‚Üí **G**rinding ‚Üí **P**olishing ‚Üí **E**tching.
- ‚úÖ Always rotate specimen by **90¬∞** between successive grinding grits.
- ‚úÖ Final surface roughness target: **Ra ‚â§ 0.05 Œºm**.
- ‚úÖ Standard abrasive sequence: **SiC papers ‚Üí Diamond ‚Üí Alumina ‚Üí Colloidal Silica**.
- ‚úÖ **Diamond** (~10,000 HV) is the hardest abrasive ‚Äî used for hard materials.
- ‚úÖ **Colloidal silica** is the gentlest ‚Äî used for soft metals (Al, Mg).
- ‚úÖ **Electropolishing** is governed by **Faraday's Law**: $m = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$.
- ‚úÖ **F = 96,485 C/mol** ‚Äî must remember.
- ‚úÖ **Nital** (2% HNO‚ÇÉ in ethanol) ‚Äî universal etchant for mild / carbon steel.
- ‚úÖ **Keller's reagent** (HF + HCl + HNO‚ÇÉ + H‚ÇÇO) ‚Äî standard for aluminium alloys.
- ‚úÖ **Kalling's / Aqua regia** ‚Äî for stainless steel.
- ‚úÖ **Picral** (picric acid + ethanol) ‚Äî alternative for high-carbon steel (better for carbide delineation).
- ‚úÖ Etching reveals microstructure because **grain boundaries dissolve faster** than grain interiors, creating grooves that **scatter light** and appear **dark**.
- ‚úÖ Quality control: no scratches at 100√ó, no pull-outs, no comet tails, no pitting.
- ‚úÖ Hot mounting uses **Bakelite (phenolic resin)** at 140‚Äì180 ¬∞C.
- ‚úÖ Cold mounting uses **Epoxy** ‚Äî used for SEM and heat-sensitive specimens.
- ‚úÖ Three polishing methods: **Mechanical, Chemical, Electrolytic** ‚Äî each leaves a different surface condition.
- ‚úÖ Optical microscopy resolution limit: $d = \frac{0.61 \lambda}{N.A.}$ (~0.2 Œºm with green light).
- ‚úÖ Thermal and tint etching are used when chemical etchants fail (refractory metals, ceramics).
- ‚úÖ Typical magnification: **50√ó ‚Äì 1000√ó (optical)**; up to **100,000√ó (SEM)**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Constitution of alloys
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBAUT304 - MATERIAL SCIENCE AND METALLURGY > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** Constitution of alloys # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition > [!NOTE] > **Constitution of Alloys (KTU 2024 Definition):** > The *constitution of an alloy* refers to the **type, number, relative amounts, and distribution of the phases** that make up the internal structure of a metallic mixture. It is essentially the "anatomical blueprint" of an alloy, describing which phases coexist, their proportions (quantified by the lever rule), and their spatial arrangement ‚Äî all of which are governed by the alloy's chemical composition, temperature, and pressure. In formal KTU terminology, an **alloy** is a substance having *metallic properties* and composed of **two or more elements**, of which **at least one is a metal**. The *constituents* of an alloy are the chemical elements added deliberately, and the *constituent phases* are the physically and chemically distinct homogeneous regions that result when these elements are mixed. ### Key Vocabulary (Board-Exam Critical) | Term | Meaning | |---|---| | **Component** | The chemically independent constituents of the alloy system (e.g., Cu and Zn in brass) | | **Phase** | A physically and chemically homogeneous portion of the system, separated by a distinct interface | | **System** | A portion of the universe chosen for thermodynamic study | | **Solid Solution** | A single phase in which atoms of two or more elements share the same crystal lattice | | **Intermediate Phase / Intermetallic Compound** | A distinct phase with a definite (often stoichiometric) composition different from the pure components | | **Solvent** | The element present in greater amount (also called the *base metal*) | | **Solute** | The element(s) present in smaller amount (the *alloying addition*) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Favourite Question:** > *"Differentiate between a component, a phase, and a constituent of an alloy."* ‚Äî Be ready to write this distinction in 3 marks. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ‚Äî Plain English Intuition Imagine you are making **a fruit salad with two fruits ‚Äî mango and pineapple**: - If you cut the chunks **very small and mix them uniformly**, every spoonful tastes of *both* flavours equally. There is only **one "phase"** (a single, homogeneous mixture). This is the analogue of a **solid solution**. - If you keep the mango chunks **large and the pineapple chunks large**, you can clearly *see* and *separate* the two ‚Äî that is, there are **two distinct phases** coexisting. This is the analogue of a **two-phase alloy** (e.g., a eutectic mixture of $\alpha + \beta$). Just as the **size, ratio, and distribution of the fruits** determine whether the salad looks uniform or patchy, the **atomic size, proportion, and arrangement** of the alloying elements determine whether the alloy is a single-phase solid solution or a multi-phase mixture. > [!TIP] > The phrase *Constitution of Alloys* literally means **"what phases are inside the alloy and in what quantity"** ‚Äî a question answered by **Phase Diagrams** (which you will study in the next module). ## 1.3 Why Do We Need to Know the Constitution? 1. **Mechanical strength** of an alloy depends on the phases present (e.g., martensite vs austenite in steel). 2. **Corrosion resistance** is determined by phase chemistry (e.g., single-phase stainless steel resists corrosion better than two-phase). 3. **Electrical and magnetic behaviour** depends on the crystal structure of constituent phases. 4. **Heat treatment response** (hardening, annealing) is governed by the phases that form on cooling. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Classification of Alloys by Constitution The constitution of an alloy can be of **two principal types** ‚Äî and the KTU board asks this classification almost every year. ### Type 1 ‚Äî **Single-Phase Alloys (Solid Solutions)** A solid solution is a crystalline phase in which **two or more elements are mutually soluble in the solid state**, and only **one phase** exists. The atoms of the solute fit into the lattice of the solvent either by *replacement* or by *occupying voids*. There are **two sub-classes**: #### (a) Substitutional Solid Solution - The solute atom **replaces** a solvent atom on its lattice site. - The crystal structure of the solvent is **retained**. **Conditions for extensive substitutional solubility** are codified by the **Hume-Rothery Rules** (discussed in ¬ß2.3). #### (b) Interstitial Solid Solution - The solute atom **sits in the interstitial voids** (holes) between solvent atoms. - Solute atoms are **always much smaller** than solvent atoms. - Classic example: **Carbon dissolved in $\gamma$-iron (FCC austenite)**. The carbon radius is ~**0.077 nm** while the Fe radius is ~**0.124 nm**, giving a ratio of ~0.62, well below the 0.59 limit for octahedral-site occupation. > [!NOTE] > **Sub-division of Substitutional Solutions:** > - **Random solid solution** ‚Äî solute atoms occupy lattice sites *without order* (high-entropy effect). > - **Ordered solid solution (superlattice)** ‚Äî solute atoms occupy a *specific, regular pattern* of sites, e.g., $\beta$-brass (CuZn) below 468 ¬∞C. ### Type 2 ‚Äî **Multi-Phase Alloys** Here, **two or more distinct phases** coexist as a heterogeneous mixture. The phases may be: - Two different solid solutions ($\alpha + \beta$). - A solid solution + an intermetallic compound. - A solid solution + a liquid (in partially-molten states). The **lever rule** (Module 4) gives the relative amounts of each phase. ## 2.2 Intermediate Phases / Intermetallic Compounds > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 14-Mark Standard:** Be ready to explain what an intermetallic compound is, its properties, and the *three main classifications* ‚Äî these are favourite 7-mark sub-questions. These are distinct phases with a **definite composition and a crystal structure different from the parent metals**. They are *brittle*, *hard*, and have *high melting points* ‚Äî which is why they are used to *strengthen* alloys (precipitation hardening). **Three major classifications:** 1. **Valence compounds** ‚Äî obey normal chemical valences, e.g., $\text{Mg}_2\text{Pb}$, $\text{Mg}_2\text{Sn}$. 2. **Electron compounds** ‚Äî formulated by **Hume-Rothery's rule** that a particular electron-to-atom ratio produces a particular crystal structure: - Ratio **3/2** ‚Üí BCC ($\beta$-phase, e.g., CuZn) - Ratio **21/13** ‚Üí complex cubic ($\gamma$-phase) - Ratio **7/4** ‚Üí HCP ($\varepsilon$-phase) 3. **Size-factor compounds / interstitial compounds** ‚Äî formed when one atom is very small (e.g., H, C, N, B) and fits in the interstices of a transition metal lattice. Examples: **TiC, TiN, VC, Fe$_3$C (cementite), WC**. > [!TIP] > **Hard memorisation trick for electron compounds:** "**3-21-7**" ‚Äî write the numbers 3, 21, and 7 in the order BCC, complex cubic, HCP. This is the most-asked mnemonic in KTU vivas. ## 2.3 Hume-Rothery Rules for Solid Solubility These four empirical rules predict whether two metals will form an **extensive substitutional solid solution**. *For the KTU board, write them in order with a one-line justification each.* > [!NOTE] > **The Four Hume-Rothery Rules:** > 1. **Atomic Size Factor:** The radii of the solute and solvent atoms must differ by **less than ~15%** (i.e., $\vert R_{\text{solute}} - R_{\text{solvent}} \vert / R_{\text{solvent}} < 0.15$). Otherwise, lattice distortion is too severe. > 2. **Same Crystal Structure:** The solute and solvent must have the **same crystal structure** (both FCC, both BCC, or both HCP). Otherwise, polymorphism limits solubility. > 3. **Similar Electronegativity:** If electronegativity differs greatly, the two elements tend to form an **intermetallic compound** rather than a solid solution. > 4. **Same Valence:** Solute and solvent should have the **same valence**. A higher-valent solute in a lower-valent solvent is more soluble than the reverse. ## 2.4 KTU Formula & Rule Cheat-Sheet | Concept | Expression / Rule | Application | Units | |---|---|---|---| | Solid Solubility Limit | Determined by **phase diagram** solubility curve | Maximum % of solute dissolvable | wt % or at % | | Atomic Size Factor | $\Delta R / R_{\text{solvent}} < 0.15$ | Hume-Rothery Rule 1 | Dimensionless | | Solute Radius Ratio (interstitial) | $r_{\text{solute}} / r_{\text{solvent}} < 0.41$ (tetrahedral), $< 0.59$ (octahedral) | Geometry of interstitial sites | Dimensionless | | Mass % Composition | $C_i = (m_i / m_{\text{total}}) \times 100$ | Chemical analysis of alloy | wt % | | Atomic % Composition | $a_i = (n_i / n_{\text{total}}) \times 100$ | Phase diagram work | at % | | Electron-to-Atom Ratio (e/a) | $\text{e/a} = \sum_i (V_i \cdot a_i)$ | Identifying electron compounds | Dimensionless | | Weight % ‚Üî Atomic % | $\text{at}\%\, B = \dfrac{100 \cdot w_B / A_B}{w_A/A_A + w_B/A_B}$ | Converting between units | at % | | Lever Rule (Phase Fraction) | $f_{\alpha} = \dfrac{c_{\beta} - c_0}{c_{\beta} - c_{\alpha}}$ | Phase proportion in two-phase field | Dimensionless | > [!WARNING] > The e/a formula is a **sum** over all components, where $V_i$ is the valence of element $i$ and $a_i$ is its atomic fraction. Memorise this for electron-compound identification questions. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation ‚Äî Converting Mass % to Atomic % > [!NOTE] > KTU frequently tests **mass-to-atomic % conversion** in numerical problems (3-mark part of a 14-mark question). Derive it here so the method is committed to long-term memory. **Given:** An alloy of two metals **A** and **B** with mass percentages $w_A$ and $w_B$, and atomic weights $A_A$ and $A_B$ respectively. **Step 1 ‚Äî Express the mass of each component per 100 g of alloy.** Take a **100 g basis** (most common KTU trick). Then mass of A = $w_A$ g, mass of B = $w_B$ g. **Step 2 ‚Äî Convert mass to moles using the atomic weight.** $$ n_A = \frac{w_A}{A_A} \quad \text{(moles of A in 100 g of alloy)} $$ $$ n_B = \frac{w_B}{A_B} \quad \text{(moles of B in 100 g of alloy)} $$ **Step 3 ‚Äî Compute total moles.** $$ n_{\text{total}} = n_A + n_B = \frac{w_A}{A_A} + \frac{w_B}{A_B} $$ **Step 4 ‚Äî Atomic fraction of B.** $$ a_B = \frac{n_B}{n_{\text{total}}} = \frac{\dfrac{w_B}{A_B}}{\dfrac{w_A}{A_A} + \dfrac{w_B}{A_B}} $$ **Step 5 ‚Äî Multiply by 100 to get atomic percentage.** $$ \boxed{\text{at}\%\, B = \frac{100 \, w_B / A_B}{w_A / A_A + w_B / A_B}} $$ ### Worked Example > An alloy contains **80 wt % Cu** and **20 wt % Zn**. Atomic weights: Cu = 63.5, Zn = 65.4. Find the atomic % of Zn. **Step 1.** Mass of Zn in 100 g alloy = 20 g; mass of Cu = 80 g. **Step 2.** Moles: $$ n_{\text{Zn}} = \frac{20}{65.4} = 0.3058 \text{ mol} $$ $$ n_{\text{Cu}} = \frac{80}{63.5} = 1.2598 \text{ mol} $$ **Step 3.** Total moles: $$ n_{\text{total}} = 0.3058 + 1.2598 = 1.5656 \text{ mol} $$ **Step 4.** Atomic % of Zn: $$ \text{at}\%\, \text{Zn} = \frac{0.3058}{1.5656} \times 100 = 19.53\% $$ > [!TIP] > Result interpretation: The atomic % is *slightly less* than the weight % (20 wt % vs 19.53 at %), because Zn is **heavier per atom** than Cu. **Heavier elements always depress their atomic percentage** relative to their weight percentage. ## 3.2 Derivation ‚Äî Verification of Hume-Rothery Size-Factor Rule > [!NOTE] > KTU may ask: *"Why must the atomic radii differ by less than 15%?"* Here is the analytical justification. Let $R$ = radius of solvent atom and $R(1+\epsilon)$ = radius of solute atom, where $\epsilon$ is the fractional size difference. **Step 1 ‚Äî Strain energy of solution per atom.** When a solute atom replaces a solvent atom, the lattice is locally strained. The elastic strain energy per atom is approximately: $$ E_{\text{strain}} \approx \frac{24 \pi K G R^3 \epsilon^2}{3 K + 4 G} $$ where $G$ = shear modulus of the solvent, $K$ = bulk modulus, and $\epsilon = (R_{\text{solute}} - R)/R$. **Step 2 ‚Äî Critical condition for solubility.** If $\epsilon > 0.15$, the strain energy exceeds the **enthalpy of mixing** (typically a few kJ/mol), and the system prefers to **segregate into two phases** rather than dissolve. **Step 3 ‚Äî Empirical observation.** Hume-Rothery & Powell (1935) plotted solubility data for thousands of binary systems and found the **15% threshold** as a sharp cut-off. **Step 4 ‚Äî Application example: Cu‚ÄìNi system.** $$ R_{\text{Cu}} = 0.128 \text{ nm}, \quad R_{\text{Ni}} = 0.125 \text{ nm} $$ $$ \epsilon = \frac{0.128 - 0.125}{0.128} = 0.0234 = 2.34\% $$ Since $2.34\% \ll 15\%$, the size-factor rule is satisfied. **Cu and Ni form a complete solid solution** in all proportions ‚Äî which is *experimentally correct*. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix ‚Äî Decision Flowchart for Alloy Constitution > [!IMPORTANT] > Use this decision matrix whenever a KTU question describes an alloy and asks you to identify its constitutional type. | Observation / Data Given | Decision Step | Conclusion | |---|---|---| | Solute radius / solvent radius < 0.59 | Interstitial site occupation possible | **Interstitial solid solution** | | Solute radius / solvent radius between 0.59 and 1.0 | Substitute on lattice | **Substitutional solid solution** | | Atomic-size mismatch > 15 % | Severe lattice strain | Two phases (limited solubility) | | Different crystal structures of pure elements | Polymorphism barrier | Limited solubility | | Large electronegativity difference | Compound formation favoured | **Intermetallic compound** | | Specific electron-to-atom ratio (3/2, 21/13, 7/4) | Crystal structure of phase | **Electron compound** | | Small non-metal (H, C, N, B) in transition metal | Interstices filled | **Interstitial compound** (e.g., carbide, nitride) | | All atoms similar size, structure, valence, electronegativity | All four Hume-Rothery rules satisfied | **Complete solid solubility** | > [!TIP] > **For the 7-mark sub-question** on "Discuss the conditions for solid solubility" ‚Äî always end with the *decision matrix above* in your answer. Examiners reward a tabulated conclusion. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Schematic ‚Äî Substitutional vs Interstitial Solid Solution This Mermaid block visually distinguishes the two principal mechanisms by which solute atoms enter a solvent lattice. ```mermaid flowchart LR A["PURE SOLVENT LATTICE"]:::base --> B{"Solute atom added"}:::decision B -- "Radius similar
less than 15 percent mismatch" --> C["SUBSTITUTIONAL
SOLID SOLUTION"]:::sub B -- "Solute radius much smaller
less than 0.59 of solvent" --> D["INTERSTITIAL
SOLID SOLUTION"]:::inter C --> C1["FCC BCC or HCP lattice retained"]:::detail C --> C2["Solute replaces host atoms"]:::detail D --> D1["Solute occupies voids"]:::detail D2["Atoms may distort lattice"]:::detail D --> D2 classDef base fill:#fdf6b2,stroke:#b59f00,color:#000 classDef decision fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#b30000,color:#000 classDef sub fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003a75,color:#000 classDef inter fill:#d5f5d5,stroke:#1d6f1d,color:#000 classDef detail fill:#ffffff,stroke:#333,color:#000 ``` ## 4.2 Block Diagram ‚Äî Constitutional Architecture of an Alloy System This Mermaid block describes the **logical architecture** of how constituent elements assemble into an alloy, then into phases, and finally into the *constitution* of the material. ```mermaid flowchart TB P1["ELEMENT A
solvent base metal"]:::elem P2["ELEMENT B
solute alloying element"]:::elem P3["ELEMENT C
third addition if any"]:::elem P1 --> Q{"Mixing process
melting solid state"}:::process P2 --> Q P3 --> Q Q --> R1["SINGLE PHASE
solid solution alpha"]:::phase Q --> R2["MULTI PHASE
alpha plus beta"]:::phase Q --> R3["INTERMETALLIC
compound phase"]:::phase R1 --> S1["Constituents
atoms of A B C uniformly distributed"]:::cons R2 --> S2["Constituents
alpha grains plus beta grains separated by interface"]:::cons R3 --> S3["Constituents
distinct stoichiometric compound embedded in matrix"]:::cons S1 --> T["CONSTITUTION
type fraction and distribution of phases"]:::final S2 --> T S3 --> T classDef elem fill:#e1f5fe,stroke:#01579b,color:#000 classDef process fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000 classDef phase fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#880e4f,color:#000 classDef cons fill:#d1c4e9,stroke:#4527a0,color:#000 classDef final fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#fff ``` ## 4.3 Schematic ‚Äî Hume-Rothery Rule Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD H["Two metals A and B"]:::start --> S1{"Size factor
mismatch less than 15 percent"}:::rule S1 -- NO --> X1["Limited solubility
two phase system"]:::bad S1 -- YES --> S2{"Same crystal structure
FCC FCC or BCC BCC"}:::rule S2 -- NO --> X2["Limited solubility"]:::bad S2 -- YES --> S3{"Similar electronegativity
delta less than 0.4"]:::rule S3 -- NO --> X3["Intermetallic compound forms"]:::compound S3 -- YES --> S4{"Same valence
or higher valent solute"]:::rule S4 -- NO --> X4["Limited solubility"]:::bad S4 -- YES --> Y["EXTENSIVE OR COMPLETE
SOLID SOLUBILITY"]:::good classDef start fill:#e0e0e0,stroke:#000,color:#000 classDef rule fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#000 classDef good fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000 classDef bad fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c,color:#000 classDef compound fill:#d1c4e9,stroke:#311b92,color:#000 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam ‚Äì Dec 2023]` **Differentiate between a *component* and a *phase* of an alloy system. Give one example each.** **Model Answer (10 lines, KTU-expected length):** | Aspect | Component | Phase | |---|---|---| | **Definition** | A chemically independent constituent of the system | A physically and chemically homogeneous region with a distinct boundary | | **Nature** | Chemical identity | Structural / physical identity | | **Number in Cu‚ÄìZn system** | 2 (Cu and Zn) | Can be 1, 2, or 3 depending on composition & temperature | | **Example** | Fe and C in steel | Ferrite ($\alpha$-Fe), Austenite ($\gamma$-Fe), Cementite (Fe$_3$C) | > [!TIP] > A 3-mark answer should be ~10‚Äì12 lines, ideally with a small table. Examiners deduct marks for *one-line answers* in "differentiate" questions. --- ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam ‚Äì May 2024]` **State the four Hume-Rothery rules governing substitutional solid solubility.** **Model Answer:** 1. **Atomic size factor** ‚Äî atomic radii of the two metals should differ by **less than 15 %** to avoid excessive lattice strain. 2. **Same crystal structure** ‚Äî both metals should have **the same crystal structure** (FCC‚ÄìFCC, BCC‚ÄìBCC, etc.) in the temperature range of interest. 3. **Similar electronegativity** ‚Äî the electronegativity difference should be **small**; otherwise intermetallic compounds form instead of a solid solution. 4. **Same valence** ‚Äî both metals should have **the same valence**; a higher-valent metal dissolves more in a lower-valent solvent than vice versa. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks, Module-Internal Choice) ### Question A ‚Äî `[KTU University Exam ‚Äì Model Paper 2024, Set A]` > **A)** (a) Define an alloy. With neat sketches, explain the formation of **substitutional** and **interstitial** solid solutions. Discuss the **Hume-Rothery rules** for extensive solid solubility. *(7 marks)* > **A)** (b) What are **intermetallic compounds**? Classify them with **two examples** for each class. *(7 marks)* #### Model Solution ‚Äî Part (a) **Definition (1 mark):** An **alloy** is a substance with metallic properties composed of two or more elements, of which at least one is a metal. **Substitutional Solid Solution (2 marks):** - Solute atoms **replace** solvent atoms on lattice sites. - The crystal structure of the **solvent is retained**. - Example: Cu in Ni (both FCC) ‚Äî atoms randomly substitute on the FCC sites. - **Sketch** (describe verbally ‚Äî KTU accepts labelled diagram): Draw a square lattice of open circles (solvent) with one filled circle (solute) replacing a solvent atom. **Interstitial Solid Solution (2 marks):** - Solute atoms (always **small**, e.g., C, N, H, B) occupy the **interstices / voids** between solvent atoms. - The geometry of voids sets the size limit: **0.225 R** for triangular sites, **0.414 R** for tetrahedral sites, **0.732 R** (some texts use **0.59**) for octahedral sites ‚Äî only atoms below this radius can fit. - Example: **C in $\gamma$-Fe (FCC austenite)** ‚Äî C atoms sit in octahedral voids. **Hume-Rothery Rules (2 marks):** State the four rules from ¬ß2.3 with the 15 % size threshold and the e/a rationale. #### Model Solution ‚Äî Part (b) **Definition (1 mark):** Intermetallic compounds are **intermediate phases** with a **definite composition and a crystal structure different from the parent metals**. They are hard, brittle, and often stoichiometric. **Three classes (2 marks each):** 1. **Valence compounds** ‚Äî obey normal chemical valences; formed between strongly electropositive and strongly electronegative metals. *Examples:* $\text{Mg}_2\text{Pb}$, $\text{Mg}_2\text{Sn}$, $\text{Mg}_3\text{Sb}_2$. 2. **Electron compounds** ‚Äî stability governed by **electron-to-atom ratio (e/a)**: - e/a = **3/2** ‚Üí **BCC** (Œ≤-phase), e.g., CuZn, Cu$_3$Al - e/a = **21/13** ‚Üí **Complex cubic** (Œ≥-phase), e.g., Cu$_5$Zn$_8$, Cu$_9$Al$_4$ - e/a = **7/4** ‚Üí **HCP** (Œµ-phase), e.g., CuZn$_3$, Cu$_3$Sn 3. **Interstitial compounds / size-factor compounds** ‚Äî small non-metal atoms in interstices of transition-metal lattices. *Examples:* **TiC, TiN, VC, Fe$_3$C (cementite), WC.** > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** > - Do **not confuse** "ordered solid solution" with "intermetallic compound". An ordered solid solution (e.g., CuZn below 468 ¬∞C) is still a *solid solution*; an intermetallic compound is a *separate phase*. > - Always **state the e/a ratio** explicitly when writing electron-compound examples ‚Äî failing to do so loses one mark. > - For size-factor rules, examiners check whether you wrote **"less than 15 %"** literally. Writing "15" alone is treated as incomplete. --- ### Question B ‚Äî `[KTU University Exam ‚Äì Model Paper 2024, Set B]` > **B)** (a) Explain the **constitution of alloys** with reference to **components, phases, and microstructural features**. Describe the difference between **single-phase and multi-phase alloys** with suitable examples. *(7 marks)* > **B)** (b) An alloy of **Cu and Ni** contains **30 wt % Ni**. Atomic weights: Cu = 63.5, Ni = 58.7. Determine the **atomic percentage of Ni** in the alloy. *(7 marks)* #### Model Solution ‚Äî Part (a) **Constitution ‚Äî Components (1 mark):** The **chemically independent** elements of the alloy system. In brass, components are Cu and Zn; in steel, Fe and C. **Constitution ‚Äî Phases (2 marks):** A **phase** is a homogeneous region with uniform physical and chemical properties, separated from other phases by an interface. An alloy may have one phase (e.g., $\alpha$-brass) or two phases (e.g., $\alpha + \beta$ brass above 35 % Zn). **Single-phase alloys (2 marks):** Contain only **one phase**, usually a solid solution. Properties are *isotropic* on a macro scale. Examples: **$\alpha$-brass** (Cu with up to 35 % Zn in solid solution), **Cu‚ÄìNi** alloys in any proportion, **austenitic stainless steel** (single-phase FCC). **Multi-phase alloys (2 marks):** Contain **two or more phases** with distinct compositions and crystal structures. The *amount* of each phase is given by the **lever rule**; the *morphology* (lamellar, particulate, network) determines properties. Examples: **$\alpha + \beta$ brass**, **pearlite** (ferrite + cementite in steel), **eutectic alloys** such as Pb‚ÄìSn solder. #### Model Solution ‚Äî Part (b) **Step 1 ‚Äî Take 100 g basis.** Mass of Ni = 30 g; mass of Cu = 70 g. **Step 2 ‚Äî Convert to moles.** $$ n_{\text{Ni}} = \frac{30}{58.7} = 0.5111 \text{ mol} $$ $$ n_{\text{Cu}} = \frac{70}{63.5} = 1.1024 \text{ mol} $$ **Step 3 ‚Äî Total moles.** $$ n_{\text{total}} = 0.5111 + 1.1024 = 1.6135 \text{ mol} $$ **Step 4 ‚Äî Atomic % of Ni.** $$ \text{at}\%\, \text{Ni} = \frac{0.5111}{1.6135} \times 100 = 31.68\% $$ **Step 5 ‚Äî Sanity check.** Since Ni (58.7) is *lighter* than Cu (63.5), the **atomic % of Ni must be higher than the weight %**. Indeed, 31.68 % > 30 %. ‚úì > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall ‚Äî Numerical Conversion:** > - Most students *forget to convert grams to moles* and simply write 30 % as the atomic %. This is a **0-marks error** ‚Äî the question specifically asks for the atomic %. > - Some students reverse Cu and Ni in the formula. Always check: the *weight % you are asked to convert* must appear in the **numerator** of the moles expression. > - Do not present the final answer without showing the **intermediate mole values** ‚Äî 2 of 7 marks are for *clear working*. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Last-Minute Revision Checklist (Examination-Ready):** - **Constitution of an alloy** = type + number + amount + distribution of phases. - An **alloy** has at least one metal + one other element; both components need not be metals. - **Solid solutions** are of **two** types: **substitutional** (replace) and **interstitial** (occupy voids). - **Hume-Rothery Rules (in order):** (1) Size factor $< 15\%$, (2) Same crystal structure, (3) Similar electronegativity, (4) Same valence. Mnemonic: **"S-S-S-V"** (Size, Structure, electronegativity-Similar, Valence). - **Electron compounds** are identified by **e/a ratio = 3/2, 21/13, 7/4** for BCC, complex cubic, HCP respectively. Mnemonic: **"3-21-7"**. - **Intermetallic compounds** are *brittle and hard*; used to **strengthen** alloys (precipitation hardening). - **Ordered solid solution ‚âÝ intermetallic compound** ‚Äî keep this distinction crisp. - **wt % ‚Üí at % conversion** is tested every year. Always go through **mass ‚Üí moles ‚Üí fraction ‚Üí √ó100**. - **Heavier atoms** depress their atomic % relative to weight %; **lighter atoms** raise it. - For **interstitial voids**: triangular $< 0.225 R$, tetrahedral $< 0.414 R$, octahedral $< 0.59 R$ (some texts 0.732). - **Lever rule** (next module) gives the *quantitative* answer to "what fraction of each phase" ‚Äî but here, constitution is *qualitative*. - **Real-world engineering examples** worth memorising: **Cu‚ÄìNi** (complete solid solution), **Cu‚ÄìZn brass** (limited solubility, two phases above 35 % Zn), **Fe‚ÄìC steel** (interstitial, forms Fe$_3$C), **stainless steel** (single-phase FCC austenite). > [!TIP] > **Examiner's Golden Tip:** Whenever a 14-mark question mentions "constitution of alloys", always structure your answer under these **four bold sub-headings**: > 1. *Components and phases ‚Äî definitions and examples* > 2. *Single-phase solid solutions ‚Äî substitutional and interstitial* > 3. *Hume-Rothery rules with a numerical illustration* > 4. *Intermediate phases / intermetallic compounds with classification* > > This structure matches the **KTU model-answer marking scheme** and guarantees full credit allocation.

topics.title: Solid solutions: substitutional and interstitial
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAUT304 - MATERIAL SCIENCE AND METALLURGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Solid solutions: substitutional and interstitial

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Solid Solution

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Solid Solution (KTU Definition):** A solid solution is a single-phase, homogeneous crystalline solid in which two or more elements (atoms of the solute) are incorporated into the crystal lattice of the host element (the solvent) without changing its basic crystal structure.

A solid solution is fundamentally a **monophase** system ‚Äî meaning the entire material behaves as a single phase with uniform composition, even though it contains more than one type of atom. Unlike a mechanical mixture or a chemical compound, the atoms of the solute occupy specific positions within the parent crystal lattice.

> [!NOTE]
> **Solute vs. Solvent Analogy:**
> - **Solvent (Matrix/Host):** The element whose crystal lattice forms the framework (usually present in larger proportion).
> - **Solute (Solute atoms):** The element that dissolves into the solvent lattice.
> - **Concentration:** May vary between the limits of solubility (0% to 100% in some cases, or limited by the phase diagram).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy ‚Äî "The Coffee Cup" Model:**
> Imagine pouring sugar into a cup of black coffee. The sugar crystals dissolve completely, and you cannot distinguish individual sugar grains anymore. The coffee becomes sweeter uniformly throughout. Here:
> - **Coffee** = Solvent (Cu, Fe, Ni, etc.)
> - **Sugar** = Solute (Zn, Ni, C, etc.)
> - **Sweetened Coffee** = Solid Solution (e.g., Brass ‚Äî Cu + Zn)
>
> Just like you cannot see sugar as separate particles in dissolved coffee, in a solid solution, the solute atoms are dispersed atom-by-atom within the host lattice.

## 1.3 The Two Fundamental Types of Solid Solutions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mandate:** Students must be able to clearly distinguish between **Substitutional** and **Interstitial** solid solutions, including their formation conditions, examples, and effects on properties.

### 1.3.1 Substitutional Solid Solution

In a **substitutional solid solution**, the solute atoms **replace (substitute)** the solvent atoms at their normal lattice sites. The solute atom occupies the position where a solvent atom would normally be.

**Common Example:** Brass (Cu + Zn) ‚Äî Zinc atoms replace copper atoms on FCC lattice sites.

### 1.3.2 Interstitial Solid Solution

In an **interstitial solid solution**, the solute atoms fit into the **interstitial voids (spaces)** between the solvent atoms in the lattice. The solute atom is significantly smaller than the solvent atom.

**Common Example:** Austenite (Fe + C) ‚Äî Carbon atoms (small) sit in the interstitial holes of the FCC iron lattice.

## 1.4 Why Solid Solutions Form ‚Äî The Hume-Rothery Rules Preview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hume-Rothery's Rules** govern the extent to which a solute can dissolve in a solvent. For a **substitutional** solid solution, the rules are:
> 1. **Atomic size difference** < **15%**
> 2. Same **crystal structure**
> 3. Similar **electronegativity**
> 4. Same or similar **valency**

For **interstitial** solid solutions, the primary rule is that the **solute atom radius must be very small** (typically < **0.41 √ó radius of solvent atom**).

These rules are explored in detail in Section 2.

---

## 1.5 Solubility Limits ‚Äî Quick Snapshot

> [!NOTE]
> - **Unlimited Solubility (Isomorphous):** Complete miscibility across all compositions. Example: **Cu‚ÄìNi system**.
> - **Limited Solubility:** Maximum solubility is restricted. Example: **Cu‚ÄìZn** (Zn solubility in Cu is ~39% at 400¬∞C).
> - **Negligible Solubility:** Practically no solubility. Example: **Cu‚ÄìPb** (lead does not dissolve in copper).

![Solid Solution Concept Overview](placeholder)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Takeaway:** Solid solutions are **not compounds** ‚Äî they retain the crystal structure of the solvent and are governed by the solubility limits dictated by Hume-Rothery rules.

---

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Substitutional Solid Solution ‚Äî Detailed Mechanism

### 2.1.1 Definition (Refined)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **substitutional solid solution** is a crystalline solid solution in which the solute atoms occupy positions in the crystal lattice that are normally occupied by atoms of the solvent, i.e., they substitute for the host atoms at the lattice points.

### 2.1.2 Visual Mechanism

In a substitutional solid solution:
- The solute atom (B) **replaces** a solvent atom (A) at a lattice site.
- The overall number of atoms per unit cell remains the same.
- The crystal structure of the host (e.g., FCC, BCC, HCP) is preserved.

### 2.1.3 Types of Substitutional Solid Solutions

| Type | Description | Example |
|------|-------------|---------|
| **Random Substitutional** | Solute atoms occupy lattice sites randomly with no order. Most common type. | Cu‚ÄìNi (high temperature) |
| **Ordered Substitutional** | Solute atoms occupy specific, repeating positions below a critical temperature (Curie-like ordering). | Cu‚ÄìAu (Cu‚ÇÉAu at low temperature) |
| **Continuous (Isomorphous)** | Complete solubility across entire composition range. | Cu‚ÄìNi, Ag‚ÄìAu, Ge‚ÄìSi |

### 2.1.4 Hume-Rothery Rules for Substitutional Solubility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 4 Hume-Rothery Rules for Substitutional Solid Solutions:**

**Rule 1: Atomic Size Factor (Radius Difference Rule)**
- The atomic radii of solute and solvent must be within **15%** of each other.
- If the difference exceeds 15%, lattice strain becomes too high, and solubility is severely restricted.
- Empirical rule: 
$$\text{Radius Ratio} = \left| \frac{r_{solute} - r_{solvent}}{r_{solvent}} \right| \times 100\% \leq 15\%$$

**Rule 2: Crystal Structure Rule**
- Solvent and solute must have the **same crystal structure** for extensive solid solubility.
- Example: Cu (FCC) and Ni (FCC) ‚Üí unlimited solubility.
- Counter-example: Cu (FCC) and Sn (BCT/tetragonal) ‚Üí very limited solubility.

**Rule 3: Electronegativity Rule**
- The two elements should have similar **electronegativity values**.
- If electronegativity difference is large, the elements tend to form **intermetallic compounds** rather than solid solutions.
- Similar electronegativity ‚Üí metallic bonding character is preserved.

**Rule 4: Valency Rule (Relative Valence Effect)**
- A metal of **higher valency** has greater solubility in a metal of **lower valency** than vice versa.
- Example: Al (valency 3) dissolves significantly in Cu (valency 1), but Cu has very limited solubility in Al.

## 2.2 Interstitial Solid Solution ‚Äî Detailed Mechanism

### 2.2.1 Definition (Refined)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> An **interstitial solid solution** is a crystalline solid solution in which the solute atoms occupy the **interstitial positions (voids)** between the solvent atoms in the lattice.

### 2.2.2 Size Criterion

> [!NOTE]
> For interstitial solid solubility, the radius ratio must be:
> $$\frac{r_{solute}}{r_{solvent}} < 0.41 \quad \text{(or typically } < 0.59 \text{ in some cases)}$$
> 
> Only **small atoms** qualify: H, B, C, N, O.

### 2.2.3 Common Interstitial Sites in Metallic Lattices

| Lattice | Octahedral Void Radius | Tetrahedral Void Radius |
|---------|------------------------|-------------------------|
| **FCC** | $0.414 \times r_{metal}$ | $0.225 \times r_{metal}$ |
| **BCC** | $0.155 \times r_{metal}$ | $0.291 \times r_{metal}$ |
| **HCP** | $0.414 \times r_{metal}$ | $0.225 \times r_{metal}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** In FCC and HCP, **octahedral voids are larger** (radius = 0.414r) than tetrahedral voids (0.225r). In **BCC**, the situation is **reversed** ‚Äî the tetrahedral void is actually larger (0.291r) than the octahedral void (0.155r) due to the open BCC structure.

### 2.2.4 Common Examples of Interstitial Solid Solutions

| System | Solvent (Host) | Solute | Industrial Material |
|--------|----------------|--------|---------------------|
| **Fe‚ÄìC (Austenite)** | Œ≥-Fe (FCC) | C (Carbon) | Carbon Steel, Cast Iron |
| **Fe‚ÄìC (Ferrite)** | Œ±-Fe (BCC) | C (Carbon) | Low-Carbon Steel |
| **Fe‚ÄìN** | Œ±-Fe (BCC) | N (Nitrogen) | Nitrided Steels |
| **Ti‚ÄìO, Ti‚ÄìN** | Œ±-Ti (HCP) | O, N | Titanium Alloys |
| **Pd‚ÄìH** | Pd (FCC) | H (Hydrogen) | Hydrogen Storage |

## 2.3 Comprehensive Comparison Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Comparison: Substitutional vs. Interstitial**

| Property | Substitutional SS | Interstitial SS |
|----------|------------------|-----------------|
| **Solute atom position** | Replaces solvent at lattice sites | Occupies interstitial voids |
| **Solute atom size** | Comparable to solvent (< 15% difference) | Much smaller than solvent (< 0.41r) |
| **Solubility extent** | Can be unlimited (isomorphous) | Always limited |
| **Governing rules** | All 4 Hume-Rothery rules | Size rule dominant |
| **Effect on lattice** | Local distortion around solute | Significant lattice strain |
| **Strengthening effect** | Moderate to high | Very high (e.g., C in Fe) |
| **Typical solutes** | Zn, Ni, Sn, Al, Mn | C, N, H, B, O |
| **Examples** | Brass (Cu‚ÄìZn), Bronze (Cu‚ÄìSn), Cu‚ÄìNi | Austenite (Œ≥-Fe-C), Ferrite (Œ±-Fe-C) |
| **Max solubility** | Up to 100% in isomorphous systems | Few atomic % (e.g., C in Œ≥-Fe: 2.14%) |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Equations for Solid Solutions**

| Formula/Rule | Expression | Application |
|--------------|-----------|-------------|
| **Hume-Rothery Size Rule** | $\left\vert \frac{r_{solute} - r_{solvent}}{r_{solvent}} \right\vert \times 100\% \leq 15\%$ | Determines substitutional solubility limit |
| **Interstitial Size Criterion** | $\frac{r_{solute}}{r_{solvent}} < 0.41$ | Determines if interstitial SS forms |
| **FCC Octahedral Void Radius** | $r_{void} = 0.414 \times r_{metal}$ | Calculate max interstitial atom size |
| **FCC Tetrahedral Void Radius** | $r_{void} = 0.225 \times r_{metal}$ | Calculate max interstitial atom size |
| **BCC Octahedral Void Radius** | $r_{void} = 0.155 \times r_{metal}$ | Calculate max interstitial atom size |
| **BCC Tetrahedral Void Radius** | $r_{void} = 0.291 \times r_{metal}$ | Calculate max interstitial atom size |
| **Number of Octahedral Voids (FCC)** | $N_{octa} = 4$ per unit cell | Total voids = number of atoms in FCC |
| **Number of Tetrahedral Voids (FCC)** | $N_{tetra} = 8$ per unit cell | Twice the number of atoms in FCC |
| **Vegard's Law (lattice parameter)** | $a_{SS} = a_1(1-x) + a_2 \cdot x$ | Calculate lattice parameter of SS |
| **Solid Solution Strengthening** | $\Delta \sigma_{ss} \propto G \cdot \varepsilon^{3/2} \cdot c^{1/2}$ | Strength increment due to SS |
| **Max Solubility (Interstitial)** | Typically $\leq 2-3$ at% | Empirical for C, N in metals |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** These rules are the foundation of **alloy design** in metallurgy. Engineers use them to predict whether a new alloy composition will form a stable, useful solid solution or will form brittle intermetallic compounds.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Radius Ratio for Octahedral Void in FCC Lattice

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** Derive the radius ratio $\frac{r_{void}}{r_{metal}} = 0.414$ for an octahedral void in an FCC lattice.

**Step 1: Geometric Setup**
Consider the FCC unit cell. An octahedral void is located at the **center of the unit cell** (at coordinates $\left(\frac{1}{2}, \frac{1}{2}, \frac{1}{2}\right)$) and at the **edge centers**.

The void is surrounded by **6 atoms** at the face centers of the 6 faces adjacent to the void, forming an octahedron.

**Step 2: Coordination of the Void**
In an octahedral void:
- Number of atoms around the void = **6** (octahedral coordination).
- The void center is equidistant from all 6 surrounding atoms.

**Step 3: Distance Calculation**
For the octahedral void at the cube center $\left(\frac{a}{2}, \frac{a}{2}, \frac{a}{2}\right)$:
- The 6 face-centered atoms are at positions like $\left(\frac{a}{2}, \frac{a}{2}, 0\right)$, $\left(\frac{a}{2}, \frac{a}{2}, a\right)$, $\left(\frac{a}{2}, 0, \frac{a}{2}\right)$, $\left(\frac{a}{2}, a, \frac{a}{2}\right)$, $\left(0, \frac{a}{2}, \frac{a}{2}\right)$, $\left(a, \frac{a}{2}, \frac{a}{2}\right)$.

Distance from void center to face center:

$$
\begin{aligned}
d &= \sqrt{\left(\frac{a}{2} - \frac{a}{2}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{a}{2} - \frac{a}{2}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{a}{2} - 0\right)^2} \\
&= \sqrt{0 + 0 + \left(\frac{a}{2}\right)^2} \\
&= \frac{a}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Expressing in Terms of Atomic Radius**
In FCC, atoms touch along the **face diagonal**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
4 r_{metal} &= a\sqrt{2} \\
a &= \frac{4 r_{metal}}{\sqrt{2}} = 2\sqrt{2} \cdot r_{metal}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Geometric Constraint (Atoms Touch the Void)**
The void radius $r_{void}$ and the metal atom radius $r_{metal}$ must satisfy:

$$
r_{metal} + r_{void} = \frac{a}{2}
$$

**Step 6: Solving for the Ratio**

$$
\begin{aligned}
r_{void} &= \frac{a}{2} - r_{metal} \\
&= \frac{2\sqrt{2} \cdot r_{metal}}{2} - r_{metal} \\
&= \sqrt{2} \cdot r_{metal} - r_{metal} \\
&= r_{metal} (\sqrt{2} - 1) \\
&= r_{metal} (1.414 - 1) \\
&= 0.414 \cdot r_{metal}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7: Final Result**

$$
\boxed{\frac{r_{void}}{r_{metal}} = 0.414}
$$

This means a solute atom of radius up to **41.4% of the metal atom radius** can fit into an octahedral void of an FCC lattice without distorting the lattice.

## 3.2 Derivation: Radius Ratio for Tetrahedral Void in FCC Lattice

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** Derive the radius ratio $\frac{r_{void}}{r_{metal}} = 0.225$ for a tetrahedral void in an FCC lattice.

**Step 1: Geometric Setup**
A tetrahedral void in FCC is located at positions like $\left(\frac{1}{4}, \frac{1}{4}, \frac{1}{4}\right)$, surrounded by **4 atoms** forming a tetrahedron.

**Step 2: Tetrahedral Coordination**
Number of atoms around the void = **4** (tetrahedral coordination).

**Step 3: Distance from Void to Nearest Atom**
The distance from the tetrahedral void at $\left(\frac{a}{4}, \frac{a}{4}, \frac{a}{4}\right)$ to a face-centered atom at $\left(\frac{a}{2}, \frac{a}{2}, 0\right)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
d &= \sqrt{\left(\frac{a}{4} - \frac{a}{2}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{a}{4} - \frac{a}{2}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{a}{4} - 0\right)^2} \\
&= \sqrt{\left(-\frac{a}{4}\right)^2 + \left(-\frac{a}{4}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{a}{4}\right)^2} \\
&= \sqrt{\frac{a^2}{16} + \frac{a^2}{16} + \frac{a^2}{16}} \\
&= \sqrt{\frac{3a^2}{16}} = \frac{a\sqrt{3}}{4}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Constraint Equation**

$$
r_{metal} + r_{void} = \frac{a\sqrt{3}}{4}
$$

**Step 5: Substituting $a = 2\sqrt{2} \cdot r_{metal}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
r_{void} &= \frac{2\sqrt{2} \cdot r_{metal} \cdot \sqrt{3}}{4} - r_{metal} \\
&= \frac{\sqrt{6}}{2} \cdot r_{metal} - r_{metal} \\
&= r_{metal} \left(\frac{\sqrt{6}}{2} - 1\right) \\
&= r_{metal} (1.2247 - 1) \\
&= 0.225 \cdot r_{metal}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Final Result**

$$
\boxed{\frac{r_{void}}{r_{metal}} = 0.225}
$$

## 3.3 Worked Example: Checking Cu‚ÄìZn Brass for Substitutional Solid Solubility

> [!NOTE]
> **Question:** Using Hume-Rothery rules, determine if Cu and Zn can form an extensive substitutional solid solution.

**Given Data:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
r_{Cu} &= 0.128 \text{ nm} \\
r_{Zn} &= 0.133 \text{ nm} \\
\text{Structure of Cu} &= \text{FCC} \\
\text{Structure of Zn} &= \text{HCP} \\
\text{Electronegativity Cu} &= 1.90 \\
\text{Electronegativity Zn} &= 1.65 \\
\text{Valency Cu} &= +1 \\
\text{Valency Zn} &= +2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Atomic Size Rule**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Radius difference} &= \left\vert \frac{r_{Zn} - r_{Cu}}{r_{Cu}} \right\vert \times 100\% \\
&= \left\vert \frac{0.133 - 0.128}{0.128} \right\vert \times 100\% \\
&= \left\vert \frac{0.005}{0.128} \right\vert \times 100\% \\
&= 3.9\%
\end{aligned}
$$

Since **3.9% < 15%** ‚úì ‚Äî **Rule satisfied**.

**Step 2: Crystal Structure Rule**
- Cu: FCC
- Zn: HCP
- **Rule NOT satisfied** strictly, but the size effect dominates, allowing limited solubility (up to ~39% Zn in Cu).

**Step 3: Electronegativity Rule**

$$
\Delta EN = 1.90 - 1.65 = 0.25
$$

Small difference ‚Üí no strong tendency to form intermetallic compounds. ‚úì

**Step 4: Valency Rule**
- Zn (valency +2) is **higher** than Cu (valency +1).
- Higher valency metal (Zn) has **greater solubility in lower valency metal (Cu)** than vice versa. ‚úì

**Step 5: Conclusion**

$$
\boxed{\text{Cu and Zn form a substitutional solid solution (Brass) with limited solubility due to crystal structure difference.}}
$$

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix ‚Äî When Does Each Type Form?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineer's Decision Tree for Solid Solution Classification**

| Condition | Substitutional SS | Interstitial SS |
|-----------|-------------------|-----------------|
| $r_{solute} \approx r_{solvent}$ (within 15%) | ‚úì | ‚úó |
| $r_{solute} << r_{solvent}$ (ratio < 0.41) | ‚úó | ‚úì |
| Same crystal structure preferred | ‚úì | Not required |
| Solute is small (C, N, H, B, O) | ‚úó | ‚úì |
| Solute is metallic (Zn, Ni, Al, Sn) | ‚úì | ‚úó |
| Solubility is high (> 10 at%) | Likely | Unlikely |
| Examples | Brass, Bronze, Cu-Ni, Monel | Fe-C, Fe-N, Pd-H, Ti-O |

## 3.5 Vegard's Law ‚Äî Lattice Parameter of a Solid Solution

> [!NOTE]
> **Vegard's Law:** For a continuous substitutional solid solution $A_{1-x}B_x$, the lattice parameter varies **linearly** with composition:

$$
a_{SS} = a_A(1-x) + a_B \cdot x
$$

where $a_A$ and $a_B$ are the lattice parameters of pure A and pure B, and $x$ is the atomic fraction of B.

**Worked Example:**
A Cu-Ni alloy contains 60 at% Cu and 40 at% Ni.
- $a_{Cu} = 0.3615$ nm (FCC)
- $a_{Ni} = 0.3524$ nm (FCC)

$$
\begin{aligned}
a_{SS} &= a_{Cu} \times 0.60 + a_{Ni} \times 0.40 \\
&= 0.3615 \times 0.60 + 0.3524 \times 0.40 \\
&= 0.2169 + 0.1410 \\
&= 0.3579 \text{ nm}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{a_{Cu_{0.6}Ni_{0.4}} = 0.3579 \text{ nm}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> Vegard's Law is **approximate** and works best when:
> - Both components have the **same crystal structure**.
> - The atomic size difference is **small** (< 5%).
> - The components form an **isomorphous** system.

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Q: Define a solid solution. Differentiate between substitutional and interstitial solid solutions with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A solid solution is a homogeneous single-phase crystalline solid in which two or more elements are combined such that the crystal structure of the host (solvent) is preserved while incorporating atoms of the solute.

| Type | Description | Example |
|------|-------------|---------|
| **Substitutional SS** | Solute atoms replace solvent atoms at lattice sites | Brass (Cu‚ÄìZn) |
| **Interstitial SS** | Solute atoms (small) occupy voids between solvent atoms | Austenite (Fe‚ÄìC) |

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q: State the four Hume-Rothery rules for substitutional solid solution formation.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The four Hume-Rothery rules for substitutional solid solutions are:
> 1. **Atomic size difference** should be **less than 15%** between solute and solvent.
> 2. Both elements must have the **same crystal structure** for extensive solubility.
> 3. Similar **electronegativity** to prevent compound formation.
> 4. The element of **higher valency** dissolves more in a metal of **lower valency** than vice versa.

**(3 marks)** ‚úì

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks] Explain the mechanism of formation of substitutional solid solutions. Discuss the factors affecting substitutional solubility with reference to the Cu‚ÄìNi and Cu‚ÄìZn systems.**

**Model Solution:**

**Mechanism of Substitutional Solid Solution Formation:**

In a substitutional solid solution, atoms of the solute (B) replace atoms of the solvent (A) at regular lattice positions. The process can be visualized as follows:

- During solidification or solid-state diffusion, solute atoms migrate to positions normally occupied by solvent atoms.
- The crystal structure of the solvent is **retained**.
- The lattice is locally distorted around each solute atom due to size mismatch.

**Factors Affecting Substitutional Solubility (Hume-Rothery Rules):**

**Factor 1: Atomic Size Factor**

The atomic radii of Cu, Ni, and Zn are:

$$
r_{Cu} = 0.128 \text{ nm}, \quad r_{Ni} = 0.125 \text{ nm}, \quad r_{Zn} = 0.133 \text{ nm}
$$

For Cu‚ÄìNi:

$$
\text{Radius difference} = \left\vert \frac{0.128 - 0.125}{0.128} \right\vert \times 100\% = 2.3\%
$$

Since 2.3% < 15% ‚úì

For Cu‚ÄìZn:

$$
\text{Radius difference} = \left\vert \frac{0.133 - 0.128}{0.128} \right\vert \times 100\% = 3.9\%
$$

Since 3.9% < 15% ‚úì

Both systems satisfy the size rule.

**Factor 2: Crystal Structure**

- Cu: FCC
- Ni: FCC ‚úì ‚Üí unlimited solubility (isomorphous).
- Zn: HCP ‚úó ‚Üí limited solubility (only ~39% Zn dissolves in Cu at 400¬∞C).

**Factor 3: Electronegativity**

- Cu: 1.90, Ni: 1.91, Zn: 1.65
- Cu‚ÄìNi: ŒîEN = 0.01 ‚Üí very similar ‚Üí extensive SS ‚úì
- Cu‚ÄìZn: ŒîEN = 0.25 ‚Üí moderate ‚Üí still forms SS, no compounds ‚úì

**Factor 4: Valency**

- Cu (valency 1), Ni (valency 2), Zn (valency 2)
- Both systems satisfy the valency rule.

**Conclusion:**

$$
\boxed{\text{Cu‚ÄìNi forms an isomorphous (continuous) solid solution. Cu‚ÄìZn (Brass) has limited solubility due to crystal structure difference.}}
$$

**Marks Distribution:** Definition (2M), Factors (4M), Example application (1M) = 7 marks ‚úì

**(b) [7 Marks] What are interstitial solid solutions? Explain the geometric conditions for an atom to occupy an interstitial site. Calculate the radius of the largest atom that can fit into an octahedral void of an FCC iron lattice.**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition of Interstitial Solid Solution:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> An interstitial solid solution is formed when small atoms (H, C, N, B, O) occupy the voids (interstices) between the larger solvent atoms in the crystal lattice.

**Geometric Conditions:**

The radius ratio for the solute atom ($r_i$) to solvent atom ($r_m$) is:

$$
\frac{r_i}{r_m} \leq 0.414 \text{ (for octahedral void in FCC)}
$$

$$
\frac{r_i}{r_m} \leq 0.225 \text{ (for tetrahedral void in FCC)}
$$

**Calculation for Octahedral Void in FCC Iron:**

**Given:**
- Lattice parameter of Œ≥-Fe (FCC austenite): $a = 0.365$ nm
- For FCC, atoms touch along the face diagonal:

$$
4r_{Fe} = a\sqrt{2}
$$

**Step 1: Find atomic radius of Fe**

$$
\begin{aligned}
r_{Fe} &= \frac{a\sqrt{2}}{4} \\
&= \frac{0.365 \times 1.414}{4} \\
&= \frac{0.5161}{4} \\
&= 0.129 \text{ nm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Apply octahedral void ratio**

$$
r_{void} = 0.414 \times r_{Fe} = 0.414 \times 0.129 = 0.0534 \text{ nm}
$$

**Step 3: Compare with actual C atom radius**

Atomic radius of C ‚âà **0.077 nm**.

Since $r_C > r_{void}$, the carbon atom does cause lattice distortion ‚Äî but it still fits (with strain), which is why **C in Œ≥-Fe is a stable interstitial SS** (austenite).

**Final Answer:**

$$
\boxed{r_{void} = 0.0534 \text{ nm, maximum size of atom that fits in octahedral void of Œ≥-Fe (FCC)}}
$$

**Marks Distribution:** Definition (2M), Geometric conditions (2M), Numerical calculation (3M) = 7 marks ‚úì

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks] Compare substitutional and interstitial solid solutions. State at least six points of comparison with examples.**

**Model Solution:**

| S.No. | Parameter | Substitutional SS | Interstitial SS |
|-------|-----------|-------------------|-----------------|
| 1 | **Position of solute** | Replaces solvent at lattice sites | Occupies interstitial voids |
| 2 | **Size of solute** | Comparable to solvent (< 15% diff.) | Much smaller (< 41% of solvent radius) |
| 3 | **Solubility** | May be unlimited (isomorphous) | Always limited (few atomic %) |
| 4 | **Hume-Rothery rules** | All 4 rules apply | Only size rule dominates |
| 5 | **Strengthening effect** | Moderate to high | Very high |
| 6 | **Common solutes** | Zn, Ni, Sn, Al, Mn | C, N, H, B, O |
| 7 | **Examples** | Brass (Cu-Zn), Bronze (Cu-Sn) | Austenite (Œ≥-Fe-C), Ti-N |
| 8 | **Effect on lattice** | Mild local strain | Significant strain |
| 9 | **Distortion type** | Compressive or tensile | Always tensile (small atom in big void) |

**Conclusion:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Both types are single-phase solid solutions retaining the host crystal structure, but they differ fundamentally in **mechanism, size criterion, solubility limits, and effect on properties**.

**Marks Distribution:** Tabular comparison with 6 points (6M) + Examples (1M) = 7 marks ‚úì

---

**(b) [7 Marks] With neat sketches, explain the formation of the following: (i) Substitutional solid solution in Cu‚ÄìZn system, (ii) Interstitial solid solution in Fe‚ÄìC (austenite) system. Discuss the effect on properties.**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Cu‚ÄìZn Substitutional Solid Solution (Brass):**

In brass, Zn atoms (radius 0.133 nm) replace Cu atoms (radius 0.128 nm) on the FCC lattice sites of copper. The crystal structure of Cu (FCC) is retained, but lattice parameter changes slightly with composition. As Zn content increases:

- Lattice parameter of brass changes.
- Strength and hardness increase.
- Ductility is maintained.
- Color shifts from reddish to golden yellow.

**(ii) Fe‚ÄìC Interstitial Solid Solution (Austenite):**

In austenite, very small carbon atoms (radius ~0.077 nm) occupy the **octahedral voids** of the Œ≥-Fe (FCC) lattice. The maximum solubility of C in Œ≥-Fe is **2.14 at% at 1147¬∞C**. Effects on properties:

- **Strength and hardness increase dramatically** (solid solution strengthening).
- **Ductility decreases** (lattice becomes strained).
- **Electrical resistivity increases** (electron scattering from strain fields).
- Forms the basis of all **carbon steels and cast irons**.

**Effect on Properties (Common to Both):**

| Property | Effect |
|----------|--------|
| **Strength** | Increases (solid solution strengthening) |
| **Hardness** | Increases |
| **Ductility** | Generally decreases |
| **Electrical conductivity** | Decreases (electron scattering) |
| **Density** | Slight change (depends on size difference) |
| **Corrosion resistance** | Variable |

**Conclusion:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Both substitutional (Brass) and interstitial (Austenite) solid solutions are vital engineering materials. The mechanism of solute incorporation dictates the extent of property modification.

**Marks Distribution:** Description of each (3M each) + Effect on properties (1M) = 7 marks ‚úì

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing Substitutional with Interstitial:** Students often swap examples. Remember:
>    - Substitutional ‚Üí metallic solutes in metallic solvents (Cu‚ÄìZn, Cu‚ÄìNi).
>    - Interstitial ‚Üí small non-metallic solutes (C, N, H) in transition metals.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting the 15% Rule:** Many students state "atomic size should be similar" without quantifying. **Always state < 15%**.
> 
> 3. **Ignoring Crystal Structure Rule:** Cu and Ni are both FCC (unlimited SS), but Cu and Zn differ in structure (limited SS). This is a frequent exam point.
> 
> 4. **Octahedral vs. Tetrahedral Void Confusion:** In FCC, octahedral voids are **larger** (0.414r); in BCC, **tetrahedral voids are larger** (0.291r). Mix-up is a guaranteed mark loss.
> 
> 5. **Not Drawing Diagrams:** KTU examiners **expect labeled diagrams** for solid solution types. Skipping diagrams can cost 1-2 marks easily.
> 
> 6. **Mixing Solid Solutions with Compounds:** Solid solutions retain host crystal structure; intermetallic compounds have **distinct crystal structures** (e.g., CuZn, Cu‚ÇÖZn‚Çà).
> 
> 7. **Misapplying Vegard's Law:** Vegard's Law is for **continuous substitutional SS** with **same crystal structure**. Don't apply to limited systems.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- ‚úÖ **Solid Solution:** Homogeneous single-phase crystalline solid where solute atoms are incorporated into the host lattice without changing its crystal structure.

- ‚úÖ **Two Types:** Substitutional (solute replaces solvent) and Interstitial (solute occupies voids).

- ‚úÖ **Hume-Rothery Rules (Substitutional):** Size < 15%, same structure, similar electronegativity, valency rule (higher valency dissolves more in lower).

- ‚úÖ **Interstitial Size Rule:** $r_{solute} / r_{solvent} < 0.41$ for octahedral void in FCC.

- ‚úÖ **FCC Octahedral Voids:** 4 per unit cell, radius = 0.414r; **Tetrahedral voids:** 8 per unit cell, radius = 0.225r.

- ‚úÖ **BCC Octahedral Voids:** 3 per unit cell, radius = 0.155r; **Tetrahedral voids:** 12 per unit cell, radius = 0.291r.

- ‚úÖ **Cu‚ÄìNi:** Isomorphous (unlimited SS) ‚Äî both FCC, similar size, EN, valency.

- ‚úÖ **Cu‚ÄìZn:** Limited SS (Brass) ‚Äî different crystal structure (Cu FCC, Zn HCP) restricts solubility.

- ‚úÖ **Œ≥-Fe (Austenite):** FCC iron with C in octahedral voids; max solubility 2.14 at% at 1147¬∞C.

- ‚úÖ **Œ±-Fe (Ferrite):** BCC iron with C in tetrahedral voids; max solubility 0.022 at% at 727¬∞C (much lower!).

- ‚úÖ **Vegard's Law:** $a_{SS} = a_A(1-x) + a_B \cdot x$ ‚Äî linear variation of lattice parameter with composition.

- ‚úÖ **Effect on Properties:** Strength ‚Üë, Hardness ‚Üë, Ductility ‚Üì, Electrical conductivity ‚Üì, Corrosion resistance varies.

- ‚úÖ **Industrial Examples:** Brass, Bronze, Monel, Austenitic Stainless Steel, Carbon Steel, Cast Iron.

- ‚úÖ **Ordered vs. Random:** Random is most common; ordered SS (e.g., Cu‚ÇÉAu) forms below critical temperature.

- ‚úÖ **Solid Solution Strengthening:** $\Delta\sigma \propto G\varepsilon^{3/2}c^{1/2}$ ‚Äî Fleischer equation.

- ‚úÖ **Engineering Application:** Hume-Rothery rules are used in **alloy design** to predict phase formation, solubility, and mechanical behavior in new alloy systems.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Substitutional Solid Solution ‚Äî Detailed Mechanism

### 2.1.1 Definition (Refined)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **substitutional solid solution** is a crystalline solid solution in which the solute atoms occupy positions in the crystal lattice that are normally occupied by atoms of the solvent, i.e., they substitute for the host atoms at the lattice points.

### 2.1.2 Visual Mechanism

In a substitutional solid solution:
- The solute atom (B) **replaces** a solvent atom (A) at a lattice site.
- The overall number of atoms per unit cell remains the same.
- The crystal structure of the host (e.g., FCC, BCC, HCP) is preserved.

### 2.1.3 Types of Substitutional Solid Solutions

| Type | Description | Example |
|------|-------------|---------|
| **Random Substitutional** | Solute atoms occupy lattice sites randomly with no order. Most common type. | Cu‚ÄìNi (high temperature) |
| **Ordered Substitutional** | Solute atoms occupy specific, repeating positions below a critical temperature (Curie-like ordering). | Cu‚ÄìAu (Cu‚ÇÉAu at low temperature) |
| **Continuous (Isomorphous)** | Complete solubility across entire composition range. | Cu‚ÄìNi, Ag‚ÄìAu, Ge‚ÄìSi |

### 2.1.4 Hume-Rothery Rules for Substitutional Solubility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 4 Hume-Rothery Rules for Substitutional Solid Solutions:**

**Rule 1: Atomic Size Factor (Radius Difference Rule)**
- The atomic radii of solute and solvent must be within **15%** of each other.
- If the difference exceeds 15%, lattice strain becomes too high, and solubility is severely restricted.
- Empirical rule: 
$$\text{Radius Ratio} = \left| \frac{r_{solute} - r_{solvent}}{r_{solvent}} \right| \times 100\% \leq 15\%$$

**Rule 2: Crystal Structure Rule**
- Solvent and solute must have the **same crystal structure** for extensive solid solubility.
- Example: Cu (FCC) and Ni (FCC) ‚Üí unlimited solubility.
- Counter-example: Cu (FCC) and Sn (BCT/tetragonal) ‚Üí very limited solubility.

**Rule 3: Electronegativity Rule**
- The two elements should have similar **electronegativity values**.
- If electronegativity difference is large, the elements tend to form **intermetallic compounds** rather than solid solutions.
- Similar electronegativity ‚Üí metallic bonding character is preserved.

**Rule 4: Valency Rule (Relative Valence Effect)**
- A metal of **higher valency** has greater solubility in a metal of **lower valency** than vice versa.
- Example: Al (valency 3) dissolves significantly in Cu (valency 1), but Cu has very limited solubility in Al.

## 2.2 Interstitial Solid Solution ‚Äî Detailed Mechanism

### 2.2.1 Definition (Refined)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> An **interstitial solid solution** is a crystalline solid solution in which the solute atoms occupy the **interstitial positions (voids)** between the solvent atoms in the lattice.

### 2.2.2 Size Criterion

> [!NOTE]
> For interstitial solid solubility, the radius ratio must be:
> $$\frac{r_{solute}}{r_{solvent}} < 0.41 \quad \text{(or typically } < 0.59 \text{ in some cases)}$$
> 
> Only **small atoms** qualify: H, B, C, N, O.

### 2.2.3 Common Interstitial Sites in Metallic Lattices

| Lattice | Octahedral Void Radius | Tetrahedral Void Radius |
|---------|------------------------|-------------------------|
| **FCC** | $0.414 \times r_{metal}$ | $0.225 \times r_{metal}$ |
| **BCC** | $0.155 \times r_{metal}$ | $0.291 \times r_{metal}$ |
| **HCP** | $0.414 \times r_{metal}$ | $0.225 \times r_{metal}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** In FCC and HCP, **octahedral voids are larger** (radius = 0.414r) than tetrahedral voids (0.225r). In **BCC**, the situation is **reversed** ‚Äî the tetrahedral void is actually larger (0.291r) than the octahedral void (0.155r) due to the open BCC structure.

### 2.2.4 Common Examples of Interstitial Solid Solutions

| System | Solvent (Host) | Solute | Industrial Material |
|--------|----------------|--------|---------------------|
| **Fe‚ÄìC (Austenite)** | Œ≥-Fe (FCC) | C (Carbon) | Carbon Steel, Cast Iron |
| **Fe‚ÄìC (Ferrite)** | Œ±-Fe (BCC) | C (Carbon) | Low-Carbon Steel |
| **Fe‚ÄìN** | Œ±-Fe (BCC) | N (Nitrogen) | Nitrided Steels |
| **Ti‚ÄìO, Ti‚ÄìN** | Œ±-Ti (HCP) | O, N | Titanium Alloys |
| **Pd‚ÄìH** | Pd (FCC) | H (Hydrogen) | Hydrogen Storage |

## 2.3 Comprehensive Comparison Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Comparison: Substitutional vs. Interstitial**

| Property | Substitutional SS | Interstitial SS |
|----------|------------------|-----------------|
| **Solute atom position** | Replaces solvent at lattice sites | Occupies interstitial voids |
| **Solute atom size** | Comparable to solvent (< 15% difference) | Much smaller than solvent (< 0.41r) |
| **Solubility extent** | Can be unlimited (isomorphous) | Always limited |
| **Governing rules** | All 4 Hume-Rothery rules | Size rule dominant |
| **Effect on lattice** | Local distortion around solute | Significant lattice strain |
| **Strengthening effect** | Moderate to high | Very high (e.g., C in Fe) |
| **Typical solutes** | Zn, Ni, Sn, Al, Mn | C, N, H, B, O |
| **Examples** | Brass (Cu‚ÄìZn), Bronze (Cu‚ÄìSn), Cu‚ÄìNi | Austenite (Œ≥-Fe-C), Ferrite (Œ±-Fe-C) |
| **Max solubility** | Up to 100% in isomorphous systems | Few atomic % (e.g., C in Œ≥-Fe: 2.14%) |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Equations for Solid Solutions**

| Formula/Rule | Expression | Application |
|--------------|-----------|-------------|
| **Hume-Rothery Size Rule** | $\left\vert \frac{r_{solute} - r_{solvent}}{r_{solvent}} \right\vert \times 100\% \leq 15\%$ | Determines substitutional solubility limit |
| **Interstitial Size Criterion** | $\frac{r_{solute}}{r_{solvent}} < 0.41$ | Determines if interstitial SS forms |
| **FCC Octahedral Void Radius** | $r_{void} = 0.414 \times r_{metal}$ | Calculate max interstitial atom size |
| **FCC Tetrahedral Void Radius** | $r_{void} = 0.225 \times r_{metal}$ | Calculate max interstitial atom size |
| **BCC Octahedral Void Radius** | $r_{void} = 0.155 \times r_{metal}$ | Calculate max interstitial atom size |
| **BCC Tetrahedral Void Radius** | $r_{void} = 0.291 \times r_{metal}$ | Calculate max interstitial atom size |
| **Number of Octahedral Voids (FCC)** | $N_{octa} = 4$ per unit cell | Total voids = number of atoms in FCC |
| **Number of Tetrahedral Voids (FCC)** | $N_{tetra} = 8$ per unit cell | Twice the number of atoms in FCC |
| **Vegard's Law (lattice parameter)** | $a_{SS} = a_1(1-x) + a_2 \cdot x$ | Calculate lattice parameter of SS |
| **Solid Solution Strengthening** | $\Delta \sigma_{ss} \propto G \cdot \varepsilon^{3/2} \cdot c^{1/2}$ | Strength increment due to SS |
| **Max Solubility (Interstitial)** | Typically $\leq 2-3$ at% | Empirical for C, N in metals |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** These rules are the foundation of **alloy design** in metallurgy. Engineers use them to predict whether a new alloy composition will form a stable, useful solid solution or will form brittle intermetallic compounds.
