# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Radius Ratio for Octahedral Void in FCC Lattice

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** Derive the radius ratio $\frac{r_{void}}{r_{metal}} = 0.414$ for an octahedral void in an FCC lattice.

**Step 1: Geometric Setup**
Consider the FCC unit cell. An octahedral void is located at the **center of the unit cell** (at coordinates $\left(\frac{1}{2}, \frac{1}{2}, \frac{1}{2}\right)$) and at the **edge centers**.

The void is surrounded by **6 atoms** at the face centers of the 6 faces adjacent to the void, forming an octahedron.

**Step 2: Coordination of the Void**
In an octahedral void:
- Number of atoms around the void = **6** (octahedral coordination).
- The void center is equidistant from all 6 surrounding atoms.

**Step 3: Distance Calculation**
For the octahedral void at the cube center $\left(\frac{a}{2}, \frac{a}{2}, \frac{a}{2}\right)$:
- The 6 face-centered atoms are at positions like $\left(\frac{a}{2}, \frac{a}{2}, 0\right)$, $\left(\frac{a}{2}, \frac{a}{2}, a\right)$, $\left(\frac{a}{2}, 0, \frac{a}{2}\right)$, $\left(\frac{a}{2}, a, \frac{a}{2}\right)$, $\left(0, \frac{a}{2}, \frac{a}{2}\right)$, $\left(a, \frac{a}{2}, \frac{a}{2}\right)$.

Distance from void center to face center:

$$
\begin{aligned}
d &= \sqrt{\left(\frac{a}{2} - \frac{a}{2}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{a}{2} - \frac{a}{2}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{a}{2} - 0\right)^2} \\
&= \sqrt{0 + 0 + \left(\frac{a}{2}\right)^2} \\
&= \frac{a}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Expressing in Terms of Atomic Radius**
In FCC, atoms touch along the **face diagonal**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
4 r_{metal} &= a\sqrt{2} \\
a &= \frac{4 r_{metal}}{\sqrt{2}} = 2\sqrt{2} \cdot r_{metal}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Geometric Constraint (Atoms Touch the Void)**
The void radius $r_{void}$ and the metal atom radius $r_{metal}$ must satisfy:

$$
r_{metal} + r_{void} = \frac{a}{2}
$$

**Step 6: Solving for the Ratio**

$$
\begin{aligned}
r_{void} &= \frac{a}{2} - r_{metal} \\
&= \frac{2\sqrt{2} \cdot r_{metal}}{2} - r_{metal} \\
&= \sqrt{2} \cdot r_{metal} - r_{metal} \\
&= r_{metal} (\sqrt{2} - 1) \\
&= r_{metal} (1.414 - 1) \\
&= 0.414 \cdot r_{metal}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7: Final Result**

$$
\boxed{\frac{r_{void}}{r_{metal}} = 0.414}
$$

This means a solute atom of radius up to **41.4% of the metal atom radius** can fit into an octahedral void of an FCC lattice without distorting the lattice.

## 3.2 Derivation: Radius Ratio for Tetrahedral Void in FCC Lattice

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** Derive the radius ratio $\frac{r_{void}}{r_{metal}} = 0.225$ for a tetrahedral void in an FCC lattice.

**Step 1: Geometric Setup**
A tetrahedral void in FCC is located at positions like $\left(\frac{1}{4}, \frac{1}{4}, \frac{1}{4}\right)$, surrounded by **4 atoms** forming a tetrahedron.

**Step 2: Tetrahedral Coordination**
Number of atoms around the void = **4** (tetrahedral coordination).

**Step 3: Distance from Void to Nearest Atom**
The distance from the tetrahedral void at $\left(\frac{a}{4}, \frac{a}{4}, \frac{a}{4}\right)$ to a face-centered atom at $\left(\frac{a}{2}, \frac{a}{2}, 0\right)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
d &= \sqrt{\left(\frac{a}{4} - \frac{a}{2}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{a}{4} - \frac{a}{2}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{a}{4} - 0\right)^2} \\
&= \sqrt{\left(-\frac{a}{4}\right)^2 + \left(-\frac{a}{4}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{a}{4}\right)^2} \\
&= \sqrt{\frac{a^2}{16} + \frac{a^2}{16} + \frac{a^2}{16}} \\
&= \sqrt{\frac{3a^2}{16}} = \frac{a\sqrt{3}}{4}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Constraint Equation**

$$
r_{metal} + r_{void} = \frac{a\sqrt{3}}{4}
$$

**Step 5: Substituting $a = 2\sqrt{2} \cdot r_{metal}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
r_{void} &= \frac{2\sqrt{2} \cdot r_{metal} \cdot \sqrt{3}}{4} - r_{metal} \\
&= \frac{\sqrt{6}}{2} \cdot r_{metal} - r_{metal} \\
&= r_{metal} \left(\frac{\sqrt{6}}{2} - 1\right) \\
&= r_{metal} (1.2247 - 1) \\
&= 0.225 \cdot r_{metal}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Final Result**

$$
\boxed{\frac{r_{void}}{r_{metal}} = 0.225}
$$

## 3.3 Worked Example: Checking Cu‚ÄìZn Brass for Substitutional Solid Solubility

> [!NOTE]
> **Question:** Using Hume-Rothery rules, determine if Cu and Zn can form an extensive substitutional solid solution.

**Given Data:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
r_{Cu} &= 0.128 \text{ nm} \\
r_{Zn} &= 0.133 \text{ nm} \\
\text{Structure of Cu} &= \text{FCC} \\
\text{Structure of Zn} &= \text{HCP} \\
\text{Electronegativity Cu} &= 1.90 \\
\text{Electronegativity Zn} &= 1.65 \\
\text{Valency Cu} &= +1 \\
\text{Valency Zn} &= +2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Atomic Size Rule**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Radius difference} &= \left\vert \frac{r_{Zn} - r_{Cu}}{r_{Cu}} \right\vert \times 100\% \\
&= \left\vert \frac{0.133 - 0.128}{0.128} \right\vert \times 100\% \\
&= \left\vert \frac{0.005}{0.128} \right\vert \times 100\% \\
&= 3.9\%
\end{aligned}
$$

Since **3.9% < 15%** ‚úì ‚Äî **Rule satisfied**.

**Step 2: Crystal Structure Rule**
- Cu: FCC
- Zn: HCP
- **Rule NOT satisfied** strictly, but the size effect dominates, allowing limited solubility (up to ~39% Zn in Cu).

**Step 3: Electronegativity Rule**

$$
\Delta EN = 1.90 - 1.65 = 0.25
$$

Small difference ‚Üí no strong tendency to form intermetallic compounds. ‚úì

**Step 4: Valency Rule**
- Zn (valency +2) is **higher** than Cu (valency +1).
- Higher valency metal (Zn) has **greater solubility in lower valency metal (Cu)** than vice versa. ‚úì

**Step 5: Conclusion**

$$
\boxed{\text{Cu and Zn form a substitutional solid solution (Brass) with limited solubility due to crystal structure difference.}}
$$

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix ‚Äî When Does Each Type Form?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineer's Decision Tree for Solid Solution Classification**

| Condition | Substitutional SS | Interstitial SS |
|-----------|-------------------|-----------------|
| $r_{solute} \approx r_{solvent}$ (within 15%) | ‚úì | ‚úó |
| $r_{solute} << r_{solvent}$ (ratio < 0.41) | ‚úó | ‚úì |
| Same crystal structure preferred | ‚úì | Not required |
| Solute is small (C, N, H, B, O) | ‚úó | ‚úì |
| Solute is metallic (Zn, Ni, Al, Sn) | ‚úì | ‚úó |
| Solubility is high (> 10 at%) | Likely | Unlikely |
| Examples | Brass, Bronze, Cu-Ni, Monel | Fe-C, Fe-N, Pd-H, Ti-O |

## 3.5 Vegard's Law ‚Äî Lattice Parameter of a Solid Solution

> [!NOTE]
> **Vegard's Law:** For a continuous substitutional solid solution $A_{1-x}B_x$, the lattice parameter varies **linearly** with composition:

$$
a_{SS} = a_A(1-x) + a_B \cdot x
$$

where $a_A$ and $a_B$ are the lattice parameters of pure A and pure B, and $x$ is the atomic fraction of B.

**Worked Example:**
A Cu-Ni alloy contains 60 at% Cu and 40 at% Ni.
- $a_{Cu} = 0.3615$ nm (FCC)
- $a_{Ni} = 0.3524$ nm (FCC)

$$
\begin{aligned}
a_{SS} &= a_{Cu} \times 0.60 + a_{Ni} \times 0.40 \\
&= 0.3615 \times 0.60 + 0.3524 \times 0.40 \\
&= 0.2169 + 0.1410 \\
&= 0.3579 \text{ nm}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{a_{Cu_{0.6}Ni_{0.4}} = 0.3579 \text{ nm}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> Vegard's Law is **approximate** and works best when:
> - Both components have the **same crystal structure**.
> - The atomic size difference is **small** (< 5%).
> - The components form an **isomorphous** system.

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Q: Define a solid solution. Differentiate between substitutional and interstitial solid solutions with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A solid solution is a homogeneous single-phase crystalline solid in which two or more elements are combined such that the crystal structure of the host (solvent) is preserved while incorporating atoms of the solute.

| Type | Description | Example |
|------|-------------|---------|
| **Substitutional SS** | Solute atoms replace solvent atoms at lattice sites | Brass (Cu‚ÄìZn) |
| **Interstitial SS** | Solute atoms (small) occupy voids between solvent atoms | Austenite (Fe‚ÄìC) |

**(3 marks)** ‚úì

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q: State the four Hume-Rothery rules for substitutional solid solution formation.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The four Hume-Rothery rules for substitutional solid solutions are:
> 1. **Atomic size difference** should be **less than 15%** between solute and solvent.
> 2. Both elements must have the **same crystal structure** for extensive solubility.
> 3. Similar **electronegativity** to prevent compound formation.
> 4. The element of **higher valency** dissolves more in a metal of **lower valency** than vice versa.

**(3 marks)** ‚úì

---

## 5.2 Part B: Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU Part B questions carry **14 marks** with internal choice. Each has two sub-parts of **7 marks** each.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks] Explain the mechanism of formation of substitutional solid solutions. Discuss the factors affecting substitutional solubility with reference to the Cu‚ÄìNi and Cu‚ÄìZn systems.**

**Model Solution:**

**Mechanism of Substitutional Solid Solution Formation:**

In a substitutional solid solution, atoms of the solute (B) replace atoms of the solvent (A) at regular lattice positions. The process can be visualized as follows:

- During solidification or solid-state diffusion, solute atoms migrate to positions normally occupied by solvent atoms.
- The crystal structure of the solvent is **retained**.
- The lattice is locally distorted around each solute atom due to size mismatch.

**Factors Affecting Substitutional Solubility (Hume-Rothery Rules):**

**Factor 1: Atomic Size Factor**

The atomic radii of Cu, Ni, and Zn are:

$$
r_{Cu} = 0.128 \text{ nm}, \quad r_{Ni} = 0.125 \text{ nm}, \quad r_{Zn} = 0.133 \text{ nm}
$$

For Cu‚ÄìNi:

$$
\text{Radius difference} = \left\vert \frac{0.128 - 0.125}{0.128} \right\vert \times 100\% = 2.3\%
$$

Since 2.3% < 15% ‚úì

For Cu‚ÄìZn:

$$
\text{Radius difference} = \left\vert \frac{0.133 - 0.128}{0.128} \right\vert \times 100\% = 3.9\%
$$

Since 3.9% < 15% ‚úì

Both systems satisfy the size rule.

**Factor 2: Crystal Structure**

- Cu: FCC
- Ni: FCC ‚úì ‚Üí unlimited solubility (isomorphous).
- Zn: HCP ‚úó ‚Üí limited solubility (only ~39% Zn dissolves in Cu at 400¬∞C).

**Factor 3: Electronegativity**

- Cu: 1.90, Ni: 1.91, Zn: 1.65
- Cu‚ÄìNi: ŒîEN = 0.01 ‚Üí very similar ‚Üí extensive SS ‚úì
- Cu‚ÄìZn: ŒîEN = 0.25 ‚Üí moderate ‚Üí still forms SS, no compounds ‚úì

**Factor 4: Valency**

- Cu (valency 1), Ni (valency 2), Zn (valency 2)
- Both systems satisfy the valency rule.

**Conclusion:**

$$
\boxed{\text{Cu‚ÄìNi forms an isomorphous (continuous) solid solution. Cu‚ÄìZn (Brass) has limited solubility due to crystal structure difference.}}
$$

**Marks Distribution:** Definition (2M), Factors (4M), Example application (1M) = 7 marks ‚úì

---

**(b) [7 Marks] What are interstitial solid solutions? Explain the geometric conditions for an atom to occupy an interstitial site. Calculate the radius of the largest atom that can fit into an octahedral void of an FCC iron lattice.**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition of Interstitial Solid Solution:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> An interstitial solid solution is formed when small atoms (H, C, N, B, O) occupy the voids (interstices) between the larger solvent atoms in the crystal lattice.

**Geometric Conditions:**

The radius ratio for the solute atom ($r_i$) to solvent atom ($r_m$) is:

$$
\frac{r_i}{r_m} \leq 0.414 \text{ (for octahedral void in FCC)}
$$

$$
\frac{r_i}{r_m} \leq 0.225 \text{ (for tetrahedral void in FCC)}
$$

**Calculation for Octahedral Void in FCC Iron:**

**Given:**
- Lattice parameter of Œ≥-Fe (FCC austenite): $a = 0.365$ nm
- For FCC, atoms touch along the face diagonal:

$$
4r_{Fe} = a\sqrt{2}
$$

**Step 1: Find atomic radius of Fe**

$$
\begin{aligned}
r_{Fe} &= \frac{a\sqrt{2}}{4} \\
&= \frac{0.365 \times 1.414}{4} \\
&= \frac{0.5161}{4} \\
&= 0.129 \text{ nm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Apply octahedral void ratio**

$$
r_{void} = 0.414 \times r_{Fe} = 0.414 \times 0.129 = 0.0534 \text{ nm}
$$

**Step 3: Compare with actual C atom radius**

Atomic radius of C ‚âà **0.077 nm**.

Since $r_C > r_{void}$, the carbon atom does cause lattice distortion ‚Äî but it still fits (with strain), which is why **C in Œ≥-Fe is a stable interstitial SS** (austenite).

**Final Answer:**

$$
\boxed{r_{void} = 0.0534 \text{ nm, maximum size of atom that fits in octahedral void of Œ≥-Fe (FCC)}}
$$

**Marks Distribution:** Definition (2M), Geometric conditions (2M), Numerical calculation (3M) = 7 marks ‚úì

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks] Compare substitutional and interstitial solid solutions. State at least six points of comparison with examples.**

**Model Solution:**

| S.No. | Parameter | Substitutional SS | Interstitial SS |
|-------|-----------|-------------------|-----------------|
| 1 | **Position of solute** | Replaces solvent at lattice sites | Occupies interstitial voids |
| 2 | **Size of solute** | Comparable to solvent (< 15% diff.) | Much smaller (< 41% of solvent radius) |
| 3 | **Solubility** | May be unlimited (isomorphous) | Always limited (few atomic %) |
| 4 | **Hume-Rothery rules** | All 4 rules apply | Only size rule dominates |
| 5 | **Strengthening effect** | Moderate to high | Very high |
| 6 | **Common solutes** | Zn, Ni, Sn, Al, Mn | C, N, H, B, O |
| 7 | **Examples** | Brass (Cu-Zn), Bronze (Cu-Sn) | Austenite (Œ≥-Fe-C), Ti-N |
| 8 | **Effect on lattice** | Mild local strain | Significant strain |
| 9 | **Distortion type** | Compressive or tensile | Always tensile (small atom in big void) |

**Conclusion:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Both types are single-phase solid solutions retaining the host crystal structure, but they differ fundamentally in **mechanism, size criterion, solubility limits, and effect on properties**.

**Marks Distribution:** Tabular comparison with 6 points (6M) + Examples (1M) = 7 marks ‚úì

---

**(b) [7 Marks] With neat sketches, explain the formation of the following: (i) Substitutional solid solution in Cu‚ÄìZn system, (ii) Interstitial solid solution in Fe‚ÄìC (austenite) system. Discuss the effect on properties.**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Cu‚ÄìZn Substitutional Solid Solution (Brass):**

In brass, Zn atoms (radius 0.133 nm) replace Cu atoms (radius 0.128 nm) on the FCC lattice sites of copper. The crystal structure of Cu (FCC) is retained, but lattice parameter changes slightly with composition. As Zn content increases:

- Lattice parameter of brass changes.
- Strength and hardness increase.
- Ductility is maintained.
- Color shifts from reddish to golden yellow.

**(ii) Fe‚ÄìC Interstitial Solid Solution (Austenite):**

In austenite, very small carbon atoms (radius ~0.077 nm) occupy the **octahedral voids** of the Œ≥-Fe (FCC) lattice. The maximum solubility of C in Œ≥-Fe is **2.14 at% at 1147¬∞C**. Effects on properties:

- **Strength and hardness increase dramatically** (solid solution strengthening).
- **Ductility decreases** (lattice becomes strained).
- **Electrical resistivity increases** (electron scattering from strain fields).
- Forms the basis of all **carbon steels and cast irons**.

**Effect on Properties (Common to Both):**

| Property | Effect |
|----------|--------|
| **Strength** | Increases (solid solution strengthening) |
| **Hardness** | Increases |
| **Ductility** | Generally decreases |
| **Electrical conductivity** | Decreases (electron scattering) |
| **Density** | Slight change (depends on size difference) |
| **Corrosion resistance** | Variable |

**Conclusion:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Both substitutional (Brass) and interstitial (Austenite) solid solutions are vital engineering materials. The mechanism of solute incorporation dictates the extent of property modification.

**Marks Distribution:** Description of each (3M each) + Effect on properties (1M) = 7 marks ‚úì

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing Substitutional with Interstitial:** Students often swap examples. Remember:
>    - Substitutional ‚Üí metallic solutes in metallic solvents (Cu‚ÄìZn, Cu‚ÄìNi).
>    - Interstitial ‚Üí small non-metallic solutes (C, N, H) in transition metals.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting the 15% Rule:** Many students state "atomic size should be similar" without quantifying. **Always state < 15%**.
> 
> 3. **Ignoring Crystal Structure Rule:** Cu and Ni are both FCC (unlimited SS), but Cu and Zn differ in structure (limited SS). This is a frequent exam point.
> 
> 4. **Octahedral vs. Tetrahedral Void Confusion:** In FCC, octahedral voids are **larger** (0.414r); in BCC, **tetrahedral voids are larger** (0.291r). Mix-up is a guaranteed mark loss.
> 
> 5. **Not Drawing Diagrams:** KTU examiners **expect labeled diagrams** for solid solution types. Skipping diagrams can cost 1-2 marks easily.
> 
> 6. **Mixing Solid Solutions with Compounds:** Solid solutions retain host crystal structure; intermetallic compounds have **distinct crystal structures** (e.g., CuZn, Cu‚ÇÖZn‚Çà).
> 
> 7. **Misapplying Vegard's Law:** Vegard's Law is for **continuous substitutional SS** with **same crystal structure**. Don't apply to limited systems.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- ‚úÖ **Solid Solution:** Homogeneous single-phase crystalline solid where solute atoms are incorporated into the host lattice without changing its crystal structure.

- ‚úÖ **Two Types:** Substitutional (solute replaces solvent) and Interstitial (solute occupies voids).

- ‚úÖ **Hume-Rothery Rules (Substitutional):** Size < 15%, same structure, similar electronegativity, valency rule (higher valency dissolves more in lower).

- ‚úÖ **Interstitial Size Rule:** $r_{solute} / r_{solvent} < 0.41$ for octahedral void in FCC.

- ‚úÖ **FCC Octahedral Voids:** 4 per unit cell, radius = 0.414r; **Tetrahedral voids:** 8 per unit cell, radius = 0.225r.

- ‚úÖ **BCC Octahedral Voids:** 3 per unit cell, radius = 0.155r; **Tetrahedral voids:** 12 per unit cell, radius = 0.291r.

- ‚úÖ **Cu‚ÄìNi:** Isomorphous (unlimited SS) ‚Äî both FCC, similar size, EN, valency.

- ‚úÖ **Cu‚ÄìZn:** Limited SS (Brass) ‚Äî different crystal structure (Cu FCC, Zn HCP) restricts solubility.

- ‚úÖ **Œ≥-Fe (Austenite):** FCC iron with C in octahedral voids; max solubility 2.14 at% at 1147¬∞C.

- ‚úÖ **Œ±-Fe (Ferrite):** BCC iron with C in tetrahedral voids; max solubility 0.022 at% at 727¬∞C (much lower!).

- ‚úÖ **Vegard's Law:** $a_{SS} = a_A(1-x) + a_B \cdot x$ ‚Äî linear variation of lattice parameter with composition.

- ‚úÖ **Effect on Properties:** Strength ‚Üë, Hardness ‚Üë, Ductility ‚Üì, Electrical conductivity ‚Üì, Corrosion resistance varies.

- ‚úÖ **Industrial Examples:** Brass, Bronze, Monel, Austenitic Stainless Steel, Carbon Steel, Cast Iron.

- ‚úÖ **Ordered vs. Random:** Random is most common; ordered SS (e.g., Cu‚ÇÉAu) forms below critical temperature.

- ‚úÖ **Solid Solution Strengthening:** $\Delta\sigma \propto G\varepsilon^{3/2}c^{1/2}$ ‚Äî Fleischer equation.

- ‚úÖ **Engineering Application:** Hume-Rothery rules are used in **alloy design** to predict phase formation, solubility, and mechanical behavior in new alloy systems.

---

> **End of Module 3 Notes ‚Äî Solid Solutions: Substitutional and Interstitial**
> 
> *Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme B.Tech PBAUT304 Syllabus and Bloom's Taxonomy Cognitive Levels (L1-L4).*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Phase diagrams: isomorphous, eutectic, eutectoid, peritectic, and peritectoid reactions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAUT304 - MATERIAL SCIENCE AND METALLURGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Phase diagrams: isomorphous, eutectic, eutectoid, peritectic, and peritectoid reactions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Phase Diagram?

A **phase diagram** (also called an **equilibrium diagram** or **constitution diagram**) is a graphical map that shows the stable phases of a material system as a function of **temperature**, **pressure**, and **composition**. It tells an engineer exactly what phases will coexist, in what proportions, and at what temperatures ‚Äî the foundation of alloy design, casting, heat treatment, and welding in metallurgy.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Exact Terminology):**
> A phase diagram is a graphical representation of the *thermodynamic equilibrium* states of a material system, plotted typically as a function of temperature (vertical axis) versus composition (horizontal axis) at constant atmospheric pressure (usually **1 atm**). It demarcates regions of stability for individual phases, two-phase fields, and invariant reactions.

### The Two Principal Variables
- **Temperature (T)** ‚Üí Plotted on the **Y-axis** (vertical).
- **Composition (wt% or at% of the second component)** ‚Üí Plotted on the **X-axis** (horizontal).

### What is a "Phase"?
A *phase* is a physically and chemically homogeneous region of matter with a **distinct crystal structure** and **uniform properties** separated from other phases by a definite *interphase boundary*.
- Example: Water (liquid), ice (solid), and water vapour (gas) are three distinct phases of the same chemical substance H‚ÇÇO.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ‚Äî The "Phase Diagram as a Recipe Map"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition for a First-Time Learner:**
> Imagine you are a chef. You have two ingredients: **Sugar** and **Water**. You want to know ‚Äî at any given room temperature and any mixing ratio ‚Äî will the mixture be a *liquid syrup*, a *slushy mix*, or *solid rock candy*?
> A phase diagram answers exactly this question for **metals and alloys**:
> - "What will the Cu‚ÄìNi alloy look like at 800 ¬∞C if it contains 40 wt% Ni?" 
> - "Will steel with 0.8 % carbon at 727 ¬∞C be solid or partially liquid?"
> 
> The diagram is your **engineer's compass** for selecting the right composition and processing temperature to achieve a desired microstructure.

---

## 1.3 The Gibbs Phase Rule (Governing Equation)

The number of degrees of freedom (F) in a system is given by:

$$F = C - P + 2$$

Where:
- **F** = Number of degrees of freedom (independent variables: T, P, composition).
- **C** = Number of components (e.g., 2 for a binary alloy).
- **P** = Number of coexisting phases at equilibrium.
- **2** = Represents the two external variables ‚Äî temperature and pressure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight (Condensed Systems):**
> Since most metallurgical processes occur at **constant atmospheric pressure (1 atm)**, the phase rule simplifies to the **Condensed Phase Rule**:
> 
> $$F = C - P + 1$$
> 
> This is the version used in **almost all KTU numerical problems**.

---

## 1.4 Classification of Phase Reactions (Overview of Module 3)

| Reaction Type | Symbolic Form | One-Line Description |
|---------------|---------------|----------------------|
| **Isomorphous** | L ‚Üí Œ± (continuous) | Two components fully soluble in each other in solid state. |
| **Eutectic** | L ‚Üí Œ± + Œ≤ | Liquid transforms to two distinct solids on cooling. |
| **Eutectoid** | Œ≥ ‚Üí Œ± + Œ≤ | One solid transforms to two other solids on cooling. |
| **Peritectic** | L + Œ± ‚Üí Œ≤ | Liquid reacts with a solid to form a new solid. |
| **Peritectoid** | Œ± + Œ≤ ‚Üí Œ≥ | Two solids react to form a third new solid. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Isomorphous Phase System

### Definition
An **isomorphous system** is a binary alloy system in which the two components exhibit **complete mutual solid solubility** in the solid state over the entire composition range (0‚Äì100 %). Only **one solid phase (Œ±)** exists across the entire composition range below the liquidus.

### Classic Example: Cu‚ÄìNi System
Copper and Nickel have:
- Same FCC crystal structure.
- Nearly identical atomic radii (Cu: 0.128 nm, Ni: 0.125 nm).
- Similar electronegativities.
‚Üí Hence complete solid solubility.

### Key Features of an Isomorphous Diagram
1. **Liquidus Line** (Upper Curve) ‚Äî Above this line, the alloy is **100 % liquid**.
2. **Solidus Line** (Lower Curve) ‚Äî Below this line, the alloy is **100 % solid Œ±**.
3. **Two-Phase (Œ± + L) Region** ‚Äî Between the two curves, a mixture of liquid and solid Œ± exists in equilibrium.

### Cooling of a 60 wt% Ni ‚Äì 40 wt% Cu Alloy (Cu‚ÄìNi)

- **Region 1 (Above Liquidus)**: 100 % liquid. Composition uniform.
- **Point A (Crossing Liquidus at T‚ÇÅ)**: First solid crystal (Œ±) nucleates. Its composition is given by the **solidus tie-line** ‚Äî richer in Ni (~ 73 wt% Ni).
- **Region 2 (Between Liquidus and Solidus)**: Œ± + L coexist. The Œ± becomes progressively richer in Cu (since Cu is rejected into the liquid). The liquid becomes richer in Cu.
- **Point B (Crossing Solidus at T‚ÇÇ)**: Last drop of liquid freezes. The solid now has the overall composition (60 % Ni, 40 % Cu). At this point, **complete equilibrium** demands uniform composition, but in practice **coring** (compositional gradients) occurs.
- **Region 3 (Below Solidus)**: 100 % solid Œ±, with slow cooling ‚Üí eventual homogenization.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Significance of Isomorphous Systems:**
> - Used in design of **Cu‚ÄìNi alloys** (cupronickel) for marine hardware and condenser tubes.
> - Ni‚ÄìCu alloys (Monel) for corrosion-resistant parts.
> - Fe‚ÄìCr, Fe‚ÄìV, Fe‚ÄìNi systems are also isomorphous (or nearly so).

---

## 2.2 The Eutectic Phase System

### Definition
A **eutectic reaction** is an **invariant three-phase reaction** in which, on cooling, a **single liquid phase** transforms simultaneously into **two distinct solid phases** at a unique temperature and composition.

### Symbolic Form
$$L \xrightarrow{\text{cooling}} \alpha + \beta$$

### Classic Example: Pb‚ÄìSn System (Solder Alloy)
- Eutectic composition: **61.9 wt% Sn, 38.1 wt% Pb**.
- Eutectic temperature: **183 ¬∞C** (lowest melting point in the system).
- The eutectic point is the **invariant point** ‚Äî a single temperature and composition where three phases (L, Œ±, Œ≤) coexist.

### Microstructure on Eutectic Cooling
- At the eutectic point, the liquid solidifies as a **lamellar (layered) mixture** of Œ± (Pb-rich) and Œ≤ (Sn-rich) phases.
- The thin alternating plates form because diffusion in the solid is sluggish, so the system deposits Œ± and Œ≤ side by side directly from the liquid.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Engineering Highlight:**
> The 61.9 % Sn ‚Äì 38.1 % Pb eutectic solder is the historical **standard solder** for electronic and plumbing joints. Modern lead-free solders (e.g., Sn‚ÄìAg‚ÄìCu) are designed to mimic the eutectic behaviour to give a sharp, low melting point.

### Hypoeutectic, Eutectic, and Hypereutectic Alloys
- **Hypoeutectic**: Composition is *less* than the eutectic composition (left of eutectic point). Final solidification ends with a proeutectic Œ± phase followed by the eutectic mixture (Œ± + Œ≤).
- **Eutectic**: Exactly at the eutectic composition ‚Üí 100 % eutectic microstructure.
- **Hypereutectic**: Composition is *more* than the eutectic composition (right of eutectic point). Final solidification ends with a proeutectic Œ≤ phase followed by the eutectic mixture (Œ± + Œ≤).

---

## 2.3 The Eutectoid Phase System

### Definition
A **eutectoid reaction** is an **invariant three-phase reaction** in which, on cooling, **one solid phase** transforms into **two new solid phases**.

### Symbolic Form
$$\gamma \xrightarrow{\text{cooling}} \alpha + \beta$$

### Classic Example: Fe‚ÄìC System (Pearlite Formation)
- Eutectoid composition: **0.76 wt% C** in iron.
- Eutectoid temperature: **727 ¬∞C**.
- The Œ≥ phase (**austenite**, FCC) decomposes into Œ± (ferrite, BCC) + Fe‚ÇÉC (cementite).

### Pearlite ‚Äî The Eutectoid Microstructure
Pearlite is a **lamellar structure** of alternating Œ±-ferrite and Fe‚ÇÉC cementite plates. It forms because:
- Austenite (Œ≥) becomes unstable below 727 ¬∞C.
- Carbon, which is more soluble in Œ≥ (~ 2.14 wt%) than in Œ± (~ 0.022 wt%), is rejected.
- The carbon enriches alternating regions ‚Üí nucleates Fe‚ÇÉC plates.
- The low-carbon regions become Œ±-ferrite.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Note:**
> The eutectoid reaction is the **backbone of all steel heat treatment**. By controlling the cooling rate from austenite, an engineer can produce:
> - **Pearlite** (slow cooling) ‚Äî soft, ductile.
> - **Bainite** (medium cooling) ‚Äî tough.
> - **Martensite** (very fast cooling/quenching) ‚Äî hard, brittle.

---

## 2.4 The Peritectic Phase System

### Definition
A **peritectic reaction** is an **invariant three-phase reaction** in which, on cooling, a **liquid phase reacts with a solid phase** to form a **new solid phase**.

### Symbolic Form
$$L + \alpha \xrightarrow{\text{cooling}} \beta$$

### Classic Example: Fe‚ÄìC System (Œ¥-Peritectic)
- At **1493 ¬∞C**, liquid iron (0.53 wt% C) reacts with Œ¥-ferrite (0.09 wt% C) to form Œ≥-austenite (0.16 wt% C).
- This is the **Œ¥-peritectic** in the Fe‚ÄìC system.

### Other Examples of Peritectic Systems
- **Cu‚ÄìZn** system (brasses).
- **Ag‚ÄìSn** system.
- **Pb‚ÄìBi** system.
- Many ceramic and intermetallic systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Significance:**
> Peritectic reactions are important in **solidification of steels** and **continuous casting**. Improper peritectic reaction leads to *cracking* and *porosity* in cast products. Therefore, KTU expects students to know why **0.10‚Äì0.16 wt% C steels** need special casting precautions.

---

## 2.5 The Peritectoid Phase System

### Definition
A **peritectoid reaction** is an **invariant three-phase reaction** in which, on cooling, **two solid phases** react to form a **third new solid phase**.

### Symbolic Form
$$\alpha + \beta \xrightarrow{\text{cooling}} \gamma$$

### Classic Example: Cu‚ÄìSn System
At ~ 586 ¬∞C, the Œ¥ phase and Œµ phase react to form the Œ∂ phase.

### Other Example: Fe‚ÄìB (Iron‚ÄìBoron) System
The Fe‚ÇÇB peritectoid forms from Fe + FeB reaction.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Hint:**
> Peritectoid reactions are **far less common** in industrial alloys than eutectic/eutectoid. They are usually asked as a 3-mark "definition + example" question.

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Rule | Description / Use |
|---|----------------|-------------------|
| 1 | $F = C - P + 2$ | Gibbs Phase Rule (general, including pressure). |
| 2 | $F = C - P + 1$ | **Condensed Phase Rule** (constant P) ‚Äî *most used in KTU*. |
| 3 | $F_{eutectic} = 2 - 3 + 1 = 0$ | Eutectic point is **invariant** (T and C both fixed). |
| 4 | $F_{two-phase} = 2 - 2 + 1 = 1$ | In two-phase region, one variable (T or C) is free. |
| 5 | $\dfrac{W_\alpha}{W_\beta} = \dfrac{C_\beta - C_0}{C_0 - C_\alpha}$ | **Lever Rule** ‚Äî for finding phase weight fractions. |
| 6 | $W_\alpha = \dfrac{C_\beta - C_0}{C_\beta - C_\alpha}$ | Weight fraction of Œ± phase. |
| 7 | $W_L = \dfrac{C_0 - C_\alpha}{C_\beta - C_\alpha}$ | Weight fraction of liquid (or Œ≤ phase by symmetry). |
| 8 | $C_0$ | Overall alloy composition (given). |
| 9 | $C_\alpha$ | Composition of Œ± phase (read from solidus tie-line). |
| 10 | $C_\beta$ | Composition of Œ≤ phase (read from solidus tie-line). |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Tip:** When using the Lever Rule in the **isomorphous** system, replace $C_\beta$ with $C_L$ (composition of the liquid, read from the **liquidus**). This is the **most commonly confused** point in KTU scripts.

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility of Phase Diagrams

1. **Alloy Design** ‚Äî Selection of composition for desired phases (e.g., stainless steel with austenite + ferrite).
2. **Casting & Foundry** ‚Äî Choosing pouring temperature above the liquidus, anticipating shrinkage at solidus.
3. **Heat Treatment** ‚Äî Heating into a single-phase region (e.g., austenitizing steel above 727 ¬∞C) for quenching.
4. **Welding** ‚Äî Predicting microstructures in the heat-affected zone (HAZ).
5. **Brazing & Soldering** ‚Äî Choosing filler metals with low eutectic temperatures.
6. **Semiconductor Industry** ‚Äî Phase diagrams of Si‚ÄìGe, GaAs, InP dictate growth of single crystals.
7. **Ceramic Processing** ‚Äî Sintering temperatures selected from oxide phase diagrams (Al‚ÇÇO‚ÇÉ‚ÄìSiO‚ÇÇ, ZrO‚ÇÇ‚ÄìY‚ÇÇO‚ÇÉ).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Gibbs Phase Rule

### Statement
The Gibbs phase rule relates the number of degrees of freedom (F) in a system to the number of components (C) and the number of phases (P).

### Derivation
**Step 1 ‚Äî Definition of Degrees of Freedom:**
The total state of a system at equilibrium is fixed by specifying:
- 1 variable for **Pressure (P)**.
- 1 variable for **Temperature (T)**.
- For each of the **C components**, we must specify its **chemical potential** in each phase.

**Step 2 ‚Äî Counting Variables:**
The total number of intensive variables is:
- 2 (for T and P) **+** (C √ó P) [composition of each of P phases].

**Step 3 ‚Äî Counting Independent Equations:**
At equilibrium, the chemical potential of each component must be equal across all phases. For each of the C components, there are (P ‚àí 1) such independent equations.
- Total equations = C √ó (P ‚àí 1).

**Step 4 ‚Äî Apply the Formula:**
Degrees of freedom = Total variables ‚àí Total equations:

$$F = \left[2 + CP\right] - \left[C(P-1)\right]$$

$$F = 2 + CP - CP + C$$

$$\boxed{F = C - P + 2}$$

**Step 5 ‚Äî Condensed Systems (Constant Pressure):**
In metallurgical systems where pressure is fixed at 1 atm:

$$\boxed{F = C - P + 1}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Lever Rule

### Problem Setup
Consider a binary alloy of overall composition $C_0$ in a two-phase (Œ± + Œ≤) region at temperature T. The compositions of the Œ± and Œ≤ phases at this temperature are $C_\alpha$ and $C_\beta$ respectively, as read from the respective phase boundaries (tie-line / isotherm).

### Step 1 ‚Äî Mass Balance (Conservation of Mass)
Total mass of the alloy = Mass of Œ± phase + Mass of Œ≤ phase.

$$W_{total} = W_\alpha + W_\beta$$

Dividing throughout by $W_{total}$ and defining $W_\alpha$ and $W_\beta$ as the weight fractions:

$$1 = W_\alpha + W_\beta$$

### Step 2 ‚Äî Component Mass Balance
Consider the solute (component B) as the marker. The total amount of B in the alloy equals the amount of B in Œ± plus the amount of B in Œ≤.

$$W_{total} \cdot C_0 = W_\alpha \cdot C_\alpha + W_\beta \cdot C_\beta$$

Dividing throughout by $W_{total}$:

$$C_0 = W_\alpha \cdot C_\alpha + W_\beta \cdot C_\beta$$

### Step 3 ‚Äî Solve the Two Equations Simultaneously
From the first equation:
$$W_\beta = 1 - W_\alpha$$

Substitute into the second equation:

$$C_0 = W_\alpha \cdot C_\alpha + (1 - W_\alpha) \cdot C_\beta$$

$$C_0 = W_\alpha \cdot C_\alpha + C_\beta - W_\alpha \cdot C_\beta$$

$$C_0 - C_\beta = W_\alpha (C_\alpha - C_\beta)$$

Rearranging for $W_\alpha$:

$$\boxed{W_\alpha = \dfrac{C_0 - C_\beta}{C_\alpha - C_\beta}}$$

This can also be written as (multiplying numerator and denominator by ‚àí1):

$$W_\alpha = \dfrac{C_\beta - C_0}{C_\beta - C_\alpha}$$

This is the **Lever Rule**. The same logic gives:

$$\boxed{W_\beta = \dfrac{C_0 - C_\alpha}{C_\beta - C_\alpha}}$$

### Geometric Interpretation
- Draw a horizontal **tie-line** (isotherm) at temperature T.
- $C_\alpha$ is on the left, $C_\beta$ is on the right, and $C_0$ lies between them.
- The weight fraction of Œ± is the **length of the segment from $C_0$ to $C_\beta$** divided by the **total length from $C_\alpha$ to $C_\beta$**.
- The lever pivots at $C_0$ ‚Äî the further $C_0$ is from a phase, the smaller the fraction of that phase.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Mnemonic for KTU:**
> "The fraction of the phase on the **opposite** side of the lever equals the ratio of the **opposite arm length** to the **total arm length**."

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example ‚Äî Lever Rule on a Cu‚ÄìNi Alloy

**Given:** A Cu‚ÄìNi alloy containing **32 wt% Ni** is heated to a temperature where it is in the (Œ± + L) two-phase region. At this temperature, the solidus composition of Œ± is $C_\alpha = 43 \text{ wt\% Ni}$ and the liquidus composition of L is $C_L = 22 \text{ wt\% Ni}$.

**Find:** The weight fraction of Œ± and of liquid.

### Step 1 ‚Äî Identify Values
- $C_0 = 32$ wt% Ni
- $C_\alpha = 43$ wt% Ni (read from solidus line)
- $C_L = 22$ wt% Ni (read from liquidus line)

### Step 2 ‚Äî Apply Lever Rule for Œ±
$$W_\alpha = \dfrac{C_L - C_0}{C_L - C_\alpha} = \dfrac{22 - 32}{22 - 43}$$

$$W_\alpha = \dfrac{-10}{-21} = \dfrac{10}{21}$$

$$W_\alpha \approx 0.476 \; \text{or} \; 47.6\%$$

### Step 3 ‚Äî Apply Lever Rule for Liquid
$$W_L = 1 - W_\alpha = 1 - 0.476 = 0.524 \; \text{or} \; 52.4\%$$

### Step 4 ‚Äî Verification
Check using the direct formula for liquid:

$$W_L = \dfrac{C_0 - C_\alpha}{C_L - C_\alpha} = \dfrac{32 - 43}{22 - 43} = \dfrac{-11}{-21} = 0.524$$

‚úÖ Both methods give the same answer.

### Step 5 ‚Äî Physical Interpretation
- Nearly **half the alloy is solid Œ±** and the other **half is liquid L**.
- The Œ± phase is **enriched in Ni (43 %)** compared to the bulk (32 %).
- The liquid is **depleted in Ni (22 %)** ‚Äî the solidification process rejects Cu into the liquid.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix ‚Äî Cooling Path Analysis for Cu‚ÄìNi (40 wt% Ni)

| Stage | Temperature Range | Phases Present | Key Event | Composition Detail |
|-------|-------------------|----------------|-----------|--------------------|
| 1 | Above Liquidus ($T > 1280\,¬∞C$ for 40 % Ni) | 100 % L | Alloy fully molten. | $C_L = 40$ wt% Ni. |
| 2 | Crossing Liquidus ($T_1$) | L + first Œ± | First solid Œ± nucleates. | $C_\alpha = 52$ wt% Ni (from solidus tie-line). |
| 3 | Between Liquidus & Solidus | L + Œ± | Solidification proceeds; Œ± grows, liquid becomes Cu-rich. | $C_L$ moves right; $C_\alpha$ moves left. |
| 4 | Crossing Solidus ($T_2$) | L + Œ± | Last liquid disappears. | $C_\alpha = 40$ wt% Ni (matches alloy). |
| 5 | Below Solidus | 100 % Œ± | Homogenization (if slow cooled). | Uniform Œ± phase. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight ‚Äî Coring vs. Homogenization:**
> In real castings, cooling is too fast for the Œ±-phase compositions to equilibrate. The centre of each grain retains a higher Ni content (formed first), while the edges are Ni-lean. This **coring** can be removed by **homogenization annealing** (holding just below the solidus).

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix ‚Äî Fe‚ÄìC Steel Cooling (Eutectoid Reaction)

**Given:** A **hypoeutectoid plain carbon steel** with $C_0 = 0.40$ wt% C, slowly cooled from the austenite region (above 727 ¬∞C).

| Stage | Temperature | Phases | Event |
|-------|-------------|--------|-------|
| 1 | Above 900 ¬∞C | 100 % Œ≥ (austenite) | Fully austenitic. |
| 2 | A‚ÇÉ temperature (‚âà 840 ¬∞C for 0.4 % C) | Œ≥ + Œ± begins | First **proeutectoid ferrite (Œ±)** nucleates at austenite grain boundaries. |
| 3 | 840 ¬∞C ‚Üí 727 ¬∞C | Œ≥ + Œ± | Ferrite grows; carbon is rejected into Œ≥, raising its C content. |
| 4 | 727 ¬∞C (Eutectoid) | Œ≥ (0.76 % C) ‚Üí Œ± + Fe‚ÇÉC | Remaining Œ≥ transforms to **pearlite** (Œ± + Fe‚ÇÉC lamellae). |
| 5 | Below 727 ¬∞C | Œ± + Fe‚ÇÉC | Final microstructure: **proeutectoid Œ± + pearlite**. |

### Lever Rule for Hypoeutectoid Steel (at just above 727 ¬∞C)
- $C_0 = 0.40$ wt% C
- $C_\alpha = 0.022$ wt% C (solubility of C in Œ± at 727 ¬∞C)
- $C_\gamma = 0.76$ wt% C (eutectoid composition)

$$W_\alpha = \dfrac{0.76 - 0.40}{0.76 - 0.022} = \dfrac{0.36}{0.738} = 0.488$$

So **~ 48.8 % of the structure is proeutectoid ferrite** and **~ 51.2 % is pearlite** at 727 ¬∞C. Just below 727 ¬∞C, the pearlite further decomposes into (Œ± + Fe‚ÇÉC), but the proeutectoid Œ± fraction remains ~ 48.8 %.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Numerical Insight:**
> This is a **frequently asked 14-mark problem** in Module 3. Practice both the **Œ±-fraction** and the **Fe‚ÇÉC-fraction in pearlite** (using the eutectoid tie-line).

## 4.3 Flow Chart ‚Äî Lever Rule Application Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[START<br/>Given alloy composition and temperature]:::start
    A1[Draw the tie line<br/>Horizontal line at the given T]:::step
    A2[Read C alpha and C beta<br/>from the phase boundaries]:::step
    A3[Identify C zero<br/>the overall alloy composition]:::step
    A4[Apply lever rule<br/>W alpha equals C beta minus C zero<br/>divided by C beta minus C alpha]:::calc
    A5[Compute W beta equals 1 minus W alpha]:::calc
    A6[Verify using<br/>mass balance check]:::check
    A7[Report weight fractions<br/>in percentage]:::end

    A0 --> A1 --> A2 --> A3 --> A4 --> A5 --> A6 --> A7
```
