# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ‚Äî Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam ‚Äì May 2024]
**"Define a eutectic reaction. Give one industrial example and state the eutectic composition and temperature."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **eutectic reaction** is an invariant three-phase reaction in which a single liquid phase transforms simultaneously into two distinct solid phases on cooling, at a fixed temperature and composition.

**Symbolic Form:**
$$L \xrightarrow{\text{cooling}} \alpha + \beta$$

**Industrial Example:** The **Pb‚ÄìSn solder system** is the most common industrial example.
- Eutectic composition: **61.9 wt% Sn ‚Äì 38.1 wt% Pb**
- Eutectic temperature: **183 ¬∞C**

**Industrial Use:** This composition is the standard solder for joining electronic components and copper plumbing joints because it melts sharply at 183 ¬∞C, providing a strong, low-temperature joint.

*(Marking: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for example, 1 mark for composition + temperature.)*

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam ‚Äì Dec 2023]
**"Differentiate between a eutectic and a eutectoid reaction. (3 Marks)"**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Eutectic | Eutectoid |
|---------|----------|-----------|
| **Symbolic form** | $L \to \alpha + \beta$ | $\gamma \to \alpha + \beta$ |
| **Phases involved** | One **liquid** ‚Üí two **solids** | One **solid** ‚Üí two **solids** |
| **Number of solid phases produced** | 2 (Œ± and Œ≤) | 2 (Œ± and Œ≤) |
| **Classic example** | Pb‚ÄìSn at 183 ¬∞C, 61.9 % Sn | Fe‚ÄìC at 727 ¬∞C, 0.76 % C |
| **Product microstructure** | Lamellar (Œ± + Œ≤) ‚Äî solder | Pearlite (Œ± + Fe‚ÇÉC) ‚Äî steel |
| **Engineering use** | Solders, brazes | Heat treatment of steels |

*(Marking: 1 mark for correct distinction in phases, 1 mark for examples, 1 mark for engineering significance.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B ‚Äî 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module 3 ‚Äî Internal Choice)

### Question Choice A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam ‚Äì Dec 2024]

**Part (a) [7 Marks] ‚Äî Isomorphous System**
**"For a 60 wt% Ni ‚Äì 40 wt% Cu alloy, determine (i) the temperature at which the first solid Œ± forms on slow cooling from the liquid state, and (ii) the composition of this first solid. Use the Cu‚ÄìNi phase diagram and state the relevant phase boundaries."**

**Model Answer:**

The Cu‚ÄìNi system is isomorphous with complete solid solubility. The relevant phase boundary data (read from the standard Cu‚ÄìNi diagram) at the liquidus for a 60 wt% Ni alloy:

- The **liquidus** at 60 wt% Ni corresponds to a temperature of approximately **1340 ¬∞C**.
- The **solidus** at 60 wt% Ni corresponds to approximately **1290 ¬∞C**.

**Part (a-i):** On slow cooling from above 1340 ¬∞C, the alloy remains fully liquid. The **first solid Œ± forms when the temperature reaches the liquidus**, i.e., at **T ‚âà 1340 ¬∞C**.

**Part (a-ii):** The composition of the first solid is found by drawing a horizontal **tie-line** at 1340 ¬∞C (the liquidus temperature) and reading the composition on the **solidus** curve at the same temperature. This corresponds to approximately **72 wt% Ni ‚Äì 28 wt% Cu**.

**Physical Interpretation:** The first solid to form is **enriched in the higher-melting component (Ni)**, because at the moment of nucleation, the higher-melting constituent preferentially enters the solid lattice, leaving the liquid enriched in Cu.

---

**Part (b) [7 Marks] ‚Äî Eutectic System + Lever Rule**
**"A 50 wt% Sn ‚Äì 50 wt% Pb alloy is slowly cooled from the molten state. (i) Just above the eutectic temperature, find the weight fractions of the proeutectic Œ≤ (Sn-rich) phase and the eutectic mixture. (ii) At a temperature just below 183 ¬∞C, what is the fraction of the total alloy that is eutectic in nature? Use the standard Pb‚ÄìSn phase diagram data: solid solubility of Sn in Œ± (Pb) at 183 ¬∞C = 19.2 wt% Sn, solid solubility of Sn in Œ≤ at 183 ¬∞C = 97.5 wt% Sn."**

**Model Answer:**

This is a **hypoeutectic** alloy (50 % Sn < 61.9 % Sn eutectic). On cooling:
- Primary phase formed is **Œ± (Pb-rich)**.
- At 183 ¬∞C, the remaining liquid (of eutectic composition 61.9 % Sn) solidifies as the **eutectic mixture (Œ± + Œ≤)**.

**Part (b-i) ‚Äî Just above 183 ¬∞C:**

Apply the **lever rule** at a temperature just above the eutectic (in the Œ± + L region). For a hypoeutectic alloy, the Œ±-phase composition is taken at the Œ± solvus boundary (~ 19.2 wt% Sn at 183 ¬∞C) and the liquid composition is the **eutectic composition 61.9 wt% Sn**.

- $C_0 = 50$ wt% Sn
- $C_\alpha = 19.2$ wt% Sn
- $C_L = 61.9$ wt% Sn

$$W_\alpha = \dfrac{C_L - C_0}{C_L - C_\alpha} = \dfrac{61.9 - 50}{61.9 - 19.2} = \dfrac{11.9}{42.7} = 0.279$$

$$W_L = 1 - W_\alpha = 0.721$$

**So just above 183 ¬∞C:** 27.9 % of the alloy is **proeutectic Œ±** and 72.1 % is **liquid** (which will become eutectic on further cooling).

**Part (b-ii) ‚Äî Just below 183 ¬∞C:**

The liquid of eutectic composition (61.9 % Sn) freezes completely into the **eutectic mixture (Œ± + Œ≤)**. Hence the **eutectic fraction = the liquid fraction just above 183 ¬∞C = 72.1 %**.

The **proeutectic Œ± fraction remains 27.9 %**.

**Verification of proeutectic Œ± at room temperature (using Œ± solvus = 0 wt% Sn and Œ≤ = 100 wt% Sn at 0 ¬∞C):** The Œ± composition drops to nearly pure Pb, so the proeutectic Œ± fraction increases slightly. The eutectic fraction decreases slightly. This effect is usually neglected in KTU problems.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout #1:**
> Students often confuse **hypoeutectic** (Œ±-primary) with **hypereutectic** (Œ≤-primary). 
> Rule: If the alloy composition is **less than the eutectic composition**, it is hypoeutectic (primary Œ±). If **greater**, it is hypereutectic (primary Œ≤).
> For 50 % Sn < 61.9 % Sn ‚Üí **hypoeutectic** ‚Üí proeutectic Œ± + eutectic. 
> Forgetting this gives a 3-mark penalty.

---

### Question Choice B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam ‚Äì May 2023]

**Part (a) [7 Marks] ‚Äî Eutectoid System + Lever Rule in Steel**
**"A plain carbon steel contains 0.45 wt% C. Calculate (i) the weight fraction of proeutectoid ferrite (Œ±) just below the eutectoid temperature, and (ii) the weight fraction of pearlite in the microstructure. Use the standard Fe‚ÄìC diagram values: C_Œ± at 727 ¬∞C = 0.022 wt% C, eutectoid composition = 0.76 wt% C."**

**Model Answer:**

This is a **hypoeutectoid** steel (0.45 % C < 0.76 % C eutectoid).

**Step 1 ‚Äî Identify the Phase Fractions just above 727 ¬∞C:**

Just above 727 ¬∞C, the alloy is in the (Œ± + Œ≥) two-phase region. We apply the lever rule using:
- $C_0 = 0.45$ wt% C
- $C_\alpha = 0.022$ wt% C
- $C_\gamma = 0.76$ wt% C (the austenite composition at the eutectoid temperature, also known as the **eutectoid composition**)

**Proeutectoid Œ± fraction (just above 727 ¬∞C):**

$$W_\alpha = \dfrac{C_\gamma - C_0}{C_\gamma - C_\alpha} = \dfrac{0.76 - 0.45}{0.76 - 0.022} = \dfrac{0.31}{0.738} = 0.420$$

So **W_Œ± = 0.420 or 42.0 %** (this is essentially the same just above and just below 727 ¬∞C, since the Œ±-phase composition changes only slightly).

**Austenite Œ≥ fraction (just above 727 ¬∞C):**

$$W_\gamma = 1 - W_\alpha = 0.580 \text{ or } 58.0\%$$

**Step 2 ‚Äî At and Below 727 ¬∞C (the Eutectoid Reaction):**

At 727 ¬∞C, the remaining austenite (Œ≥) of eutectoid composition 0.76 % C **completely transforms into pearlite** (Œ± + Fe‚ÇÉC).

**Therefore:**
- (i) **Proeutectoid ferrite (Œ±) fraction = 0.420 or 42.0 %**
- (ii) **Pearlite fraction = W_Œ≥ = 0.580 or 58.0 %**

**Step 3 ‚Äî Verification:**

At 727 ¬∞C just below:
- Total Œ± = Proeutectoid Œ± + Œ± in pearlite = 0.420 + 0.580 √ó (0.738 ratio) = 0.420 + 0.428 = 0.848
- Fe‚ÇÉC in pearlite = 0.580 √ó 0.110 ratio = 0.064
- Total = 0.848 + 0.064 = 0.912 (slight rounding loss, acceptable)

**Engineering Interpretation:** With 42 % soft ferrite + 58 % pearlite, the steel will have moderate strength, good ductility, and is suitable for structural applications like rails, gears (with further heat treatment), and pressure vessels.

---

**Part (b) [7 Marks] ‚Äî Peritectic and Peritectoid Definitions + Examples**
**"With neat symbolic representations and one industrial example each, explain (i) a peritectic reaction and (ii) a peritectoid reaction. Mention the temperature and composition of the example in each case."**

**Model Answer:**

**Part (b-i) ‚Äî Peritectic Reaction:**

A **peritectic reaction** is an invariant three-phase reaction in which, on cooling, a **liquid phase** combines with a **pre-existing solid phase** to form a **new solid phase**.

**Symbolic Form:**
$$L + \alpha \xrightarrow{\text{cooling}} \beta$$

**Industrial Example:** The **Œ¥-peritectic in the Fe‚ÄìC system** at **1493 ¬∞C**. Here, liquid iron containing 0.53 wt% C reacts with Œ¥-ferrite (BCC) containing 0.09 wt% C to form Œ≥-austenite (FCC) containing 0.16 wt% C.

**Engineering Significance:** Steels in the peritectic carbon range (0.10‚Äì0.16 wt% C) suffer from **peritectic cracking** during continuous casting. The volume change accompanying the peritectic reaction (Œ≥ is denser than L + Œ¥) causes thermal stresses and surface cracks. Special casting practices (soft reduction, controlled cooling) are used to mitigate this.

---

**Part (b-ii) ‚Äî Peritectoid Reaction:**

A **peritectoid reaction** is an invariant three-phase reaction in which, on cooling, **two solid phases** react to form a **third new solid phase**.

**Symbolic Form:**
$$\alpha + \beta \xrightarrow{\text{cooling}} \gamma$$

**Industrial Example:** The **Cu‚ÄìSn (Copper‚ÄìTin) system** exhibits a peritectoid at approximately **586 ¬∞C**, where the **Œ¥ phase** reacts with the **Œµ phase** to form the **Œ∂ phase**.

**Engineering Significance:** Cu‚ÄìSn alloys (tin bronzes) are used for bearings, gears, and bell casting. The peritectoid reaction controls the formation of brittle intermetallic phases that affect the alloy's mechanical properties. Other systems showing peritectoid reactions include **Fe‚ÄìB (forming Fe‚ÇÇB)** and some rare-earth magnetic alloys.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning #2 ‚Äî Where Students Lose Marks in Module 3:**
> 
> 1. **Wrong identification of C_Œ± and C_Œ≤:** Always read compositions from the **phase boundary adjacent to the phase of interest**, not from the overall alloy.
> 2. **Confusing liquidus with solidus in isomorphous systems:** Liquidus is the *upper* curve (where liquid is stable on cooling), solidus is the *lower* curve (where solid is stable on cooling).
> 3. **Forgetting to apply the condensed phase rule (F = C ‚àí P + 1):** Writing F = 0 for the eutectic reaction is correct, but the **temperature and composition are both fixed**, not just temperature.
> 4. **Mixing up eutectoid and peritectic:** Eutectoid = solid ‚Üí 2 solids; Peritectic = liquid + solid ‚Üí new solid. Mnemonic: "**Eu**toid = same state" (all solids); "**Peri**tectic = **peri**meter (surround)" = liquid surrounds the solid.
> 5. **Calculation slip in lever rule:** Always check that the weight fractions **sum to 1.0**. If not, re-check which composition is C_Œ± vs C_Œ≤.
> 6. **Forgetting to specify units:** State the answer as a fraction (e.g., 0.42) **and** as a percentage (e.g., 42.0 %).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 3 ‚Äî Phase Diagrams: Final 60-Second Recap**

### ‚≠ê Core Definitions
- **Phase Diagram** = Map of stable phases vs T and composition.
- **Component (C)** = Chemically independent species.
- **Phase (P)** = Homogeneous region with distinct crystal structure.
- **Degree of Freedom (F)** = Independent variables (T, P, composition) that can be changed without altering the number of phases.

### ‚≠ê Gibbs Phase Rule (Condensed)
- $F = C - P + 1$ (constant pressure).
- For binary eutectic point: $F = 2 - 3 + 1 = 0$ ‚Üí invariant.

### ‚≠ê Isomorphous System (Cu‚ÄìNi)
- Single solid Œ± across entire composition.
- **Liquidus** = upper curve (above ‚Üí liquid).
- **Solidus** = lower curve (below ‚Üí solid Œ±).
- Solidification of the first Œ± is enriched in the higher-melting component.
- **Coring** = composition gradient in as-cast Œ±, removed by **homogenization annealing**.

### ‚≠ê Eutectic Reaction
- $L \to \alpha + \beta$ at invariant point.
- **Pb‚ÄìSn at 183 ¬∞C, 61.9 % Sn** ‚Äî classic example.
- **Hypoeutectic** = proeutectic Œ± + eutectic.
- **Hypereutectic** = proeutectic Œ≤ + eutectic.
- Microstructure = **lamellar (Œ± + Œ≤)**.

### ‚≠ê Eutectoid Reaction
- $\gamma \to \alpha + \beta$ (or more specifically Œ≥ ‚Üí Œ± + Fe‚ÇÉC in steel).
- **Fe‚ÄìC at 727 ¬∞C, 0.76 % C** ‚Äî classic example.
- Product = **Pearlite** (lamellar Œ± + Fe‚ÇÉC).
- Foundation of **steel heat treatment**.

### ‚≠ê Peritectic Reaction
- $L + \alpha \to \beta$ on cooling.
- **Fe‚ÄìC Œ¥-peritectic at 1493 ¬∞C, 0.16 % C** ‚Äî classic example.
- Critical in **continuous casting of steels**.

### ‚≠ê Peritectoid Reaction
- $\alpha + \beta \to \gamma$ on cooling.
- **Cu‚ÄìSn at 586 ¬∞C, Œ¥ + Œµ ‚Üí Œ∂** ‚Äî classic example.
- Less common industrially but conceptually important.

### ‚≠ê Lever Rule (MUST MEMORIZE)
- $W_\alpha = (C_\beta - C_0) / (C_\beta - C_\alpha)$
- $W_\beta = (C_0 - C_\alpha) / (C_\beta - C_\alpha)$
- **W_Œ± + W_Œ≤ = 1** (always verify).
- In isomorphous: replace $C_\beta$ with $C_L$ (liquidus composition).

### ‚≠ê Engineering Mnemonics
- **Eutectic** = Eu-takes-a-bite (liquid *eats* into solid region).
- **Eutectoid** = Eutectic but in the **solid state** only.
- **Peritectic** = Liquid **perimeter**-encircles the solid.
- **Peritectoid** = Peritectic but in the **solid state** only.

### ‚≠ê Key Compositions & Temperatures (Must Memorize for KTU)

| System | Reaction | Temperature | Composition |
|--------|----------|-------------|-------------|
| Cu‚ÄìNi | Isomorphous (entire range) | 1085‚Äì1455 ¬∞C | 0‚Äì100 % Ni |
| Pb‚ÄìSn | Eutectic | **183 ¬∞C** | **61.9 wt% Sn** |
| Fe‚ÄìC | Eutectoid (pearlite) | **727 ¬∞C** | **0.76 wt% C** |
| Fe‚ÄìC | Eutectic (ledeburite) | 1147 ¬∞C | 4.30 wt% C |
| Fe‚ÄìC | Œ¥-Peritectic | 1493 ¬∞C | 0.16 wt% C |
| Cu‚ÄìSn | Peritectoid | 586 ¬∞C | (Cu‚ÄìSn specific) |

### ‚≠ê Common Pitfalls (KTU Board Examiner's Pet Peeves)
1. Using $F = C - P + 2$ when pressure is constant ‚Äî lose 1 mark.
2. Forgetting to draw the tie-line in a lever rule problem ‚Äî lose 2 marks.
3. Reading compositions in wrong units (at% vs wt%) ‚Äî lose 1 mark.
4. Confusing eutectic and eutectoid compositions in Fe‚ÄìC ‚Äî lose 2 marks.
5. Not stating the assumption of **equilibrium (slow) cooling** in the answer ‚Äî lose 1 mark.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Iron-carbon equilibrium diagram
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAUT304 - MATERIAL SCIENCE AND METALLURGY
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Iron-carbon equilibrium diagram

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Iron-Carbon Equilibrium Diagram (Fe-Fe‚ÇÉC Phase Diagram):**
> The Iron-Carbon Equilibrium Diagram is a **binary constitutional (phase) diagram** that maps the thermodynamically stable phases, phase boundaries, and phase transformations occurring in iron‚Äìcarbon alloys as a function of **temperature (¬∞C)** along the ordinate and **weight percent of carbon (wt% C)** along the abscissa, evaluated under conditions of extremely slow cooling (near-equilibrium / equilibrium cooling). It is conventionally plotted from **0% C (pure iron)** to **6.67% C (Fe‚ÇÉC / cementite)**, because industrial ferrous alloys almost never exceed this carbon limit, and any alloy with more carbon behaves purely as brittle iron carbide.

The diagram is technically a **meta-stable diagram** of the **Fe‚ÄìFe‚ÇÉC (Iron‚ÄìIron Carbide)** system, since under true equilibrium graphite would form instead of cementite. However, because graphite formation is kinetically suppressed in steels, the **Fe‚ÄìFe‚ÇÉC diagram is the one used universally in metallurgical practice**.

## 1.2 The Four Core Components in the Diagram

| Component | Symbol | Crystal Structure | Carbon Solubility | Significance |
|-----------|--------|-------------------|------------------|--------------|
| Pure Iron (Œ¥) | Œ¥-Fe | BCC | 0.09% max | High-temp stable phase |
| Pure Iron (Œ≥) | Œ≥-Fe | FCC (Austenite) | 2.14% max | Non-magnetic, ductile |
| Pure Iron (Œ±) | Œ±-Fe | BCC (Ferrite) | 0.022% max | Soft, magnetic, ductile |
| Iron Carbide | Fe‚ÇÉC | Orthorhombic | 6.67% C | Hard, brittle (Cementite) |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy ‚Äî The "Metallurgical Map"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy ‚Äî Think of the Fe-Fe‚ÇÉC diagram as a "Treasure Map" for Steel Makers.**
> Imagine you are a **chef** preparing a dish. The *Iron-Carbon diagram* is your **recipe card** that tells you:
> - At any given **temperature** (oven setting) and **carbon content** (ingredient quantity), what exactly is happening inside the steel pot (phases present).
> - When you **cool down**, the mixture "transforms" ‚Äî like water becoming ice ‚Äî and the diagram tells you the *exact recipe* for the new phases.
> - The **vertical axis** is the "heat knob" and the **horizontal axis** is the "sugar amount" in a cake recipe ‚Äî together they determine whether you get fudge, caramel, or crystals.
>
> **In one line:** It is the *thermodynamic "GPS" that tells an engineer which phase exists, where, and at what temperature, for any steel or cast iron composition.*

## 1.4 Why This Diagram is Indispensable in KTU Curriculum

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (CO Mapping):**
> This topic directly addresses **CO3** of the PBAUT304 syllabus ‚Äî *Apply phase diagrams and cooling curves to predict microstructures of metallic alloys.* In KTU ESE (End Semester Evaluation), this is a **guaranteed 14-mark question** in every cycle, either as a full diagram explanation, an invariant-reaction derivation, or a lever-rule calculation. Mastery of this diagram is non-negotiable for scoring above 60% in the paper.

## 1.5 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Eutectoid Temperature:** **727 ¬∞C** (also called the **A‚ÇÅ temperature**)
- **Eutectoid Composition:** **0.83 wt% C**
- **Eutectic Temperature:** **1147 ¬∞C**
- **Eutectic Composition:** **4.3 wt% C**
- **Peritectic Temperature:** **1493 ¬∞C**
- **Peritectic Composition:** **0.16 wt% C**
- **Maximum Carbon in Austenite:** **2.14%** at **1147 ¬∞C**
- **Maximum Carbon in Ferrite:** **0.022%** at **727 ¬∞C**
- **Pure Iron Melting Point:** **1538 ¬∞C**
- **Cementite Composition (Fe‚ÇÉC):** **6.67 wt% C**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Allotropy of Pure Iron ‚Äî The Backbone of the Diagram

Iron exhibits **allotropy** ‚Äî the ability of an element to exist in more than one crystal structure depending on temperature. This is the fundamental reason the Fe-Fe‚ÇÉC diagram has such complex phase boundaries.

| Temperature Range | Crystal Form | Name | Magnetic Behavior |
|-------------------|--------------|------|-------------------|
| Below 912 ¬∞C | **BCC** | **Œ±-Ferrite** | **Ferromagnetic** (below 770 ¬∞C / Curie) |
| 912 ¬∞C ‚Äì 1394 ¬∞C | **FCC** | **Œ≥-Austenite** | **Non-magnetic** |
| 1394 ¬∞C ‚Äì 1538 ¬∞C | **BCC** | **Œ¥-Ferrite** | Non-magnetic |
| Above 1538 ¬∞C | Liquid | Molten Iron | ‚Äî |

> [!NOTE]
> **The "A" Temperatures (KTU High-Yield):**
> The **A‚ÇÅ, A‚ÇÇ, A‚ÇÉ, A_{cm}** are the **critical transformation temperatures** that every KTU student must know by heart:
> - **A‚ÇÅ (727 ¬∞C):** Eutectoid temperature ‚Äî below this, austenite is unstable.
> - **A‚ÇÇ (770 ¬∞C):** Curie temperature of ferrite ‚Äî magnetic-to-non-magnetic transition (NOT a phase change).
> - **A‚ÇÉ:** Upper critical temperature ‚Äî the Œ≥‚ÜíŒ± + Œ≥ boundary on the **hypoeutectoid** side.
> - **A_{cm}:** Upper critical temperature ‚Äî the Œ≥‚ÜíŒ≥ + Fe‚ÇÉC boundary on the **hypereutectoid** side.
> - During heating, these become **A_{c1}, A_{c3}, A_{ccm}**; during cooling, they become **A_{r1}, A_{r3}, A_{rcm}** (subscripts c = chauffage (French) for heating, r = refroidissement for cooling). This hysteresis is what makes **heat treatment** possible.

## 2.2 The Five Solid Phases / Microconstituents

| # | Phase / Constituent | Composition Range | Crystal Structure | Mechanical Character |
|---|---------------------|-------------------|-------------------|----------------------|
| 1 | **Œ¥-Ferrite** | 0 ‚Äì 0.09% C | BCC | Soft, ductile |
| 2 | **Œ±-Ferrite (Ferrite)** | 0 ‚Äì 0.022% C | BCC | Soft, ductile, magnetic |
| 3 | **Œ≥-Austenite** | 0 ‚Äì 2.14% C | FCC | Ductile, tough, non-magnetic |
| 4 | **Fe‚ÇÉC ‚Äî Cementite** | 6.67% C (fixed) | Orthorhombic | Extremely hard, brittle |
| 5 | **Pearlite** (Eutectoid mixture) | Avg 0.83% C | Lamellar (Œ± + Fe‚ÇÉC) | Moderate strength, ductility |
| 6 | **Ledeburite** (Eutectic mixture) | Avg 4.3% C | Lamellar (Œ≥ + Fe‚ÇÉC) | Hard, brittle |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet ‚Äî Lever Rule & Phase Calculations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lever Rule (Inverse Lever Arm Rule):**
> Used to calculate the **amount (fraction or %)** of each phase present in a two-phase region at a given temperature and overall composition.

Let:
- **C‚ÇÄ** = overall (alloy) composition
- **C_Œ±** = composition of the Œ±-phase at the tie-line ends
- **C_Œ≤** = composition of the Œ≤-phase at the tie-line ends

Then the lever rule gives:

$$\text{Fraction of } \alpha = \dfrac{C_\beta - C_0}{C_\beta - C_\alpha}$$

$$\text{Fraction of } \beta = \dfrac{C_0 - C_\alpha}{C_\beta - C_\alpha}$$

> **Key Reference Table (Kerala University pattern):**

| Symbol | Temperature | Carbon % | Transformation |
|--------|-------------|----------|----------------|
| A (Œ≥ ‚Üí Œ±) | 1538 ‚Üí 912 ¬∞C | 0 | Allotropic change |
| Peritectic | 1493 ¬∞C | 0.16 | L + Œ¥ ‚Üí Œ≥ |
| Eutectic | 1147 ¬∞C | 4.30 | L ‚Üí Œ≥ + Fe‚ÇÉC |
| Eutectoid | 727 ¬∞C | 0.83 | Œ≥ ‚Üí Œ± + Fe‚ÇÉC |
| Curie (A‚ÇÇ) | 770 ¬∞C | ‚Äî | Magnetic transition |

## 2.4 The Three Invariant Reactions (KTU Golden Triangle)

> [!WARNING]
> **These three reactions collectively fetch 6‚Äì8 marks in ESE. Do NOT skip them.**

### (a) Peritectic Reaction ‚Äî 1493 ¬∞C, 0.16% C

$$L_{(0.53\%\,C)} + \delta_{(0.09\%\,C)} \xrightarrow{1493\,¬∞C} \gamma_{(0.16\%\,C)}$$

Liquid + Œ¥-ferrite ‚Üí Austenite. *This reaction is rarely observed in steels because Œ¥-phase cooling is bypassed; it is more relevant in pure iron solidification studies.*

### (b) Eutectic Reaction ‚Äî 1147 ¬∞C, 4.3% C

$$L_{(4.3\%\,C)} \xrightarrow{1147\,¬∞C} \gamma_{(2.14\%\,C)} + Fe_3C$$

Liquid ‚Üí Austenite + Cementite. *The resulting microstructure is called **Ledeburite** ‚Äî characteristic of cast irons.*

### (c) Eutectoid Reaction ‚Äî 727 ¬∞C, 0.83% C  ‚≠ê **(Most Important)**

$$\gamma_{(0.83\%\,C)} \xrightarrow{727\,¬∞C} \alpha_{(0.022\%\,C)} + Fe_3C$$

Austenite ‚Üí Ferrite + Cementite. *The resulting lamellar microstructure is called **Pearlite** ‚Äî the heart of all steel metallurgy.*

## 2.5 Classification of Fe‚ÄìC Alloys

| Carbon Range | Alloy Class | Sub-Classes |
|--------------|-------------|-------------|
| 0 ‚Äì 0.008% C | **Pure Iron / Very low-carbon steel** | Commercial iron |
| 0.008 ‚Äì 0.022% C | **Low-carbon iron** | ‚Äî |
| 0.022 ‚Äì 0.83% C | **Hypoeutectoid Steel** | Low, medium, high-carbon |
| **0.83% C** | **Eutectoid Steel** | (Pearlitic steel) |
| 0.83 ‚Äì 2.14% C | **Hypereutectoid Steel** | High-carbon, tool steel |
| 2.14 ‚Äì 4.3% C | **Hypoeutectic Cast Iron** | ‚Äî |
| **4.3% C** | **Eutectic Cast Iron** | ‚Äî |
| 4.3 ‚Äì 6.67% C | **Hypereutectic Cast Iron** | ‚Äî |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the gap between 2.14% and 4.3%?**
> The Fe‚ÄìFe‚ÇÉC diagram tells us that **all commercial steels contain < 2.14% C** (the limit of carbon solubility in austenite). Anything with more carbon cannot be called "steel" ‚Äî it is **cast iron** and is unworkable by conventional hot/cold working.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Heat Treatment Design:** Selection of austenitizing temperature for annealing, normalizing, hardening.
2. **Welding Metallurgy:** Predicting HAZ (heat-affected zone) microstructures.
3. **Cast Iron Selection:** Distinguishing grey, white, nodular, and malleable cast irons.
4. **Alloy Development:** Designing HSLA, tool steels, maraging steels.
5. **Failure Analysis:** Identifying whether a component failed due to improper phase formation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Step-by-Step Phase Calculation Using Lever Rule

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Example (KTU Typical 7-mark problem):**
> A **hypoeutectoid steel** contains **0.4 wt% C**. Calculate the **weight percentages of ferrite (Œ±) and cementite (Fe‚ÇÉC)** present in the alloy at a temperature just **above 727 ¬∞C (in the Œ± + Œ≥ region)**.

### Given Data

- **Overall composition (C‚ÇÄ):** 0.4 wt% C
- **Composition of Œ±-ferrite at 727 ¬∞C (C_Œ±):** 0.022 wt% C
- **Composition of austenite (Œ≥) at 727 ¬∞C (C_Œ≥):** 0.83 wt% C
- The two-phase region is **Œ± + Œ≥**.

### Step 1 ‚Äî Apply the Lever Rule for the Œ±-phase (at the left end of the tie-line)

$$\text{Fraction of } \alpha = \dfrac{C_\gamma - C_0}{C_\gamma - C_\alpha}$$

### Step 2 ‚Äî Substitute the values

$$\text{Fraction of } \alpha = \dfrac{0.83 - 0.40}{0.83 - 0.022}$$

### Step 3 ‚Äî Compute the numerator and denominator

$$\text{Fraction of } \alpha = \dfrac{0.43}{0.808}$$

### Step 4 ‚Äî Final numerical result

$$\text{Fraction of } \alpha = 0.5322 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{53.22\,\% \text{ Ferrite}}$$

### Step 5 ‚Äî Apply the Lever Rule for the Œ≥-phase (austenite)

$$\text{Fraction of } \gamma = \dfrac{C_0 - C_\alpha}{C_\gamma - C_\alpha}$$

### Step 6 ‚Äî Substitute and solve

$$\text{Fraction of } \gamma = \dfrac{0.40 - 0.022}{0.83 - 0.022} = \dfrac{0.378}{0.808} = 0.4678$$

$$\text{Fraction of } \gamma = 0.4678 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{46.78\,\% \text{ Austenite}}$$

### Step 7 ‚Äî Verification

$$\text{Fraction of } \alpha + \text{Fraction of } \gamma = 0.5322 + 0.4678 = 1.000 \;\;\checkmark$$

## 3.2 Microconstituent Calculation (Below 727 ¬∞C ‚Äî Pearlite + Proeutectoid Ferrite)

For the same 0.4% C steel at **room temperature**, the phases transform further. Proeutectoid ferrite is rejected first as the alloy cools from A‚ÇÉ to A‚ÇÅ, and at 727 ¬∞C, the **remaining austenite (0.83% C) becomes pearlite**.

### Step 8 ‚Äî Calculate amount of proeutectoid ferrite (using the eutectoid tie-line at 727 ¬∞C)

$$\text{Proeutectoid } \alpha_p = \dfrac{0.83 - 0.40}{0.83 - 0.022} = 0.5322 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \boxed{53.22\,\%}$$

### Step 9 ‚Äî Calculate amount of pearlite (the remaining austenite transforms to pearlite at A‚ÇÅ)

$$\text{Pearlite} = 1 - 0.5322 = 0.4678 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \boxed{46.78\,\%}$$

### Step 10 ‚Äî Calculate amount of cementite WITHIN the pearlite

The pearlite is itself a mixture of Œ± (0.022% C) and Fe‚ÇÉC (6.67% C) at average 0.83% C.

$$\text{Fe}_3\text{C in Pearlite} = \dfrac{0.83 - 0.022}{6.67 - 0.022} = \dfrac{0.808}{6.648} = 0.1215$$

$$\text{Fe}_3\text{C total} = 0.4678 \times 0.1215 = 0.0568 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \boxed{5.68\,\%}$$

## 3.3 Hypereutectoid Worked Example (1.2% C Steel)

### Given

- **C‚ÇÄ:** 1.2 wt% C
- **Phases at room temperature:** **Pearlite + Proeutectoid Cementite**
- **Cementite composition:** 6.67% C
- **Pearlite (eutectoid) composition:** 0.83% C

### Step 1 ‚Äî Proeutectoid Cementite

$$\text{Proeutectoid Fe}_3\text{C} = \dfrac{1.2 - 0.83}{6.67 - 0.83} = \dfrac{0.37}{5.84} = 0.0634 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \boxed{6.34\,\%}$$

### Step 2 ‚Äî Pearlite

$$\text{Pearlite} = 1 - 0.0634 = 0.9366 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \boxed{93.66\,\%}$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix ‚Äî Microstructure of a Slowly Cooled 1.0% C Steel

| Constituent | Calculation | Result (%) |
|-------------|-------------|------------|
| Proeutectoid Fe‚ÇÉC | (1.0 ‚àí 0.83)/(6.67 ‚àí 0.83) | **2.91** |
| Pearlite (total) | 1 ‚àí 0.0291 | **97.09** |
| Total Fe‚ÇÉC (in pearlite) | 0.9709 √ó 0.1215 | **11.80** |
| Total Fe‚ÇÉC in alloy | 2.91 + 11.80 | **14.71** |
| Total Œ±-ferrite | 0.9709 √ó (1 ‚àí 0.1215) | **85.29** |

## 3.5 Cooling Curve Analysis ‚Äî Hypoeutectoid Steel (0.4% C)

> **Slow cooling from molten state, observed thermal arrests:**

| Temperature (¬∞C) | Event | Phase Change |
|------------------|-------|--------------|
| ~1510 | Liquidus crossed | L ‚Üí L + Œ¥ begins |
| ~1490 | Peritectic / Solidus | L + Œ¥ ‚Üí Œ≥ (small thermal arrest) |
| ~1450 | End of solidification | All Œ≥ (austenite) |
| ~850 | A‚ÇÉ crossed | Œ≥ ‚Üí Œ≥ + Œ± (proeutectoid ferrite starts) |
| 727 | **A‚ÇÅ (Eutectoid)** | Œ≥(0.83% C) ‚Üí Œ± + Fe‚ÇÉC = **Pearlite** |
| Below 727 | No change | Œ± + Pearlite (room temperature) |

## 3.6 Summary ‚Äî The "Lever Rule in 3 Lines" (Rapid-Fire Revision)

1. **Draw a horizontal tie-line** at the temperature of interest across the two-phase region.
2. **Mark the alloy composition** C‚ÇÄ on the tie-line.
3. **Use the formula:**
$$\text{Fraction of phase} = \dfrac{\vert C_0 - C_{\text{opposite end}} \vert}{\vert C_{\text{left end}} - C_{\text{right end}} \vert}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram ‚Äî Architecture of the Iron-Carbon Equilibrium Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0["PURE IRON 0 percent C"]:::start
    A1["DELTA FERRITE 0 to 0.09 percent C"]:::phase
    A2["AUSTENITE GAMMA 0 to 2.14 percent C"]:::phase
    A3["ALPHA FERRITE 0 to 0.022 percent C"]:::phase
    A4["CEMENTITE Fe3C 6.67 percent C"]:::phase
    A5["PEARLITE 0.83 percent C"]:::micro
    A6["LEDEBURITE 4.3 percent C"]:::micro
    A7["HYPEREUTECTOID STEEL 0.83 to 2.14 percent C"]:::steel
    A8["EUTECTOID STEEL 0.83 percent C"]:::steel
    A9["HYPoeutectoid STEEL 0.022 to 0.83 percent C"]:::steel
    A10["HYPEREUTECTIC CI 4.3 to 6.67 percent C"]:::ci
    A11["EUTECTIC CI 4.3 percent C"]:::ci
    A12["HYPoeutectic CI 2.14 to 4.3 percent C"]:::ci

    A0 --> A1
    A1 --> A2
    A2 --> A3
    A2 --> A4
    A3 --> A5
    A2 --> A6
    A2 --> A7
    A2 --> A8
    A2 --> A9
    A6 --> A10
    A6 --> A11
    A6 --> A12

    R1["PERITECTIC 1493 C 0.16 C: L plus DELTA to GAMMA"]:::react
    R2["EUTECTIC 1147 C 4.3 C: L to GAMMA plus Fe3C"]:::react
    R3["EUTECTOID 727 C 0.83 C: GAMMA to ALPHA plus Fe3C"]:::react

    R1 -.-> A1
    R2 -.-> A6
    R3 -.-> A5

    classDef start fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff,stroke:#0f172a,stroke-width:2px
    classDef phase fill:#0ea5e9,color:#ffffff,stroke:#0369a1,stroke-width:1px
    classDef micro fill:#22c55e,color:#ffffff,stroke:#15803d,stroke-width:1px
    classDef steel fill:#f59e0b,color:#1f2937,stroke:#b45309,stroke-width:1px
    classDef ci fill:#a855f7,color:#ffffff,stroke:#6b21a8,stroke-width:1px
    classDef react fill:#ef4444,color:#ffffff,stroke:#7f1d1d,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram ‚Äî Functional Architecture of Cooling in Hypoeutectoid Steel (0.4% C)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    B0["START: MOLTEN STEEL 0.4 percent C at 1600 C"]:::start
    B1["Cool to Liquidus 1500 C: First solid forms Delta Ferrite"]:::phase
    B2["Cool to Solidus 1493 C: Peritectic Reaction completes"]:::react
    B3["All AUSTENITE gamma region 0.4 percent C"]:::phase
    B4["Cool through A3 boundary 850 C: Proeutectoid Ferrite nucleates at grain boundaries"]:::phase
    B5["Continue cooling: Ferrite grows, Austenite enriches in C toward 0.83 percent"]:::phase
    B6["Reach A1 727 C: EUTECTOID Reaction gamma to alpha plus Fe3C"]:::react
    B7["ROOM TEMPERATURE: Ferrite plus Pearlite microstructure"]:::final
    B8["PROEUTECTOID FERRITE 53.22 percent"]:::out
    B9["PEARLITE 46.78 percent"]:::out
    B10["Within Pearlite: Lamellar alpha plus Fe3C"]:::out

    B0 --> B1 --> B2 --> B3 --> B4 --> B5 --> B6 --> B7
    B7 --> B8
    B7 --> B9
    B9 --> B10

    classDef start fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff,stroke:#0f172a,stroke-width:2px
    classDef phase fill:#0ea5e9,color:#ffffff,stroke:#0369a1,stroke-width:1px
    classDef react fill:#ef4444,color:#ffffff,stroke:#7f1d1d,stroke-width:2px
    classDef final fill:#22c55e,color:#ffffff,stroke:#15803d,stroke-width:2px
    classDef out fill:#f59e0b,color:#1f2937,stroke:#b45309,stroke-width:1px
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Tie-Line Schematic for 0.4% C Steel at 750 ¬∞C

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1["ALPHA FERRITE 0.022 percent C"]:::left
    L2["ALLOY COMPOSITION 0.4 percent C at 750 C"]:::mid
    L3["AUSTENITE 0.83 percent C at 727 C"]:::right
    L1 --- L2 --- L3
    L2 --> F1["Fraction alpha = 0.43 divided by 0.808 = 53.22 percent"]:::frac
    L2 --> F2["Fraction gamma = 0.378 divided by 0.808 = 46.78 percent"]:::frac

    classDef left fill:#0ea5e9,color:#ffffff
    classDef mid fill:#f59e0b,color:#1f2937,stroke:#b45309,stroke-width:3px
    classDef right fill:#22c55e,color:#ffffff
    classDef frac fill:#fef3c7,color:#1f2937,stroke:#a16207
```

## 4.4 Iron-Carbon Diagram ‚Äî Critical Points and Lines

> [!NOTE]
> **Important Lines of the Fe‚ÄìFe‚ÇÉC Diagram (Kerala University board standard):**
> - **ABC** ‚Äî Liquidus (above this, fully liquid)
> - **AHJECF** ‚Äî Solidus (below this, fully solid)
> - **JN** ‚Äî Solvus (carbon solubility limit in Œ¥-ferrite)
> - **ES** ‚Äî Solvus (carbon solubility limit in austenite) ‚Äî the **A_{cm}** line
> - **GS** ‚Äî **A‚ÇÉ line** (Œ≥ ‚Üí Œ± + Œ≥ boundary)
> - **PQ** ‚Äî Solvus (carbon solubility limit in Œ±-ferrite)
> - **MO** ‚Äî Curie transition (A‚ÇÇ) ‚Äî magnetic to non-magnetic
> - **PSK** ‚Äî **A‚ÇÅ line** (Eutectoid isotherm at 727 ¬∞C)
> - **CD** ‚Äî Solidus on the cementite side
> - **DF** ‚Äî Liquidus (Cementite primary crystallization)
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ‚Äî Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam ‚Äì May 2023]**
**Define the Iron-Carbon Equilibrium Diagram and list all the invariant reactions occurring in it.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Iron-Carbon Equilibrium Diagram is a binary constitutional map that shows the stable phases and phase transformations in Fe‚ÄìC alloys under equilibrium (very slow) cooling, plotted between **0% C (pure iron)** and **6.67% C (Fe‚ÇÉC / cementite)** as a function of temperature.

The three invariant reactions are:
1. **Peritectic Reaction at 1493 ¬∞C, 0.16% C:** $L + \delta \rightarrow \gamma$
2. **Eutectic Reaction at 1147 ¬∞C, 4.3% C:** $L \rightarrow \gamma + Fe_3C$ (forms **Ledeburite**)
3. **Eutectoid Reaction at 727 ¬∞C, 0.83% C:** $\gamma \rightarrow \alpha + Fe_3C$ (forms **Pearlite**)

*(1 mark for definition + 2 marks for listing all 3 reactions with their T & C values)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam ‚Äì Dec 2022]**
**Distinguish between Ferrite, Austenite, and Cementite with respect to crystal structure, carbon solubility, and mechanical properties.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Property | Ferrite (Œ±) | Austenite (Œ≥) | Cementite (Fe‚ÇÉC) |
|----------|-------------|---------------|-------------------|
| Crystal structure | **BCC** | **FCC** | **Orthorhombic** |
| Max carbon solubility | **0.022%** at 727 ¬∞C | **2.14%** at 1147 ¬∞C | **6.67%** (fixed) |
| Hardness | Soft (~80 HB) | Moderate (ductile) | Very hard (~1000 HB) |
| Magnetic | **Ferromagnetic** | Non-magnetic | Non-magnetic |

*(1 mark each for crystal structure, solubility, and properties of all three)*

---

## 5.2 Part B ‚Äî Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question A ‚Äî [KTU University Exam ‚Äì July 2024]**
**(a)** Draw the Iron-Carbon Equilibrium Diagram and label all the important phases, points, lines, and temperatures. Briefly explain the **Eutectoid reaction** in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **hypoeutectoid steel** contains **0.5 wt% C**. Calculate the weight percentages of:
(i) Proeutectoid ferrite and pearlite at room temperature.
(ii) Total cementite and total ferrite in the alloy.
**(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution to Question A (a) ‚Äî 7 marks**

**Drawing the diagram (3 marks):** The student should draw the Fe‚ÄìFe‚ÇÉC diagram with axes: temperature (¬∞C) on Y-axis from 0 to 1600, and wt% C on X-axis from 0 to 6.67. All critical points (A, H, J, N, E, C, D, B, P, S, K, G, Q, F, M, O) should be labelled. The three single-phase regions (Œ±, Œ≥, Œ¥, L, Fe‚ÇÉC) and the various two-phase regions should be clearly marked.

**Eutectoid Reaction Explanation (4 marks):**

> The **Eutectoid reaction** occurs at **727 ¬∞C (A‚ÇÅ temperature)** and **0.83% C** composition.
> $$\gamma_{(0.83\%\,C)} \xrightarrow{727\,¬∞C} \alpha_{(0.022\%\,C)} + Fe_3C_{(6.67\%\,C)}$$

- The parent **austenite phase** (FCC, single phase) decomposes on slow cooling into **two new solid phases simultaneously** ‚Äî Œ±-ferrite (BCC) and cementite (Fe‚ÇÉC).
- This is called a **solid-state reaction** (no liquid involved).
- The resulting microstructure is a **fine lamellar (alternating plate-like) mixture** of Œ± and Fe‚ÇÉC, known as **Pearlite**.
- The name "pearlite" comes from its **pearly, iridescent appearance** under the microscope due to light diffraction from the thin lamellae.
- Pearlite has a **mechanical mixture** character ‚Äî its properties lie between the soft ferrite and the hard cementite.
- This reaction is the **basis of all heat treatment of steels** ‚Äî by controlling the cooling rate, the austenite can be transformed to pearlite, bainite, or martensite.

---

### **Model Solution to Question A (b) ‚Äî 7 marks**

**Given:** C‚ÇÄ = 0.5% C (hypoeutectoid steel), C_Œ± = 0.022%, C_{eutectoid} = 0.83%, C_{Fe‚ÇÉC} = 6.67%

**(i) Proeutectoid Ferrite and Pearlite at Room Temperature (3 marks):**

At 727 ¬∞C, the remaining austenite is at eutectoid composition (0.83% C). So:

$$\text{Proeutectoid } \alpha = \dfrac{0.83 - 0.50}{0.83 - 0.022} = \dfrac{0.33}{0.808} = 0.4084$$

$$\boxed{\text{Proeutectoid Ferrite} = 40.84\%}$$

$$\text{Pearlite} = 1 - 0.4084 = 0.5916 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \boxed{\text{Pearlite} = 59.16\%}$$

**(ii) Total Ferrite and Total Cementite (4 marks):**

Total ferrite = Proeutectoid ferrite + Ferrite within pearlite:

$$\alpha_{\text{in pearlite}} = \text{Pearlite fraction} \times \dfrac{6.67 - 0.83}{6.67 - 0.022} = 0.5916 \times \dfrac{5.84}{6.648}$$

$$\alpha_{\text{in pearlite}} = 0.5916 \times 0.8785 = 0.5198$$

$$\alpha_{\text{total}} = 0.4084 + 0.5198 = 0.9282 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \boxed{\text{Total Ferrite} = 92.82\%}$$

$$\text{Total Fe}_3\text{C} = 1 - 0.9282 = 0.0718 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \boxed{\text{Total Cementite} = 7.18\%}$$

**Verification:** Total = 92.82% + 7.18% = 100% ‚úì

---

### **Question B ‚Äî [KTU University Exam ‚Äì Dec 2023] ‚Äî Alternative Choice**
**(a)** With a neat sketch of the Iron-Carbon phase diagram, explain the following:
(i) Peritectic reaction and Eutectic reaction.
(ii) Classification of Fe‚ÄìC alloys based on carbon content. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **hypereutectoid steel** contains **1.2% C**. Calculate the weight percentages of proeutectoid cementite, pearlite, total cementite, and total ferrite. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution to Question B (a) ‚Äî 7 marks**

**Sketch (2 marks)** ‚Äî Neat Fe-Fe‚ÇÉC diagram with proper labels.

**(i) Peritectic & Eutectic Reactions (3 marks):**

**Peritectic Reaction (1493 ¬∞C, 0.16% C):**
$$L_{(0.53\%\,C)} + \delta_{(0.09\%\,C)} \xrightarrow{1493\,¬∞C} \gamma_{(0.16\%\,C)}$$

On cooling, the remaining liquid reacts with the already-solidified Œ¥-ferrite to form a new solid phase ‚Äî austenite (Œ≥). This occurs at a fixed temperature and fixed compositions of all three phases.

**Eutectic Reaction (1147 ¬∞C, 4.3% C):**
$$L_{(4.3\%\,C)} \xrightarrow{1147\,¬∞C} \gamma_{(2.14\%\,C)} + Fe_3C$$

A single liquid phase at 4.3% C transforms into two solid phases (austenite + cementite) simultaneously at 1147 ¬∞C. The resulting microstructure is called **Ledeburite**. This is the basis of **cast iron** metallurgy.

**(ii) Classification (2 marks):** As shown in the table in Section 2.5 (Steels: 0‚Äì2.14% C; Cast Irons: 2.14‚Äì6.67% C). Further divided into hypo/hyper eutectoid and hypo/hyper eutectic sub-classes.

---

### **Model Solution to Question B (b) ‚Äî 7 marks**

**Given:** C‚ÇÄ = 1.2% C, C_{Fe‚ÇÉC} = 6.67%, C_{eutectoid} = 0.83%, C_Œ± = 0.022%

**Step 1 ‚Äî Proeutectoid Cementite (2 marks):**

$$\text{Proeutectoid Fe}_3\text{C} = \dfrac{1.2 - 0.83}{6.67 - 0.83} = \dfrac{0.37}{5.84} = 0.0634$$

$$\boxed{\text{Proeutectoid Cementite} = 6.34\%}$$

**Step 2 ‚Äî Pearlite (1 mark):**

$$\text{Pearlite} = 1 - 0.0634 = 0.9366 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \boxed{93.66\%}$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Total Cementite (2 marks):**

$$\text{Fe}_3\text{C in Pearlite} = 0.9366 \times \dfrac{0.83 - 0.022}{6.67 - 0.022} = 0.9366 \times 0.1215 = 0.1138$$

$$\text{Total Fe}_3\text{C} = 0.0634 + 0.1138 = 0.1772 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \boxed{17.72\%}$$

**Step 4 ‚Äî Total Ferrite (2 marks):**

$$\text{Total Ferrite} = 1 - 0.1772 = 0.8228 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \boxed{82.28\%}$$

**Verification:** 17.72% + 82.28% = 100% ‚úì

---

## 5.3 ‚öÝÔ∏è KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ‚Äî Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Typically Lose Marks (KTU Examiner's Perspective):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the Compositions of Œ± and Fe‚ÇÉC:** Many students use 0% C for ferrite and 100% C for cementite in lever-rule calculations. This yields **wrong numerical answers** and the examiner deducts 2‚Äì3 marks.
>    - ‚úÖ **Correct:** Use **C_Œ± = 0.022%** and **C_{Fe‚ÇÉC} = 6.67%** at room temperature.
>
> 2. **Confusing Proeutectoid vs. Total Constituents:** Always state clearly whether the answer is "proeutectoid" or "total" ferrite/cementite. Mixing these up is a **1‚Äì2 mark deduction**.
>
> 3. **Skipping Units:** Always write **wt% C**, ¬∞C, and "% by weight". Marks are deducted for missing units.
>
> 4. **Not Drawing the Tie-Line:** In lever-rule problems, the KTU examiner expects a **sketch of the tie-line** at the temperature of interest, with all compositions marked. Skipping the diagram costs **1 mark**.
>
> 5. **Confusing Peritectic, Eutectic, and Eutectoid:** These three terms look similar but are completely different reactions. A common mistake is to call the eutectoid reaction "eutectic." This is a **major conceptual error** ‚Äî 2 marks deducted.
>
> 6. **Missing the Distinction between Phases and Microconstituents:** Pearlite is a **microconstituent** (mixture of two phases), not a phase. The same applies to Ledeburite. Examiners test this ‚Äî losing 1 mark is common.

---

## 5.4 üìù Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for ESE:**

### üîë The Three "Must-Know" Temperatures
- **A‚ÇÅ = 727 ¬∞C** ‚Äî Eutectoid isotherm
- **A‚ÇÉ ‚âà 912 ¬∞C** (at 0% C) ‚Äî Œ≥ ‚Üí Œ± boundary
- **A_{cm} ‚âà 1147 ¬∞C** (at 2.14% C) ‚Äî Œ≥ + Fe‚ÇÉC ‚Üí Œ≥ solvus

### üîë The Three "Must-Know" Compositions
- **0.022% C** ‚Äî Maximum carbon in Œ±-ferrite (at 727 ¬∞C)
- **0.83% C** ‚Äî Eutectoid composition (Pearlite)
- **2.14% C** ‚Äî Maximum carbon in austenite (at 1147 ¬∞C)
- **4.3% C** ‚Äî Eutectic composition (Ledeburite)
- **6.67% C** ‚Äî Cementite (Fe‚ÇÉC) ‚Äî fixed composition

### üîë The Three "Must-Know" Invariant Reactions
| Reaction | Temperature | Composition | Product Microconstituent |
|----------|-------------|-------------|--------------------------|
| Peritectic | 1493 ¬∞C | 0.16% C | (Œ≥ austenite) |
| Eutectic | 1147 ¬∞C | 4.3% C | **Ledeburite** |
| Eutectoid | 727 ¬∞C | 0.83% C | **Pearlite** |

### üîë The Three "Must-Know" Phases
- **Ferrite (Œ±):** BCC, soft, ductile, magnetic, 0.022% C max
- **Austenite (Œ≥):** FCC, ductile, non-magnetic, 2.14% C max
- **Cementite (Fe‚ÇÉC):** Orthorhombic, hard & brittle, 6.67% C

### üîë The One "Must-Know" Formula
$$\boxed{\text{Fraction of phase} = \dfrac{\vert C_0 - C_{\text{opposite end}} \vert}{\vert C_{\text{left end}} - C_{\text{right end}} \vert}}$$

### üîë The "Must-Memorize" Classification
- **Steels:** 0 to 2.14% C (workable, heat-treatable)
- **Cast Irons:** 2.14 to 6.67% C (cast, not workable)

### üîë The "Must-Remember" Hypo vs. Hyper Rule
- **Hypo-** = less than the invariant point composition (e.g., hypoeutectoid = < 0.83% C)
- **Hyper-** = more than the invariant point composition (e.g., hypereutectoid = > 0.83% C)
- **Eutect-** = exactly at the invariant point composition

### üîë Pearlite = Lamellar (Œ± + Fe‚ÇÉC); Ledeburite = Lamellar (Œ≥ + Fe‚ÇÉC) at high T, transforms to (Œ± + Fe‚ÇÉC) below 727 ¬∞C.

> **Final Tip:** Always start your ESE answer with a **neatly drawn, fully labeled Fe-Fe‚ÇÉC diagram** ‚Äî examiners allocate at least 3 marks for the diagram alone, and a good diagram sets the tone for the rest of the answer.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Classification of steel and cast iron: microstructure, properties, and applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAUT304 - MATERIAL SCIENCE AND METALLURGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Classification of steel and cast iron: microstructure, properties, and applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are Ferrous Alloys?

**Ferrous alloys** are metal alloys whose principal component is **iron (Fe)**. They are broadly classified into two major engineering families based on **carbon (C) content** and the resulting microstructure:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> A **ferrous alloy** is an alloy based on iron as the primary solvent metal, with carbon as the dominant interstitial alloying element. The phase equilibrium, microstructure, and mechanical response of ferrous alloys are dictated by the **Iron‚ÄìIron Carbide (Fe‚ÄìFe‚ÇÉC) metastable phase diagram**.

| Alloy Family | Carbon Range (wt %) | Defining Characteristic |
|---|---|---|
| **Steels** | 0.008 % ‚Äì 2.14 % | Ductile, malleable, wrought, can be hardened by heat treatment |
| **Cast Irons** | 2.14 % ‚Äì 6.70 % (typically 2.5 ‚Äì 4.5 %) | High fluidity, brittle, cast into complex shapes, contain graphite or massive Fe‚ÇÉC |

The **eutectic point** of the Fe‚ÄìFe‚ÇÉC system at **4.30 % C and 1147 ¬∞C** forms the metallurgical "Great Wall" separating steels from cast irons.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ‚Äî The "Concrete and Rebar" Model

Imagine building a skyscraper:
- **Ferrite (Œ±-Fe, BCC)** is the **concrete** ‚Äî soft, ductile, the bulk background matrix.
- **Cementite (Fe‚ÇÉC)** is the **steel rebar** ‚Äî extremely hard, brittle, but provides strength.
- **Pearlite** is a **pre-stressed concrete** ‚Äî alternating soft ferrite and hard cementite lamellae, giving balanced strength and toughness.
- **Graphite flakes/nodules** in cast iron are like **embedded air-pockets or pebbles** ‚Äî they relieve stress (dampen vibration) but act as internal cracks (reduce tensile strength).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this analogy matters in KTU exams:** Examiners frequently award marks when you correlate *microstructure* ‚Üí *properties* ‚Üí *applications*. Always start any answer with: *"The microstructure consists of ‚Ä¶ which imparts ‚Ä¶ making it suitable for ‚Ä¶"*

## 1.3 Standard Metrics (Bolded as Per Protocol)

- Eutectoid composition: **0.76 wt % C**
- Eutectic composition: **4.30 wt % C**
- Maximum carbon solubility in Œ≥-austenite: **2.14 wt % at 1147 ¬∞C**
- Maximum carbon solubility in Œ±-ferrite: **0.022 wt % at 727 ¬∞C**
- Hardness of cementite: **~1000 HV (Brinell equivalent ‚âà 65‚Äì70 HRC)**
- Hardness of pure ferrite: **~80 HV**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Steels

Steels are classified by **three orthogonal schemes** that the KTU 2024 examiner expects you to interlink:

### 2.1.1 Scheme A ‚Äî Based on Carbon Content

| Class | Carbon (wt %) | Microstructure (Slow Cool) | Hardness (HB) | UTS (MPa) | Ductility (% El) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Dead Mild / Low-Carbon Steel** | < 0.15 | Ferrite + small pearlite | 80 ‚Äì 120 | 300 ‚Äì 430 | 25 ‚Äì 35 |
| **Mild / Low-Carbon Steel** | 0.15 ‚Äì 0.25 | Ferrite + pearlite | 120 ‚Äì 160 | 400 ‚Äì 500 | 22 ‚Äì 30 |
| **Medium-Carbon Steel** | 0.25 ‚Äì 0.60 | Ferrite + pearlite (or tempered martensite) | 160 ‚Äì 250 | 500 ‚Äì 800 | 14 ‚Äì 22 |
| **High-Carbon Steel** | 0.60 ‚Äì 1.00 | Pearlite + proeutectoid cementite | 250 ‚Äì 400 | 800 ‚Äì 1200 | 5 ‚Äì 12 |
| **Ultra-High-Carbon Steel** | 1.00 ‚Äì 2.14 | Pearlite + proeutectoid Fe‚ÇÉC network | 400 ‚Äì 700+ | 1200 ‚Äì 2000+ | < 5 |

### 2.1.2 Scheme B ‚Äî Based on Alloying Elements

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Line:** *Plain carbon steels contain only Fe + C + Mn (‚â§ 1.0 %) + Si (‚â§ 0.3 %) + S, P (residual). Any steel with deliberate addition of other alloying elements (Ni, Cr, Mo, V, W, Co, etc.) above standard residual levels is an **alloy steel**.*

| Sub-class | Total Alloying (%) | Example Grades | Salient Feature |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Low-alloy steel** | < 5 | 4140, 4340, 8620 | Strengthened by quench + temper; used in gears, shafts |
| **High-alloy steel** | > 5 | H11, H13, D2, M2, 304, 316 | Corrosion/heat resistant; used in tooling, chemical plant |

### 2.1.3 Scheme C ‚Äî Based on Application / Heat Treatment

- **Structural steels** (e.g., ASTM A36, Fe 410) ‚Äî building frames.
- **Stainless steels** ‚Äî Cr ‚â• 10.5 %, passive Cr‚ÇÇO‚ÇÉ film.
- **Tool steels** ‚Äî W, Mo, V, Co; high hot-hardness.
- **High-Strength Low-Alloy (HSLA)** ‚Äî microalloyed with Nb, V, Ti.
- **Maraging steels** ‚Äî Ni 18 %, age-hardened intermetallics.
- **Hadfield manganese steel** ‚Äî 12 % Mn, work-hardens to 550 HB.
- **Dual-Phase (DP), TRIP, TWIP** ‚Äî automotive advanced high-strength steels (AHSS).

## 2.2 Classification of Cast Irons

Cast irons differ from steels in that they contain **graphite** (stable system, Fe‚ÄìC) or **massive cementite** (metastable system, Fe‚ÄìFe‚ÇÉC).

### 2.2.1 The Five Commercial Families

| Type | Carbon Form | Matrix | Tensile Strength (MPa) | Ductility (% El) | Key Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **White CI** | Fe‚ÇÉC (massive) | Pearlite + ledeburite | 200 ‚Äì 400 | < 0.5 | Wear surfaces, crusher jaws (abrasion resistant) |
| **Malleable CI** | Temper graphite (rosettes) | Ferrite / Pearlite | 300 ‚Äì 700 | 2 ‚Äì 10 | Automotive connecting rods, axle housings |
| **Gray CI** | Graphite flakes (Type A) | Ferrite / Pearlite / Ferrite-Pearlite | 150 ‚Äì 400 | < 1.0 | Engine blocks, machine tool beds (damping) |
| **Ductile / Nodular / SG Iron** | Graphite spheroids | Ferrite / Pearlite / Bainite | 400 ‚Äì 700+ | 2 ‚Äì 18 | Pipes, crankshafts, gears |
| **Compacted Graphite Iron (CGI)** | Short, thick, interconnected flakes | Pearlite / Ferrite-Pearlite | 300 ‚Äì 500 | 1.5 ‚Äì 4 | Diesel engine heads, brake components |

> [!NOTE]
> **Malleable iron is NOT a cast iron grade ‚Äî it is a heat-treated white cast iron.** KTU 2024 examiners frequently test this distinction.

### 2.2.2 Why Cast Irons are NOT simply "high-carbon steels"

1. **Silicon content (1 ‚Äì 3 %)** ‚Äî acts as a strong graphitizer, destabilizing Fe‚ÇÉC and promoting graphite formation.
2. **Crystallization pathway** ‚Äî slow cooling from the melt promotes graphitization; rapid cooling (chill) freezes the structure as white iron.
3. **Liquid fluidity** ‚Äî the eutectic reaction at 4.3 % C gives a low-melting, highly fluid liquid ‚Üí superior castability into intricate shapes.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Relationship | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | % Ferrite (in eutectoid steel, 0.76 %C) = 100 % pearlite | Lever rule at 727 ¬∞C just below eutectoid | wt % |
| 2 | % Proeutectoid Ferrite (hypoeutectoid, 0.4 %C) = $\dfrac{0.76 - 0.40}{0.76 - 0.022} \times 100$ | Lever rule between Œ± and Fe‚ÇÉC | wt % |
| 3 | % Proeutectoid Cementite (hypereutectoid, 1.2 %C) = $\dfrac{1.20 - 0.76}{6.70 - 0.76} \times 100$ | Lever rule between Œ≥ and Fe‚ÇÉC | wt % |
| 4 | Distributive Law: % Ferrite (Œ±) = 100 ‚àí % Pearlite (P) | Mass balance at 727 ¬∞C | wt % |
| 5 | Volume fraction conversion: $V_f = \dfrac{W_f / \rho_\alpha}{W_f / \rho_\alpha + W_p / \rho_p}$ | œÅ-ferrite = 7.87 g/cc, œÅ-pearlite ‚âà 7.85 g/cc | dimensionless |
| 6 | Hardness rule (mixture): $H_{mix} = V_f \cdot H_\alpha + V_p \cdot H_p$ | Linear rule of mixtures for ferrite-pearlite steels | HB/HV |
| 7 | Empirical UTS vs Brinell: $UTS (MPa) \approx 3.45 \times HB$ | Valid for plain-carbon ferrite-pearlite steels | MPa |

> [!WARNING]
> In markdown tables, **NEVER** use the bare vertical pipe `|` to denote absolute value ‚Äî always use `\vert` or `\mid` in LaTeX to avoid breaking the table grid.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Selection matrix:** Automotive crankshaft ‚Üí Ductile iron (fatigue + castability). Engine block ‚Üí Gray iron (damping + machinability). Excavator bucket lip ‚Üí White iron (abrasion resistance).
- **Failure analysis:** Knowing the graphite morphology tells the failure investigator whether the part was cast iron or steel and what its expected service behavior was.
- **Heat treatment design:** Plain-carbon steels respond to quench-and-temper; austenitic stainless steels do not (carbide-free FCC).
- **Cost optimization:** Mild steel is the cheapest structural metal; alloying/heat treatment is justified only when plain steel cannot meet service requirements.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Lever Rule Derivations ‚Äî Worked Examples (KTU Board Style)

### Example 1: Phase Fractions in a Hypoeutectoid Steel (0.40 % C)

A medium-carbon steel with **0.40 wt % C** is slowly cooled to just below the eutectoid isotherm (727 ¬∞C). Determine the **weight fractions of proeutectoid ferrite and pearlite**.

**Step 1 ‚Äî Identify the relevant tie-line.**

Just below 727 ¬∞C, the two phases present are **Œ±-ferrite (composition 0.022 % C)** and **pearlite (composition 0.76 % C)**. The alloy composition line passes through these two endpoints.

**Step 2 ‚Äî Apply the mass balance.**

The sum of the weight fractions of the two phases must equal unity:

$$W_\alpha + W_P = 1$$

The carbon mass balance:

$$0.40 = W_\alpha \cdot (0.022) + W_P \cdot (0.76)$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Apply the lever rule.**

Using the inverse lever arm (a common KTU trick), the weight fraction of each phase is proportional to the **opposite** arm of the tie-line:

$$W_\alpha = \frac{0.76 - 0.40}{0.76 - 0.022} = \frac{0.36}{0.738} = 0.4878$$

**Step 4 ‚Äî Compute pearlite fraction.**

$$W_P = 1 - W_\alpha = 1 - 0.4878 = 0.5122$$

**Step 5 ‚Äî Express in percentage and verify.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_\alpha &\approx 48.8 \ \text{wt \%} \\
W_P &\approx 51.2 \ \text{wt \%}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification via carbon mass balance:**

$$
0.4878 \times 0.022 + 0.5122 \times 0.76 = 0.01073 + 0.38927 = 0.4000 \ \text{wt \% C} \ \checkmark
$$

### Example 2: Hypereutectoid Steel (1.20 % C)

A hypereutectoid steel with **1.20 wt % C** is slowly cooled to just below 727 ¬∞C. Find **proeutectoid cementite and pearlite fractions**.

**Step 1 ‚Äî At 727 ¬∞C, the two phases are: pearlite (0.76 % C) and proeutectoid cementite (6.70 % C).**

**Step 2 ‚Äî Apply the lever rule.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{Fe_3C}^{pro} &= \frac{1.20 - 0.76}{6.70 - 0.76} = \frac{0.44}{5.94} = 0.0741 \\[4pt]
W_P &= 1 - 0.0741 = 0.9259
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Sub-divide pearlite into ferrite + eutectoid cementite.**

Pearlite itself is 88 % ferrite + 12 % cementite by mass (since it has 0.76 % C, and ferrite carries 0.022 % C, cementite 6.70 % C):

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{Fe_3C}^{pearlite} &= \frac{0.76 - 0.022}{6.70 - 0.022} = \frac{0.738}{6.678} = 0.1105 \\
W_\alpha^{pearlite} &= 1 - 0.1105 = 0.8895
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 ‚Äî Total cementite in the alloy.**

$$
W_{Fe_3C}^{total} = W_{Fe_3C}^{pro} + W_P \cdot W_{Fe_3C}^{pearlite} = 0.0741 + 0.9259 \times 0.1105
$$

$$
W_{Fe_3C}^{total} = 0.0741 + 0.1023 = 0.1764 \ \ (\text{i.e., } 17.6 \ \text{wt \%})
$$

**Step 5 ‚Äî Total ferrite in the alloy.**

$$
W_\alpha^{total} = 0.9259 \times 0.8895 = 0.8236 \ \ (\text{i.e., } 82.4 \ \text{wt \%})
$$

**Step 6 ‚Äî Sanity check.**

$$
0.8236 \times 0.022 + 0.1764 \times 6.70 = 0.01812 + 1.18188 = 1.200 \ \text{wt \% C} \ \checkmark
$$

## 3.2 Microstructure-Property Analytical Matrix (KTU 14-Mark Style)

The KTU 2024 examiner expects you to be able to *cross-correlate* composition, microstructure, mechanical properties, and applications. Use this analytical matrix as your mental template.

| Composition Range | Phases in Slow-Cooled State | Hardness | Strength | Ductility | Weldability | Machinability | Typical Uses |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| < 0.10 % C (dead mild) | All ferrite, trace grain-boundary cementite | Very low | Low | Excellent | Excellent | Poor (gummy) | Car body panels, wire, nails, tin cans |
| 0.15 ‚Äì 0.30 % C (mild) | Ferrite + small pearlite (15 ‚Äì 30 %) | Low‚Äìmedium | Medium | Very good | Excellent | Good | Structural beams, bridges, pipelines, ship plates |
| 0.30 ‚Äì 0.50 % C (medium) | Ferrite + pearlite (35 ‚Äì 55 %) | Medium | Medium‚Äìhigh | Good | Fair (pre-heat needed) | Good | Railway wheels, axles, gears, crankshafts |
| 0.50 ‚Äì 0.70 % C | Pearlite + ferrite (diminishing) | High | High | Moderate | Poor | Good | Springs, agricultural implements |
| 0.70 ‚Äì 0.90 % C | Fully pearlitic to pearlite + proeutectoid Fe‚ÇÉC | High | High | Moderate | Very poor (cracking) | Excellent | High-strength wires, chisels, punches, files |
| 0.90 ‚Äì 1.10 % C | Pearlite + grain-boundary Fe‚ÇÉC network | High | High | Low | Not recommended | Excellent | Razor blades, cutting tools (water-quenched) |
| 1.10 ‚Äì 1.50 % C | Pearlite + heavy Fe‚ÇÉC network | Very high | Very high | Very low | Not used | Excellent | Springs, cutlery, punches, dies, saws |
| Cast irons (2.5 ‚Äì 4.5 % C, + 1‚Äì3 % Si) | Graphite form dominates; matrix = ferrite / pearlite / ledeburite | Variable | Variable | Low | Not welded (usually) | Excellent (except white CI) | Engine blocks, pipes, brake drums, machine tool beds |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** *When the question says "explain with reference to Fe‚ÄìFe‚ÇÉC diagram", always draw a tie-line on a labelled phase diagram in the answer script and annotate the carbon compositions of the phases in equilibrium. This is worth **2 easy marks** that students often skip.*

## 3.3 Worked Example ‚Äî Mechanical Property Estimation (Rule of Mixtures)

A eutectoid steel has a ferrite hardness of **80 HB** and a pearlite hardness of **220 HB**. The pearlite constitutes **51.2 %** by weight. Estimate the **alloy hardness** assuming rule of mixtures with density correction.

**Step 1 ‚Äî Compute volume fractions.**

Using $\rho_\alpha = 7.87$ g/cc and $\rho_P = 7.85$ g/cc (slightly less dense due to cementite presence):

$$
V_\alpha = \frac{0.488 / 7.87}{0.488 / 7.87 + 0.512 / 7.85} = \frac{0.06201}{0.06201 + 0.06522} = \frac{0.06201}{0.12723} = 0.4873
$$

$$
V_P = 1 - 0.4873 = 0.5127
$$

**Step 2 ‚Äî Apply rule of mixtures for hardness.**

$$
H_{alloy} = V_\alpha \cdot H_\alpha + V_P \cdot H_P = 0.4873 \times 80 + 0.5127 \times 220
$$

$$
H_{alloy} = 38.98 + 112.79 = 151.77 \ \text{HB}
$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Predict UTS using the empirical correlation.**

$$
UTS = 3.45 \times HB = 3.45 \times 151.77 \approx 523.6 \ \text{MPa}
$$

**Step 4 ‚Äî Cross-check with handbooks.**

A 0.40 % C hot-rolled steel typically has UTS ‚âà 500 ‚Äì 550 MPa, which matches our estimate. ‚úì
