# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ‚Äî Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam ‚Äì May 2024, Set B]

> **Q: Differentiate between ferrite, austenite, and cementite with respect to crystal structure, carbon solubility, and mechanical properties.**

**Model Answer (Concise ‚Äî KTU Expects 3 Crisp Points √ó 1 Mark Each):**

| Phase | Crystal Structure | Max Carbon Solubility | Hardness (HV) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Ferrite (Œ±-Fe)** | BCC | 0.022 % at 727 ¬∞C | 80 (softest) |
| **Austenite (Œ≥-Fe)** | FCC | 2.14 % at 1147 ¬∞C | 200 (tough, ductile) |
| **Cementite (Fe‚ÇÉC)** | Orthorhombic (intermetallic) | 6.70 % (stoichiometric) | ~1000 (hardest, brittle) |

- **Ferrite** is the soft, ductile, magnetic phase stable at room temperature in low-carbon steels.
- **Austenite** is the high-temperature ductile FCC phase, non-magnetic, capable of dissolving up to 2.14 % C ‚Äî the basis of all heat treatment.
- **Cementite** is a hard, brittle intermetallic compound that strengthens steel by load transfer but reduces ductility when present as a continuous network.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam ‚Äì Dec 2023, Model Paper]

> **Q: Why is malleable cast iron preferred over white cast iron for components requiring impact loading?**

**Model Answer:**

- White cast iron contains **massive Fe‚ÇÉC (ledeburite)**, making it extremely hard (~400 HB) and **brittle** (impact energy < 5 J) ‚Äî it fails catastrophically under shock.
- Malleable iron is produced by **annealing white cast iron at 800 ‚Äì 900 ¬∞C for extended periods**, decomposing Fe‚ÇÉC into **temper graphite rosettes**.
- The rosette-shaped graphite has **rounded, stress-free morphologies**, eliminating the notch effect of sharp flakes, and the matrix becomes **ferritic or pearlitic** with significant ductility (2 ‚Äì 10 % elongation, impact energy 10 ‚Äì 30 J).
- Hence malleable iron is used for **automotive connecting rods, axle housings, and agricultural implement yokes** that require shock resistance.
- **(1 mark)** for the phase change description, **(1 mark)** for the property improvement, **(1 mark)** for the application.

---

## 5.2 Part B ‚Äî Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, ESE Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Regulation:** *ESE Part B carries 14 marks per module. Each question has an OR-option. Sub-part (a) typically 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. The model answer below shows the examiner-key depth.*

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam ‚Äì May 2024, Set A, Module 3]

> **(a) Classify steels based on (i) carbon content and (ii) alloying elements. For each class, state the typical microstructure, two mechanical properties, and one engineering application. (7 marks)**
>
> **(b) With the help of the Fe‚ÄìFe‚ÇÉC equilibrium diagram, explain the eutectoid reaction. Determine the phase fractions of proeutectoid ferrite and pearlite in a 0.40 % C hypoeutectoid steel at a temperature just below 727 ¬∞C. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution ‚Äî (a)

**Step 1 ‚Äî Classification Table (3 marks):**

| Class | Carbon (wt %) | Microstructure | UTS (MPa) | Ductility (% El) | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Dead mild | < 0.15 | All ferrite | 300 ‚Äì 430 | 25 ‚Äì 35 | Car body panels, wire |
| Mild | 0.15 ‚Äì 0.30 | Ferrite + pearlite | 400 ‚Äì 500 | 22 ‚Äì 30 | Bridges, ship plates |
| Medium | 0.30 ‚Äì 0.60 | Ferrite + pearlite | 500 ‚Äì 800 | 14 ‚Äì 22 | Rails, gears, axles |
| High | 0.60 ‚Äì 1.00 | Pearlite + proeutectoid Fe‚ÇÉC | 800 ‚Äì 1200 | 5 ‚Äì 12 | Springs, chisels |
| Ultra-high | 1.00 ‚Äì 2.14 | Pearlite + Fe‚ÇÉC network | 1200+ | < 5 | Razor blades, dies |

**Step 2 ‚Äî Alloy Steel Classification (2 marks):**

- **Low-alloy steels** (< 5 % total alloy): 4140, 4340, 8620 ‚Äî used in gears, shafts; high hardenability.
- **High-alloy steels** (> 5 %): 304 stainless (Cr 18 %, Ni 8 %); H13 tool steel (Cr 5 %, Mo 1.5 %, V 1 %); maraging (Ni 18 %); Hadfield manganese (Mn 12 %).

**Step 3 ‚Äî Mechanism Connecting Composition to Properties (2 marks):**

- As C increases ‚Üí **pearlite fraction increases** (each increment of 0.1 % C adds ~12 % pearlite).
- Pearlite is a two-phase lamellar composite of hard Fe‚ÇÉC in soft ferrite ‚Üí raises **hardness and UTS** linearly.
- **Ductility decreases** because Fe‚ÇÉC lamellae block dislocation motion and act as crack initiators.
- Alloying elements form **fine carbides / solid-solution strengthening** ‚Üí enhance strength, hardenability, and special properties (corrosion, heat resistance).

#### Model Solution ‚Äî (b)

**Step 1 ‚Äî Eutectoid Reaction Explanation (3 marks):**

The eutectoid reaction occurs at **727 ¬∞C and 0.76 wt % C**:

$$\gamma \text{ (FCC, 0.76 % C)} \xrightarrow{727 \,^\circ\text{C}} \alpha \text{ (BCC, 0.022 % C)} + Fe_3C \text{ (6.70 % C)}$$

- The single solid Œ≥-phase decomposes into **two distinct solid phases** on cooling.
- Both phases grow as **alternating lamellae** ‚Üí pearlite, with ~88 % ferrite and ~12 % cementite by weight.
- Reaction is **invariant** (3 phases, T and C fixed) and **diffusion-controlled**.
- Above the eutectoid line (A‚ÇÅ = 727 ¬∞C) austenite is stable; below, ferrite + cementite are the equilibrium phases.

**Step 2 ‚Äî Phase-Fraction Calculation for 0.40 % C Steel (4 marks):**

Using the lever rule on the tie-line between Œ± (0.022 % C) and pearlite (0.76 % C):

$$
W_\alpha = \frac{0.76 - 0.40}{0.76 - 0.022} = \frac{0.36}{0.738} = 0.4878 \approx 48.8 \ \text{wt \%}
$$

$$
W_P = \frac{0.40 - 0.022}{0.76 - 0.022} = \frac{0.378}{0.738} = 0.5122 \approx 51.2 \ \text{wt \%}
$$

**Final answer:** **48.8 % proeutectoid ferrite** and **51.2 % pearlite**.

**Verification:**

$$
0.4878 \times 0.022 + 0.5122 \times 0.76 = 0.4000 \ \text{wt \% C} \ \checkmark
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam ‚Äì Dec 2023, Set A, Module 3]

> **(a) Compare the microstructure, mechanical properties, and applications of gray cast iron, white cast iron, and ductile (nodular) cast iron. (7 marks)**
>
> **(b) Explain the role of silicon in cast iron. With a neat sketch, describe the manufacturing process of malleable cast iron and state two applications. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution ‚Äî (a)

| Property | Gray CI | White CI | Ductile (SG) Iron |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Carbon form** | Graphite flakes (Type A) | Massive Fe‚ÇÉC (ledeburite) | Graphite spheroids (Mg-treated) |
| **Matrix** | Ferrite / Pearlite / Ferrite-Pearlite | Pearlite + ledeburite | Ferrite / Pearlite / Bainite |
| **Tensile strength** | 150 ‚Äì 400 MPa | 200 ‚Äì 400 MPa | 400 ‚Äì 700+ MPa |
| **Ductility (% El)** | < 1.0 % | Negligible (brittle) | 2 ‚Äì 18 % |
| **Hardness** | 150 ‚Äì 250 HB | 350 ‚Äì 550 HB | 150 ‚Äì 300 HB |
| **Damping capacity** | Excellent (graphite flakes) | Very poor | Good (less than gray) |
| **Machinability** | Excellent (graphite acts as chip-breaker) | Poor (abrasive) | Good |
| **Cost** | Lowest | Low | Moderate (Mg treatment cost) |
| **Typical applications** | Engine blocks, machine tool beds, manhole covers, brake drums | Crusher jaws, grinding balls, slurry pumps, wear liners | Water pipes, crankshafts, gear blanks, wind turbine hubs, heavy-duty valves |

**Explanation (4 marks):**

- **Gray cast iron** flakes act as internal stress concentrators ‚Üí low tensile strength, but they absorb vibrational energy ‚Üí excellent damping (machine tool beds).
- **White cast iron** has no free graphite; instead, all C is locked as Fe‚ÇÉC ‚Üí very hard and abrasion-resistant but brittle, used for wear surfaces.
- **Ductile iron** is produced by adding Mg or Ce to the melt before pouring, which de-wets the graphite and forces it to grow as spheroids. Spheroids minimize the stress-concentration effect, providing high strength AND ductility.

#### Model Solution ‚Äî (b)

**Step 1 ‚Äî Role of Silicon in Cast Iron (3 marks):**

- Silicon (typically 1 ‚Äì 3 %) is a **strong graphitizer** that destabilizes Fe‚ÇÉC and promotes the formation of free graphite during solidification.
- Each **1 % Si** is equivalent to ~0.3 % C in graphitizing power (it shifts the eutectic composition leftward in the phase diagram).
- Si also **strengthens the ferrite matrix** by solid-solution strengthening and reduces the cooling rate required for graphitization.
- Low Si (< 0.5 %) ‚Üí white iron; high Si (> 3 %) ‚Üí gray iron at standard cooling rates.

**Step 2 ‚Äî Manufacturing Process of Malleable Cast Iron (3 marks):**

1. **Cast as white iron:** Melt Fe + C (2.5 ‚Äì 3.5 %) + low Si (0.5 ‚Äì 1.0 %), pour into moulds, cool rapidly ‚Üí all C locked as Fe‚ÇÉC (white iron).
2. **Heat to malleabilizing temperature (800 ‚Äì 900 ¬∞C).**
3. **Hold for long time (20 ‚Äì 60 hours) in oxidizing/neutral atmosphere.** During this stage, metastable Fe‚ÇÉC decomposes via:
$$Fe_3C \rightarrow 3Fe (\alpha) + C \text{ (graphite)}$$
4. **Graphite nucleates as irregular rosettes (temper graphite / temper carbon).**
5. **Cool slowly to room temperature.**
6. Final structure: **ferrite (or pearlite) matrix with rosette graphite clusters**, providing ductility.

**Step 3 ‚Äî Applications (1 mark):**

- Automotive **connecting rods**, **axle housings**, **agricultural equipment brackets**, and **railway coupler yokes**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls ‚Äî Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to draw the Fe‚ÄìFe‚ÇÉC diagram** when the question says *"with the help of the Fe‚ÄìFe‚ÇÉC diagram"*. This is **2 marks lost instantly** in a 7-mark sub-part. Always draw a sketch ‚Äî even a labelled schematic is enough.
> 2. **Confusing malleable iron with ductile iron.** Malleable = heat-treated **white** iron; Ductile = Mg-treated liquid iron. Two completely different processes.
> 3. **Misapplying the lever rule** ‚Äî students often write 0.76 ‚àí 0.40 = 0.36 for ferrite fraction directly, forgetting to divide by the tie-line length 0.738.
> 4. **Writing "iron is hard" or "graphite is hard"** without specifying the matrix ‚Äî KTU expects phase + morphology + matrix description.
> 5. **Forgetting the role of Si** in cast iron ‚Äî graphitization is the most important concept in ferrous physical metallurgy.
> 6. **Not mentioning hypoeutectoid vs hypereutectoid** when giving proeutectoid phase names.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Bulleted Summary ‚Äî Revise Before Every Test**

- **Steel** = Fe + C (0.008 ‚Äì 2.14 %) ‚Üí wrought, ductile, heat-treatable.
- **Cast iron** = Fe + C (2.14 ‚Äì 6.70 %) + Si (1 ‚Äì 3 %) ‚Üí castable, contains graphite or Fe‚ÇÉC.
- **Eutectoid point:** 0.76 % C, 727 ¬∞C; **eutectic point:** 4.30 % C, 1147 ¬∞C.
- **Hypoeutectoid steel (< 0.76 % C)** ‚Üí proeutectoid ferrite + pearlite.
- **Hypereutectoid steel (> 0.76 % C)** ‚Üí proeutectoid cementite + pearlite.
- **Plain carbon steel**: only Fe, C, Mn, Si, S, P residuals.
- **Alloy steel**: deliberate addition of Ni, Cr, Mo, V, W, Co, etc.
- **Low-alloy (< 5 %)** for gears, shafts; **high-alloy (> 5 %)** for stainless, tool, heat-resistant grades.
- **Stainless steel** = Cr ‚â• 10.5 % (passive Cr‚ÇÇO‚ÇÉ film); 304 = 18/8 Cr/Ni austenitic.
- **Hadfield steel** = 12 % Mn, work-hardens to 550 HB, used in rock-crushers, railway crossings.
- **White CI**: massive Fe‚ÇÉC, no graphite, brittle, abrasion-resistant (crusher jaws).
- **Gray CI**: graphite flakes (Type A), damping, machine tool beds, engine blocks.
- **Malleable CI**: white CI annealed 800 ‚Äì 900 ¬∞C for 20 ‚Äì 60 h ‚Üí temper graphite rosettes, ferrite matrix.
- **Ductile / SG iron**: Mg- or Ce-treated liquid ‚Üí graphite spheroids, high strength + ductility.
- **Compacted Graphite Iron (CGI)**: short, thick interconnected graphite flakes; intermediate between gray and ductile.
- **Si role**: graphitizer ‚Äî pushes the system from metastable Fe‚ÄìFe‚ÇÉC toward stable Fe‚ÄìC with free graphite.
- **Lever rule formula** (just below 727 ¬∞C for hypoeutectoid): $W_\alpha = (0.76 - C_0) / (0.76 - 0.022)$; for hypereutectoid cementite: $W_{Fe_3C} = (C_0 - 0.76) / (6.70 - 0.76)$.
- **Pearlite composition (eutectoid)** = 88 % ferrite + 12 % Fe‚ÇÉC (by mass).
- **Hardness rule of mixtures**: $H_{alloy} = V_\alpha H_\alpha + V_P H_P$.
- **Empirical UTS‚ÄìBrinell relation**: $UTS (MPa) \approx 3.45 \times HB$ (for plain-carbon ferrite-pearlite steels).
- **Weldability decreases** with rising carbon and alloy content; **machinability** peaks around 0.20 % C (in free-cutting steels, with S, Pb) and again at 0.80 ‚Äì 1.00 % C (pearlite + Fe‚ÇÉC).
- **Ductility vs Carbon** is **inversely** related; **Strength vs Carbon** is **directly** related (up to ~0.80 % C).
- **Damping capacity** in cast iron follows: Gray > CGI > Ductile > White > Steel (graphite flakes absorb vibration).
- **Malleable iron is NOT a cast iron grade** ‚Äî it is a heat-treated white cast iron. This is a frequent KTU trap question.
- **Always link**: composition ‚Üí cooling rate ‚Üí microstructure ‚Üí properties ‚Üí application in any descriptive answer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Heat treatment: time-temperature-transformation (TTT) and continuous cooling transformation (CCT) diagrams with applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAUT304 - MATERIAL SCIENCE AND METALLURGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Heat treatment: time-temperature-transformation (TTT) and continuous cooling transformation (CCT) diagrams with applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 ‚Äî Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Heat Treatment: The Engineer's "Pause and Play" Button

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Scope):**
> *Heat treatment is a controlled process of heating and cooling of metals/alloys in the solid state to obtain desired microstructure and mechanical properties ‚Äî without changing the product shape or chemical composition.*

Heat treatment allows a metallurgist to manipulate the **internal architecture (microstructure)** of a metal while leaving its **external shape** untouched. Two of the most powerful tools to design and predict these microstructures are the **TTT** and **CCT** diagrams.

---

## 1.2 TTT Diagram ‚Äî The "Isothermal Transformation" Map

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Academic Definition:**
> A **Time-Temperature-Transformation (TTT) diagram**, also called the **Isothermal Transformation (IT) diagram** or **S-Curve** (because of its characteristic shape), is a plot of **Temperature (Y-axis)** versus **logarithm of time (X-axis)** that maps the **kinetics of phase transformation** of a supercooled austenite into equilibrium/non-equilibrium products when held at a **constant temperature** for varying durations.

### Conceptual Analogy ‚Äî The "Cooling Tunnel" Metaphor

Imagine austenite as a **live, energetic crowd of dancers** at a hot, loud party. If you suddenly throw open the windows (quench to a specific temperature), the dancers don't immediately stop ‚Äî they take time to slow down and form orderly groups (pearlite, bainite, or martensite). The **TTT diagram is the choreographer's chart** that tells you:
- **How long** they will keep dancing (incubation/start time),
- **When** they begin forming orderly groups (start of transformation),
- **When** the entire crowd is reorganized (end of transformation),
- And **what formation** they will take depending on the room temperature.

In real metallurgy terms, the "dancers" are **carbon atoms trapped in FCC austenite (Œ≥-Fe)**, and the "orderly groups" are **ferrite + cementite lamellae (pearlite)**, **acicular ferrite + carbides (bainite)**, or **supersaturated BCT lattice (martensite)**.

---

## 1.3 CCT Diagram ‚Äî The "Continuous Cooling" Reality Map

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Academic Definition:**
> A **Continuous Cooling Transformation (CCT) diagram** is a plot of **Temperature (Y-axis)** versus **logarithm of time (X-axis)** that shows the **progress of phase transformation of austenite** as it is **continuously cooled** at various constant rates from the austenitizing temperature to room temperature.

### Conceptual Analogy ‚Äî The "Escalator vs. Elevator"

The TTT diagram is like an **elevator** ‚Äî you pick one floor (temperature) and stay there forever. The CCT diagram is like an **escalator** ‚Äî you pass through many temperatures continuously, and the transformations that occur at *each temperature as you sweep through* are what determines the final microstructure. Since real-world heat treatment (e.g., quenching in oil, air cooling, furnace cooling) is **continuous**, the CCT diagram is the **more practical, real-world tool**.

---

## 1.4 Key Standardized Metrics (KTU Board Favourites)

The following constants and reference lines appear in **every KTU question** on TTT/CCT:

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Value (Eutectoid Steel, 0.77% C) |
|--------|---------|------------------------------------------|
| $A_1$ | Lower critical temperature (eutectoid) | **727 ¬∞C** |
| $A_3$ | Upper critical temperature (hypoeutectoid) | Varies with %C |
| $A_{cm}$ | Upper critical temperature (hypereutectoid) | Varies with %C |
| $M_s$ | Martensite start temperature | **~220 ¬∞C** |
| $M_f$ | Martensite finish temperature | **~50 ¬∞C** |
| $M_{50}$ | 50% martensite temperature | **~190 ¬∞C** |
| Nose temp | Temperature of fastest transformation | **~550 ¬∞C** |
| Incubation time | Time before transformation begins | **~1 second (at nose)** |

---

## 1.5 The Four Critical Curves Inside a TTT Diagram

A standard TTT diagram for eutectoid steel contains four critical curves. Memorize these for KTU:

1. **Curve 1 ‚Äî Austenite ‚Üí Pearlite start (Ps):** Upper boundary of pearlite region.
2. **Curve 2 ‚Äî Austenite ‚Üí Pearlite finish (Pf):** Lower boundary of pearlite region.
3. **Curve 3 ‚Äî Austenite ‚Üí Bainite start (Bs):** Upper boundary of bainite region.
4. **Curve 4 ‚Äî Martensite start line (Ms):** Horizontal line (~220 ¬∞C) ‚Äî transformation begins *instantaneously* on reaching $M_s$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Memory Anchor:** "**S-curve is Bounded by P-start, P-finish, and B-start. Below $M_s$, time = 0 transformation to martensite.**"

---

## 1.6 Why Both Diagrams? ‚Äî Engineering Necessity

| Aspect | TTT (Isothermal) | CCT (Continuous Cooling) |
|--------|------------------|--------------------------|
| **Process simulated** | Holding at constant T | Continuous cooling |
| **Practical use** | Austempering, patenting, isothermal annealing | Quenching, normalizing, annealing |
| **Time scale** | Slightly shorter | Shifted to **longer times** & **lower temps** |
| **Transformation products** | Pearlite, Bainite, Martensite | Same, but proportions differ |
| **KTU exam weightage** | High (definitions + interpretation) | High (cooling curve overlay) |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Mantra:** *A CCT diagram is essentially a TTT diagram pushed to the right and downward because continuous cooling delays the start of transformation and shifts the nose.*

---

## 1.7 Chapter Roadmap (What You'll Master)

By the end of these notes, you will be able to:

- [x] **Draw & label** the TTT diagram for eutectoid steel from memory.
- [x] **Explain** the shape, position, and physical meaning of every curve.
- [x] **Superimpose** cooling curves on a TTT diagram to predict microstructure.
- [x] **Convert** TTT ‚Üí CCT by applying the Scheil additivity rule.
- [x] **Calculate** critical cooling rates using the lever rule.
- [x] **Apply** TTT/CCT to design industrial heat treatments (austempering, martempering, normalizing, annealing).

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 ‚Äî Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Thermodynamic Driver ‚Äî Why Austenite Transforms at All

When steel is heated **above $A_1$ (727 ¬∞C)**, the stable phase is **austenite (Œ≥-Fe, FCC)**. As we cool below $A_1$, austenite becomes **thermodynamically unstable** because FCC iron with dissolved carbon has a **higher Gibbs free energy** than the equilibrium mixture of **ferrite (Œ±-Fe, BCC) + cementite (Fe‚ÇÉC)**.

The thermodynamic driving force per mole is:

$$\Delta G = G_{\gamma} - (G_{\alpha} + G_{Fe_3C})$$

This driving force **increases** as we cool further below $A_1$. However, transformation is also opposed by a **nucleation barrier** ($\Delta G^*$) and requires **atomic diffusion** of carbon. This is the **"battle"** that TTT/CCT diagrams resolve by plotting *time* (kinetics) against *temperature* (thermodynamics).

---

## 2.2 The Avrami Equation ‚Äî Mathematical Backbone of TTT

The fraction of austenite transformed ($f$) as a function of time ($t$) at constant temperature follows the **Johnson‚ÄìMehl‚ÄìAvrami (JMA) equation**:

$$f(t) = 1 - \exp(-k \cdot t^n)$$

Where:
- $f$ = volume fraction of transformation product
- $k$ = rate constant (depends on temperature)
- $t$ = time at isothermal hold
- $n$ = Avrami exponent (depends on transformation mechanism)

| Transformation | Typical $n$ | Mechanism |
|----------------|-------------|-----------|
| Pearlite (diffusion-controlled) | 3 ‚Äì 4 | Nucleation + 3D growth |
| Bainite (displacive + diffusion) | 2 ‚Äì 3 | Autocatalytic nucleation |
| Martensite (diffusionless) | ‚âà 0 | Athermal (instantaneous) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Martensite is **athermal** ‚Äî it forms *instantly* the moment $M_s$ is reached. The amount of martensite is a function of the **lowest temperature reached**, **NOT** of time held at that temperature.

---

## 2.3 The Nose of the TTT Curve ‚Äî The "Danger Zone"

The **nose** is the leftmost (shortest time) point of the C-curve, typically around **550 ¬∞C** for eutectoid steel, with an incubation time of about **1 second**. It represents the temperature at which:

$$\text{Nucleation rate} \times \text{Growth rate} = \text{MAXIMUM}$$

- **Above the nose (high T):** Diffusion is fast, but thermodynamic driving force is small.
- **Below the nose (low T):** Driving force is large, but carbon diffusion is sluggish.
- **At the nose:** The product is maximum ‚Äî transformation occurs in the **shortest possible time**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Cooling Curve Pitfall:** If a cooling curve **misses the nose entirely to the left** (i.e., cools faster than the upper critical cooling rate), **no diffusion-controlled product** (pearlite, bainite) forms. The result is **100% martensite**.

---

## 2.4 The CCT Diagram ‚Äî Construction from TTT

A CCT diagram is built by:

1. Superimposing **multiple cooling curves** (each with a different cooling rate) on a TTT diagram.
2. Recording the **start and finish temperatures** of each transformation during continuous cooling.
3. Connecting these points to form new C-curves (shifted right and down).

The shift obeys the **Scheil Additivity Rule**:

$$\sum_{i} \frac{\Delta t_i}{\tau_i} = 1$$

Where:
- $\Delta t_i$ = time spent in temperature interval $i$
- $\tau_i$ = incubation time at that temperature (read from TTT)

When the sum equals 1, **transformation begins**.

---

## 2.5 The Two Critical Cooling Rates (CCT)

Two cooling rates are paramount in CCT diagrams and frequently asked in KTU exams:

### 2.5.1 Upper Critical Cooling Rate (UCR)

The **minimum cooling rate** required to **avoid the nose** of the pearlite/bainite C-curve entirely and produce **100% martensite**.

$$UCR = \frac{727 - M_f}{t_{nose}}$$

Typical value for eutectoid steel: **~140 ¬∞C/s** (water quench equivalent).

### 2.5.2 Lower Critical Cooling Rate (LCR)

The **maximum cooling rate** that still produces **100% pearlite** (no bainite, no martensite). Above LCR, bainite starts to appear.

$$LCR = \frac{727 - 550}{t_{nose}^{pearlite}}$$

Typical value: **~35 ¬∞C/s** (air cooling equivalent).

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **Save this table. Every KTU numerical from this module is solved using exactly these formulas.**

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---------|---------|-------|
| 1 | $f(t) = 1 - e^{-kt^n}$ | Avrami transformation kinetics | dimensionless |
| 2 | $UCR = \dfrac{727 - M_f}{t_{nose}}$ | Upper critical cooling rate | ¬∞C/s |
| 3 | $LCR = \dfrac{727 - 550}{t_{nose}^P}$ | Lower critical cooling rate | ¬∞C/s |
| 4 | $\sum \dfrac{\Delta t_i}{\tau_i} = 1$ | Scheil additivity (CCT from TTT) | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\%M = 100 - \%A_{untrans}$ | Martensite percentage | % |
| 6 | $\%M \approx 100\% \ \text{below} \ M_{90}$ | $M_{90}$ ~ 90% martensite | ¬∞C |
| 7 | $CCT_{time} \approx 1.5 \times TTT_{time}$ | Empirical shift factor | seconds |
| 8 | $CCT_{temp} \approx TTT_{temp} - 50\degree C$ | Empirical temperature shift | ¬∞C |
| 9 | $\Delta G^* = \dfrac{16\pi \sigma^3}{3(\Delta G_v)^2}$ | Critical nucleation barrier | J |
| 10 | $\dot{T}_{nose} = \dfrac{\Delta T}{\Delta t_{nose}}$ | Cooling rate at nose | ¬∞C/s |

> **Important Notation Note:** Vertical bars for absolute values (e.g., $\vert \Delta G \vert$) are written using `\vert` in LaTeX to avoid breaking the markdown table above.

---

## 2.7 Effect of Alloying Elements on TTT/CCT

Alloying elements shift the **nose to the right** (longer times), making martensite formation **easier**. This is the basis for **hardenability**.

| Element | Effect on Nose | Effect on $M_s$ | Effect on Hardenability |
|---------|----------------|-----------------|--------------------------|
| C | Shifts right | Lowers (strong) | Increases (strong) |
| Mn | Shifts right | Lowers | Increases (strong) |
| Cr | Shifts right | Lowers | Increases |
| Ni | Shifts right | Lowers | Increases (moderate) |
| Mo | Shifts right (strong) | Lowers (mild) | Increases (strong) |
| Co | Shifts left | Raises | **Decreases** |
| Si | Mild right shift | Mild | Mild |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Memory Trick:** All elements except **Co** and **Al** increase hardenability. **Mn, Cr, Mo, Ni** are the "Big Four" hardenability boosters.

---

## 2.8 Bainite vs. Pearlite ‚Äî Critical Distinction

| Feature | Pearlite | Bainite |
|---------|----------|---------|
| Formation temperature | 727 ‚Äì 550 ¬∞C | 550 ‚Äì 250 ¬∞C |
| Mechanism | Diffusion (nucleation + growth) | Displacive + diffusion |
| Microstructure | Lamellar (alternating Œ± + Fe‚ÇÉC) | Acicular (needle-like ferrite + carbides) |
| Carbon diffusion | Long-range | Short-range |
| Hardness | 200 ‚Äì 400 HV | 400 ‚Äì 600 HV |
| Toughness | Ductile | Tough (better than martensite) |

---

## 2.9 Real-World Engineering Applications

### 2.9.1 Austempering (TTT-Based)

> **Process:** Austenitize ‚Üí Quench to **300 ‚Äì 400 ¬∞C** (bainite region) ‚Üí Hold isothermally until **Bs finished** ‚Üí Air cool.

**Cooling curve** passes through the nose? **NO** ‚Äî the quench is fast enough to bypass the pearlite nose, and holding isothermally produces 100% bainite (no martensite, no distortion).

**Applications:** Springs, agricultural tools, chisels, thin knives.

### 2.9.2 Martempering (TTT-Based)

> **Process:** Austenitize ‚Üí Quench in **hot bath (just above $M_s$)** ‚Üí Hold until temperature equalizes ‚Üí Air cool to room temperature.

**Result:** Martensite forms **uniformly** across the cross-section (no thermal gradient distortion). Temper afterwards.

**Applications:** Gears, bearing races, tool bits.

### 2.9.3 Patenting (TTT-Based)

> **Process:** Austenitize ‚Üí Quench to **500 ‚Äì 550 ¬∞C** (nose region) ‚Üí Hold isothermally ‚Üí Cool.

**Result:** Fine pearlitic structure with high tensile strength.

**Applications:** Wire rope, spring wire, prestressed concrete wire.

### 2.9.4 Normalizing (CCT-Based)

> **Process:** Austenitize ‚Üí **Air cool** continuously.

**Result:** Finer pearlite than annealing, moderate strength, uniform grain size.

### 2.9.5 Full Annealing (CCT-Based)

> **Process:** Austenitize ‚Üí **Furnace cool** very slowly.

**Result:** Coarse pearlite, soft, machinable.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 ‚Äî Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Critical Cooling Rate from TTT Geometry

### Given Data (Standard Eutectoid Steel)

- $A_1$ = 727 ¬∞C
- $M_f$ = 50 ¬∞C
- Nose temperature ($T_{nose}$) = 550 ¬∞C
- Incubation time at nose ($t_{nose}$) = **1 second**

### Goal

Derive the **upper critical cooling rate (UCR)** that just barely misses the nose of the TTT diagram.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 ‚Äî Identify the temperature range to be traversed without transformation.**

The cooling curve must start from the austenitizing temperature (above $A_1 = 727$ ¬∞C) and end below $M_f = 50$ ¬∞C, while **bypassing the nose** which lies at 550 ¬∞C.

The relevant temperature interval to escape the nose is:

$$\Delta T = T_{nose} - M_f = 550 - 50 = 500\ \text{¬∞C}$$

> *Logic:* To avoid forming pearlite or bainite, the cooling curve must pass the 550 ¬∞C isotherm in **less than 1 second** (the incubation time at the nose).

**Step 2 ‚Äî Express the minimum time allowed.**

The minimum time available at the nose to avoid transformation is the incubation time:

$$t_{min} = t_{nose} = 1\ \text{second}$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Apply the definition of cooling rate.**

Cooling rate is the rate of change of temperature with time:

$$\dot{T} = \frac{dT}{dt} \approx \frac{\Delta T}{\Delta t}$$

**Step 4 ‚Äî Substitute.**

$$\dot{T}_{UCR} = \frac{\Delta T}{t_{nose}} = \frac{500\ \text{¬∞C}}{1\ \text{s}}$$

$$\boxed{\dot{T}_{UCR} = 500\ \text{¬∞C/s}}$$

> **Engineering Interpretation:** The steel must be cooled at *at least* 500 ¬∞C/s (e.g., water quench) from 727 ¬∞C to 50 ¬∞C to ensure **100% martensite**. If the cooling rate is less, the nose is intersected, and some pearlite or bainite forms.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Volume Fraction Transformed (Avrami)

### Problem

At 600 ¬∞C, eutectoid steel is held isothermally. The Avrami constants are $k = 0.02\ \text{s}^{-1}$ and $n = 2$. Find the volume fraction transformed after $t = 5$ seconds.

### Step 1 ‚Äî Write the Avrami equation.**

$$f(t) = 1 - e^{-kt^n}$$

### Step 2 ‚Äî Compute the exponent.**

$$kt^n = (0.02)(5)^2 = (0.02)(25) = 0.5$$

### Step 3 ‚Äî Compute the exponential.**

$$e^{-0.5} = 0.6065$$

### Step 4 ‚Äî Compute fraction transformed.**

$$f(5) = 1 - 0.6065 = 0.3935$$

$$\boxed{f = 39.35\%}$$

**Engineering meaning:** After 5 seconds at 600 ¬∞C, about **39% of austenite has transformed** to pearlite. The remaining 61% is still austenite.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix ‚Äî Predicting Microstructure from a Cooling Curve

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **master decision matrix** for any KTU question where a cooling curve is superimposed on a TTT diagram. Memorize it.

| Cooling Curve Behaviour | Intersection with TTT | Final Microstructure |
|------------------------|----------------------|----------------------|
| Misses nose completely to the **left** (very fast quench) | None ‚Äî passes $M_s$ directly | **100% Martensite** |
| Tangent to nose | Just touches pearlite start | **Mostly martensite + trace pearlite** |
| Crosses pearlite region, then $M_s$ | Yes (pearlite + martensite) | **Pearlite + Martensite** |
| Crosses pearlite + bainite regions, then $M_s$ | Yes (P + B + M) | **Pearlite + Bainite + Martensite** |
| Crosses only bainite region, then $M_s$ | Yes (bainite + M) | **Bainite + Martensite** |
| Crosses only bainite region, **finishes** there | Yes (bainite) | **100% Bainite (Austempering)** |
| Crosses pearlite region, **finishes** there | Yes (pearlite) | **100% Pearlite (Patenting/Annealing)** |
| Very slow cooling (furnace cool) | Yes, finishes in pearlite | **Coarse Pearlite (Full Annealing)** |

---

## 3.4 Step-by-Step: Scheil Additivity Application

### Problem

A eutectoid steel is continuously cooled from 727 ¬∞C. The TTT diagram shows:
- At 700 ¬∞C: incubation time = 100 s; the cooling curve spends 10 s here.
- At 650 ¬∞C: incubation time = 20 s; the cooling curve spends 4 s here.
- At 600 ¬∞C: incubation time = 5 s; the cooling curve spends 1 s here.

**Determine if transformation begins during this cooling.**

### Step 1 ‚Äî Recall Scheil's Rule.**

Transformation begins when:

$$\sum_{i} \frac{\Delta t_i}{\tau_i} = 1$$

### Step 2 ‚Äî Compute each term.**

$$\frac{\Delta t_1}{\tau_1} = \frac{10}{100} = 0.10$$

$$\frac{\Delta t_2}{\tau_2} = \frac{4}{20} = 0.20$$

$$\frac{\Delta t_3}{\tau_3} = \frac{1}{5} = 0.20$$

### Step 3 ‚Äî Sum the contributions.**

$$\sum = 0.10 + 0.20 + 0.20 = 0.50$$

### Step 4 ‚Äî Compare with unity.**

$$0.50 < 1$$

### Step 5 ‚Äî Conclusion.**

$$\boxed{\text{Transformation has NOT yet begun.}}$$

> *Interpretation:* The cumulative "damage" to austenite stability has reached only 50% of the threshold. The cooling must continue further for transformation to start.

---

## 3.5 Step-by-Step: Martensite Percentage from $M_s$ Cooling

### Problem

A steel is quenched from austenitizing temperature. The $M_s$ = 220 ¬∞C, $M_{50}$ = 190 ¬∞C, $M_{90}$ = 100 ¬∞C, and $M_f$ = 50 ¬∞C. If the steel is cooled to 100 ¬∞C, what is the percentage of martensite?

### Step 1 ‚Äî Use the $M_{90}$ convention.**

The temperature at which 90% martensite forms is given as $M_{90} = 100$ ¬∞C. By definition:

$$\boxed{\%M \approx 90\%}$$

> **Note:** For very accurate work, Koster's relation is used:

$$\%M = 100 \cdot \left[1 - e^{-0.011 \cdot (M_s - T_q)}\right]$$

where $T_q$ is the quench temperature.

### Step 2 ‚Äî Verification using Koster's equation.**

$$M_s - T_q = 220 - 100 = 120$$

$$\%M = 100 \cdot \left[1 - e^{-0.011 \times 120}\right]$$

$$= 100 \cdot \left[1 - e^{-1.32}\right]$$

$$= 100 \cdot \left[1 - 0.2670\right]$$

$$= 73.30\%$$

$$\boxed{\%M \approx 73.3\% \ \text{(more accurate, used in research)}}$$

---

## 3.6 Worked Example: Predicting Microstructure from CCT Diagram

### Problem

A eutectoid steel is cooled at three different rates: (a) 200 ¬∞C/s, (b) 50 ¬∞C/s, (c) 5 ¬∞C/s. The CCT diagram shows:
- $M_s$ = 220 ¬∞C
- $M_f$ = 50 ¬∞C
- Pearlite nose at 550 ¬∞C, 5 s
- Bainite region: 550 ‚Äì 250 ¬∞C

**Predict the microstructure for each case.**

### Case (a): 200 ¬∞C/s

- **Cooling time from 727 to 50 ¬∞C:** $(727 - 50) / 200 = 3.4$ s.
- The nose is at 550 ¬∞C, requires ~5 s to start. We take 3.4 s ‚Äî **misses nose**.
- Passes $M_s$ at 220 ¬∞C in less than a second ‚Äî martensite forms.
- **Result:** 100% Martensite.

### Case (b): 50 ¬∞C/s

- **Cooling time from 727 to 550 ¬∞C:** $(727 - 550) / 50 = 3.5$ s. ‚Üí Less than 5 s, so it **skips the pearlite nose**.
- But below 550 ¬∞C, the cooling enters the bainite region before reaching $M_s$.
- **Result:** Bainite + Martensite mixture.

### Case (c): 5 ¬∞C/s

- **Cooling time from 727 to 550 ¬∞C:** $(727 - 550) / 5 = 35.4$ s. ‚Üí Greater than 5 s, so the curve **intersects the pearlite nose**.
- Transformation completes in pearlite region.
- **Result:** 100% Pearlite (coarse).

---

## 3.7 KTU-Style Numerical Problem & Full Solution

### Question

> A 0.77% C steel is austenitized at 760 ¬∞C and then quenched into a salt bath maintained at 350 ¬∞C. The TTT diagram data is:
> - At 350 ¬∞C: Bainite start ($B_s$) at 800 s; Bainite finish ($B_f$) at 5000 s.
> - At 350 ¬∞C: Martensite start ($M_s$) = 220 ¬∞C.
>
> **Find:** (a) The phase obtained after holding for 1 hour. (b) The hardness expected. (c) Name the heat treatment process.

### Step 1 ‚Äî Compare hold time with $B_s$.**

Hold time = 1 hour = **3600 s**.

Since $3600\ \text{s} > 800\ \text{s}$ (Bs) and $3600\ \text{s} < 5000\ \text{s}$ (Bf):

Transformation is **in progress**, but **not complete**. Bainite is forming.

### Step 2 ‚Äî Identify the phase.**

$$\boxed{\text{Phase obtained: Bainite (partial)}}$$

### Step 3 ‚Äî Expected Hardness.**

Bainite at 350 ¬∞C is **lower bainite** ‚Äî acicular structure with fine carbides. Hardness range:

$$\boxed{H = 400 - 550\ \text{HV} \ (\approx 40 - 50\ \text{HRC})}$$

### Step 4 ‚Äî Process name.**

The process of quenching to a temperature **between $B_s$ and $M_s$** and holding isothermally is:

$$\boxed{\textbf{Austempering}}$$

---

## 3.8 Comparative Analysis Table ‚Äî Heat Treatments

| Heat Treatment | Quench Medium | Final Microstructure | Hardness (HRC) | Distortion? | Application |
|----------------|---------------|----------------------|----------------|-------------|-------------|
| Full Annealing | Furnace cool | Coarse Pearlite | 10 ‚Äì 20 | None | Machining feedstock |
| Normalizing | Air | Fine Pearlite | 20 ‚Äì 30 | Low | Structural components |
| Spheroidizing | Very slow cool | Spheroidite | 15 ‚Äì 25 | None | Bearing stock |
| Quenching | Water/Oil | Martensite | 55 ‚Äì 65 | **High** | Cutting tools |
| Austempering | Salt bath (300‚Äì400 ¬∞C) | Bainite | 40 ‚Äì 50 | **Low** | Springs, chisels |
| Martempering | Hot oil (just above $M_s$) | Martensite | 55 ‚Äì 60 | **Very Low** | Gears, bearings |
| Tempering (after quench) | Air | Tempered Martensite | 30 ‚Äì 50 | Low | Tools, dies |
| Patenting | Lead bath (~550 ¬∞C) | Fine Pearlite | 35 ‚Äì 45 | None | Spring wire |

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 ‚Äî Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 TTT Diagram ‚Äî Mermaid Schematic (Eutectoid Steel)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Start: Austenite at 760 C] --> B{Above A1 727 C}
    B -- Hold --> C[Stable Austenite gamma]
    B -- Cool to 700 C --> D[Cooling Curve Path]
    D --> E{Intersection with Ps curve?}
    E -- No --> F1[Passes to Ms 220 C]
    E -- Yes --> G1[Pearlite starts]
    G1 --> G2{Hold time vs Pf}
    G2 -- Greater --> H1[100 percent Pearlite]
    G2 -- Less --> H2[Pearlite plus Austenite]
    F1 --> F2[Quench below Mf 50 C]
    F2 --> F3[100 percent Martensite]
    D --> I{Intersection with Bs 550 C}
    I -- Yes --> J1[Bainite starts]
    J1 --> J2{Hold time vs Bf}
    J2 -- Greater --> K1[100 percent Bainite: Austempering]
    J2 -- Less --> K2[Bainite plus Austenite]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture ‚Äî TTT vs CCT Mapping

```mermaid
graph LR
    A1[Austenitization Furnace 760 to 900 C] --> B1{Cooling Strategy}
    B1 -- Isothermal Hold --> C1[TTT Path]
    B1 -- Continuous Cooling --> C2[CCT Path]
    C1 --> D1[Hold at Constant T]
    D1 --> E1[Transformation at Single T]
    E1 --> F1[Single Phase Product]
    C2 --> D2[Cool at Constant Rate]
    D2 --> E2[Transformation across T range]
    E2 --> F2[Mixed Phases possible]
    C1 --> G1[Predict via Ps, Pf, Bs, Bf, Ms, Mf]
    C2 --> G2[Predict via Critical Cooling Rates]
    G1 --> H1[Pearlite, Bainite, Martensite]
    G2 --> H2[Pearlite plus Martensite etc]
    F1 --> I1[Lab scale: Austempering, Patenting]
    F2 --> I2[Industry: Quenching, Normalizing, Annealing]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid ‚Äî Heat Treatment Decision Tree (Industrial Application)

```mermaid
graph TD
    Start[Raw Steel Component] --> H1{Austenitize Above A1}
    H1 --> H2{Desired Property}
    H2 -- Soft and Machinable --> H3[Full Anneal: Furnace Cool]
    H2 -- Moderate Strength --> H4[Normalize: Air Cool]
    H2 -- High Hardness --> H5{Concern about Distortion}
    H5 -- Yes --> H6[Martemper: Hot Bath near Ms]
    H5 -- No --> H7[Direct Quench: Water or Oil]
    H6 --> H8[Temper for Toughness]
    H7 --> H8
    H2 -- Tough and Ductile --> H9[Austemper: Salt Bath 300 to 400 C]
    H2 -- Wire with High TS --> H10[Patent: Lead Bath 500 to 550 C]
    H3 --> End1[Coarse Pearlite]
    H4 --> End2[Fine Pearlite]
    H6 --> End3[Untempered Martensite uniform]
    H7 --> End4[Martensite plus Retained Austenite]
    H8 --> End5[Tempered Martensite]
    H9 --> End6[Bainite]
    H10 --> End7[Fine Pearlite Sorbitic]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid ‚Äî CCT Diagram Microstructure Predictor (Logic Flow)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Cooling Rate Selection] --> B{Compare to UCR}
    B -- Greater --> M1[100 percent Martensite]
    B -- Less --> C{Compare to LCR}
    C -- Greater --> M2[Bainite plus Martensite]
    C -- Less --> D{Compare to Pearlite finish}
    D -- Greater --> M3[Pearlite plus Bainite plus Martensite]
    D -- Less --> M4[100 percent Pearlite]
    M1 --> OUT[Final Microstructure]
    M2 --> OUT
    M3 --> OUT
    M4 --> OUT
```

**Reading rules for examiners:**
- A cooling curve entering from top-left and exiting bottom-left **to the left of the nose** = martensite.
- A cooling curve entering and crossing pearlite region = pearlite.
- A horizontal hold inside bainite region = bainite (austempering).
- A cooling curve tangent to the nose = upper critical cooling rate.
