<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 ‚Äî KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ‚Äî Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1

> **[KTU University Exam ‚Äî May 2024]**
> *Define a TTT diagram. Why is it also called an S-curve or isothermal transformation diagram?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A TTT (Time-Temperature-Transformation) diagram is a graphical representation that plots **temperature on the Y-axis** and **logarithm of time on the X-axis**, showing the **kinetics of phase transformation** of a supercooled austenite when held at a **constant temperature** (isothermal condition). It is called an **isothermal transformation (IT) diagram** because transformations are studied at constant temperature. It is called an **S-curve** because the start-of-transformation curve resembles the letter "S" ‚Äî flat at the top (near $A_1$), curving leftward to a "nose," and then flat at the bottom (near $M_s$). (3 marks)

---

### Question A2

> **[KTU University Exam ‚Äî Dec 2023]**
> *Distinguish between TTT and CCT diagrams.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| S.No. | TTT Diagram | CCT Diagram |
|-------|-------------|-------------|
| 1 | Transformation studied at **constant temperature** | Transformation during **continuous cooling** |
| 2 | Curves are at **shorter times**, higher temperatures | Curves **shifted right (longer time)** and **downward** |
| 3 | Used for **isothermal processes** (austempering, patenting) | Used for **real industrial cooling** (quenching, normalizing) |
| 4 | Easier to construct in lab | Constructed from TTT + cooling curves (Scheil additivity) |

(3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B ‚Äî Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question B1 ‚Äî Choice A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam ‚Äî Dec 2024, Model Paper]**
> *(a) Draw a neat labelled TTT diagram for a eutectoid steel and explain the significance of each region. (7 marks)*
> *(b) What is the upper critical cooling rate? A 0.77 % C steel has a nose incubation time of 1 s at 550 ¬∞C. Calculate the UCR to obtain 100 % martensite. (7 marks)*

### Model Solution

#### Part (a) ‚Äî TTT Diagram for Eutectoid Steel (7 Marks)

The diagram is drawn with **temperature (¬∞C)** on the Y-axis (727 ¬∞C at top, Mf at bottom) and **log time (s)** on the X-axis.

**Key features to label (each carries marks):**

1. **$A_1$ line at 727 ¬∞C** ‚Äî the eutectoid temperature. Above this, austenite is stable.
2. **Pearlite-start ($P_s$) curve** ‚Äî upper C-curve; austenite starts transforming to pearlite.
3. **Pearlite-finish ($P_f$) curve** ‚Äî lower boundary of pearlite region.
4. **Bainite-start ($B_s$) curve** ‚Äî begins at ~550 ¬∞C and extends to $M_s$.
5. **Bainite-finish ($B_f$) curve** ‚Äî below $B_s$.
6. **Nose at 550 ¬∞C, ~1 s** ‚Äî point of fastest transformation.
7. **$M_s$ line at ~220 ¬∞C** ‚Äî martensite start (horizontal).
8. **$M_f$ line at ~50 ¬∞C** ‚Äî martensite finish (horizontal).

**Significance of each region:**
- **Above $A_1$:** Stable austenite (Œ≥-Fe, FCC).
- **Between $A_1$ and nose:** Austenite transforms to **coarse pearlite** (slow cooling) ‚Äî soft, ductile.
- **Around the nose (550‚Äì600 ¬∞C):** **Fine pearlite** forms (fastest transformation).
- **Between nose and $M_s$ (550‚Äì250 ¬∞C):** **Bainite** forms ‚Äî acicular, tough.
- **Below $M_s$:** **Martensite** forms (athermal, diffusionless).
- **Between $M_s$ and $M_f$:** Mixture of martensite + retained austenite.

(7 marks ‚Äî 2 for diagram, 3 for labels, 2 for significance)

---

#### Part (b) ‚Äî UCR Calculation (7 Marks)

**Definition (2 marks):** The **upper critical cooling rate (UCR)** is the **minimum rate of cooling** required to **avoid the nose** of the TTT diagram and produce **100 % martensite**.

**Given:**
- $A_1$ = 727 ¬∞C
- Nose temperature = 550 ¬∞C
- $M_f$ = 50 ¬∞C
- $t_{nose}$ = 1 s

**Step 1 ‚Äî Temperature range to escape nose:**

$$\Delta T = T_{nose} - M_f = 550 - 50 = 500\ \text{¬∞C}$$

**Step 2 ‚Äî Time available at the nose:**

$$t_{nose} = 1\ \text{s}$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Apply cooling rate formula:**

$$\dot{T} = \frac{\Delta T}{t_{nose}} = \frac{500\ \text{¬∞C}}{1\ \text{s}}$$

$$\boxed{\dot{T}_{UCR} = 500\ \text{¬∞C/s}}$$

**Engineering interpretation (2 marks):** The steel must be cooled at a rate **greater than 500 ¬∞C/s** (e.g., water quenching) to ensure the cooling curve passes to the **left of the nose**, producing **100 % martensite**. Slower cooling would intersect the nose and produce pearlite/bainite.

(7 marks)

---

### Question B1 ‚Äî Choice B (14 Marks) [Internal Choice Alternative]

> **[KTU University Exam ‚Äî May 2023, Retest Paper]**
> *(a) Explain the construction of a CCT diagram from a TTT diagram. What is the Scheil additivity rule? (7 marks)*
> *(b) A eutectoid steel is continuously cooled from 800 ¬∞C. The cooling curve data is:*
> *- 750 ‚Üí 700 ¬∞C in 5 s (incubation at 725 ¬∞C: 100 s)*
> *- 700 ‚Üí 650 ¬∞C in 6 s (incubation at 675 ¬∞C: 20 s)*
> *- 650 ‚Üí 600 ¬∞C in 8 s (incubation at 625 ¬∞C: 4 s)*
> *Determine whether transformation begins.*

### Model Solution

#### Part (a) ‚Äî CCT from TTT (7 Marks)

A **CCT diagram** is constructed by **superimposing cooling curves** of various constant rates on a TTT diagram and recording the **start and finish temperatures** of each transformation. The procedure (3 marks):

1. Heat the steel to a fully austenitic state (above $A_1$).
2. Cool at a known **constant rate** (e.g., 100 ¬∞C/s, 10 ¬∞C/s, 1 ¬∞C/s) to room temperature.
3. Record the **transformation start temperature** (where the cooling curve crosses the TTT start curve).
4. Record the **transformation finish temperature**.
5. Repeat for multiple cooling rates and plot all such points to obtain CCT curves.

The CCT diagram is **shifted to the right and downward** compared to the TTT diagram because:
- Continuous cooling spends **less time** at each temperature than isothermal holds.
- The actual start of transformation is **delayed** because austenite needs cumulative time.

**Scheil Additivity Rule (4 marks):**

The Scheil rule states that during continuous cooling, transformation begins when the **sum of fractional incubation times** at all temperatures traversed equals unity:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{\Delta t_i}{\tau_i} = 1$$

Where:
- $\Delta t_i$ = time spent in temperature interval $i$
- $\tau_i$ = isothermal incubation time at the mean temperature of interval $i$

If the sum is less than 1, transformation has **not begun**. If greater than 1, transformation has **started**.

(7 marks)

---

#### Part (b) ‚Äî Scheil Calculation (7 Marks)

**Step 1 ‚Äî Identify each interval and compute the fractional terms.**

| Interval | $\Delta t_i$ (s) | $\tau_i$ (s) | $\Delta t_i / \tau_i$ |
|----------|------------------|--------------|------------------------|
| 750 ‚Üí 700 ¬∞C (avg 725 ¬∞C) | 5 | 100 | 5/100 = 0.05 |
| 700 ‚Üí 650 ¬∞C (avg 675 ¬∞C) | 6 | 20 | 6/20 = 0.30 |
| 650 ‚Üí 600 ¬∞C (avg 625 ¬∞C) | 8 | 4 | 8/4 = 2.00 |

**Step 2 ‚Äî Sum the contributions.**

$$\sum = 0.05 + 0.30 + 2.00 = 2.35$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Compare with unity.**

$$2.35 > 1$$

**Step 4 ‚Äî Conclusion.**

$$\boxed{\text{Transformation has begun.}}$$

> *Interpretation:* The cumulative damage threshold was **exceeded during the 650 ‚Üí 600 ¬∞C interval**. The steel began to transform at approximately 625 ¬∞C (midway through the third interval). To avoid transformation, the cooling rate would need to be increased to reduce the $\Delta t$ terms.

(7 marks)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Mistakes (Read before writing your exam!):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to take logarithm** ‚Äî TTT X-axis is $\log t$, not $t$. Always mention "logarithm of time" in the definition.
> 2. **Confusing $M_s$ and $M_f$** ‚Äî $M_s$ is the START (~220 ¬∞C), $M_f$ is the FINISH (~50 ¬∞C). Writing them in reverse order costs 1 full mark.
> 3. **Treating martensite as time-dependent** ‚Äî Martensite is **athermal**. Holding at $M_s$ does **not** increase martensite %; only **lowering the temperature** does.
> 4. **Wrong sign in UCR formula** ‚Äî Always subtract $M_f$ from $T_{nose}$, not the other way around: $\Delta T = T_{nose} - M_f$.
> 5. **Not drawing the nose** ‚Äî In TTT diagrams, the **nose is mandatory**. Examiners deduct 2 marks if the nose is missing.
> 6. **Scheil sum errors** ‚Äî Use the **mean temperature** of each interval for $\tau_i$, not the upper or lower bound.
> 7. **Austempering vs. Martempering confusion** ‚Äî Austempering = quench to **bainite region** (300‚Äì400 ¬∞C); Martempering = quench to **just above $M_s$**, then air cool.
> 8. **Forgetting the right-shift of CCT** ‚Äî If a question asks to convert TTT to CCT, students often draw the same diagram. CCT is shifted **right by ~1.5√ó and down by ~50 ¬∞C**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print this page before exam!)**

### 5.4.1 Definitions You Must Memorize

- [ ] **TTT diagram** = Isothermal Transformation diagram = S-curve.
- [ ] **CCT diagram** = Continuous Cooling Transformation diagram.
- [ ] **$A_1$** = 727 ¬∞C (eutectoid temperature).
- [ ] **$M_s$** = ~220 ¬∞C, **$M_f$** = ~50 ¬∞C (for 0.77 % C steel).
- [ ] **Nose** = 550 ¬∞C, 1 s (fastest transformation).
- [ ] **UCR** = Upper Critical Cooling Rate (for 100% martensite).
- [ ] **LCR** = Lower Critical Cooling Rate (for 100% pearlite).
- [ ] **Austempering** = isothermal hold in bainite region.
- [ ] **Martempering** = quench to just above $M_s$, then air cool.
- [ ] **Patenting** = isothermal hold near nose (500‚Äì550 ¬∞C).

### 5.4.2 Formulas to Memorize

- [ ] $UCR = \dfrac{T_{nose} - M_f}{t_{nose}}$
- [ ] $LCR = \dfrac{727 - 550}{t_{nose}^P}$
- [ ] $f(t) = 1 - e^{-kt^n}$ (Avrami)
- [ ] $\sum \dfrac{\Delta t_i}{\tau_i} = 1$ (Scheil)
- [ ] Koster: $\%M = 100(1 - e^{-0.011(M_s - T_q)})$

### 5.4.3 Microstructure vs. Cooling Rate (Quick Lookup)

| Cooling Rate | Microstructure |
|--------------|----------------|
| $> UCR$ | 100 % Martensite |
| $LCR$ to $UCR$ | Martensite + Bainite |
| Very slow (furnace) | Coarse Pearlite |
| Air cool | Fine Pearlite |
| Salt bath (300‚Äì400 ¬∞C) | Bainite (austempering) |

### 5.4.4 Industrial Applications Snapshot

- **Gears & bearings** ‚Üí Martempering.
- **Springs, chisels** ‚Üí Austempering.
- **Wire rope, prestressed wire** ‚Üí Patenting.
- **Cutting tools** ‚Üí Quench + Temper.
- **Machining stock** ‚Üí Full Anneal (spheroidize).
- **Structural plates** ‚Üí Normalize.

### 5.4.5 Five Common KTU Traps to Avoid

1. ‚ùå Drawing a smooth TTT curve that **does not have a nose** ‚Äî always show the nose.
2. ‚ùå Stating that martensite forms with time ‚Äî it forms with **temperature drop**.
3. ‚ùå Forgetting that bainite forms **below the nose**, not above.
4. ‚ùå Saying the CCT and TTT diagrams are the same ‚Äî CCT is shifted.
5. ‚ùå Confusing retained austenite (between $M_s$ and $M_f$) with proeutectoid ferrite (in hypoeutectoid steels).

---

> **End of Module 3 Notes ‚Äî Heat Treatment: TTT & CCT Diagrams with Applications**
> *You've now mastered the most heavily-weighted topic in PBAUT304. Revise the formula sheet and the analytical matrix the night before the exam. Best of luck!* üéì
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tempering
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAUT304 - MATERIAL SCIENCE AND METALLURGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Tempering

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tempering** is a sub-critical heat treatment process applied to quench-hardened (martensitic) steels, involving reheating to a temperature **below the lower critical temperature (A1 ‚âà 727 ¬∞C)**, holding (soaking) for a controlled duration, and subsequent cooling ‚Äî primarily to **reduce brittleness, relieve internal stresses, and restore a balanced combination of hardness, strength, and toughness**.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context (PBAUT304 ‚Äì Material Science and Metallurgy), tempering is invariably treated as the **final, indispensable step** of the *hardening-and-tempering* sequence. Without tempering, as-quenched martensite is **extremely hard but unacceptably brittle**, and the component would fail catastrophically under service loads.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ‚Äî Plain English Intuition

Imagine you have just **frozen a rubber ball in liquid nitrogen** (this is analogous to *quenching*). The ball becomes glass-hard and extremely brittle ‚Äî drop it and it shatters like glass. To make it useful, you must **warm it up gradually** so the internal stresses relax and the structure finds a more stable, less aggressive configuration. The rubber ball regains some flexibility while keeping much of its hardness.

**That "warming up" is exactly what tempering does to hardened steel:**

- **Quenched steel** = the frozen rubber ball ‚Üí hard, brittle, full of trapped internal stress.
- **Tempered steel** = the warmed rubber ball ‚Üí still hard, but tougher, with stresses relieved.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Tempering is a *deliberate trade-off*. You give up a small amount of hardness to gain a large improvement in toughness and dimensional stability. The art of heat treatment is choosing **how much** hardness to trade away, which is governed by the **tempering temperature**.

## 1.3 Physical Parameters & Standard Metrics

- **Tempering Temperature Range:** 100 ¬∞C ‚Äì 650 ¬∞C (always below A1)
- **Hardness HRC (typical, for 0.4 % C steel after quenching):** ~60 HRC (untempered martensite) ‚Üí 20‚Äì55 HRC (after tempering)
- **Holding Time:** typically 1 hour per 25 mm of cross-section
- **Cooling Medium:** usually still air (oil is used to avoid temper embrittlement in certain alloy steels)
- **Common Process Designation:** *Tempering Temperature in ¬∞C* (e.g., "tempered at 400 ¬∞C")

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Tempering is Needed ‚Äî The Driving Forces

When steel is rapidly quenched from above A3 / Acm:

1. **Martensite is supersaturated** with carbon (BCT lattice) ‚Üí thermodynamically unstable.
2. **Retained austenite** exists (especially in high-carbon steels) ‚Üí unstable at room temperature.
3. **Quenching stresses** (thermal + transformational) ‚Üí can cause cracking.

Tempering allows the carbon atoms to **diffuse out of the distorted BCT lattice**, forming fine carbides, and the system moves toward a lower free-energy, more stable configuration.

## 2.2 The Four Stages of Tempering (KTU Syllabus Highlight)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU board questions frequently ask: *"Explain the stages of tempering of martensite."* Memorize the four stages with their temperature ranges and microstructural products.

| Stage | Temperature Range | Microstructural Change | Effect on Properties |
|:-----:|:-----------------:|:-----------------------|:---------------------|
| **Stage I** | 100 ‚Äì 250 ¬∞C | Precipitation of **Œµ-carbide (Fe2.4C)** from supersaturated martensite ‚Üí low-carbon martensite + Œµ-carbide | Slight hardness reduction; relief of stresses |
| **Stage II** | 200 ‚Äì 300 ¬∞C | Decomposition of **retained austenite** into bainitic ferrite + carbide | Hardness may *increase* slightly in high-C steels (austenite ‚Üí bainite is harder) |
| **Stage III** | 250 ‚Äì 350 ¬∞C | Decomposition of low-carbon martensite ‚Üí **ferrite + cementite (Fe3C)**; Œµ-carbide dissolves and is replaced by cementite | Significant drop in hardness, increase in toughness |
| **Stage IV** | 350 ‚Äì 650 ¬∞C | **Coarsening of cementite** particles (Ostwald ripening), recovery and recrystallization of ferrite matrix | Continued softening; large increase in toughness and ductility |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula / Relation | Description | Typical Use |
|:-------------------|:------------|:------------|
| $T_{temper} < A_1 \approx 727\,¬∞C$ | Tempering must be sub-critical | Process design constraint |
| $\Delta HRC \approx f(T_{temper},\,t_{hold})$ | Hardness drop increases with temperature and time | Predicting final hardness |
| $\sigma_{y} \approx \sigma_{0} + k_y \cdot d^{-1/2}$ (Hall-Petch, ferrite matrix) | Yield strength depends on ferrite grain size | Stage IV tempering recrystallization |
| $d_{mean} = d_{0} \cdot \exp\!\left(\frac{Q}{RT}\right) \cdot t^{1/3}$ (Ostwald ripening, simplified) | Carbide coarsening law (Stage IV) | Predicting over-tempering |
| $M_{s}\,(¬∞C) \approx 539 - 423\cdot\%C - 30.4\cdot\%Mn - 12.1\cdot\%Cr - 17.7\cdot\%Ni - 7.5\cdot\%Mo$ | Martensite start temperature (Andrews' equation) | Calculating % retained austenite tendency |
| $V_{ret.\,aust.} \approx 1 - \exp\!\big(-0.011\cdot(M_{s} - T_{q})\big)$ | Empirical retained austenite fraction (where $T_q$ is quench temperature) | Predicting Stage II behavior |

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of Thumb (KTU-favorite):** *"Higher tempering temperature ‚Üí lower hardness, higher toughness."* The **Hollomon‚ÄìJaffe Tempering Parameter** combines temperature and time: $P = T(\log t + C)$, where $T$ is in Kelvin, $t$ in hours, and $C \approx 20$ for plain-carbon steels. Same $P$ ‚áí same hardness.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive gears & shafts** ‚Äî tempered at 400‚Äì600 ¬∞C to achieve a tough core with adequate surface hardness.
- **Cutting tools (drill bits, taps)** ‚Äî tempered at 150‚Äì200 ¬∞C (low-temperature tempering) to retain HRC ‚â• 60.
- **Springs** ‚Äî tempered at 300‚Äì450 ¬∞C (medium-temperature tempering) for the optimal strength-elasticity balance.
- **Railway rails & axles** ‚Äî high-temperature tempering to absorb impact energy and prevent brittle fracture.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Energy Analysis: Why Martensite Wants to Temper

Martensite is a **body-centered tetragonal (BCT)** phase produced by a *diffusionless* shear transformation. Its free energy is far higher than that of the equilibrium aggregate (ferrite + cementite). The driving force for tempering is:

$$\Delta G_{temper} = G_{martensite} - \big[\,G_{\alpha\text{-Fe}} + G_{Fe_3C}\,\big]$$

> Since $\Delta G_{temper} \gg 0$ for supersaturated martensite, the transformation to (ferrite + cementite) is spontaneous once atomic mobility is provided by heating.

**Step-by-step reasoning of how $\Delta G$ is released:**

1. **Carbon atoms** are trapped in octahedral interstitial sites of the BCT lattice ‚Äî this creates elastic strain energy $\propto c^{2}_{\text{C-in-martensite}}$.
2. On heating, the carbon diffusivity rises rapidly: $D_C \propto \exp(-Q/RT)$.
3. Carbon segregates to lattice defects and nucleates **Œµ-carbide** (Stage I) ‚Üí strain energy released.
4. Retained austenite, also high in free energy, decomposes (Stage II).
5. Carbide structure transforms to equilibrium **cementite (Fe3C)** (Stage III).
6. Cementite particles **coarsen** to minimize total interfacial energy (Stage IV).

## 3.2 The Hollomon‚ÄìJaffe Tempering Parameter ‚Äî Full Derivation

The **Hollomon‚ÄìJaffe parameter** is one of the most important equations in industrial heat-treatment design. It is a frequent KTU 14-mark question.

**Starting premise:** For a given steel, the *hardness* after tempering depends only on a single combined parameter of temperature and time (this is an empirical observation, not a derivation from first principles).

Let:
- $T$ = tempering temperature in **Kelvin**
- $t$ = tempering time in **hours**
- $C$ = a steel-dependent constant (‚âà 20 for plain-carbon steels)

The empirical relation is:

$$P = T \cdot (\log_{10} t + C)$$

**Step 1 ‚Äî Combine $T$ and $t$ into a single variable.**

Suppose two tempering schedules $(T_1, t_1)$ and $(T_2, t_2)$ give the **same hardness** $H$. Then:

$$T_1 \cdot (\log_{10} t_1 + C) = T_2 \cdot (\log_{10} t_2 + C)$$

**Step 2 ‚Äî Solve for the unknown time (or temperature).**

If $T_1$, $t_1$, and $T_2$ are known and we want $t_2$:

$$(\log_{10} t_2 + C) = \frac{T_1}{T_2} \cdot (\log_{10} t_1 + C)$$

$$\log_{10} t_2 = \frac{T_1}{T_2} \cdot (\log_{10} t_1 + C) - C$$

$$t_2 = 10^{\,\frac{T_1}{T_2}(\log_{10} t_1 + C) - C}$$

**Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style):**

> AISI 1040 steel is tempered at $T_1 = 873\,\text{K}$ (600 ¬∞C) for $t_1 = 1$ h and reaches 25 HRC. What time $t_2$ at $T_2 = 773\,\text{K}$ (500 ¬∞C) will give the same hardness? Take $C = 20$.

**Step A:** Compute $\log_{10} t_1 + C = 0 + 20 = 20$.

**Step B:** Form the ratio:

$$\frac{T_1}{T_2} = \frac{873}{773} = 1.1294$$

**Step C:** Multiply:

$$\frac{T_1}{T_2} \cdot (\log_{10} t_1 + C) = 1.1294 \times 20 = 22.588$$

**Step D:** Subtract $C$:

$$\log_{10} t_2 = 22.588 - 20 = 2.588$$

**Step E:** Exponentiate:

$$t_2 = 10^{2.588} \approx 387\,\text{hours}$$

> **Physical interpretation:** Lowering the tempering temperature from 600 ¬∞C to 500 ¬∞C increases the required time by nearly **400√ó** to achieve the same hardness. This is the reason industrial specifications are *very sensitive* to furnace-temperature control.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Mechanical-Property vs. Tempering Temperature

For a quenched **0.4 % C plain-carbon steel** (starting hardness ‚âà 60 HRC, fully martensitic):

| Tempering Temperature (¬∞C) | Approx. Hardness (HRC) | Tensile Strength (MPa) | Impact Energy (J) | Typical Application |
|:--------------------------:|:----------------------:|:----------------------:|:-----------------:|:--------------------|
| Untempered (as-quenched) | 60 | ~2100 | < 5 (very brittle) | None ‚Äî must be tempered |
| 100 ‚Äì 200 (Low) | 58 ‚Äì 60 | ~2000 | 5 ‚Äì 10 | Cutting tools, dies, files |
| 200 ‚Äì 350 (Low‚ÄìMed) | 50 ‚Äì 58 | ~1700 | 10 ‚Äì 25 | Bearings, gauges |
| 350 ‚Äì 500 (Medium) | 38 ‚Äì 50 | ~1300 | 30 ‚Äì 60 | Springs, crankshafts |
| 500 ‚Äì 650 (High) | 20 ‚Äì 38 | ~800 | 60 ‚Äì 120 | Structural shafts, gears, rails |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often write *"tempering always decreases hardness"*. This is **incorrect** for **Stage II** in high-carbon steels, where retained-austenite decomposition can cause a *secondary hardening peak* or arrest the hardness drop.

## 3.4 Worked Derivation: Carbide Coarsening Kinetics (Stage IV)

In Stage IV, cementite particles grow by **Ostwald ripening** (small particles dissolve, large particles grow).

The Lifshitz‚ÄìSlyozov‚ÄìWagner (LSW) law gives the mean particle radius as a function of time:

$$\bar{r}(t)^{3} - \bar{r}_0^{3} = \frac{8 \gamma D C_{\infty} V_m^2}{9 R T} \cdot t$$

Where:
- $\bar{r}(t)$ = mean carbide radius at time $t$
- $\bar{r}_0$ = initial mean radius
- $\gamma$ = carbide‚Äìmatrix interfacial energy
- $D$ = carbon diffusivity in ferrite
- $C_{\infty}$ = carbon solubility in ferrite at temperature $T$
- $V_m$ = molar volume of cementite
- $R$ = universal gas constant
- $T$ = absolute temperature

**Step 1:** Since $D = D_0 \exp(-Q/RT)$, the growth rate is:

$$\frac{d(\bar{r}^{3})}{dt} = \frac{8 \gamma V_m^2 C_{\infty} D_0}{9RT} \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{Q}{RT}\right)$$

**Step 2:** This shows the growth rate *accelerates* with temperature through the Arrhenius term ‚Äî explaining why over-temperature tempering rapidly softens steel.

**Step 3:** The **toughness improvement** in Stage IV is partially due to the **increased inter-particle spacing $\lambda$** (since $\lambda \propto \bar{r}$ for a given carbide volume fraction), which reduces the local stress concentration around each carbide.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process Flow Diagram ‚Äî Tempering as Part of the Hardening Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Annealed Steel - Soft, Equilibrium Ferrite + Pearlite] --> B[Austenitizing: Heat to 30-50 C above Ac3]
    B --> C[Soak at temperature for homogenization]
    C --> D[Quench rapidly in Water or Oil]
    D --> E[As-Quenched Martensite - Very Hard, Very Brittle, High Internal Stresses]
    E --> F[Re-heat to Temperature BELOW A1 = 727 C]
    F --> G{Tempering Temperature Selected?}
    G -->|100 to 200 C| H[Low-Temp Tempering - Tools, Gauges]
    G -->|200 to 350 C| I[Medium-Temp Tempering - Bearings]
    G -->|350 to 500 C| J[Medium-Temp Tempering - Springs, Crankshafts]
    G -->|500 to 650 C| K[High-Temp Tempering - Gears, Rails]
    H --> L[Hold 1 hour per 25 mm section]
    I --> L
    J --> L
    K --> L
    L --> M[Cool in Still Air or Oil]
    M --> N[Final Tempered Product - Balanced Hardness and Toughness]
```

## 4.2 Microstructural Evolution During Tempering ‚Äî Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph "QUENCHED STATE"
        Q1[BCT Martensite] --> Q2[Retained Austenite]
        Q3[High Internal Stress] --> Q4[Supersaturated Carbon]
    end
    subgraph "STAGE I 100 to 250 C"
        S1A[Carbon Segregation] --> S1B[Epsilon Carbide Fe2.4C Precipitation]
        S1B --> S1C[Low-C Martensite plus Epsilon Carbide]
    end
    subgraph "STAGE II 200 to 300 C"
        S2A[Retained Austenite Decomposition] --> S2B[Bainitic Ferrite plus Carbide]
    end
    subgraph "STAGE III 250 to 350 C"
        S3A[Residual Martensite Decomposition] --> S3B[Epsilon Carbide Dissolves]
        S3B --> S3C[Equilibrium Cementite Fe3C Forms]
    end
    subgraph "STAGE IV 350 to 650 C"
        S4A[Carbide Coarsening - Ostwald Ripening] --> S4B[Recovery of Ferrite Matrix]
        S4B --> S4C[Recrystallization of Ferrite]
    end
    Q1 --> S1A
    Q2 --> S2A
    S1C --> S3A
    S2B --> S3A
    S3C --> S4A
    S4C --> OUT[Tempered Ferrite plus Coarsened Cementite]
```

## 4.3 Hardness vs. Tempering Temperature ‚Äî Schematic Block Representation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[HRC 60 - As Quenched Martensite] --> B[HRC 58 - Stage I to II - 100 to 250 C]
    B --> C[HRC 55 - Stage II to III - 250 to 350 C]
    C --> D[HRC 45 - Stage III - 350 to 450 C]
    D --> E[HRC 32 - Stage IV - 450 to 550 C]
    E --> F[HRC 22 - Stage IV Over-Tempered - 550 to 650 C]
    F -.->|Reversion possible only by re-austenitizing| A
```

## 4.4 Decision Block: Avoiding Temper Embrittlement

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Plain-Carbon vs Alloy Steel?] --> P1{Contains Mn, Cr, Ni, Sb, P, Sn, As?}
    P1 -->|No| R1[Safe to cool in air from any tempering temperature]
    P1 -->|Yes| R2[Tempering in 250 to 400 C range]
    R2 --> R3[At Risk of Blue Brittleness]
    R3 --> R4[Avoid this range OR temper below 250 C]
    P1 -->|Yes, with Cr Ni Mn| S1[Tempering in 400 to 600 C range]
    S1 --> S2[At Risk of Tempered Martensite Embrittlement TME]
    S2 --> S3[Cool rapidly through this range OR temper above 600 C]
    R1 --> END[Component ready for service]
    R4 --> END
    S3 --> END
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read these diagrams in the exam:** KTU board examiners reward diagrams that are *labelled* with both *temperatures* and *phase names*. A diagram with just boxes and arrows, no labels, will get partial credit only.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ‚Äî Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam ‚Äì May 2024]

**Define tempering. Why is it considered a necessary step after quenching of steel?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Tempering is the process of reheating a quench-hardened steel to a temperature **below the lower critical temperature (A1)**, holding it for a prescribed time, and then cooling, usually in air.

It is necessary because as-quenched martensite is **(i)** extremely hard but brittle, **(ii)** contains high internal stresses that may cause cracking, and **(iii)** is thermodynamically unstable due to supersaturated carbon. Tempering relieves stresses, reduces brittleness, decomposes retained austenite, and produces a useful combination of hardness and toughness.

*(Allocation: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for "why needed" ‚Äî three reasons, 1 mark for concluding statement of property improvement.)*

### Q2. [KTU University Exam ‚Äì Dec 2023]

**List the four stages of tempering of quenched steel and state the temperature range of each stage.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Stage | Temperature Range |
|:-----:|:-----------------:|
| Stage I ‚Äî Precipitation of Œµ-carbide | 100 ‚Äì 250 ¬∞C |
| Stage II ‚Äî Decomposition of retained austenite | 200 ‚Äì 300 ¬∞C |
| Stage III ‚Äî Formation of cementite from Œµ-carbide + residual martensite | 250 ‚Äì 350 ¬∞C |
| Stage IV ‚Äî Carbide coarsening and ferrite recrystallization | 350 ‚Äì 650 ¬∞C |

*(Allocation: 1 mark for listing the four stages, 1 mark for the temperature ranges, 1 mark for microstructural products.)*

## 5.2 Part B ‚Äî Essay Questions (14 Marks Each)

> Choose **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each question has two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

### Question A ‚Äî [KTU University Exam ‚Äì May 2023, Modified]

**A(a).** Explain in detail the structural changes that occur during the tempering of quenched plain-carbon steel. Discuss the effect of tempering temperature on the mechanical properties. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Structural changes (4 marks):** Tempering of as-quenched martensite progresses through four overlapping stages:

1. **Stage I (100 ‚Äì 250 ¬∞C):** Carbon diffuses to lattice defects and forms very fine **Œµ-carbide (Fe2.4C)** platelets coherently with the matrix. Martensite loses its tetragonality gradually and becomes low-carbon martensite.
2. **Stage II (200 ‚Äì 300 ¬∞C):** The **retained austenite** (present in high-carbon steels due to incomplete Mf) decomposes into a mixture of **bainitic ferrite and carbide**. In high-C steels this may cause a small hardness *increase* (secondary hardening peak).
3. **Stage III (250 ‚Äì 350 ¬∞C):** The remaining martensite decomposes, Œµ-carbide dissolves, and equilibrium **cementite (Fe3C)** nucleates and grows as discrete particles in a ferritic matrix.
4. **Stage IV (350 ‚Äì 650 ¬∞C):** Cementite particles **coarsen (Ostwald ripening)**, and the ferrite matrix undergoes **recovery and recrystallization**, producing equiaxed ferrite grains with spheroidized carbides.

**Effect on mechanical properties (3 marks):** As the tempering temperature rises:
- **Hardness and tensile strength decrease** progressively (from ~60 HRC at 200 ¬∞C to ~22 HRC at 650 ¬∞C).
- **Toughness and impact energy increase** dramatically (from < 5 J in as-quenched state to > 100 J after high-temperature tempering).
- **Ductility (% elongation, % reduction in area) improves.**
- **Internal quenching stresses are relieved**, improving dimensional stability.

**A(b).** What is temper embrittlement? Explain the two main types and the methods to avoid them. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (1 mark):** Temper embrittlement is the loss of **toughness (reduction in impact energy)** that occurs when certain alloy steels are tempered, or cooled slowly through, specific temperature ranges, without an accompanying significant change in hardness.

**Type 1 ‚Äî Blue Brittleness (250 ‚Äì 400 ¬∞C) (2.5 marks):**
- Occurs in plain-carbon and low-alloy steels when tempered (or plastically deformed) in the range 250‚Äì400 ¬∞C.
- Caused by **dynamic strain aging** ‚Äî interstitial nitrogen and carbon atoms pin dislocations during plastic deformation at these temperatures.
- Manifests as a **peak in tensile strength and hardness, but a trough in impact energy**.
- The blue oxide tarnish formed on the steel surface in this range gives the name "blue brittleness".
- **Avoidance:** Avoid working or tempering in this range; use steels with Al or Ti additions that tie up nitrogen as AlN / TiN.

**Type 2 ‚Äî Tempered-Martensite Embrittlement (TME) (2.5 marks):**
- Occurs in alloy steels containing **Cr, Ni, Mn, Sb, P, Sn, As**, tempered in the range **350 ‚Äì 575 ¬∞C** and then slowly cooled.
- Caused by **segregation of impurity elements (P, Sn, Sb) to the prior-austenite grain boundaries**, weakening them.
- Results in **intergranular (IGC) fracture** along prior-austenite boundaries.
- **Avoidance:**
  1. Rapidly cool through the 350‚Äì575 ¬∞C range after tempering.
  2. Use higher tempering temperatures (> 600 ¬∞C) followed by quenching.
  3. Add **Mo (~0.5 %)** which forms fine Mo2C that blocks grain-boundary segregation.
  4. Use extra-low-impurity steels (ELI grades) or vacuum-arc remelting.

**Alternative Mitigation Strategy (1 mark):** For components that must operate in the embrittlement range, select **austempered** or **mar-tempered** microstructures that avoid the critical temperature window.

### Question B ‚Äî [KTU University Exam ‚Äì Dec 2022, Modified]

**B(a).** A plain-carbon steel is tempered at 600 ¬∞C for 1 hour and attains a hardness of 230 HB. Using the Hollomon‚ÄìJaffe tempering parameter (with C = 20), calculate the time required at 500 ¬∞C to obtain the same hardness. Comment on the practical significance of the result. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 ‚Äî Write the Hollomon‚ÄìJaffe equation:**

$$P = T \cdot (\log_{10} t + C)$$

**Step 2 ‚Äî Set $P_1 = P_2$ (same hardness):**

$$T_1 \cdot (\log_{10} t_1 + C) = T_2 \cdot (\log_{10} t_2 + C)$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Convert temperatures to Kelvin:**

$$T_1 = 600 + 273 = 873\,\text{K}$$
$$T_2 = 500 + 273 = 773\,\text{K}$$

**Step 4 ‚Äî Substitute and solve for $\log_{10} t_2$:**

$$873 \cdot (\log_{10} 1 + 20) = 773 \cdot (\log_{10} t_2 + 20)$$

$$873 \cdot (0 + 20) = 773 \cdot (\log_{10} t_2 + 20)$$

$$17460 = 773 \cdot (\log_{10} t_2 + 20)$$

$$\log_{10} t_2 + 20 = \frac{17460}{773} = 22.589$$

$$\log_{10} t_2 = 2.589$$

**Step 5 ‚Äî Solve for $t_2$:**

$$t_2 = 10^{2.589} \approx 388\,\text{hours}$$

*(1 mark for formula, 1 mark for substitution, 1 mark for K conversion, 2 marks for algebra, 1 mark for final answer, 1 mark for comment.)*

**Step 6 ‚Äî Practical comment (1 mark):**
Lowering the tempering temperature by just 100 ¬∞C (from 600 ¬∞C to 500 ¬∞C) increases the required soaking time by a factor of nearly **400**. This has two major industrial consequences:
- Furnace temperature control must be tight (¬± 5‚Äì10 ¬∞C) to ensure reproducible hardness.
- It is often **more economical to temper at a higher temperature for a shorter time** than at a lower temperature for a long time, even though the resulting toughness may be slightly lower.

**B(b).** Compare the microstructure, hardness, and typical applications of steels tempered at (i) 150 ¬∞C, (ii) 400 ¬∞C, and (iii) 600 ¬∞C. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | (i) 150 ¬∞C ‚Äî Low-Temp Tempering | (ii) 400 ¬∞C ‚Äî Medium-Temp Tempering | (iii) 600 ¬∞C ‚Äî High-Temp Tempering |
|:--------|:--------------------------------|:--------------------------------------|:--------------------------------------|
| **Microstructure** | Tempered martensite + Œµ-carbide (Fe2.4C) | Tempered martensite + fine cementite (Fe3C) | Equiaxed ferrite + coarsened spheroidized cementite |
| **Hardness (HRC)** | 58 ‚Äì 60 | 40 ‚Äì 45 | 22 ‚Äì 30 |
| **Tensile strength (MPa)** | ~2000 | ~1250 | ~800 |
| **Impact toughness** | Low (5 ‚Äì 10 J) | Moderate (30 ‚Äì 60 J) | High (> 100 J) |
| **Stage of tempering** | Stage I dominant | End of Stage III / beginning of Stage IV | Stage IV complete |
| **Typical applications** | Cutting tools, files, drill bits, dies, taps, razors, gauges | Springs, leaf springs, clutch plates, hand tools (hammers) | Gears, shafts, axles, railway rails, structural components, bolts |

*(Allocation: 1 mark for identifying microstructure, 1 mark for hardness, 1 mark for toughness, 1 mark for stage, 2 marks for accurate application, 1 mark for concluding comparative remark.)*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall ‚Äî Common Marks Lost:**
> 1. **Writing "tempering decreases hardness"** as a blanket statement. **Wrong.** Stage II in high-C steels can *increase* hardness.
> 2. **Confusing "blue brittleness" (250‚Äì400 ¬∞C, dynamic strain aging)** with **"TME" (350‚Äì575 ¬∞C, grain-boundary segregation)**. These are *different* mechanisms.
> 3. **Forgetting to convert ¬∞C to Kelvin** in the Hollomon‚ÄìJaffe equation ‚Äî this alone causes a full 2-mark deduction.
> 4. **Calling martensite a "phase" without specifying BCT** when compared with ferrite (BCC).
> 5. **Omitting the cooling step** when describing the tempering process. The sequence is: *reheat ‚Üí hold ‚Üí cool*, not just *reheat*.
> 6. **Listing only 2 stages instead of 4** in tempering ‚Äî KTU's official answer key requires all four.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist ‚Äî Tempering**

- ‚òê **Definition:** Sub-critical reheating of quench-hardened steel to relieve stress and improve toughness.
- ‚òê **Temperature:** Always **< A1 = 727 ¬∞C**; range 100‚Äì650 ¬∞C.
- ‚òê **Four Stages:**
  - **I** (100‚Äì250 ¬∞C): Œµ-carbide precipitation
  - **II** (200‚Äì300 ¬∞C): Retained austenite decomposition
  - **III** (250‚Äì350 ¬∞C): Cementite (Fe3C) formation
  - **IV** (350‚Äì650 ¬∞C): Carbide coarsening + ferrite recrystallization
- ‚òê **Hollomon‚ÄìJaffe Parameter:** $P = T \cdot (\log_{10} t + C)$, $C \approx 20$ for plain-carbon steels.
- ‚òê **Rule of thumb:** Higher tempering temperature ‚áí **lower hardness**, **higher toughness**, **higher ductility**, **lower strength**.
- ‚òê **Three Tempering Categories:**
  - **Low (100‚Äì250 ¬∞C):** Tools, dies, bearings, files
  - **Medium (350‚Äì500 ¬∞C):** Springs, crankshafts
  - **High (500‚Äì650 ¬∞C):** Gears, shafts, axles, structural parts
- ‚òê **Blue Brittleness:** 250‚Äì400 ¬∞C, plain-carbon steels, dynamic strain aging by N/C, *avoid this range or use Al/Ti-killed steels*.
- ‚òê **Tempered-Martensite Embrittlement (TME):** 350‚Äì575 ¬∞C, alloy steels (Cr, Ni, Mn), P/Sn/Sb segregation at prior-austenite grain boundaries, *avoid by fast cooling or adding 0.5 % Mo*.
- ‚òê **Cooling:** Usually still air; oil-cool for alloy steels to bypass TME range.
- ‚òê **Key phrase for 2-mark definitions:** *"Reheating a hardened steel to a temperature below the lower critical temperature (A1), holding, and cooling ‚Äî to reduce brittleness and relieve internal stresses while retaining useful hardness."*
- ‚òê **Industrial tip:** Under-tempering ‚áí brittle, prone to cracking. Over-tempering ‚áí soft, wears quickly. *Aim for the lowest tempering temperature that still meets toughness requirements.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hardenability and Jominy end quench test
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAUT304 - MATERIAL SCIENCE AND METALLURGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Hardenability and Jominy end quench test

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Hardenability

**Hardenability** is defined as the ability of a ferrous alloy (steel) to form **martensite** when quenched from a temperature above the upper critical temperature ($A_{c3}$ or $A_{cm}$) into a cooling medium. It is fundamentally a measure of the **depth to which a steel can be hardened** by quenching, rather than the maximum hardness attainable.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> Hardenability is the property that determines the depth and distribution of hardness produced in a steel by quenching. It is distinguished from **hardness** itself ‚Äî hardness is a mechanical property (resistance to indentation), while hardenability is a metallurgical property (ease of martensite formation).

**Quantitative Definition (Grossmann):** Hardenability is quantified as the **critical diameter ($D_C$)** ‚Äî the maximum diameter of a round bar that will through-harden (produce 50% martensite) at its center when subjected to a specific quenching medium.

### 1.2 Hardenability vs. Hardness ‚Äî The Critical Distinction

| Property | Definition | Depends On |
|----------|------------|------------|
| **Hardness** | Resistance to plastic deformation / indentation | Carbon content primarily |
| **Hardenability** | Ability to form martensite to a certain depth | Alloying elements, grain size, quenching severity |

> [!NOTE]
> A high-carbon steel (e.g., **AISI 1080**) is **hard** but not necessarily **hardenable to a great depth**. Conversely, a low-carbon alloy steel (e.g., **AISI 4340**) may be only moderately hard but **hardenable to very large sections**.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy ‚Äî The "Bread Baking" Analogy

Imagine you are baking a long loaf of bread in an oven. You want the **entire loaf** to be cooked (hardened) through, not just the crust.

- **Hardness** = How well-done the **surface** becomes (browning of the crust) ‚Üí depends on oven temperature & time.
- **Hardenability** = How deeply the heat (analogous to the quenching front) **penetrates** into the loaf ‚Üí depends on the bread's internal thermal conductivity and composition.

Just as a thick loaf of dense rye bread cooks slowly and unevenly, a **large section of unalloyed steel** cannot be hardened through its entire cross-section, because heat cannot escape fast enough from the center. Adding **alloying elements** is like adding channels inside the bread that allow the heat front to propagate deeper, achieving uniform hardening.

### 1.4 Physical Metrics & Standards

- **Standard Test:** ASTM A255 / ISO 642 ‚Äî Jominy End Quench Test
- **Standard Specimen:** $\phi 25\ \text{mm} \times 100\ \text{mm}$ cylindrical bar
- **Quench Water Temperature:** $24\ ^\circ\text{C} \pm 5\ ^\circ\text{C}$
- **Quench Time:** 10 minutes minimum
- **Hardness Measurement Locations:** Flat ground along the length, typically at **1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 10, 12, 16, 20, 24, 28, 32 mm** from the quenched end
- **Austenitizing Temperature (for plain carbon steel):** $A_{c3} + 30\ ^\circ\text{C} \approx 870\ ^\circ\text{C} - 927\ ^\circ\text{C}$

### 1.5 Why Hardenability Matters in Engineering

- Determines whether a thick component (gear, shaft, axle, crankshaft) can be **through-hardened**.
- Guides **alloy selection** for critical load-bearing components.
- Influences **quench medium selection** (water, brine, oil, polymer).
- Predicts **distortion and cracking tendencies** during quenching.
- Essential for designing **heat treatment process routes** for medium/large cross-sections.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Factors Affecting Hardenability

Hardenability is governed by the rate of **austenite decomposition** to non-martensitic products (pearlite, bainite, ferrite) versus its retention as martensite. The following factors control this race:

#### (a) Carbon Content
- Increases the **maximum attainable hardness** (martensite hardness rises with %C up to ~0.6%).
- **Does not significantly increase hardenability** beyond ~0.6% C.
- Hypereutectoid steels (C > 0.8%) may show **reduced** hardenability due to proeutectoid cementite networks that nucleate pearlite.

#### (b) Alloying Elements
Alloying elements that **increase** hardenability (in order of effectiveness):
- **Manganese (Mn)**, **Chromium (Cr)**, **Molybdenum (Mo)**, **Vanadium (V)**, **Silicon (Si)**, **Nickel (Ni)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mechanism:** These elements **delay the pearlitic and bainitic (diffusional) transformation** by retarding carbon diffusion and shifting the **TTT/CCT curves to the right**, thereby giving martensite more time to form at the quench rate.

#### (c) Austenite Grain Size
- **Coarse grain** = higher hardenability (fewer grain boundaries = fewer nucleation sites for pearlite).
- Measured by **ASTM grain size number (G)** ‚Äî lower G = coarser grain.
- Coarse grains, however, increase **quench-cracking risk** and reduce toughness.

#### (d) Quenching Medium Severity
- The cooling rate of the medium controls the rate of heat extraction from the surface.
- **Severity of Quench (H-value)** ‚Äî Grossmann's quantitative measure:
  - **Oil (still):** $H \approx 0.25 - 0.30$
  - **Water (still, 18 ¬∞C):** $H \approx 1.0$
  - **Brine (10% NaCl, 18 ¬∞C):** $H \approx 2.0$
  - **Ideal Quench:** $H = \infty$

### 2.2 Grossmann's Critical Diameter Concept

**Grossmann and Bain (1930s)** developed the foundational hardenability framework. Three key parameters:

1. **Critical Diameter ($D_C$):** The largest bar diameter in which the **center** achieves 50% martensite when quenched in a given medium.
2. **Ideal Critical Diameter ($D_I$):** The largest bar diameter in which the **center** achieves 50% martensite under an **ideal quench** ($H = \infty$, infinite cooling rate at surface).
3. **Severity of Quench (H):** A dimensionless number characterizing the quenching medium's heat extraction capability.

**Grossmann's relationship** (linking $D_C$, $D_I$, and $H$):

$$D_C = D_I \cdot f(H)$$

where $f(H)$ is a function tabulated by Grossmann (always $\leq 1$, since $D_C \leq D_I$).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Symbol | Expression | Units | Meaning |
|---|---------------------|--------|------------|-------|---------|
| 1 | Critical Diameter | $D_C$ | $D_C = D_I \cdot f(H)$ | mm | Largest through-hardenable diameter in given quench |
| 2 | Ideal Critical Diameter | $D_I$ | Read from chart for given steel | mm | Through-hardenability in ideal quench |
| 3 | Severity of Quench | $H$ | Tabulated value | dimensionless | Quench intensity |
| 4 | Grain Size Multiplier | ‚Äî | $D_I$ depends on ASTM grain size $G$ | ‚Äî | Coarse grain ‚Üí higher $D_I$ |
| 5 | Carbon Multiplier | ‚Äî | Each alloying element multiplies $D_I$ | ‚Äî | Alloying factor $f_i$ |
| 6 | Grossmann's Multiplying Factors | $f_{Mn}, f_{Cr}, f_{Ni}, f_{Mo}, f_{Si}$ | Tabulated vs. %alloy √ó grain size | dimensionless | Alloy hardenability contribution |
| 7 | Cooling Rate at Jominy Position | $dT/dt$ | Decreases exponentially with distance $d$ | ¬∞C/s | Faster near quenched end |
| 8 | Hardness at Distance $d$ | $HRC_d$ | Read from Jominy curve | HRC | Direct test output |
| 9 | Effective Cooling Rate | ‚Äî | $C.R. = $ function of $d$ | ¬∞C/s | Maps $d$ to CCT position |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Insight:** The Jominy test essentially **maps position (mm from quenched end) to cooling rate (¬∞C/s)** ‚Äî which then maps to a point on the **CCT diagram** determining the microstructure (and hence hardness).

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Gears (e.g., 20MnCr5):** Selection of case-hardening vs. through-hardening steel based on hardenability.
- **Aircraft Landing Gear (e.g., 300M):** Requires high $D_I$ to ensure through-hardening of $\phi 100+$ mm sections.
- **Railway Axles (e.g., AAR M-101):** Hardenability must be controlled to specified Jominy limits.
- **Tool Steels:** Deep-hardening tool steels (e.g., H13, D2) for hot-work and die applications rely on Cr, Mo, V additions for $D_I$ > 150 mm.
- **Power Generation Shafts (e.g., 30CrMoNiV5-11):** Rotor shafts of turbines require exceptional hardenability to avoid soft cores.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Grossmann's Critical Diameter Relation

The relationship $D_C = D_I \cdot f(H)$ is **empirically derived** from a master set of experiments where cylindrical bars of various diameters were quenched in media of different $H$ values, and the largest diameter achieving 50% martensite at the center was recorded.

**Step 1: Define the standard of reference (ideal quench).**
Under an **ideal quench** ($H \to \infty$), the bar surface instantly reaches bath temperature, and cooling proceeds solely by **internal conduction**. The largest diameter through-hardened in this condition is defined as $D_I$ (a function of steel composition and austenite grain size only).

**Step 2: Define the actual quench condition.**
Under a real quench medium with severity $H$, surface cooling is finite, governed by the surface heat transfer coefficient $h$:

$$h \propto H$$

**Step 3: Centerline cooling rate at the critical condition.**
For a cylindrical bar of diameter $D$ in a medium of severity $H$, the cooling rate at the center is approximately:

$$\left(\frac{dT}{dt}\right)_{\text{center}} \approx \frac{2 h (T_s - T_m)}{\rho c_p D} \cdot F(\text{Bi})$$

where $\text{Bi} = \frac{hD}{2k}$ is the **Biot number**, and $F(\text{Bi}) < 1$ accounts for finite internal conduction.

**Step 4: Hardenability criterion.**
For 50% martensite at the center, the centerline cooling rate must equal or exceed a critical value $\left(\frac{dT}{dt}\right)_{\text{crit}}$ determined by the CCT diagram:

$$\left(\frac{dT}{dt}\right)_{\text{center}} \geq \left(\frac{dT}{dt}\right)_{\text{crit}}$$

**Step 5: Substitution and simplification.**
Substituting the cooling rate expression and solving for $D$ at the limiting condition yields the bar diameter that just satisfies the criterion ‚Äî this is the **critical diameter**:

$$D_C = \frac{2 h (T_s - T_m) \cdot F(\text{Bi})}{\rho c_p \left(\frac{dT}{dt}\right)_{\text{crit}}}$$

**Step 6: Separation into two functions.**
Recognizing that $h$ is a property of the **quench medium** while the rest is a property of the **steel**, Grossmann separated the relation as:

$$D_C = D_I \cdot f(H)$$

$$\boxed{D_C = D_I \cdot f(H)}$$

This is the **master hardenability equation**. $D_I$ depends only on the steel (composition + grain size), and $f(H)$ depends only on the quench medium.

### 3.2 Worked Example ‚Äî Calculating Critical Diameter

**Given:**
- Steel: AISI 4140 (composition: 0.40% C, 0.80% Mn, 1.0% Cr, 0.20% Mo)
- ASTM grain size number: $G = 7$ (fine grain)
- Quench medium: Still oil at 60 ¬∞C ‚Üí $H = 0.30$

**Step 1: Find base $D_I$ for 0.40% C at $G = 7$.**
From Grossmann's tables, for plain carbon base:
$$D_{I,\text{base}} = 0.18\ \text{in} \approx 4.6\ \text{mm}$$

**Step 2: Apply multiplying factors for each alloying element.**

| Element | % Present | Factor $f_i$ (for $G = 7$) | Multiplying Effect |
|---------|-----------|------------------------------|---------------------|
| Mn | 0.80 | $f_{Mn} = 1.50$ | √ó 1.50 |
| Cr | 1.00 | $f_{Cr} = 2.08$ | √ó 2.08 |
| Mo | 0.20 | $f_{Mo} = 1.30$ | √ó 1.30 |

**Step 3: Compute total $D_I$.**
$$D_I = D_{I,\text{base}} \times f_{Mn} \times f_{Cr} \times f_{Mo}$$

$$D_I = 4.6 \times 1.50 \times 2.08 \times 1.30$$

$$D_I = 4.6 \times 4.056 = 18.66\ \text{mm}$$

**Step 4: Apply severity of quench factor.**
For $H = 0.30$ (still oil at 60 ¬∞C), Grossmann's table gives:
$$f(H = 0.30) = 0.385$$

**Step 5: Compute $D_C$.**
$$D_C = D_I \cdot f(H) = 18.66 \times 0.385$$

$$\boxed{D_C \approx 7.18\ \text{mm} \approx 0.283\ \text{in}}$$

**Interpretation:** A bar of 4140 with diameter up to ~7.2 mm will through-harden (50% martensite at center) when quenched in still oil at 60 ¬∞C. Larger sections will have a soft core.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix ‚Äî Comparison of Steels via Jominy Test

| Steel | %C | %Mn | %Cr | %Ni | %Mo | Distance to 50 HRC (Jominy) | $D_I$ (mm) |
|-------|-----|------|------|------|------|------------------------------|-------------|
| AISI 1040 | 0.40 | 0.75 | ‚Äî | ‚Äî | ‚Äî | ~3 mm | 6 ‚Äì 8 |
| AISI 4140 | 0.40 | 0.80 | 1.00 | ‚Äî | 0.20 | ~15 mm | 25 ‚Äì 40 |
| AISI 4340 | 0.40 | 0.70 | 0.80 | 1.85 | 0.25 | ~25 mm | 50 ‚Äì 75 |
| AISI 8620 | 0.20 | 0.80 | 0.50 | 0.55 | 0.20 | ~12 mm | 30 ‚Äì 45 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** Adding Cr, Ni, Mo shifts the Jominy curve "outward" ‚Äî the bar remains hard at greater distances from the quenched end. This visually represents increased hardenability.

### 3.4 Conversion: Jominy Distance to Cooling Rate

The Jominy distance $d$ (in mm) corresponds approximately to a specific cooling rate at 700 ¬∞C ($CR_7$, in ¬∞C/s). An empirical relation (R. A. Grange) is:

$$CR_7 \approx \frac{650}{d} \quad \text{(approximate, for } d \text{ in mm, } d > 2\text{)}$$

**Example:** At $d = 10$ mm,
$$CR_7 \approx \frac{650}{10} = 65\ ^\circ\text{C/s}$$

This cooling rate is then located on the **CCT diagram** of the steel to predict the resulting microstructure (martensite, bainite, pearlite) at that position.

### 3.5 Worked Example ‚Äî Reading Microstructure from Jominy Position

**Problem:** A 4140 steel specimen (Jominy bar) shows $HRC = 38$ at $d = 25$ mm. Given that full martensite hardness is 56 HRC and 100% pearlite hardness is ~22 HRC, estimate the microstructure.

**Step 1: Apply the linear rule of mixtures (approximation for hardness in mixed structures):**

$$HRC_{\text{measured}} = f_M \cdot HRC_M + (1 - f_M) \cdot HRC_P$$

$$38 = f_M \cdot 56 + (1 - f_M) \cdot 22$$

**Step 2: Solve for martensite fraction $f_M$:**

$$38 = 22 + f_M (56 - 22)$$

$$f_M = \frac{38 - 22}{56 - 22} = \frac{16}{34}$$

$$\boxed{f_M \approx 0.47 \ \text{or}\ 47\%\ \text{martensite}}$$

**Step 3: Interpret via CCT diagram.**
At $d = 25$ mm, the cooling rate is:
$$CR_7 \approx \frac{650}{25} = 26\ ^\circ\text{C/s}$$

On the 4140 CCT diagram, this rate falls in the **bainite + martensite region** ‚Äî confirming the mixed microstructure with bainite forming first in the slower-cooling center.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Architecture ‚Äî Jominy End Quench Test Procedure

The following diagram maps the **stepwise functional flow** of the Jominy end quench test, from specimen preparation to interpretation of the hardenability curve.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1: Specimen Preparation] --> B[Step 2: Austenitization at 870 to 927 C for 30 to 40 min]

    D --> E[Step 5: Flat Grind Two Opposite Sides 0.4 mm depth]
    E --> F[Step 6: Hardness Measurements at 1 2 3 4 5 6 7 8 10 12 16 20 24 28 32 mm]
    F --> G[Step 7: Plot Jominy Curve HRC versus Distance]
    G --> H[Step 8: Compare with Specification Band or CCC Diagram]
    H --> I[Step 9: Determine Hardness at Critical Locations]
    I --> J[Output: Hardenability Curve and D I Value]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A ‚Äî Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam ‚Äî May 2024]**
**Differentiate between hardness and hardenability. Why is it incorrect to use these terms interchangeably?**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Hardness | Hardenability |
|--------|----------|----------------|
| Definition | Resistance of a material to localized plastic deformation (indentation) | Ability of a steel to form martensite to a certain depth on quenching |
| Measured by | HRC, HBN, HV | Jominy curve, $D_C$, $D_I$ |
| Depends on | Carbon content primarily | Alloying elements, grain size, quench severity |
| Property type | Mechanical | Metallurgical/Heat-treatment related |

They are **not interchangeable** because a steel can be very hard (high carbon, e.g., AISI 1095) but only harden to a shallow depth, while a low-carbon alloy steel (e.g., 4340) can be hardened through large sections despite lower surface hardness capability. **Hardness** is what you measure at a point; **hardenability** describes the spatial depth profile of hardness.

**Marks Distribution (KTU pattern):**
- Correct distinction: 2 marks
- Example / justification: 1 mark

**Q2. [KTU University Exam ‚Äî Dec 2023]**
**What is meant by "severity of quench"? Mention H-values for two common quench media.**

**Model Answer:**

**Severity of Quench (H)** is a dimensionless parameter (introduced by Grossmann) that quantifies the **intensity of heat extraction** at the surface of a steel bar during quenching. It is related to the surface heat transfer coefficient $h$ between the metal and the quench medium.

Higher H ‚Üí faster surface cooling ‚Üí deeper hardening.

| Quench Medium | H-Value |
|---------------|---------|
| Still Oil (60 ¬∞C) | 0.25 ‚Äì 0.30 |
| Still Water (18 ¬∞C) | 1.0 |
| Brine (10% NaCl, 18 ¬∞C) | 2.0 |
| Ideal Quench | $\infty$ |

**Marks Distribution:**
- Definition: 2 marks
- Two H-values: 1 mark
