### 5.2 Part B ‚Äî Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) ‚Äî [KTU University Exam ‚Äî Dec 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the **Jominy End Quench Test** in detail with a neat sketch. State the specimen dimensions, austenitizing conditions, and hardness measurement procedure. Mention the significance of the Jominy curve. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **factors affecting hardenability** of steels. How do alloying elements influence hardenability? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Jominy End Quench Test:**

**Specimen (per ASTM A255):**
- Cylindrical bar: **$\phi 25\ \text{mm} \times 100\ \text{mm}$** length.
- A small flange or head may be provided at the top to hold it in a fixture.

**Procedure:**

1. **Austenitization:** The specimen is heated to $A_{c3} + 30\ ^\circ\text{C}$ (typically **870 ‚Äì 927 ¬∞C** for carbon steels) and held for **30 ‚Äì 40 minutes** to ensure complete transformation to homogeneous austenite.

2. **Transfer:** The bar is rapidly transferred (in < 5 s) from the furnace to a special jig.

3. **Quenching:** A **water jet at 24 ¬∞C ¬± 5 ¬∞C** is sprayed onto the **bottom end face** of the bar. The water jet continues for at least **10 minutes** to ensure complete cooling.

4. **Flat Grinding:** After cooling, two parallel flats are ground on **opposite sides** of the bar to a depth of **0.4 mm**, providing a flat surface for hardness measurement.

5. **Hardness Measurement:** **Rockwell C (HRC)** hardness is measured at specific distances from the quenched end, typically:
   $d = 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 10, 12, 16, 20, 24, 28, 32\ \text{mm}$.

6. **Plotting:** HRC vs. $d$ is plotted to obtain the **Jominy curve (hardenability curve)**.

**Significance of Jominy Curve:**
- Provides a **quantitative measure of hardenability** for a given steel.
- Allows comparison of different steels.
- Enables prediction of hardness at any point in a quenched bar of any diameter (using the relationship between Jominy distance and cooling rate).
- Helps in **selection of steel** and **quench medium** for a given application.

**Sketch (Block Schematic):**

```
                |  Specimen  |
                |  œÜ 25 mm   |
                | 100 mm     |
                |____________|   ‚Üê austenitized
                    ‚Üë holding fixture
                ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~  ‚Üê furnace
                |            |
                |____________|
                    ||
                    || ‚Üê transfer < 5 s
                    ||
            ‚îå‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚î¥‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îê
            ‚îÇ Water Jet     ‚îÇ ‚Üê 24 ¬∞C, 10 min
            ‚îÇ (Quenching)   ‚îÇ
            ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îò
   (Measure HRC along flats ground on sides)
```

**(b) Factors Affecting Hardenability:**

1. **Carbon Content:** Determines maximum attainable martensite hardness, but does not significantly increase hardenability above 0.6% C. Below 0.6%, increasing C slightly increases hardenability.

2. **Alloying Elements:** The most significant factor. Elements that **retard pearlitic/bainitic transformation** by slowing carbon diffusion:
   - **Mn, Cr, Mo, V, Si, Ni** ‚Äî all increase hardenability.
   - **Mo** is especially powerful ‚Äî it forms stable carbides and strongly delays pearlite nucleation.
   - **Ni** improves hardenability without reducing toughness.
   - These elements shift the **TTT/CCT curves to the right**, giving martensite more time to form.

3. **Austenite Grain Size:**
   - **Coarse grains** ‚Üí fewer grain boundaries ‚Üí fewer nucleation sites for pearlite ‚Üí **higher hardenability**.
   - **Fine grains** ‚Üí more boundaries ‚Üí more nucleation ‚Üí **lower hardenability**.
   - Measured by ASTM grain size number $G$ (lower $G$ = coarser).

4. **Quenching Medium:**
   - Higher severity of quench ($H$) ‚Üí faster surface cooling ‚Üí harder and deeper martensite.
   - Sequence: **Brine > Water > Oil > Air**.

5. **Homogeneity of Austenite:**
   - Inhomogeneous austenite (un-dissolved carbides, segregated regions) reduces hardenability because undissolved carbides act as pearlite nucleation sites.

**Mechanism of Alloying Element Action (detailed):**
Alloying elements act through three primary mechanisms:
- **Diffusion retardation:** Mn, Cr, Mo slow down carbon diffusion in austenite, retarding the time for pearlite nucleation/growth.
- **Carbide stabilization:** Cr, Mo, V form stable alloy carbides that resist dissolution during austenitization, reducing the carbon available in solution.
- **TTT/CCT shift:** All these effects manifest as a **rightward shift** of the C-curve, moving the nose of the curve to longer times.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) ‚Äî [KTU University Exam ‚Äî May 2023]**

**(a)** Define **critical diameter ($D_C$)** and **ideal critical diameter ($D_I$)**. Derive the Grossmann relationship $D_C = D_I \cdot f(H)$ and explain its significance. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A plain carbon steel containing 0.40% C, 0.80% Mn, 0.25% Si, 0.20% Cr, 0.10% Mo is austenitized at 870 ¬∞C, producing a **grain size of ASTM 7**. Calculate the **ideal critical diameter ($D_I$)**. Use Grossmann's multiplying factors given below. **(7 marks)**

| Element | $f_i$ at $G=7$ |
|---------|------------------|
| Mn (0.80%) | 1.50 |
| Si (0.25%) | 1.04 |
| Cr (0.20%) | 1.20 |
| Mo (0.10%) | 1.10 |

For 0.40% C plain carbon steel at $G = 7$: **$D_{I,\text{base}} = 4.6\ \text{mm}$**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Definition and Derivation:**

**Definitions:**
- **$D_C$ (Critical Diameter):** The maximum diameter of a round bar in which the **center achieves 50% martensite** when quenched in a **specific medium** of severity $H$.
- **$D_I$ (Ideal Critical Diameter):** The maximum diameter of a round bar in which the center achieves 50% martensite under an **ideal quench** ($H = \infty$, surface instantly reaches bath temperature). $D_I$ is a property of the **steel alone** (composition + grain size).

**Derivation (Step-by-step as in Section 3.1):**

1. Under an ideal quench, surface cooling is instantaneous; centerline cooling rate is determined by **internal conduction only**.
2. The centerline cooling rate for a bar of diameter $D$ is proportional to $1/D$ under ideal conditions.
3. The minimum cooling rate needed at the center to form 50% martensite is set by the **CCT diagram** of the steel ‚Äî this is $\left(\frac{dT}{dt}\right)_{\text{crit}}$.
4. Under an ideal quench, the largest $D$ that satisfies this criterion is **$D_I$**.
5. Under a real quench of severity $H$, the surface cooling is **slower** (finite $h$), so the centerline cooling rate is reduced. The bar diameter that satisfies the criterion becomes **$D_C < D_I$**.
6. Grossmann found empirically that the ratio $\frac{D_C}{D_I}$ depends only on $H$ (not on steel composition), leading to:

$$\boxed{D_C = D_I \cdot f(H)}$$

**Significance:**
- Allows **quantitative prediction** of hardenability for any quench medium.
- Enables **selection of appropriate steel and quench medium** for a given component size.
- Forms the basis of all modern hardenability calculations.

**(b) Calculation of $D_I$:**

**Step 1:** Start with the base value:
$$D_{I,\text{base}} = 4.6\ \text{mm}$$

**Step 2:** Multiply by each alloy factor:

$$D_I = D_{I,\text{base}} \times f_{Mn} \times f_{Si} \times f_{Cr} \times f_{Mo}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute values:

$$D_I = 4.6 \times 1.50 \times 1.04 \times 1.20 \times 1.10$$

**Step 4:** Compute step-by-step:

$$4.6 \times 1.50 = 6.90$$

$$6.90 \times 1.04 = 7.176$$

$$7.176 \times 1.20 = 8.6112$$

$$8.6112 \times 1.10 = 9.47232$$

**Step 5:** Final answer:

$$\boxed{D_I \approx 9.47\ \text{mm}}$$

**Step 6 (Optional Interpretation):** If this steel is quenched in still oil ($H = 0.30$, $f(H) = 0.385$):
$$D_C = 9.47 \times 0.385 \approx 3.65\ \text{mm}$$

Thus, bars up to ~3.65 mm diameter will through-harden in still oil. To through-harden larger sections, a more severe quench (water or brine) or a more hardenable alloy steel must be used.

**Marks Distribution (KTU pattern for 7-mark sub-part):**
- Correct formula and substitution: 3 marks
- Step-by-step computation: 2 marks
- Final answer with units: 1 mark
- Interpretation/Engineering meaning: 1 mark

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams**
>
> 1. **Confusing "Hardness" with "Hardenability"** ‚Äî Examiners explicitly test this distinction. A 3-mark question may ask for the difference; mixing them up costs full marks.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the Grain Size Variable** ‚Äî In $D_I$ calculations, students often multiply only by alloy factors and forget that the **base $D_I$ value itself depends on ASTM grain size $G$**. Always state $G$ explicitly.
>
> 3. **Wrong Units in $D_C$ / $D_I$** ‚Äî Mixing mm and inches leads to 10√ó errors. Be consistent.
>
> 4. **Missing the "Why" in the Derivation** ‚Äî The KTU board requires the **physical meaning** of each step, not just formulas. Always state that alloying elements **shift the CCT curve to the right** (delay pearlitic transformation).
>
> 5. **Skipping the Jominy Specimen Dimensions** ‚Äî Always quote $\phi 25\ \text{mm} \times 100\ \text{mm}$ and water temperature $24\ ^\circ\text{C} \pm 5\ ^\circ\text{C}$.
>
> 6. **Not Mentioning the 50% Martensite Convention** ‚Äî $D_C$ is defined as the diameter where the **center has 50% martensite**, not 100%. Missing this is a common error.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Hardenability ‚âÝ Hardness.** Hardness is a point measurement; hardenability is a depth/profile measurement.
- **Jominy Test Standard:** ASTM A255, $\phi 25\ \text{mm} \times 100\ \text{mm}$ specimen, water-quenched at one end ($24\ ^\circ\text{C}$, 10 min), hardness measured along a ground flat at specific distances.
- **Jominy Distance to Cooling Rate:** $CR_7 \approx 650/d$ (¬∞C/s, d in mm) ‚Äî maps the Jominy curve to a CCT diagram.
- **Grossmann's Master Equation:** $D_C = D_I \cdot f(H)$ ‚Äî the foundation of quantitative hardenability.
- **$D_I$ is a steel property; $f(H)$ is a quench-medium property.** $D_C$ is the combined outcome.
- **Alloying Elements (Mn, Cr, Mo, Ni, Si, V)** increase hardenability by **delaying pearlitic transformation** (shifting CCT/TTT curves to the right).
- **Coarse Austenite Grain ‚Üí Higher Hardenability** (but lower toughness and higher quench-crack risk).
- **Higher Severity of Quench $H$ ‚Üí Deeper Hardening.** Sequence: Brine (2.0) > Water (1.0) > Oil (0.25‚Äì0.30) > Air (<0.1).
- **Standard H-values to memorize:** Oil ‚âà 0.3, Water ‚âà 1.0, Brine ‚âà 2.0, Ideal = $\infty$.
- **4340 (Ni‚ÄìCr‚ÄìMo steel) has $D_I \approx 50$‚Äì$75$ mm** ‚Äî extremely high hardenability, used in aerospace and heavy shafts.
- **Always quote specimen size, austenitizing temperature, and 50% martensite criterion** in Jominy-related answers.
- **Engineering link:** Jominy data feeds directly into **selection of alloy steel for gears, axles, and rotors**; the Jominy curve dictates whether through-hardening or case-hardening is appropriate.
- **A Jominy curve dropping slowly** = high hardenability; **dropping steeply** = low hardenability.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Surface hardening methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAUT304 - MATERIAL SCIENCE AND METALLURGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Surface hardening methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Surface Hardening (Case Hardening):** A group of heat treatment processes used to increase the hardness and wear resistance of the **outer surface (case)** of a steel component while keeping the **interior (core)** tough and ductile. This is achieved by altering the surface chemistry (diffusion methods) or by selectively heating and quenching the surface (selective heating methods).

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, surface hardening is a **thermochemical or selective heat treatment** that produces a **hardness gradient** across the cross-section ‚Äî a **hard case (typically 0.5 mm to 6 mm deep)** and a **softer, tougher core**.

## 1.2 Why Surface Hardening? ‚Äî The Engineering Need

Many machine components experience the following:
- **Gears, camshafts, bearings, piston pins, axles** ‚Üí surface is in contact, wears out, fatigues
- **Core** must remain tough to absorb shock loads and prevent brittle fracture
- A uniform hard part would be too brittle and crack under impact

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (CO Mapping):** Surface hardening is mapped to **CO3** ‚Äî *Apply heat treatment techniques to tailor microstructure and mechanical properties of metals and alloys*. Expect 14-mark questions combining process description, microstructure evolution, and property comparisons.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy ‚Äî "The Armored Knight"

Imagine a medieval **knight**:
- His **outer armor (surface)** is extremely hard and tough against sword strikes ‚Äî *this is the case*.
- His **body underneath (core)** is still flexible, allowing him to move, dodge, and absorb falls without shattering ‚Äî *this is the tough core*.
- Surface hardening is exactly this: putting a "metallurgical armor" on the outside while keeping the inside soft and impact-resistant.

## 1.4 Classification Overview (Master Map)

Surface hardening methods are broadly classified into **three families**:

| Family | Mechanism | Key Processes |
|---|---|---|
| **Diffusion / Thermochemical Methods** | Change surface **chemistry** by adding C, N, or both | Carburizing, Nitriding, Cyaniding, Carbonitriding, Boriding, Chromizing |
| **Selective Surface Heating Methods** | Rapid heat surface only, then quench | Flame Hardening, Induction Hardening, Laser Hardening, Electron Beam Hardening |
| **Mechanical / Cold Working Methods** | Plastic deformation induces surface compressive stresses | Shot Peening, Cold Rolling, Burnishing |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Distinction:** Diffusion methods are **slow (hours)** and **chemical in nature** (atomic diffusion at 800‚Äì950 ¬∞C). Selective heating methods are **fast (seconds)** and **purely thermal** (no chemistry change).

## 1.5 Core Terminology (Define-Before-Use)

- **Case:** The hardened outer layer of the component.
- **Case Depth:** Perpendicular distance from the surface to a defined hardness limit (usually **Rockwell C 50 / HV 550**).
- **Core:** The interior region that retains the original (untouched) microstructure and properties.
- **Case-to-Core Ratio:** Ratio of case depth to total thickness ‚Äî a critical design parameter.
- **Diffusion Zone:** Region where alloying atoms (C, N) have penetrated and altered the chemistry.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Process-by-Process Deep Analysis

### A. CARBURIZING

Carburizing is the **diffusion of carbon** into the surface of low-carbon steel (**0.15‚Äì0.25% C**) at high temperature in a carbon-rich environment.

**Three industrial variants:**

#### (i) Pack Carburizing
- **Setup:** Components sealed in a steel box packed with **charcoal + energizer (BaCO‚ÇÉ or Na‚ÇÇCO‚ÇÉ, ~10%)**.
- **Temperature:** **900‚Äì950 ¬∞C**
- **Time:** 6‚Äì8 hours
- **Chemistry:** $\text{BaCO}_3 \rightarrow \text{BaO} + \text{CO}_2$ ; $\text{CO}_2 + \text{C} \rightarrow 2\text{CO}$ ; $2\text{CO} \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + \text{C}$ (atomic C diffuses into steel)
- **Advantages:** Cheapest, simplest
- **Disadvantages:** Slow, uneven heating, limited case depth (~1‚Äì2 mm)

#### (ii) Liquid Carburizing
- **Setup:** Components immersed in a molten salt bath (e.g., **NaCN + NaCl + BaCl‚ÇÇ** or non-toxic **Na‚ÇÇCO‚ÇÉ + NaCl + SiC** at 850‚Äì950 ¬∞C).
- **Mechanism:** Cyanide decomposes ‚Üí releases atomic carbon and nitrogen.
- **Advantages:** Faster, uniform heating, simultaneous C+N pickup.
- **Disadvantages:** Cyanide salts are **highly toxic**; require special handling.

#### (iii) Gas Carburizing
- **Setup:** Components in a sealed furnace with **carrier gas (methanol, propane, or natural gas) + enriching gas**.
- **Temperature:** **900‚Äì930 ¬∞C**
- **Time:** 1‚Äì6 hours
- **Advantages:** Excellent control of case depth via **carbon potential (Cp)** sensors, suitable for continuous production.
- **Disadvantages:** Requires sophisticated furnace and gas-handling system.

> [!NOTE]
> **Post-Carburizing Treatment:** After carburizing, the component has high C at surface but coarse grains. It MUST be **hardened by reheating to ~760‚Äì780 ¬∞C (austenite) and quenching** to form martensite, followed by **tempering at 150‚Äì200 ¬∞C**.

**Case Depth Formula (Diffusion-Controlled):**

$$x = \sqrt{D \cdot t}$$

Where:
- $x$ = case depth (m or mm)
- $D$ = diffusion coefficient of carbon in austenite (m¬≤/s)
- $t$ = time (seconds)

The temperature dependence of $D$ follows an **Arrhenius equation:**

$$D = D_0 \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{Q}{RT}\right)$$

Where:
- $D_0$ = pre-exponential factor (depends on material, ~ $2.3 \times 10^{-5}$ m¬≤/s for C in Œ≥-Fe)
- $Q$ = activation energy (~ 137 kJ/mol for C in Œ≥-Fe)
- $R$ = universal gas constant = **8.314 J/(mol¬∑K)**
- $T$ = absolute temperature (K)

### B. NITRIDING

Nitriding is the **diffusion of nitrogen** into the surface of **medium-carbon steel containing strong nitride formers (Al, Cr, Mo, V, Ti)** at relatively low temperatures.

- **Temperature:** **500‚Äì550 ¬∞C** (well below A‚ÇÅ, no phase transformation in core)
- **Time:** 20‚Äì100 hours
- **Medium:** Ammonia gas ($\text{NH}_3$) that decomposes: $2\text{NH}_3 \rightarrow 2\text{N} + 3\text{H}_2$
- **No quenching required** ‚Äî the surface is already very hard due to formation of **alloy nitrides (AlN, CrN, VN, etc.)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why use alloy steel (e.g., 38CrAlMo7-10, Nitralloy)?** Plain carbon steel forms only Fe‚ÇÇN / Fe‚ÇÑN which dissociate at high temperatures and provide low hardness. Alloying elements form **stable, hard, finely dispersed nitrides** that give case hardness of **~ 1000‚Äì1200 HV** ‚Äî much higher than carburized surfaces (~ 800 HV).

**Stages of Nitride Layer Formation:**
1. **Adsorption:** N atoms adsorb on steel surface.
2. **Diffusion:** N atoms migrate inward along grain boundaries and through the lattice.
3. **Compound Zone (white layer):** Surface region of Œµ-Fe‚ÇÇ‚ÇÉN‚ÇÉ and Œ≥‚Ä≤-Fe‚ÇÑN ‚Äî very hard but brittle.
4. **Diffusion zone:** Solid solution of N in Œ±-Fe + finely dispersed alloy nitrides (gives strength).

### C. CYANIDING

A **hybrid process** ‚Äî simultaneous carburizing + nitriding using molten cyanide salts.

- **Bath composition:** NaCN (20‚Äì30%) + NaCl + Na‚ÇÇCO‚ÇÉ
- **Temperature:** **800‚Äì870 ¬∞C**
- **Time:** 30 min to 3 hours (very short)
- **Result:** Thin case (**0.25‚Äì0.75 mm**) with very high surface hardness (~ 60‚Äì65 HRC).
- **Limitation:** **Toxicity** of cyanide ‚Äî replaced largely by **carbonitriding** in modern industry.

### D. CARBONITRIDING

Modern, **non-toxic** replacement for cyaniding.

- **Medium:** Mixture of **ammonia (NH‚ÇÉ) + carrier gas (endothermic gas, methanol vapor)** at 700‚Äì900 ¬∞C.
- **Mechanism:** Both C and N diffuse simultaneously ‚Äî N accelerates C diffusion.
- **Result:** Slightly harder case than pure carburizing, with better wear resistance and less distortion.

### E. INDUCTION HARDENING

A **selective surface heating method** using **electromagnetic induction**.

**Principle:** A high-frequency alternating current (typically **10 kHz to 500 kHz**) flows through a water-cooled copper coil. The steel part placed in the coil has eddy currents induced in its surface (skin effect). These heat the surface rapidly to **austenitizing temperature (850‚Äì900 ¬∞C)** within seconds. Immediate water spray quench produces martensite.

**Skin Depth Formula:**

$$\delta = \sqrt{\frac{\rho}{\pi \cdot \mu \cdot f}}$$

Where:
- $\delta$ = depth of current penetration = effective case depth (m)
- $\rho$ = electrical resistivity of steel (~ $1.5 \times 10^{-7}$ Œ©¬∑m)
- $\mu$ = magnetic permeability (H/m)
- $f$ = frequency (Hz)

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Heuristic:** Higher frequency = shallower, harder case. For deep cases (5‚Äì10 mm), use lower frequency (~ 10 kHz); for shallow cases (1‚Äì2 mm), use high frequency (100 kHz+).

**Advantages:**
- Fast (seconds per part)
- Controllable case depth via frequency selection
- Minimal distortion (only surface heated)
- Energy efficient, easily automated

**Disadvantages:**
- Requires expensive induction generator
- Coil design for complex geometry is challenging
- Limited to medium/high carbon steels (> 0.3% C)

### F. FLAME HARDENING

Selective surface heating using an **oxy-acetylene flame** (~ 3000 ¬∞C).

- **Process:** Surface heated to austenitizing range; immediately quenched with water spray.
- **Methods:** Stationary, progressive, spinning, and combination.
- **Depth:** 1‚Äì6 mm
- **Disadvantage:** Operator skill dependent; less uniform than induction; risk of overheating.

### G. LASER HARDENING & ELECTRON BEAM HARDENING

**Laser Hardening:**
- High-power CO‚ÇÇ or Nd:YAG laser scans the surface.
- Surface heated to austenitizing temperature, then self-quenched by conduction into cold bulk.
- Case depth: 0.25‚Äì1.5 mm
- Hardness: 60‚Äì65 HRC
- Excellent for localized hardening (e.g., cylinder bores, gear flanks).

**Electron Beam Hardening:**
- Similar concept but uses focused electron beam in vacuum.
- Very high energy density, but expensive and limited chamber size.

### H. SHOT PEENING (Mechanical Surface Hardening)

- **Mechanism:** Hard spherical shots (steel, glass, ceramic) are blasted at high velocity onto the surface.
- **Effect:** Surface layer undergoes **plastic deformation**, inducing **compressive residual stresses** that:
  - Increase surface hardness
  - Improve fatigue life
  - Suppress crack initiation
- **Used for:** Springs, gear teeth, aircraft components, welded structures.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $x = \sqrt{D \cdot t}$ | Case depth (diffusion) | m |
| 2 | $D = D_0 \cdot \exp(-Q/RT)$ | Arrhenius diffusion | m¬≤/s |
| 3 | $\delta = \sqrt{\rho / (\pi \mu f)}$ | Induction skin depth | m |
| 4 | $T(\text{K}) = T(\degree\text{C}) + 273.15$ | Temperature conversion | K |
| 5 | $H_{\text{case}} \approx 0.5 \cdot \text{Vol\% Carbide} + 700$ | Empirical case hardness | HV |
| 6 | $R = 1/(2\delta)$ | Resistance heating skin factor | m‚Åª¬π |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive:** Carburized gears, nitrided crankshafts, induction-hardened cam lobes.
- **Aerospace:** Shot-peened turbine blades, laser-hardened landing gear.
- **Tool industry:** Through-hardened drills; case-hardened forming dies.
- **Heavy machinery:** Flame-hardened bulldozer sprockets and rail crossings.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Rule-of-Thumb:** Choose **carburizing** for deep cases (gears), **nitriding** for precision thin cases (shafts, sleeves), **induction/flame** for mass production (axles, pins), and **shot peening** for fatigue-critical parts.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation: Case Depth vs. Time (Fick's Second Law)

The diffusion of carbon into steel during carburizing is governed by **Fick's Second Law** in semi-infinite solid form.

### Step 1: Governing PDE

For one-dimensional diffusion into a semi-infinite solid with constant surface concentration:

$$\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial x^2}$$

### Step 2: Boundary Conditions

$$
\begin{aligned}
C(x, 0) &= C_0 \quad \text{(initial carbon content of steel)} \\
C(0, t) &= C_s \quad \text{(surface carbon, maintained constant by atmosphere)} \\
C(\infty, t) &= C_0 \quad \text{(far from surface, no change)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Solution (Error Function Form)

The solution is:

$$
C(x, t) = C_s - (C_s - C_0) \cdot \text{erf}\left(\frac{x}{2\sqrt{Dt}}\right)
$$

### Step 4: Define Case Depth

The case depth $x_c$ is the distance where $C(x_c, t) = C_{\text{eutectoid}} = 0.77\%\text{C}$ (for steel) or some specified hardness equivalent.

$$
\text{erf}\left(\frac{x_c}{2\sqrt{Dt}}\right) = \frac{C_s - 0.77}{C_s - C_0}
$$

### Step 5: Effective Case Depth Approximation

For most practical cases where $C_s - 0.77 \approx \frac{C_s - C_0}{2}$, then erf ‚âà 0.5, so:

$$\frac{x_c}{2\sqrt{Dt}} \approx 0.48$$

Solving:

$$x_c \approx 0.96 \cdot \sqrt{Dt} \approx \sqrt{Dt}$$

**This confirms the KTU textbook approximation:**

$$\boxed{\,x_c \approx \sqrt{D \cdot t}\,}$$

### Step 6: Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Given:** A low-carbon steel (0.20% C) is gas-carburized at 930 ¬∞C (1203 K). The diffusion coefficient of C in Œ≥-Fe at 1203 K is $D = 5.5 \times 10^{-11}$ m¬≤/s. The atmosphere maintains surface carbon at 1.0%.

**Find:** (a) Case depth after 4 hours. (b) Time to achieve 1.5 mm case depth.

#### (a) Case depth after 4 hours

$$
\begin{aligned}
t &= 4 \text{ hours} = 4 \times 3600 = 14400 \text{ s} \\
x_c &= \sqrt{D \cdot t} \\
&= \sqrt{(5.5 \times 10^{-11}) \times 14400} \\
&= \sqrt{7.92 \times 10^{-7}} \\
&= 8.90 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} \\
&\approx \mathbf{0.89 \text{ mm}}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### (b) Time to achieve 1.5 mm case depth

$$
\begin{aligned}
t &= \frac{x_c^2}{D} \\
&= \frac{(1.5 \times 10^{-3})^2}{5.5 \times 10^{-11}} \\
&= \frac{2.25 \times 10^{-6}}{5.5 \times 10^{-11}} \\
&= 4.09 \times 10^4 \text{ s} \\
&= \mathbf{11.37 \text{ hours}}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.2 Detailed Derivation: Induction Skin Depth

### Step 1: Maxwell's Equations in a Conductor

The skin effect arises because AC current concentrates near the surface due to self-induced eddy currents. The current density $J$ decays exponentially:

$$J(x) = J_0 \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{x}{\delta}\right)$$

### Step 2: Derivation from EM Wave Penetration

The skin depth is defined as the depth at which $J$ drops to $1/e$ (‚âà 37%) of surface value. From electromagnetic wave theory:

$$\delta = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}}$$

where $\sigma = 1/\rho$ is electrical conductivity.

### Step 3: Rewrite Using Resistivity

$$\boxed{\,\delta = \sqrt{\frac{\rho}{\pi f \mu}}\,}$$

### Step 4: Numerical Estimate

For medium-carbon steel at room temperature:
- $\rho \approx 1.5 \times 10^{-7}$ Œ©¬∑m
- $\mu \approx 500 \mu_0 = 500 \times 4\pi \times 10^{-7} = 6.28 \times 10^{-4}$ H/m
- $f = 100$ kHz $= 10^5$ Hz

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta &= \sqrt{\frac{1.5 \times 10^{-7}}{\pi \times 10^5 \times 6.28 \times 10^{-4}}} \\
&= \sqrt{\frac{1.5 \times 10^{-7}}{0.197}} \\
&= \sqrt{7.61 \times 10^{-7}} \\
&= 8.72 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} \\
&\approx \mathbf{0.87 \text{ mm}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This shows that with 100 kHz induction heating, the effective hardening depth is **less than 1 mm** ‚Äî perfectly matching the case depths observed in industrial induction hardening.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU Exam Gold)

| Process | Temperature (¬∞C) | Time | Case Depth (mm) | Surface Hardness | Quench? | Core Toughness |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Pack Carburizing** | 900‚Äì950 | 6‚Äì8 h | 1‚Äì2 | 55‚Äì62 HRC | Yes | Good |
| **Gas Carburizing** | 900‚Äì930 | 1‚Äì6 h | 0.5‚Äì4 | 55‚Äì62 HRC | Yes | Good |
| **Liquid Carburizing** | 850‚Äì950 | 1‚Äì2 h | 0.5‚Äì2 | 60‚Äì65 HRC | Yes | Good |
| **Nitriding** | 500‚Äì550 | 20‚Äì100 h | 0.3‚Äì0.7 | 60‚Äì70 HRC / 1000+ HV | **No** | Excellent |
| **Cyaniding** | 800‚Äì870 | 0.5‚Äì3 h | 0.25‚Äì0.75 | 60‚Äì65 HRC | Yes | Moderate |
| **Carbonitriding** | 700‚Äì900 | 0.5‚Äì4 h | 0.5‚Äì2 | 58‚Äì64 HRC | Yes | Good |
| **Induction Hardening** | 850‚Äì900 (surface) | Seconds | 0.5‚Äì10 | 55‚Äì65 HRC | Yes (self) | Good |
| **Flame Hardening** | 850‚Äì900 (surface) | Seconds | 1‚Äì6 | 50‚Äì60 HRC | Yes (water spray) | Good |
| **Laser Hardening** | 1000‚Äì1500 (spot) | Milliseconds | 0.25‚Äì1.5 | 60‚Äì65 HRC | Self-quench | Good |
| **Shot Peening** | Room | Seconds | 0.1‚Äì0.5 | +20‚Äì30% | None | Improved fatigue |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Surface Hardening Method Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Surface Hardening Methods] --> B[Thermochemical Diffusion]
    A --> C[Selective Surface Heating]
    A --> D[Mechanical Cold Working]

    B --> B1[Carburizing]
    B --> B2[Nitriding]
    B --> B3[Cyaniding]
    B --> B4[Carbonitriding]
    B --> B5[Boriding]
    B --> B6[Chromizing]

    B1 --> B1a[Pack Carburizing]
    B1 --> B1b[Liquid Carburizing]
    B1 --> B1c[Gas Carburizing]

    B2 --> B2a[Gas Nitriding]
    B2 --> B2b[Plasma Nitriding]
    B2 --> B2c[Salt Bath Nitriding]

    C --> C1[Flame Hardening]
    C --> C2[Induction Hardening]
    C --> C3[Laser Hardening]
    C --> C4[Electron Beam Hardening]

    D --> D1[Shot Peening]
    D --> D2[Cold Rolling]
    D --> D3[Burnishing]
```

## 4.2 Carburizing Process Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FURNACE [Sealed Carburizing Furnace 900-950C]
        A1[Low Carbon Steel 0.20 percent C] --> A2[Heat to Austenite Phase]
        A2 --> A3[Expose to Carbon Rich Atmosphere]
        A3 --> A4[Atomic C Diffuses into Surface]
    end
    A4 --> A5[Carburized Component High C Surface Low C Core]
    A5 --> A6[Reheat to 760-780C Austenite]
    A6 --> A7[Quench in Oil or Water]
    A7 --> A8[Hard Case Martensite 60 HRC]
    A8 --> A9[Temper at 150-200C]
    A9 --> A10[Final Component Hard Case plus Tough Core]
```

## 4.3 Induction Hardening ‚Äî Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph POWER [High Frequency Power Supply]
        P1[AC Mains] --> P2[Rectifier]
        P2 --> P3[Inverter to High Frequency 10kHz to 500kHz]
        P3 --> P4[Oscillator and Control]
    end
    P4 --> COIL[Water Cooled Copper Induction Coil]
    COIL -->|Electromagnetic Field| SURFACE[Steel Component Surface]
    SURFACE --> EDDY[Induced Eddy Currents]
    EDDY --> HEAT[Rapid Surface Heating 850 to 900C]
    HEAT --> QUENCH[Immediate Water Spray Quench]
    QUENCH --> MART[Martensite Formation Hard Case]
    MART --> PART[Finished Induction Hardened Part]
```

## 4.4 Case-Core Microstructure Cross-Section Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Outer Surface Hard Case Martensite plus Retained Austenite] --> B[Transition Zone Martensite plus Ferrite]
    B --> C[Core Original Microstructure Ferrite plus Pearlite]
    A -.->|0.5 to 6 mm| B
    B -.->|Gradient| C
    style A fill:#1f4e79,color:#fff
    style B fill:#2e75b6,color:#fff
    style C fill:#9dc3e6,color:#000
```

## 4.5 Shot Peening Process Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Shot Media Steel Glass Ceramic] --> S2[Blast Wheel or Nozzle]
    S2 -->|High Velocity| S3[Component Surface]
    S3 --> S4[Plastic Deformation of Surface Layer]
    S4 --> S5[Compressive Residual Stresses]
    S4 --> S6[Strain Hardening Work Hardening]
    S5 --> S7[Improved Fatigue Life]
    S6 --> S8[Increased Surface Hardness]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A ‚Äî Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Differentiate between case hardening and through hardening. [3 Marks] **[KTU University Exam ‚Äî May 2024]**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Case Hardening | Through Hardening |
|---|---|---|
| **Region hardened** | Only outer surface (case) | Entire cross-section |
| **Core microstructure** | Untouched ‚Äî soft and tough | Same as surface ‚Äî uniform hardness |
| **Composition change** | Yes (for diffusion methods) | None |
| **Applications** | Gears, cams, axles (wear + impact) | Cutting tools, dies (uniform high hardness) |
| **Brittleness** | Low (tough core absorbs impact) | Higher (brittle throughout) |

Case hardening is preferred when the surface experiences wear/abrasion while the core must remain tough to resist shock loading.

---

### Q2. Why are alloying elements like Al, Cr, Mo, and V essential for nitriding steels? [3 Marks] **[KTU University Exam ‚Äî Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

These elements are strong **nitride formers** and produce **stable, hard, finely dispersed alloy nitrides** (AlN, CrN, Mo‚ÇÇN, VN) in the diffusion zone. These nitrides:

1. Provide very high surface hardness (up to 1000‚Äì1200 HV).
2. Retain hardness at elevated temperatures (nitride stability).
3. Prevent coarsening during long nitriding cycles.
4. Plain carbon steel forms only Fe‚ÇÇN and Fe‚ÇÑN, which dissociate above 500 ¬∞C and yield only ~ 300‚Äì400 HV ‚Äî insufficient for engineering use.

Hence, **nitralloy-type steels (38CrAlMo7-10 etc.)** are used.

---

## 5.2 PART B ‚Äî Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain in detail the **gas carburizing process** with a neat sketch. Discuss the process parameters, chemistry of the atmosphere, microstructural changes, and post-carburizing treatments. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Compare **carburizing and nitriding** with respect to (i) temperature, (ii) time, (iii) case depth, (iv) hardness achieved, (v) quenching requirement, and (vi) applications. **[7 Marks]**

---

**Model Solution for Q-A (a): Gas Carburizing**

**1. Definition:** Gas carburizing is a thermochemical surface hardening process in which low-carbon steel (0.15‚Äì0.25% C) is heated in a sealed furnace in a carbon-rich gaseous atmosphere (typically 900‚Äì930 ¬∞C), allowing atomic carbon to diffuse into the surface.

**2. Furnace Setup:**
- **Sealed retort furnace** (gas-tight, alloy heating elements).
- Components loaded on trays, doors sealed, atmosphere purged.
- **Carrier gas:** Endothermic gas (endo gas) ‚Äî produced by cracking methanol or propane with limited air.
- **Enriching gas:** Propane, butane, or natural gas (CH‚ÇÑ, C‚ÇÉH‚Çà) supplies extra carbon.
- **Carbon potential (Cp) sensor** (IR or oxygen probe) controls atmosphere to maintain target surface carbon (~ 0.8‚Äì1.0%).

**3. Process Parameters:**
- Temperature: **900‚Äì930 ¬∞C** (above A‚ÇÉ, in austenitic region).
- Time: **1 to 6 hours** (depending on required case depth).
- Cp controlled to avoid soot formation (above saturation) or decarburization (below 0.8%).
- Furnace atmosphere: typically 20‚Äì40% CO, 30‚Äì40% H‚ÇÇ, balance N‚ÇÇ + CH‚ÇÑ.

**4. Chemistry of Atmosphere:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{CH}_4 \rightarrow \text{C (atomic)} + 2\text{H}_2 \\
&2\text{CO} \rightarrow \text{C (atomic)} + \text{CO}_2 \\
&\text{CO}_2 + \text{CH}_4 \rightarrow 2\text{CO} + 2\text{H}_2
\end{aligned}
$$
The atomic carbon dissolves in Œ≥-iron at the surface and diffuses inward along concentration gradient (Fick's law).

**5. Microstructural Changes:**
- Surface region: **high-carbon austenite** (saturated with C).
- Subsurface: gradually decreasing C ‚Üí hypoeutectoid.
- Core: original ferrite + pearlite (0.20% C) ‚Äî unchanged.
- Grain coarsening occurs due to long high-temperature exposure ‚Äî corrected in subsequent hardening.

**6. Post-Carburizing Treatments (critical):**
- **Slow cool** OR **direct quench from carburizing temperature** (direct quench may cause distortion and retained austenite).
- **Reheat to 760‚Äì780 ¬∞C** (refine grain + homogenize C profile).
- **Quench in oil** ‚Üí forms **martensite** in the case (high C ‚Üí very hard martensite).
- **Temper at 150‚Äì200 ¬∞C** ‚Üí relieve quench stresses, improve toughness, reduce brittleness.
- Final: Hard martensitic case (~ 60 HRC) + tough ferrite-pearlite core.

**7. Advantages over Pack/Liquid Carburizing:**
- Faster, more uniform.
- Precise control of case depth and carbon content.
- Clean, no toxic cyanide salts.
- Suitable for continuous mass production.

---

**Model Solution for Q-A (b): Comparison Table**

| Property | Carburizing | Nitriding |
|---|---|---|
| **(i) Temperature** | 900‚Äì950 ¬∞C | 500‚Äì550 ¬∞C |
| **(ii) Time** | 1‚Äì8 hours | 20‚Äì100 hours |
| **(iii) Case depth** | 0.5‚Äì6 mm (deeper) | 0.3‚Äì0.7 mm (shallower) |
| **(iv) Hardness** | 55‚Äì62 HRC (~ 800 HV) | 60‚Äì70 HRC (~ 1000‚Äì1200 HV) |
| **(v) Quenching** | Required | **Not required** |
| **(vi) Steel type** | Low-carbon (0.15‚Äì0.25% C) | Alloy steel with Al, Cr, Mo (Nitralloy) |
| **(vii) Distortion** | More (quench from high T) | Minimal (no quench, low T) |
| **(viii) Applications** | Gears, pinions, camshafts | Crankshafts, sleeves, dies, gauges |
| **(ix) Core strength** | Limited (C diffuses in only case) | Good (no phase change in core) |
| **(x) Cost** | Moderate | Higher (long cycle + alloy steel) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students often confuse "carburizing temperature" with "austenitizing temperature for hardening." Remember: **carburizing is done in austenite to enable C diffusion**, then a **second austenitize at lower T (760‚Äì780 ¬∞C)** refines grain before quenching. Writing only one heating step loses 2‚Äì3 marks.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) ‚Äî *Internal Choice Alternative*

**(a)** Describe the **induction surface hardening process**. Explain the principle of skin effect and derive the relation between case depth and frequency. Discuss its advantages and limitations. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Explain the **nitriding process** in detail. Discuss the role of alloying elements, the microstructure of the nitrided case, and the reason nitriding does not require quenching. **[7 Marks]**

---

**Model Solution for Q-B (a): Induction Hardening**

**1. Definition:** Induction hardening is a selective surface heat treatment in which the surface of a medium- or high-carbon steel component is rapidly heated to austenitizing temperature using **electromagnetic induction**, and immediately quenched to form a hard martensitic case.

**2. Principle ‚Äî Electromagnetic Induction:**
A high-frequency alternating current (10 kHz to 500 kHz) is passed through a **water-cooled copper inductor coil**. By **Faraday's law**, an alternating magnetic field is produced around the coil. When a steel component is placed inside the coil, **eddy currents** are induced in the component (Lenz's law). These eddy currents flow against the resistance of the steel, generating **I¬≤R heat** that rapidly raises the surface temperature.

**3. Skin Effect (derived in Section 3.2):**
The induced current concentrates near the surface and decays exponentially with depth:

$$J(x) = J_0 \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{x}{\delta}\right)$$

The **skin depth** is:

$$\delta = \sqrt{\frac{\rho}{\pi \mu f}}$$

**Effect of frequency:**
- **Higher $f$** ‚Üí shallower $\delta$ ‚Üí thinner case.
- **Lower $f$** ‚Üí deeper $\delta$ ‚Üí deeper case.
- Rough industry rule: $f \approx 1500/\delta^2$ where $\delta$ is in mm and $f$ in kHz.

**4. Process Steps:**
- Component placed in coil.
- High-frequency current applied for **2‚Äì10 seconds** ‚Äî surface reaches 850‚Äì900 ¬∞C.
- Power cut off, **water spray** (built into the coil) immediately quenches the surface.
- Surface ‚Üí austenite ‚Üí martensite; core remains unheated.
- Optional: low-temperature tempering to relieve stresses.

**5. Microstructure:**
- **Case:** Martensite (+ minor retained austenite).
- **Core:** Untouched (ferrite + pearlite for medium carbon steel).
- Sharp transition; case depth well controlled by frequency and power input.

**6. Advantages:**
- Very fast (seconds per part) ‚Äî ideal for mass production.
- Selective heating ‚Üí minimal distortion and oxidation.
- Energy efficient (85‚Äì90% efficient).
- Controllable case depth by frequency selection.
- Easily automated on production lines.
- No skilled operator required.

**7. Limitations:**
- High capital cost for power supply.
- Inductor coil must be designed for each part geometry.
- Sharp corners and complex shapes are difficult.
- Limited to steels with **> 0.3% C** (otherwise insufficient martensitic hardness).
- Surface should be free of scale and decarburization.

**8. Applications:**
- Crankshaft journals, cam lobes, gear teeth, axle journals, piston pins, splined shafts.

---

**Model Solution for Q-B (b): Nitriding**

**1. Definition:** Nitriding is a thermochemical surface hardening process in which **nitrogen is diffused into the surface of alloy steel** at relatively low temperatures (500‚Äì550 ¬∞C), forming a hard nitrided case without the need for quenching.

**2. Process Steps:**
- Component is **machined, heat-treated (quenched + tempered) to its final core properties first**, because nitriding temperatures are low and don't transform the core.
- Surface must be clean and free of scale; sometimes passivated (oxidized) to control nitriding.
- Component loaded into a sealed **nitriding furnace**.
- **Ammonia gas (NH‚ÇÉ)** is introduced and dissociates on contact with the hot steel surface:

$$2\text{NH}_3 \rightarrow 2\text{N (atomic)} + 3\text{H}_2$$

- Atomic nitrogen is absorbed by the surface and diffuses inward.
- Process runs at **500‚Äì550 ¬∞C for 20‚Äì100 hours** (long, but temperature is low).
- Furnace is cooled slowly under NH‚ÇÉ atmosphere to prevent decarburization.

**3. Role of Alloying Elements:**
Steel must contain **strong nitride formers** ‚Äî **Al, Cr, Mo, V, Ti, Mn**. These elements:
- Form **stable, finely dispersed nitrides** (AlN, CrN, Cr‚ÇÇN, Mo‚ÇÇN, VN).
- These nitrides are **extremely hard** and remain stable up to 500‚Äì600 ¬∞C.
- Provide case hardness of **1000‚Äì1200 HV** (vs. ~ 300 HV for plain carbon steel).
- Examples of nitriding steels: **38CrAlMo7-10, 31CrMoV9, Nitralloy 135, Nitralloy N**.

**4. Microstructure of Nitrided Case:**
From surface inward, two distinct zones form:
- **Compound (white) layer:** Œµ-Fe‚ÇÇ‚Çã‚ÇÉN and Œ≥‚Ä≤-Fe‚ÇÑN ‚Äî extremely hard (1000+ HV) but **brittle**. Often removed by grinding or controlled to be thin.
- **Diffusion zone:** Solid solution of N in Œ±-Fe + finely dispersed alloy nitrides ‚Äî this zone gives the **engineering hardness** and toughness.

**5. Why No Quenching is Required:**
- Nitriding temperature (500‚Äì550 ¬∞C) is **far below A‚ÇÅ (~ 727 ¬∞C)** ‚Äî no phase transformation (no austenite formation) in the core.
- Hardness comes from **nitride precipitation**, not from martensite formation.
- The case is already hard on slow cooling from the nitriding temperature.
- This is a huge advantage: **no distortion, no cracking, no need for tempering.**

**6. Properties of Nitrided Surface:**
- Hardness: **1000‚Äì1200 HV** (highest of all surface treatments).
- Retains hardness up to ~ 500 ¬∞C.
- Excellent wear resistance and fatigue strength.
- Corrosion resistance improved (N in solid solution).
- Very good dimensional stability.

**7. Limitations:**
- Very long process time (20‚Äì100 h).
- Requires expensive nitriding-grade alloy steel.
- Thin case depth (max ~ 0.7 mm).
- Brittle white layer (must be controlled or removed).
- High capital cost for sealed furnace with NH‚ÇÉ handling.

**8. Applications:**
- Crankshafts, cylinder liners, valve stems, gear teeth, plastic-mold cavities, gauges, dies, machine tool spindles.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout for Induction Hardening Question:** Students frequently:
> 1. Forget to mention that **induction hardening does NOT change the chemistry** ‚Äî it's a purely thermal process (vs. carburizing/nitriding which change chemistry).
> 2. Write the skin-depth formula but forget the **Arrhenius-type temperature dependence** of $\rho$ and $\mu$, which also affects case depth.
> 3. Confuse "induction" with "dielectric heating" ‚Äî induction uses a **magnetic field**, dielectric uses an **electric field** for non-conductors.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary ‚Äî Read Before Exam**

- **Surface hardening = hard case + tough core.** Three families: **Thermochemical (diffusion), Selective heating, Mechanical (cold work).**
- **Carburizing:** Adds C, low-carbon steel, 900‚Äì950 ¬∞C, several hours, needs **quench + temper**, case ~ 55‚Äì62 HRC, deep case (0.5‚Äì6 mm).
- **Gas > Liquid > Pack** (in order of modern industrial preference).
- **Nitriding:** Adds N, **alloy steel** (Al, Cr, Mo), 500‚Äì550 ¬∞C, very long (20‚Äì100 h), **NO quench**, case ~ 1000‚Äì1200 HV, thin case (0.3‚Äì0.7 mm), best hot hardness.
- **Cyaniding:** Hybrid C+N using NaCN salt bath ‚Äî fast, shallow, **toxic**.
- **Carbonitriding:** Non-toxic modern replacement for cyaniding, uses NH‚ÇÉ + carrier gas.
- **Induction hardening:** **Selective heating**, 10‚Äì500 kHz, skin depth $\delta = \sqrt{\rho/(\pi\mu f)}$, seconds, needs quench, no chemistry change, mass production.
- **Flame hardening:** Oxy-acetylene flame heating + water spray quench, operator-skill dependent.
- **Laser / electron beam hardening:** Localized, self-quenching, very thin and precise case.
- **Shot peening:** Mechanical, induces **compressive residual stresses**, improves fatigue, no thermal effect.
- **Key formulas to memorize:** $x = \sqrt{Dt}$, $D = D_0 \exp(-Q/RT)$, $\delta = \sqrt{\rho/(\pi\mu f)}$.
- **Comparison triggers to remember:**
  - **Toughest core:** Nitriding (no phase change).
  - **Highest hardness:** Nitriding (1000+ HV).
  - **Deepest case:** Gas carburizing.
  - **Fastest:** Induction hardening (seconds).
  - **Most economical for mass production:** Induction.
  - **Best dimensional control:** Nitriding.
- **Microstructure of case:** Martensite (carburized, induction, flame, laser) | Martensite + alloy nitrides (nitrided) | Compound + diffusion zone (nitrided detail).
- **KTU-Favorite Trick Question:** "Why is induction hardening possible only for medium/high carbon steel?" ‚Üí Because sufficient carbon must be present to form hard martensite on quenching; low-carbon steel would yield soft, low-carbon martensite.
- **Another KTU Favorite:** "Why is nitriding done after quenching and tempering?" ‚Üí Because nitriding temperature is too low to re-austenitize and harden the core; hence the core must already be in its final hardened-and-tempered state.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Need for alloying
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAUT304 - MATERIAL SCIENCE AND METALLURGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Need for alloying

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT] Core Definition
> **Alloying** is the process of combining a base (parent) metal with one or more metallic or non-metallic elements ‚Äî known as **alloying elements** ‚Äî to produce a new metallic substance called an **alloy**, whose resultant properties are superior to those of the pure parent metal in one or more engineering aspects (strength, hardness, corrosion resistance, toughness, etc.).

A pure metal, although useful in limited contexts, seldom possesses the **combination of properties** demanded by modern engineering applications. The systematic addition of carefully selected alloying elements compensates for these inherent limitations.

> [!NOTE] Distinction (KTU Board Favourite)
> - **Pure Metal** ‚Üí Contains a single metallic element (e.g., pure Fe, pure Cu, pure Al).
> - **Alloy** ‚Üí A metallic substance composed of two or more elements, where at least one is a metal, exhibiting metallic characteristics.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are making **tea (chai)**. Plain hot water is just water ‚Äî boring and of limited use. But when you add **tea leaves, sugar, ginger, and milk**, the resulting drink is far more flavourful, energetic, and useful. 

In the same way:
- **Pure metal (water)** = Basic, but limited in strength and versatility.
- **Alloying elements (tea leaves, sugar, ginger)** = Each adds a special "flavour" ‚Äî strength, hardness, corrosion resistance, machinability.
- **Alloy (chai)** = The final, powerful, application-ready material.

The art of alloying is essentially the **engineering art of "spicing up"** a base metal to suit a specific engineering need.

---

## 1.3 Why Pure Metals Are Inadequate

A pure metal such as pure iron, copper, or aluminium suffers from several limitations:

| Limitation of Pure Metal | Engineering Consequence |
|---|---|
| Very low tensile strength | Cannot bear structural loads |
| Poor hardness | Wears out rapidly under friction |
| Low fatigue resistance | Fails under cyclic loading |
| Poor corrosion resistance | Degrades in moist/chemical environments |
| Low creep resistance | Deforms at elevated temperatures |
| Limited electrical/magnetic control | Cannot be tailored for specialised electronics |
| Excessive softness (e.g., pure Al) | Difficult to machine and form |
| Low yield strength | Undergoes plastic deformation easily |

> [!TIP] KTU Exam Tip
> Examiners frequently test the **"limitations of pure metals"** table. Memorise at least **5 limitations** with examples for a full 3-mark answer.

---

## 1.4 The Central Premise of Alloying

$$ \text{Base Metal (Matrix)} + \text{Alloying Element(s)} \xrightarrow{\text{Melting \& Solidification}} \text{Alloy with Superior Properties} $$

The improvement arises because alloying:
1. Distorts the crystal lattice (solid solution strengthening).
2. Introduces new phases (intermetallics, carbides, etc.).
3. Refines grain structure.
4. Hinders dislocation motion.
5. Alters transformation temperatures.

> [!IMPORTANT] These five mechanisms are the *physical root cause* of why alloying works. They will appear in **Part B (14-mark)** questions of KTU ESE.

---

## 1.5 General Objectives of Alloying (Summary List)

- To **increase strength and hardness** (e.g., Fe + C ‚Üí Steel).
- To **improve toughness and ductility** simultaneously.
- To **enhance corrosion and oxidation resistance** (e.g., Fe + Cr + Ni ‚Üí Stainless Steel).
- To **improve wear resistance** (e.g., Fe + Mn ‚Üí Hadfield steel).
- To **improve machinability** (e.g., Fe + S ‚Üí Free-cutting steel).
- To **enhance high-temperature strength / creep resistance** (e.g., Ni-based superalloys).
- To **improve castability** and reduce casting defects.
- To **achieve special physical properties** (magnetic, electrical, thermal).
- To **reduce cost** by replacing expensive pure metals with cheaper alloying additions.
- To **stabilise desired phases** (e.g., Ti + V stabilises Œ≤-phase).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The "Why" of Alloying ‚Äî A Structured Theoretical Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Atomic-Level Mechanism

Alloying alters the internal architecture of the metal in **five fundamental ways**:

1. **Solid Solution Strengthening**
   - The solute atoms fit into the host lattice either substitutionally or interstitially.
   - This creates **local lattice strain fields** that resist dislocation motion.
   - Result ‚Üí Increase in yield strength and hardness.

2. **Grain Boundary Strengthening (Hall‚ÄìPetch Effect)**
   - Alloying elements often act as **grain refiners**.
   - Smaller grains ‚Üí More grain boundaries ‚Üí Greater resistance to dislocation slip.

3. **Precipitation / Age Hardening**
   - Solute atoms form **fine, dispersed second-phase particles** (precipitates) within the matrix.
   - These precipitates obstruct dislocation motion.
   - Result ‚Üí Major increase in strength (e.g., Al-Cu alloys ‚Üí Duralumin).

4. **Formation of New Phases**
   - Alloying can produce **intermetallic compounds, carbides, or nitrides**.
   - These are typically very hard (e.g., Fe‚ÇÉC in steel, TiC in cutting tools).

5. **Phase Stabilisation**
   - Certain elements (Ni, Mn, Co) **stabilise austenite (FCC)** in iron.
   - Others (Cr, Mo, W, V) **stabilise ferrite (BCC)**.
   - This allows tailoring of the microstructure for specific applications.

---

### 2.1.2 The "How" ‚Äî Engineering Parameters Affected

$$ \text{Alloying} \;\Rightarrow\; \Delta(\text{Strength, Hardness, Toughness, Corrosion Resistance, Wear Resistance, Creep, Fatigue, Machinability, Cost}) $$

| Property Improved | Typical Mechanism | Industrial Example |
|---|---|---|
| Strength & Hardness | Solid solution + precipitation | Al-Cu (Duralumin) |
| Corrosion Resistance | Passivation oxide layer | Fe-Cr-Ni (Stainless Steel) |
| Toughness at low temp | Grain refinement, FCC stabilisation | Fe-Ni (Invar / Cryogenic steel) |
| Wear Resistance | Hard carbide formation | Fe-Cr-W (Tool Steel) |
| High-Temp Strength | Œ≥' precipitate formation | Ni-Cr-Al (Superalloys) |
| Machinability | Inclusions act as chip breakers | Fe-S-P (Free-cutting steel) |
| Magnetic Properties | Domain control, phase control | Fe-Si (Transformer steel) |
| Dimensional Stability | Low thermal expansion | Fe-Ni (Invar alloy) |

---

## 2.2 Hume-Rothery Rules (Governing Solubility & Alloy Formation)

> [!IMPORTANT] The **Hume-Rothery Rules** are tested frequently in KTU Part A (3 marks) as well as in Part B derivations. Memorise all 5 rules.

The success of alloying ‚Äî i.e., the extent to which one metal dissolves in another ‚Äî is governed by **five empirical conditions**:

| # | Hume-Rothery Rule | Condition for High Solubility | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Atomic Size Factor** | Difference in atomic radii < **15%** | ‚â§ 15 % |
| 2 | **Same Crystal Structure** | Both metals must have identical lattice type in the allotropic form | BCC-BCC, FCC-FCC, HCP-HCP |
| 3 | **Same Valency** | Solute and solvent should have similar valency | Œîvalence small |
| 4 | **Same Electronegativity** | Electronegativity values should be close | ŒîEN small |
| 5 | **Position in Electrochemical Series** | Similar position to avoid compound formation | Both noble or both active |

> [!TIP] When 4 out of 5 rules are satisfied, **extensive solid solubility** (> 5 atomic %) is observed. When rules are violated, only **limited solubility** or **intermetallic compound formation** occurs.

---

## 2.3 Classification of Alloys (Based on Phase Composition)

| Type | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Single-Phase Alloys** | Uniform solid solution (substitutional or interstitial) | Cu-Ni (complete solubility) |
| **Polyphase / Multi-Phase Alloys** | Combination of solid solution + intermetallic/secondary phase | Fe-C (Pearlite = Ferrite + Cementite) |
| **Eutectic Alloys** | Distinct eutectic mixture with sharp melting point | Pb-Sn (solder) |
| **Peritectic Alloys** | Formed by peritectic reaction | Cu-Zn (Brass) |
| **Intermetallic Compounds** | Stoichiometric, hard, brittle phases | CuAl‚ÇÇ, Ni‚ÇÉAl, Fe‚ÇÉC |

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation / Concept | Mathematical Form | Physical Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Solid Solution Strengthening (Labusch) | $\Delta\tau \propto G \cdot \varepsilon^{3/2} \cdot c^{1/2}$ | Shear stress increase due to misfit strain | MPa |
| 2 | Hall‚ÄìPetch Relation | $\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y \cdot d^{-1/2}$ | Yield strength vs. grain size | MPa, mm$^{-1/2}$ |
| 3 | Orowan Looping (Precipitation Strengthening) | $\Delta\tau \approx \dfrac{G \cdot b}{L - 2r}$ | Stress needed to bow dislocations between particles | MPa |
| 4 | Hume-Rothery Size Factor | $\Delta r / r_{\text{solvent}} \leq 15\%$ | Maximum allowed atomic size mismatch | % |
| 5 | Rule of Mixtures (Properties) | $P_{\text{alloy}} = P_A \cdot f_A + P_B \cdot f_B$ | Property as weighted average of phases | Varies |
| 6 | Phase Fraction (Lever Rule) | $f_\alpha = \dfrac{C_0 - C_\beta}{C_\alpha - C_\beta}$ | Amount of Œ± phase in two-phase region | Fraction |
| 7 | Diffusion (Fick's First Law) | $J = -D \cdot \dfrac{dC}{dx}$ | Atomic flux due to concentration gradient | atoms/m¬≤¬∑s |
| 8 | Activation Energy (Diffusion) | $D = D_0 \cdot \exp(-Q/RT)$ | Temperature dependence of diffusion | m¬≤/s |

> [!NOTE]
> In the table above, $\vert \cdot \vert$ notation is avoided ‚Äî instead, expressions like $C_0 - C_\beta$ and $\sigma_0 + k_y d^{-1/2}$ are written explicitly to keep the markdown table intact.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The need for alloying is not academic ‚Äî it is the **backbone of modern industry**:

- **Aerospace**: Ti-6Al-4V alloy for turbine blades (lightweight + high-temp strength).
- **Automotive**: Al-Si alloys for pistons (low density + wear resistance).
- **Marine**: Cu-Ni alloys for ship hulls (corrosion resistance in seawater).
- **Power Generation**: Ni-Cr-Fe superalloys in steam/gas turbines.
- **Medical**: Ti-Al-V alloys for implants (biocompatibility + strength).
- **Electronics**: Cu-Be alloys for springs and connectors (high conductivity + strength).
- **Construction**: Low-alloy high-strength steels (HSLA) for bridges and skyscrapers.

> The **unalloyed world** would be a world of weak, soft, short-lived machines. Alloying is what enables modern civilisation.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 ‚Äî Hall‚ÄìPetch Strengthening Equation

The Hall‚ÄìPetch relation is fundamental to **grain-boundary strengthening**, one of the major outcomes of alloying. It quantifies how grain refinement (often achieved through alloying) increases yield strength.

### Step 1 ‚Äî Start with the Empirical Observation

Experiments show that yield strength $\sigma_y$ of a polycrystalline metal varies inversely with the square root of its average grain diameter $d$. Mathematically:

$$ \sigma_y \propto \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{d}} $$

### Step 2 ‚Äî Introduce a Constant of Proportionality

A constant $k_y$ is introduced to convert proportionality into equality. Another constant $\sigma_0$ accounts for the intrinsic lattice resistance (friction stress) of the material.

$$ \sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y \cdot \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{d}} $$

### Step 3 ‚Äî Physical Justification

- A dislocation pile-up at a grain boundary generates a stress concentration.
- This stress must be high enough to activate slip in the adjacent grain.
- Smaller grain size ‚Üí Fewer dislocations per pile-up ‚Üí Higher applied stress needed.
- Hence, $\sigma_y$ rises as $d$ falls.

### Step 4 ‚Äî Finalised Form

$$ \boxed{\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y \cdot d^{-1/2}} $$

Where:
- $\sigma_y$ = Yield strength
- $\sigma_0$ = Friction stress (lattice resistance)
- $k_y$ = Hall‚ÄìPetch coefficient (material constant)
- $d$ = Average grain diameter

### Step 5 ‚Äî Significance in Alloying

Alloying elements such as **Nb, Ti, V, Al** form fine carbides/nitrides (e.g., NbC, TiN) that **pin grain boundaries** and prevent grain growth during hot working. This refines the grain structure, which ‚Äî per the Hall‚ÄìPetch equation ‚Äî **increases the yield strength** of the alloy.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 ‚Äî Solid Solution Strengthening (Labusch Model)

### Step 1 ‚Äî Concept

When solute atoms (different size from solvent) substitute in a host lattice, they produce a **misfit strain** $\varepsilon$ around themselves. This local distortion creates a stress field that opposes dislocation motion.

### Step 2 ‚Äî Misfit Strain Expression

$$ \varepsilon = \dfrac{r_{\text{solute}} - r_{\text{solvent}}}{r_{\text{solvent}}} $$

### Step 3 ‚Äî Strengthening Contribution

The increase in critical resolved shear stress (CRSS) is given by the Labusch expression:

$$ \Delta\tau \propto G \cdot \varepsilon^{3/2} \cdot c^{1/2} $$

Where:
- $G$ = Shear modulus of the solvent
- $\varepsilon$ = Misfit strain
- $c$ = Atomic concentration of solute

### Step 4 ‚Äî Interpretation

- **Higher misfit strain** (larger atomic size difference) ‚Üí Greater strengthening.
- **Higher solute concentration** ‚Üí Greater strengthening (but with a square-root dependency).
- This is the *theoretical basis* of why substitutional solid solutions (e.g., Cu in Ni, Zn in Cu) are stronger than their pure metal constituents.

### Step 5 ‚Äî Application

A Cu-Zn brass (Œ±-brass) is stronger than pure Cu because Zn atoms create local lattice strain that impedes dislocation motion. The higher the Zn content (within solubility limits), the higher the strength ‚Äî illustrating the **Labusch relationship** qualitatively.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 ‚Äî Orowan Looping (Precipitation Strengthening)

### Step 1 ‚Äî Setup

Consider a dislocation encountering hard, non-shearable precipitate particles of average radius $r$ and average inter-particle spacing $L$.

### Step 2 ‚Äî Condition for Bypassing

The dislocation cannot cut through the hard particles. Instead, it bows out and forms a loop around each particle (Orowan loop).

### Step 3 ‚Äî Stress for Orowan Looping

The required shear stress to bow the dislocation to a critical radius of curvature equal to half the inter-particle spacing is:

$$ \Delta\tau_{\text{Orowan}} = \dfrac{G \cdot b}{L - 2r} $$

Where:
- $G$ = Shear modulus
- $b$ = Burgers vector
- $L$ = Centre-to-centre spacing of particles
- $r$ = Particle radius

### Step 4 ‚Äî Simplification for Fine, Closely Spaced Particles

When $L \gg 2r$, the denominator approximates to $L$, giving:

$$ \boxed{\Delta\tau \approx \dfrac{G \cdot b}{L}} $$

### Step 5 ‚Äî Engineering Relevance

- **Finer and more closely spaced precipitates** ‚Üí Higher $\Delta\tau$ ‚Üí Higher strength.
- This is why **age-hardened alloys** (e.g., Al-Cu-Mg, Cu-Be, Ni-based superalloys) require carefully controlled heat treatment ‚Äî to produce optimum precipitate size and distribution.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU Style)

**Problem:**
A low-carbon steel has an average grain diameter of $20 \;\mu\text{m}$ and a yield strength of $250 \;\text{MPa}$. After a special thermomechanical treatment involving alloying, the grain diameter is reduced to $5 \;\mu\text{m}$. If $\sigma_0 = 100 \;\text{MPa}$ and $k_y = 0.7 \;\text{MPa}\cdot\text{m}^{1/2}$, calculate the new yield strength.

### Step 1 ‚Äî Apply Hall‚ÄìPetch Equation

$$ \sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y \cdot d^{-1/2} $$

### Step 2 ‚Äî Convert Units

$$ d_1 = 20 \;\mu\text{m} = 20 \times 10^{-6} \;\text{m} $$
$$ d_2 = 5 \;\mu\text{m} = 5 \times 10^{-6} \;\text{m} $$

### Step 3 ‚Äî Compute New $d^{-1/2}$

$$ d_2^{-1/2} = (5 \times 10^{-6})^{-1/2} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{5 \times 10^{-6}}} $$

$$ d_2^{-1/2} = \dfrac{1}{2.236 \times 10^{-3}} = 447.2 \;\text{m}^{-1/2} $$

### Step 4 ‚Äî Substitute Into Equation

$$ \sigma_{y,\text{new}} = 100 + (0.7)(447.2) $$

$$ \sigma_{y,\text{new}} = 100 + 313.04 $$

$$ \boxed{\sigma_{y,\text{new}} \approx 413 \;\text{MPa}} $$

### Step 5 ‚Äî Verification

$$ \sigma_{y,\text{old}} = 100 + (0.7)(223.6) = 100 + 156.5 = 256.5 \;\text{MPa} \;\;\checkmark $$

The grain refinement (from 20 ¬µm to 5 ¬µm) has **increased the yield strength by ~62 %** ‚Äî a clear illustration of why alloying elements that refine grain size are so highly valued.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: How Alloying Transforms a Pure Metal

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **causal chain** from a base metal to an engineering-grade alloy. It traces the four key transformation pathways enabled by alloying elements.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Pure Base Metal] --> B[Introduction of Alloying Elements]
    B --> C{Solidification Path}

    C -->|Path 1| D1[Substitutional Solid Solution]
    C -->|Path 2| D2[Interstitial Solid Solution]
    C -->|Path 3| D3[New Phase / Intermetallic Formation]
    C -->|Path 4| D4[Eutectic or Peritectic Reaction]

    D1 --> E[Mechanical Property Improvement]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> F[Hard Phase Reinforcement]
    D4 --> G[Special Microstructure]

    E --> H[Final Alloy with Tailored Properties]
    F --> H
    G --> H

    H --> H1[High Strength]
    H --> H2[High Hardness]
    H --> H3[Corrosion Resistance]
    H --> H4[Wear Resistance]
    H --> H5[Creep Resistance]
    H --> H6[Improved Machinability]
    H --> H7[Special Magnetic or Electrical Behaviour]

    classDef base fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000;
    classDef path fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#996600,color:#000;

```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam ‚Äì Dec 2023]`
**"Why are pure metals generally not used for engineering applications? List any four limitations."**

**Model Answer:**

Pure metals possess insufficient mechanical and chemical properties for most engineering requirements. Four key limitations are:

1. **Low Strength** ‚Äî Pure metals such as pure iron or aluminium have very low yield and tensile strength, making them unsuitable for load-bearing applications.

2. **Poor Hardness and Wear Resistance** ‚Äî They are too soft and wear out quickly under friction, limiting their use in cutting tools, gears, and bearings.

3. **Low Corrosion Resistance** ‚Äî Most pure metals (e.g., pure Fe) oxidise readily in moist or chemical environments, leading to rapid degradation.

4. **Insufficient High-Temperature Strength** ‚Äî Pure metals creep and lose strength rapidly at elevated temperatures, restricting their use in turbines, boilers, and reactors.

Additionally, pure metals offer limited scope for property tailoring ‚Äî alloying overcomes all these deficiencies by manipulating the microstructure.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam ‚Äì June 2024]`
**"State the Hume-Rothery rules for the formation of substitutional solid solutions."**

**Model Answer:**

For extensive substitutional solubility between two metals, **Hume-Rothery** proposed the following empirical conditions:

1. **Atomic Size Factor** ‚Äî The difference in atomic radii of solute and solvent should not exceed **15%**.
2. **Same Crystal Structure** ‚Äî Both metals should have identical crystal structures in their alloying temperature range.
3. **Same Valency** ‚Äî Solute and solvent should have similar valency (electropositive metals dissolve in electropositive, and electronegative in electronegative).
4. **Similar Electronegativity** ‚Äî Close electronegativity values prevent the formation of intermetallic compounds.
5. **Similar Electrochemical Character** ‚Äî Both metals should occupy comparable positions in the electrochemical series.

When **4 out of 5 rules** are satisfied, extensive solid solubility is observed. Violation leads to limited solubility or compound formation.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) ‚Äî Internal Choice Format

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam ‚Äì Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** Discuss in detail the **various reasons/need for alloying** of metals. Explain **any five major objectives** of alloying with suitable examples. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** What are the **Hume-Rothery rules**? Explain each rule in detail and discuss their **significance in predicting alloying behaviour**. **[7 Marks]**

---

### **Model Answer for Question A (a)**

The need for alloying arises from the inability of pure metals to satisfy the diverse and demanding requirements of modern engineering. Pure metals, although useful in limited contexts, lack the combination of properties needed for structural, chemical, and thermal applications.

The **five major objectives of alloying** are:

1. **To Increase Strength and Hardness**
   - Solid solution strengthening and precipitation hardening are the two principal mechanisms.
   - Example: Adding **carbon to iron** to form steel dramatically increases both strength and hardness. Similarly, **Cu added to Al** produces Duralumin, a high-strength aerospace alloy.

2. **To Improve Corrosion and Oxidation Resistance**
   - Alloying with elements like **Cr, Ni, and Mo** forms a stable, adherent, self-healing passive oxide film on the surface.
   - Example: **Stainless steel (Fe-18%Cr-8%Ni)** is highly resistant to rusting due to the Cr‚ÇÇO‚ÇÉ passive layer.

3. **To Improve Wear and Abrasion Resistance**
   - Hard carbides (e.g., Cr‚ÇÇ‚ÇÉC‚ÇÜ, WC, VC) formed by alloying act as abrasive-resistant phases.
   - Example: **High-speed tool steel (Fe-Cr-W-V)** retains hardness and wear resistance at red heat.

4. **To Improve High-Temperature Strength and Creep Resistance**
   - Alloying with **Ni, Cr, Mo, W, Co** produces alloys that retain strength above 600 ¬∞C.
   - Example: **Ni-based superalloys (Inconel, Nimonic)** used in jet engine turbine blades.

5. **To Achieve Special Physical Properties**
   - **Magnetic properties**: Fe-Si (transformer steel) reduces eddy-current losses.
   - **Low thermal expansion**: Fe-Ni (Invar, 36% Ni) used in precision instruments.
   - **High electrical conductivity with strength**: Cu-Be alloys for spring contacts.

**Other objectives** include improving machinability (Fe + S ‚Üí free-cutting steel), enhancing castability (Fe + Si ‚Üí cast iron), reducing cost, and stabilising desired phases.

Thus, alloying is an indispensable tool for tailoring metallic materials to meet specific engineering needs.

---

### **Model Answer for Question A (b)**

**Hume-Rothery Rules** are a set of empirical conditions that predict the **extent of solid solubility** between two metals in the substitutional solid solution state. These rules were developed by **William Hume-Rothery** based on extensive experimental observations.

**The Five Rules:**

1. **Atomic Size Factor Rule**
   - For extensive solid solubility, the difference in atomic radii of solute and solvent must be **less than 15%**.
   - If the size difference exceeds 15%, the lattice strain energy becomes too high, and solubility is severely limited.
   - Example: Cu (radius 0.128 nm) and Ni (radius 0.125 nm) ‚Üí Difference ‚âà 2.3% ‚Üí Complete solid solubility.

2. **Crystal Structure Rule**
   - Both metals should have **the same crystal structure** (BCC, FCC, or HCP) in the temperature range of interest.
   - Allotropic transformations may limit solubility if the structures differ at alloying temperature.
   - Example: Cu (FCC) and Ni (FCC) ‚Üí Complete solubility. Cu (FCC) and Zn (HCP at RT, but FCC above) ‚Üí Limited solubility at room temperature.

3. **Valency Rule**
   - A metal of higher valency dissolves more readily in a metal of lower valency than vice versa.
   - The valency difference influences the electron concentration (e/a ratio) at which a new phase appears.
   - Example: Zn (valency 2) dissolves extensively in Cu (valency 1), but Cu has limited solubility in Zn.

4. **Electronegativity Rule**
   - If the electronegativity difference is large, the metals tend to form **intermetallic compounds** rather than solid solutions.
   - Close electronegativity values favour substitutional solubility.
   - Example: Cu and Ni have similar electronegativities ‚Üí Solid solution. Cu and Mg ‚Üí Form CuMg‚ÇÇ compound.

5. **Electrochemical / Chemical Affinity Rule**
   - Metals with widely different electrochemical characters (one noble, one active) tend to form compounds.
   - Metals of similar character (both noble or both active) form solid solutions.
   - Example: Cu-Au (both noble) ‚Üí Complete solid solubility. Cu-Mg (noble + active) ‚Üí Compound formation.

**Significance:**
- These rules are extensively used in **alloy design** to predict whether two metals will form an extensive solid solution, a limited solution, or a compound.
- They form the **theoretical basis for binary alloy phase diagrams**.
- They guide material scientists in selecting alloying elements for specific applications (e.g., selecting austenite stabilisers in steel design).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam ‚Äì Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** Explain the **five principal mechanisms by which alloying strengthens a metal**, with appropriate equations. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Discuss the **classification of alloys** based on phase composition. List the **effects of common alloying elements in steel** (any eight) in a tabular form. **[7 Marks]**

---

### **Model Answer for Question B (a)**

Alloying strengthens a metal through **five principal mechanisms**, each operating at a different microstructural scale.

1. **Solid Solution Strengthening**
   - Solute atoms distort the host lattice, generating internal stress fields.
   - These stress fields interact with dislocations, increasing the critical resolved shear stress required for slip.
   - **Labusch Equation** (for substitutional solutes):

$$ \Delta\tau \propto G \cdot \varepsilon^{3/2} \cdot c^{1/2} $$

Where $\varepsilon$ is the misfit strain and $c$ is the solute concentration.

   - Example: Zn in Cu (Œ±-brass) increases both strength and hardness.

2. **Grain Boundary Strengthening (Hall‚ÄìPetch Strengthening)**
   - Alloying elements such as **Nb, Ti, V** form fine, stable carbides/nitrides that pin grain boundaries and prevent growth.
   - Smaller grains increase the number of grain boundaries, impeding dislocation motion.
   - **Hall‚ÄìPetch Equation**:

$$ \sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y \cdot d^{-1/2} $$

   - Example: HSLA (High-Strength Low-Alloy) steels.

3. **Precipitation / Age Hardening**
   - A supersaturated solid solution is aged to form fine, dispersed precipitates.
   - Dislocations must either cut through or bow around these particles (Orowan looping).
   - **Orowan Stress**:

$$ \Delta\tau \approx \dfrac{G \cdot b}{L} $$

   - Example: Al-Cu alloys (Duralumin), Cu-Be alloys, Ni-based superalloys (Œ≥' precipitates).

4. **Dispersion Strengthening**
   - Hard, inert, non-coherent particles (e.g., oxides, carbides) are dispersed in a soft matrix.
   - Similar to precipitation hardening, but the dispersoids are **non-reactive and thermally stable** at high temperatures.
   - Example: TD-Nickel (Ni with ThO‚ÇÇ dispersoids), SAP (Sintered Aluminium Powder).

5. **Phase Transformation Strengthening**
   - Alloying alters transformation temperatures and produces stronger, harder phases.
   - Example: Carbon in iron enables the formation of **martensite** (BCT structure) on quenching, which is far stronger than austenite or ferrite.
   - Alloying elements like **Ni, Mn** lower the Ms (martensite start) temperature, influencing the retained austenite fraction.

These five mechanisms are often **synergistic** ‚Äî a single alloy may utilise two or more simultaneously to achieve the desired property profile.

---

### **Model Answer for Question B (b)**

**Classification of Alloys Based on Phase Composition:**

Alloys are classified into the following broad categories depending on the number and nature of phases present in their microstructure:

1. **Single-Phase Alloys (Homogeneous Solid Solutions)**
   - Composed of a single, uniform phase throughout.
   - Formed when solute and solvent have complete mutual solubility.
   - Example: **Cu-Ni** system (complete solid solubility at all compositions).

2. **Polyphase / Multi-Phase Alloys**
   - Consist of two or more distinct phases.
   - The combination of phases (e.g., soft + hard) provides a balance of strength and toughness.
   - Example: **Pearlitic steel** (Ferrite + Cementite); **Al-Si pistons** (Œ±-Al + Si particles).

3. **Eutectic Alloys**
   - Exhibit a sharp, low melting point at the eutectic composition.
   - Have a characteristic lamellar or rod-like microstructure.
   - Example: **Pb-Sn solder** (61.9% Sn, 38.1% Pb, eutectic at 183 ¬∞C).

4. **Peritectic Alloys**
   - Formed by a peritectic reaction during solidification.
   - Example: **Cu-Zn (Brass)** system exhibits peritectic behaviour at 902 ¬∞C (60% Zn).

5. **Intermetallic Compound Alloys**
   - Contain stoichiometric intermetallic phases (e.g., CuAl‚ÇÇ, Ni‚ÇÉAl, Fe‚ÇÉC).
   - These are hard, brittle, and often used for precipitation strengthening.
   - Example: **Ni‚ÇÉAl** (Œ≥' phase) in nickel superalloys.

**Effects of Common Alloying Elements in Steel (Any Eight):**

| S.No | Element | Function / Effect |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Carbon (C)** | Increases strength and hardness; forms hard cementite (Fe‚ÇÉC) |
| 2 | **Manganese (Mn)** | Deoxidiser and desulphuriser; increases hardenability; austenite stabiliser |
| 3 | **Silicon (Si)** | Deoxidiser; increases strength and elastic limit; ferrite stabiliser |
| 4 | **Chromium (Cr)** | Improves corrosion resistance; forms hard Cr-carbides; ferrite stabiliser |
| 5 | **Nickel (Ni)** | Improves toughness, especially at low temperatures; austenite stabiliser |
| 6 | **Molybdenum (Mo)** | Improves creep resistance and high-temperature strength; minimises temper brittleness |
| 7 | **Vanadium (V)** | Grain refiner; forms very hard VC carbides; secondary hardness on tempering |
| 8 | **Tungsten (W)** | High hot-hardness; used in high-speed tool steels (forms Fe‚ÇÑW‚ÇÇC) |
| 9 | **Cobalt (Co)** | High-temperature strength; magnetic properties; raises Curie temperature |
| 10 | **Titanium (Ti)** | Grain refiner; forms stable TiC; prevents weld decay in stainless steel |
| 11 | **Niobium (Nb)** | Grain refinement and precipitation strengthening in HSLA steels |
| 12 | **Sulphur (S)** | Improves machinability (forms MnS inclusions) |
| 13 | **Phosphorus (P)** | Increases strength; lowers toughness; improves atmospheric corrosion resistance |

---

> [!WARNING] KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ‚Äî Common Pitfalls
> 1. **Confusing *alloying* with *impurity control*** ‚Äî Alloying is a deliberate, controlled addition to *improve* properties; impurities are unintentional and degrade properties. Examiners deduct marks if these are used interchangeably.
> 2. **Forgetting units in Hall‚ÄìPetch** ‚Äî Always state $\sigma_y$ in **MPa**, $d$ in **m** (or ¬µm with proper conversion), and $k_y$ in **MPa¬∑m$^{1/2}$**. Skipping units costs ¬Ω‚Äì1 mark.
> 3. **Mixing up ferrite stabilisers and austenite stabilisers** ‚Äî A frequent error. Memorise: *FCC stabilisers = Ni, Mn, Co, Cu, N, C*; *BCC stabilisers = Cr, Mo, W, V, Si, Al, Ti, Nb*.
> 4. **Stating only *strength* as the benefit of alloying** ‚Äî The question is "need for alloying"; you must list at least **3‚Äì4 different benefits** (corrosion, wear, machinability, cost, etc.). Single-point answers are scored poorly.
> 5. **Omitting examples** ‚Äî KTU answers are evaluated on *specificity*. "Improves strength" is incomplete; "Adding C to Fe to form steel improves strength" is acceptable.
> 6. **Writing Hume-Rothery rules without the 15% size limit** ‚Äî Examiners specifically look for the numerical threshold.
> 7. **Confusing the Orowan model with the Friedel cutting model** ‚Äî These describe different precipitate-dislocation interactions. Use the Orowan model for **non-shearable** particles.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Alloying** = deliberate addition of one or more elements to a base metal to produce an **alloy** with superior properties.
- Pure metals have **5 major limitations**: low strength, low hardness, poor corrosion resistance, low wear resistance, and inadequate high-temperature behaviour.
- The **5 strengthening mechanisms** are: solid solution, grain boundary, precipitation, dispersion, and phase transformation.
- **Hume-Rothery's 5 rules**: atomic size (<15% difference), same crystal structure, same valency, similar electronegativity, similar electrochemical character.
- **Hall‚ÄìPetch equation** (test favourite): $\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y \cdot d^{-1/2}$ ‚Äî smaller grains ‚Üí higher strength.
- **Orowan looping** dominates when precipitates are non-shearable; stress $\approx G b / L$.
- **Labusch equation** (solid solution): $\Delta\tau \propto G \cdot \varepsilon^{3/2} \cdot c^{1/2}$.
- **FCC (austenite) stabilisers**: Ni, Mn, Co, Cu, C, N ‚Äî relevant for cryogenic and stainless steels.
- **BCC (ferrite) stabilisers**: Cr, Mo, W, V, Si, Al, Ti, Nb ‚Äî relevant for tool and heat-resistant steels.
- **Carbide formers** (strong to weak): Ti, Nb, V, Mo, W, Cr, Mn, Fe.
- **Intermetallic compounds** (e.g., Ni‚ÇÉAl, CuAl‚ÇÇ, Fe‚ÇÉC) are hard and brittle ‚Äî used for dispersion strengthening.
