- **Eutectic alloy example**: Pb-Sn solder; **peritectic example**: Cu-Zn brass.
- **Rule of Mixtures**: $P_{\text{alloy}} = P_A f_A + P_B f_B$ ‚Äî basic property prediction.
- **Famous alloy examples** (must know): Stainless steel (Fe-Cr-Ni), Duralumin (Al-Cu-Mg), Brass (Cu-Zn), Bronze (Cu-Sn), Invar (Fe-Ni), Inconel (Ni-Cr-Fe), Ti-6Al-4V, Cu-Be, Hadfield steel (Fe-Mn).
- **Alloying ‚âÝ Impurity addition** ‚Äî Always emphasise the *deliberate, controlled* nature of alloying.
- For 14-mark questions, structure your answer with **diagrams, examples, equations, and tabular data** ‚Äî KTU examiners reward visual organisation.
- Always cite **specific examples** alongside general statements (e.g., "Mn acts as a deoxidiser in steelmaking").
- Remember that alloying effects are **synergistic** ‚Äî multiple mechanisms often operate simultaneously.
- The **purpose of alloying** is not to *replace* the base metal, but to *enhance* its inherent capabilities for specific engineering applications.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Classification of alloys
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBAUT304 - MATERIAL SCIENCE AND METALLURGY > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Classification of alloys # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition An **alloy** is a metallic substance composed of two or more elements, where at least one of the constituents is a metal, and the resulting material exhibits metallic properties superior to or distinct from those of its individual parent components. Alloys are engineered primarily to enhance mechanical strength, corrosion resistance, electrical conductivity, machinability, and thermal stability beyond what pure metals can offer. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** > *"An alloy is a metallic material constituted by the combination of a base metal (solvent) with one or more alloying elements (solutes), produced to achieve a tailored combination of physical, chemical, and mechanical properties unattainable by a single pure metal."* ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy Imagine making a smoothie. A single fruit (say, a banana) has its own flavor, but when you blend it with strawberries, yogurt, and honey, you get a completely new, enriched product. The base fruit is the **solvent (base metal)**, the additives are **solutes (alloying elements)**, and the final smoothie is the **alloy** ‚Äî engineered to taste better, be more nutritious, and serve a specific purpose (breakfast, recovery, dessert). In the same way, pure iron is relatively soft and rusts easily. Add carbon, chromium, and nickel, and you get **stainless steel** ‚Äî strong, corrosion-resistant, and durable. The "recipe" defines the alloy's personality. ## 1.3 Why Classify Alloys? Classification is essential because: - **Selection:** Engineers must pick the right alloy for aerospace, automotive, marine, biomedical, or structural use. - **Processing:** Casting, forging, rolling, and heat treatment differ drastically across alloy families. - **Failure Analysis:** Understanding the class helps predict corrosion, fatigue, and creep behavior. - **Cost Optimization:** Ferrous alloys are cheap and abundant; non-ferrous alloys offer specialized premium performance. > [!NOTE] > **Base Metal Concentration Threshold:** By KTU convention, a metal constitutes the **base (solvent)** if it represents **‚â• 50%** of the total composition by weight, with the rest being alloying elements. ## 1.4 Primary Classification Frameworks (KTU Module 4 Scope) | Criterion | Main Categories | |-----------|----------------| | Presence of Iron | **Ferrous** vs **Non-Ferrous** | | Atomic Arrangement | **Substitutional** vs **Interstitial** vs **Intermetallic** | | Number of Phases | **Single-Phase (Homogeneous)** vs **Multi-Phase (Heterogeneous)** | | Method of Manufacture | **Cast Alloys** vs **Wrought Alloys** | | Phase Diagram Behavior | **Eutectic**, **Peritectic**, **Solid-Solution**, **Compound-forming** | # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Classification Based on Iron Content ### 2.1.1 Ferrous Alloys (Iron is the principal base metal) Ferrous alloys dominate the engineering world ‚Äî over **85% by tonnage** of all metallic materials used industrially are iron-based. **A. Steels (Fe + C, where C ‚â§ 2% by weight)** | Sub-Class | Carbon Content | Key Properties | Typical Applications | |-----------|---------------|----------------|---------------------| | Low Carbon Steel (Mild Steel) | < 0.25% | Ductile, weldable, machinable | Structural beams, car bodies, wires | | Medium Carbon Steel | 0.25% ‚Äì 0.6% | Higher strength, heat-treatable | Rails, gears, axles, crankshafts | | High Carbon Steel | 0.6% ‚Äì 1.0% | Very hard, wear-resistant | Springs, blades, chisels | | Ultra-High Carbon Steel | 1.0% ‚Äì 2.0% | Extremely hard, brittle | Cutlery, punches, dies | | Stainless Steel | C ‚â§ 1.2%, Cr ‚â• 10.5% | Corrosion resistant, hygienic | Surgical instruments, kitchenware, chemical plants | | Tool Steel | Variable | High hardness, red-hardness | Cutting tools, molds, drills | **B. Cast Irons (Fe + C, where C > 2% by weight)** - **White Cast Iron:** Carbon present as **iron carbide (Fe‚ÇÉC / cementite)**. Hard, brittle, wear-resistant. Used in crusher jaws, grinding balls. - **Gray Cast Iron:** Carbon present as **graphite flakes**. Excellent damping, machinable. Used in engine blocks, machine bases. - **Malleable Cast Iron:** Heat-treated white iron; carbon present as **temper graphite (rosettes)**. Tough and ductile. - **Ductile (Nodular) Cast Iron:** Graphite in **spheroidal form** (induced by Mg or Ce treatment). High strength and ductility. Used in pipes, crankshafts. - **Alloy Cast Iron:** Contains Ni, Cr, Mo for special properties (heat resistance, abrasion resistance). ### 2.1.2 Non-Ferrous Alloys (Iron is not the base metal) | Alloy Family | Base Metal | Examples | Major Applications | |--------------|-----------|----------|-------------------| | Copper Alloys | Cu | Brass (Cu+Zn), Bronze (Cu+Sn), Cupronickel (Cu+Ni) | Plumbing, marine hardware, electrical contacts, coins | | Aluminum Alloys | Al | Duralumin (Al-Cu-Mg), Al-Si casting alloys | Aircraft structures, automotive parts, packaging | | Nickel Alloys | Ni | Monel, Inconel, Nichrome, Hastelloy | Jet engines, chemical reactors, resistance heating | | Titanium Alloys | Ti | Ti-6Al-4V, Ti-5Al-2.5Sn | Aerospace, biomedical implants, marine | | Magnesium Alloys | Mg | Mg-Al-Zn, Mg-Zn-Zr | Lightweight automotive, aerospace, electronics | | Lead & Tin Alloys | Pb, Sn | Solder (Pb-Sn), Babbitt (Sn-Sb-Cu) | Electronics soldering, bearings, radiation shielding | | Zinc Alloys | Zn | Zamak, Brass substitutes | Die casting, galvanizing, batteries | ## 2.2 Classification Based on Atomic Structure (Solid Solutions) ### 2.2.1 Substitutional Solid Solutions The solute atoms **replace** solvent atoms on the crystal lattice sites. Governed by the **Hume-Rothery Rules**: 1. **Atomic Size Factor:** Solute and solvent atomic radii must differ by **less than 15%** ($\frac{\vert r_s - r_m \vert}{r_m} \times 100\% \leq 15\%$). 2. **Crystal Structure:** Both metals should have the **same crystal structure** (e.g., both FCC, both BCC). 3. **Valency:** Metals of the **same valency** form extensive solid solutions; otherwise, solubility is limited. 4. **Electronegativity:** Closely similar electronegativities favor solubility (avoid compound formation). > **Example:** Cu-Ni system ‚Äî both FCC, similar atomic radii, complete solid solubility at all compositions. ### 2.2.2 Interstitial Solid Solutions Small solute atoms (typically **H, B, C, N**) fit into the **interstices (holes)** of the solvent lattice. Limited to small atomic sizes. > **Example:** Carbon in iron (Fe-C system, basis of all steels and cast irons). ### 2.2.3 Intermetallic Compounds Stoichiometric compounds with definite atomic ratios, often brittle, hard, with high melting points. > **Examples:** Fe‚ÇÉC (cementite), CuZn (Œ≤-brass), Ni‚ÇÉAl, TiAl, Mg‚ÇÇSi. ## 2.3 Classification Based on Phase Diagram Behavior - **Solid-Solution Type:** Single phase across a wide composition range (e.g., Cu-Ni). - **Eutectic Alloys:** Exhibit a low-melting eutectic point (e.g., Pb-Sn solders, Al-Si cast alloys). - **Peritectic Alloys:** Undergo peritectic reaction L + Œ± ‚Üí Œ≤ on cooling (e.g., Fe-Ni, Cu-Zn). - **Compound-Forming Alloys:** Form intermetallic phases (e.g., Mg-Si, Cu-Zn). - **Monotectic / Syntectic:** Involve liquid-liquid immiscibility (less common). ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > Use `\vert` (not `|`) for absolute value within tables to prevent markdown breakage. | # | Formula / Rule | Description | Units / Limits | |---|---------------|-------------|----------------| | 1 | $\% C_{wt} = \frac{m_C}{m_C + m_{Fe}} \times 100$ | Weight percent of carbon in steel | $0 \leq \% C \leq 2$ for steel | | 2 | $\% C_{at} = \frac{n_C / M_C^{-1}}{n_C / M_C^{-1} + n_{Fe} / M_{Fe}^{-1}} \times 100$ | Atomic percent of carbon | Used for stoichiometric calculations | | 3 | $\Delta r = \frac{\vert r_{solute} - r_{solvent} \vert}{r_{solvent}} \times 100\%$ | Hume-Rothery size factor | Must be $\leq 15\%$ for substitutional | | 4 | $\rho_{alloy} = \frac{1}{\sum_i \frac{w_i}{\rho_i}}$ | Rule of mixtures for density | $w_i$ = weight fraction | | 5 | $E_{alloy} = \sum_i w_i E_i$ | Young's modulus (rule of mixtures) | $w_i$ = weight fraction | | 6 | $W_\alpha = \frac{C_0 - C_\beta}{C_\alpha - C_\beta}$ | Lever rule: weight fraction of Œ±-phase | $0 \leq W_\alpha \leq 1$ | | 7 | $W_\beta = \frac{C_\alpha - C_0}{C_\alpha - C_\beta}$ | Lever rule: weight fraction of Œ≤-phase | $0 \leq W_\beta \leq 1$ | | 8 | $\sigma_{alloy} = \sigma_0 + k \cdot G \cdot \varepsilon^{1/2}$ | Hall-Petch strengthening relation | œÉ in MPa | | 9 | $V_{alloy} = \frac{\sum_i \frac{w_i}{\rho_i}}{\sum_i w_i} \cdot \rho_{alloy}$ | Volume fraction conversion | For microstructural analysis | | 10 | $\% Cr_{stainless} \geq 10.5\%$ | Minimum Cr for passivity in stainless steel | AISI standard threshold | ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Aerospace:** Ti-6Al-4V offers strength-to-weight ratio 4√ó that of mild steel. - **Biomedical:** Co-Cr-Mo alloys used in hip implants due to biocompatibility and wear resistance. - **Marine:** Cu-Ni (70/30) resists seawater corrosion; used in ship hulls and condenser tubes. - **Electronics:** Pb-free solders (Sn-Ag-Cu) replace toxic Pb-Sn solders. - **Energy:** Inconel 718 survives 700¬∞C in gas turbine blades. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Analytical Derivation: Lever Rule for Two-Phase Alloys When a binary alloy of overall composition $C_0$ is at a temperature where two phases ($\alpha$ and $\beta$) coexist, the **lever rule** gives the relative amounts of each phase. ### 3.1.1 Derivation Setup Consider 1 kg of alloy at temperature $T_1$ in a two-phase field: - $\alpha$-phase has composition $C_\alpha$ (solute-lean) - $\beta$-phase has composition $C_\beta$ (solute-rich) - Overall alloy composition: $C_0$ - Let $W_\alpha$ = weight fraction of Œ±, $W_\beta$ = weight fraction of Œ≤ ### 3.1.2 Mass Balance Total mass balance: $$W_\alpha + W_\beta = 1$$ Solute mass balance (solute = 1 kg of alloy times $C_0$ on each side): $$W_\alpha \cdot C_\alpha + W_\beta \cdot C_\beta = C_0$$ ### 3.1.3 Solving the System Substitute $W_\beta = 1 - W_\alpha$: $$W_\alpha \cdot C_\alpha + (1 - W_\alpha) \cdot C_\beta = C_0$$ Expand: $$W_\alpha \cdot C_\alpha + C_\beta - W_\alpha \cdot C_\beta = C_0$$ Group $W_\alpha$ terms: $$W_\alpha (C_\alpha - C_\beta) = C_0 - C_\beta$$ Solve for $W_\alpha$: $$W_\alpha = \frac{C_0 - C_\beta}{C_\alpha - C_\beta}$$ Similarly, by symmetry: $$W_\beta = \frac{C_\alpha - C_0}{C_\alpha - C_\beta}$$ > [!NOTE] > This is the **Lever Rule**. The two compositions $C_\alpha$ and $C_\beta$ act as the lever pivot, and the alloy composition $C_0$ is the load ‚Äî the closer $C_0$ is to one pole, the more of that phase exists. ## 3.2 Analytical Derivation: Hume-Rothery Size Factor For substitutional solid solubility: $$\Delta r (\%) = \frac{\vert r_{solute} - r_{solvent} \vert}{r_{solvent}} \times 100$$ ### Worked Example: Cu-Zn System Given: - $r_{Cu} = 0.128$ nm (FCC) - $r_{Zn} = 0.134$ nm (HCP ‚Äî different structure!) Step 1: Compute size difference: $$\Delta r = \frac{\vert 0.134 - 0.128 \vert}{0.128} \times 100\%$$ $$\Delta r = \frac{0.006}{0.128} \times 100\%$$ $$\Delta r = 4.69\%$$ Step 2: Check crystal structure match: - Cu is FCC; Zn is HCP ‚Üí **Structure mismatch**. Conclusion: **Limited solubility** (Œ±-brass only up to ~39% Zn at room temperature), confirming Hume-Rothery predictions. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Alloy Classification Decision Tree The following step-by-step procedure lets you identify any alloy from its composition: ``` STEP 1: Is Fe the principal element (‚â• 50% by weight)? ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ YES ‚Üí Go to STEP 2 ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ NO ‚Üí Go to STEP 3 (Non-Ferrous) STEP 2: Is Carbon % > 2% by weight? ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ YES ‚Üí CAST IRON family ‚Üí Go to STEP 2a ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ NO ‚Üí STEEL family ‚Üí Go to STEP 2b STEP 2a: Form of carbon? ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ Fe‚ÇÉC (carbide) ‚Üí White Cast Iron ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ Graphite flakes ‚Üí Gray Cast Iron ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ Temper carbon rosettes ‚Üí Malleable Cast Iron ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ Spheroidal graphite ‚Üí Ductile (Nodular) Cast Iron STEP 2b: Total alloying elements (excluding C) > 5%? ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ NO ‚Üí Plain Carbon Steel ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ YES ‚Üí Alloy Steel / Stainless Steel (if Cr ‚â• 10.5%) STEP 3: Identify base metal: ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ Cu ‚Üí Brass (Zn), Bronze (Sn), Cupronickel (Ni) ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ Al ‚Üí Al-Cu, Al-Si, Al-Zn (wrought vs cast) ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ Ni ‚Üí Monel, Inconel, Hastelloy ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ Ti ‚Üí Œ±, Œ≤, Œ±+Œ≤ titanium alloys ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ Mg ‚Üí Mg-Al-Zn (AZ series), Mg-Zn-Zr ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ Sn / Pb ‚Üí Solders, Babbitts ``` ## 3.4 Quantitative Worked Example: Stainless Steel Classification **Problem:** An alloy contains Fe-18%Cr-8%Ni by weight. Classify it. **Solution:** Step 1: Base metal = Fe (‚â• 50%) ‚Üí **Ferrous**. Step 2: Carbon content not specified, but if ‚â§ 2% and Cr ‚â• 10.5% ‚Üí **Stainless Steel family**. Step 3: Cr = 18% (> 10.5%) ‚úì, Ni = 8% (austenite stabilizer). Step 4: Microstructure: The 18-8 composition corresponds to **Austenitic Stainless Steel (AISI 304)**. Key Properties: - Non-magnetic - Excellent corrosion resistance - Cannot be hardened by heat treatment (only by cold working) - Used in kitchenware, chemical tanks, food processing > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Exam Tip:** Always check **all three** criteria ‚Äî Cr ‚â• 10.5%, C content, and whether austenite or ferrite stabilizers dominate ‚Äî before naming the stainless steel type. ## 3.5 Comparison Matrix: Ferrous vs Non-Ferrous | Property | Ferrous Alloys | Non-Ferrous Alloys | |----------|----------------|---------------------| | Density | High (7.0‚Äì8.0 g/cm¬≥) | Low to Medium (1.7‚Äì8.9 g/cm¬≥) | | Corrosion Resistance | Poor (except stainless) | Generally Good | | Strength | High | Moderate to High | | Cost | Low (abundant Fe ore) | Higher | | Magnetic | Usually Yes | Usually No (except Ni) | | Recyclability | Excellent | Excellent | | Examples | Steel, Cast Iron | Brass, Duralumin, Inconel | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block: Master Classification Hierarchy ```mermaid flowchart TD start([Alloy]) --> q1{Fe is the
base metal?} q1 -- YES --> ferrous[Ferrous Alloys] q1 -- NO --> nonferrous[Non-Ferrous Alloys] ferrous --> q2{Carbon
content?} q2 -- "C ‚â§ 2%" --> steel[Steels] q2 -- "C > 2%" --> castiron[Cast Irons] steel --> pcs[Plain Carbon Steel] steel --> ass[Alloy Steel] steel --> sst[Stainless Steel
Cr ‚â• 10.5%] pcs --> lcs[Low Carbon
C less than 0.25%] pcs --> mcs[Medium Carbon
0.25% to 0.6%] pcs --> hcs[High Carbon
0.6% to 1.0%] castiron --> wci[White Cast Iron] castiron --> gci[Gray Cast Iron] castiron --> mci[Malleable Cast Iron] castiron --> dci[Ductile Cast Iron] nonferrous --> cualloy[Cu Alloys] nonferrous --> alalloy[Al Alloys] nonferrous --> nialloy[Ni Alloys] nonferrous --> tialloy[Ti Alloys] nonferrous --> mgalloy[Mg Alloys] cualloy --> brass[Brass Cu plus Zn] cualloy --> bronze[Bronze Cu plus Sn] alalloy --> duralumin[Duralumin Al Cu Mg] alalloy --> alsicasting[Al-Si Casting Alloys] nialloy --> monel[Monel Ni Cu] nialloy --> inconel[Inconel Ni Cr Fe] classDef rootClass fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff classDef ferrousClass fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#fff classDef nonferrousClass fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#000,color:#fff classDef subClass fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#666,color:#000 class start rootClass class ferrous,castiron,steel ferrousClass class nonferrous,cualloy,alalloy,nialloy,tialloy,mgalloy nonferrousClass class wci,gci,mci,dci,pcs,ass,sst,brass,bronze,duralumin,alsicasting,monel,inconel,lcs,mcs,hcs subClass ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Block: Atomic Structure Classification ```mermaid graph LR A[Alloy] --> B[Single Phase
Solid Solution] A --> C[Multi Phase
Mixture] B --> D[Substitutional SS] B --> E[Interstitial SS] D --> D1[Cu-Ni System] D --> D2[Cu-Zn Œ±-brass] E --> E1[Fe-C Austenite] E --> E2[Fe-C Ferrite] C --> F[Eutectic] C --> G[Peritectic] C --> H[Intermetallic Compound] F --> F1[Pb-Sn Solder] F --> F2[Al-Si Cast Alloy] G --> G1[Fe-Ni System] H --> H1[Fe3C Cementite] H --> H2[CuZn Beta Brass] style A fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,color:#fff style B fill:#b3d9ff,stroke:#003366 style C fill:#ffd9b3,stroke:#663300 style D fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366 style E fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366 style F fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#663300 style G fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#663300 style H fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#663300 ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Block: Application-Based Selection Map ```mermaid flowchart LR app[Engineering
Application] --> aer[Aerospace] app --> auto[Automotive] app --> mar[Marine] app --> med[Biomedical] app --> elec[Electronics] aer --> ti[Ti-6Al-4V] aer --> inal[Inconel 718] auto --> mild[Mild Steel] auto --> alal[Al-Si Cast] mar --> cuni[Cu-Ni 70/30] mar --> ssaust[SS 316 Austenitic] med --> cocrmo[Co-Cr-Mo] med --> tigrade[Commercially Pure Ti] elec --> solder[Sn-Ag-Cu Solder] elec --> copper[OFC Copper] classDef domainClass fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#000,color:#fff classDef matClass fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#666,color:#000 class app,domainClass,mar,med,elec,aer,auto domainClass class ti,inal,mild,alal,cuni,ssaust,cocrmo,tigrade,solder,copper matClass ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Classify the following alloys and state one characteristic application of each:** **(a) Ductile (Nodular) Cast Iron (b) Duralumin (c) Austenitic Stainless Steel (AISI 304)** **Model Answer:** **(a) Ductile (Nodular) Cast Iron** belongs to the **ferrous alloy family** (cast iron sub-group with C > 2%). Its graphite appears as **spheroidal nodules** due to Mg/Ce inoculation. **Application:** Automotive crankshafts, pipe fittings, and heavy-duty gearboxes ‚Äî wherever high strength with good ductility is required. **(b) Duralumin** is a **non-ferrous aluminum alloy** (Al-Cu-Mg system, typically 4% Cu, 0.5% Mg, 0.5% Mn). It is heat-treatable and age-hardenable. **Application:** Aircraft structural parts (wings, fuselage skins), rivets, truck wheels. **(c) AISI 304 Stainless Steel** is a **ferrous alloy** of the austenitic stainless family containing **18% Cr, 8% Ni, ‚â§ 0.08% C**. It is non-magnetic and offers excellent corrosion resistance due to the Cr‚ÇÇO‚ÇÉ passive film. **Application:** Kitchenware, food processing equipment, chemical storage tanks, and architectural cladding. --- ### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **State the four Hume-Rothery rules for extensive substitutional solid solubility, with one example of a system that obeys them.** **Model Answer:** The **Hume-Rothery Rules** are empirical criteria that predict whether two metals will form a complete (or extensive) substitutional solid solution: 1. **Atomic Size Factor:** $\Delta r = \frac{\vert r_{solute} - r_{solvent} \vert}{r_{solvent}} \times 100\% \leq 15\%$. Atom sizes must be similar. 2. **Same Crystal Structure:** Both metals should have identical structures (e.g., both FCC or both BCC) in their pure states. 3. **Same Valency:** Solute and solvent should have the same valence. Higher valence metals tend to dissolve more in lower valence solvents. 4. **Similar Electronegativity:** The electronegativity difference should be small to avoid intermetallic compound formation. **Example:** The **Cu-Ni system** satisfies all four rules ‚Äî both FCC, similar atomic radii (r_Cu = 0.128 nm, r_Ni = 0.125 nm), same valence (+2 effectively), and similar electronegativities. They exhibit **complete solid solubility** across the entire composition range. --- ## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks) ‚Äî Module Internal Choice ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` **(a)** Classify alloys on the basis of (i) the presence of iron as a major constituent, and (ii) the number of phases present. Give at least two examples for each category. **[7 Marks]** **(b)** Explain the **Hume-Rothery rules** in detail. Using the rules, justify why the **Cu-Zn system** shows only limited solid solubility in the Œ±-phase at room temperature, while the **Cu-Ni system** shows complete solid solubility. **[7 Marks]** #### Model Solution **(a) Classification of Alloys** **(i) Based on presence of iron (chemical composition):** - **Ferrous Alloys:** Iron is the base metal (‚â• 50% by weight). Further divided into **Steels** (C ‚â§ 2%) and **Cast Irons** (C > 2%). - *Examples:* Mild Steel (0.2% C steel), AISI 304 Stainless Steel (18% Cr, 8% Ni), Gray Cast Iron (3.5% C), Ductile Cast Iron. - **Non-Ferrous Alloys:** Iron is absent or is not the base metal. - *Examples:* Brass (Cu-Zn), Duralumin (Al-Cu-Mg), Inconel 718 (Ni-Cr-Fe). **(ii) Based on number of phases (microstructure):** - **Single-Phase Alloys (Homogeneous):** Consist of one phase only ‚Äî typically a solid solution. - *Examples:* Œ±-brass, Cu-Ni solid solution, austenitic stainless steel at room temperature. - **Multi-Phase Alloys (Heterogeneous):** Consist of two or more distinct phases with different compositions and structures. - *Examples:* Pearlite (Œ±-ferrite + Fe‚ÇÉC) in plain carbon steel, gray cast iron (ferrite + graphite flakes), eutectic Pb-Sn solder. **(b) Hume-Rothery Rules and Application to Cu-Zn vs Cu-Ni** The **Hume-Rothery Rules** govern the extent of substitutional solid solubility between two metals. They were proposed by William Hume-Rothery in 1934 based on experimental observations of binary phase diagrams. | Rule | Criterion | Cu-Ni | Cu-Zn | |------|-----------|-------|-------| | 1. Atomic Size | $\Delta r \leq 15\%$ | $\frac{\vert 0.125 - 0.128 \vert}{0.128} \times 100\% = 2.3\%$ ‚úì | $\frac{\vert 0.134 - 0.128 \vert}{0.128} \times 100\% = 4.7\%$ ‚úì | | 2. Crystal Structure | Same structure | Both FCC ‚úì | Cu = FCC, Zn = HCP ‚úó | | 3. Valency | Same valency preferred | Both effectively divalent ‚úì | Cu = +1/+2, Zn = +2 ‚Äî partial mismatch | | 4. Electronegativity | Similar values | Cu = 1.90, Ni = 1.91 (very close) ‚úì | Cu = 1.90, Zn = 1.65 (greater difference) | **Justification:** - **Cu-Ni System:** Satisfies **all four** Hume-Rothery rules ‚Üí **Complete solid solubility** at all compositions across the entire phase diagram (isomorphous system). The liquidus and solidus lines form a lens-shaped curve, and at any composition, a single homogeneous FCC solid solution exists below the solidus. - **Cu-Zn System:** Fails the **crystal structure rule** (Cu is FCC, Zn is HCP) and shows greater electronegativity difference ‚Üí **Limited Œ±-phase solid solubility** (only up to ~39% Zn at 450¬∞C, less at room temperature). Beyond this, second phases like Œ≤'-brass (ordered BCC, CuZn) and Œ≥-brass (Cu‚ÇÖZn‚Çà) form, producing a **multi-phase** microstructure with distinct intermetallic compounds. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often **forget to mention the crystal structure rule** for Cu-Zn, which is the **decisive** reason. Don't just list the rules ‚Äî **apply** them to the given system with explicit numerical verification. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` **(a)** Differentiate between **ferrous and non-ferrous alloys** with respect to composition, properties, and applications. Provide a comparative table. **[7 Marks]** **(b)** With a neat sketch of the **lever rule** on a binary phase diagram, derive the expressions for the **weight fractions of two co-existing phases** ($\alpha$ and $\beta$) in a binary alloy of overall composition $C_0$. **[7 Marks]** #### Model Solution **(a) Ferrous vs Non-Ferrous Alloys ‚Äî Comparative Analysis** | Aspect | Ferrous Alloys | Non-Ferrous Alloys | |--------|---------------|---------------------| | **Base Metal** | Iron (Fe ‚â• 50%) | Cu, Al, Ni, Ti, Mg, Sn, Pb, Zn | | **Composition** | Fe + C, Cr, Ni, Mo, V, Mn, Si | Pure base metal + alloying elements | | **Density** | High (7.0‚Äì8.0 g/cm¬≥) | Low to medium (1.7‚Äì8.9 g/cm¬≥) | | **Strength** | High tensile and yield strength | Moderate (some, like Ti, are exceptional) | | **Corrosion Resistance** | Poor (excellent in stainless grades) | Generally good to excellent | | **Ductility** | Moderate to high | Generally high (especially Al, Cu) | | **Magnetic Behavior** | Ferromagnetic (except austenitic SS) | Generally non-magnetic | | **Cost** | Low (abundant Fe ore) | Higher (extraction + processing) | | **Recyclability** | Excellent | Excellent | | **Heat Treatability** | Yes (steels harden via quenching) | Limited (Al alloys age-harden) | | **Common Applications** | Structural beams, machinery, tools, rails | Aircraft, marine hardware, electrical wiring, implants | **Example Alloys:** - *Ferrous:* Mild Steel (0.2% C), AISI 316 SS, Gray Cast Iron, Duralumin (NOT ferrous ‚Äî counter-example often confused) - *Non-Ferrous:* Brass, Bronze, Duralumin, Inconel, Ti-6Al-4V, Monel --- **(b) Derivation of the Lever Rule** Consider the **binary eutectic phase diagram** with a generic two-phase region (Œ± + Œ≤): ``` Temperature ‚îÇ L ‚îÇ Liquidus ‚îÇ ‚ï± ‚ï≤ ‚îÇ ‚ï± ‚ï≤ ‚îÇ‚ï± ‚ï≤ T‚ÇÅ ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÇ‚óè‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚óè‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ Isotherm at T‚ÇÅ ‚îÇ‚ï≤ ‚ï± ‚îÇ ‚ï≤____‚ï± ‚îÇ Solidus ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚î¨‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚î¨‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚î¨‚îÄ‚îÄ‚ñ∫ Composition (wt% B) C_Œ± C_0 C_Œ≤ ``` At temperature $T_1$, an alloy of overall composition $C_0$ consists of two phases: - **Œ±-phase** (B-lean) of composition $C_\alpha$ - **Œ≤-phase** (B-rich) of composition $C_\beta$ **Step 1: Total mass balance** $$W_\alpha + W_\beta = 1$$ **Step 2: Component (solute B) mass balance** $$W_\alpha \cdot C_\alpha + W_\beta \cdot C_\beta = 1 \cdot C_0$$ **Step 3: Substitute** $W_\beta = 1 - W_\alpha$ $$W_\alpha C_\alpha + (1 - W_\alpha) C_\beta = C_0$$ $$W_\alpha C_\alpha + C_\beta - W_\alpha C_\beta = C_0$$ $$W_\alpha (C_\alpha - C_\beta) = C_0 - C_\beta$$ **Step 4: Solve for $W_\alpha$** $$W_\alpha = \frac{C_0 - C_\beta}{C_\alpha - C_\beta}$$ **Step 5: By symmetry, solve for $W_\beta$** $$W_\beta = \frac{C_\alpha - C_0}{C_\alpha - C_\beta}$$ **Verification:** $W_\alpha + W_\beta = \frac{(C_0 - C_\beta) + (C_\alpha - C_0)}{C_\alpha - C_\beta} = \frac{C_\alpha - C_\beta}{C_\alpha - C_\beta} = 1$ ‚úì > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall (Lever Rule):** Students frequently: > - Confuse the numerator and denominator (mixing up $W_\alpha$ and $W_\beta$). > - Forget that $C_\alpha$ and $C_\beta$ are the **phase compositions at the tie-line ends**, NOT the overall alloy composition. > - Omit the **verification step** (showing $W_\alpha + W_\beta = 1$). > > Always state clearly: *"The lever pivots at the phase boundary compositions, and the alloy composition is the load."* --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist ‚Äî Classification of Alloys** - **Definition:** An alloy is a metallic substance with at least one metal as the base, combined with one or more elements to improve properties. - **Two Major Families:** **Ferrous** (Fe-based) and **Non-Ferrous** (Cu, Al, Ni, Ti, Mg, etc.). - **Ferrous Sub-Divisions:** Steels (C ‚â§ 2%) vs Cast Irons (C > 2%). - **Cast Iron Varieties:** White (Fe‚ÇÉC, hard, brittle), Gray (graphite flakes, good damping), Malleable (temper carbon), Ductile (spheroidal graphite). - **Steel Varieties:** Plain carbon (low/medium/high C), Alloy steel, Stainless steel (Cr ‚â• 10.5% ‚Äî austenitic, ferritic, martensitic, duplex). - **Non-Ferrous Highlights:** Brass (Cu-Zn), Bronze (Cu-Sn), Duralumin (Al-Cu-Mg), Monel (Ni-Cu), Inconel (Ni-Cr-Fe), Ti-6Al-4V. - **Atomic Classification:** Substitutional (solute replaces solvent atoms) vs Interstitial (solute fits in lattice voids). - **Hume-Rothery Rules:** Size (‚â§ 15%), Structure, Valency, Electronegativity ‚Äî must satisfy **all** for complete solid solubility. - **Phase-Diagram Behavior:** Solid-solution, eutectic, peritectic, compound-forming, monotectic. - **Lever Rule:** $W_\alpha = \frac{C_0 - C_\beta}{C_\alpha - C_\beta}$, $W_\beta = \frac{C_\alpha - C_0}{C_\alpha - C_\beta}$ ‚Äî use to compute phase fractions in two-phase regions. - **Rule of Mixtures (Density):** $\rho_{alloy} = \left( \sum_i \frac{w_i}{\rho_i} \right)^{-1}$ ‚Äî applies to alloys with non-interacting constituents. - **Key Threshold Values:** Steel ‚Üî Cast Iron at **2% C**; Stainless Steel at **Cr ‚â• 10.5%**; Hume-Rothery size limit at **15%**. - **Manufacturing Methods:** Cast alloys (cast iron, die-cast Zn/Al) vs Wrought alloys (rolled, forged, extruded ‚Äî most steels, brass, aluminum). - **Application Snapshots:** Aerospace ‚Üí Ti-6Al-4V; Marine ‚Üí Cu-Ni / SS 316; Biomedical ‚Üí Co-Cr-Mo / CP-Ti; Electronics ‚Üí Sn-Ag-Cu solder. > **Final Exam Mantra:** *"Classify by Fe content first, then by C content, then by microstructure, and finally by phase diagram behavior."*

topics.title: Hume-Rothery rules
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAUT304 - MATERIAL SCIENCE AND METALLURGY
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Hume-Rothery rules

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!NOTE]
> **Hume-Rothery Rules** are a set of empirical conditions formulated by **William Hume-Rothery (1934)** that govern the formation and extent of **substitutional solid solutions** in metallic alloy systems. They predict whether two metals will form a primary substitutional solid solution and estimate the maximum solid solubility based on atomic, crystallographic, and electronic factors.

A **substitutional solid solution** is a single-phase crystalline alloy where atoms of the *solute* element replace (substitute) atoms of the *solvent* element at random lattice sites, while preserving the original crystal structure of the solvent.

## 1.2 The Five Cardinal Conditions

For extensive substitutional solid solubility between two metals (A = solvent, B = solute), **all five** Hume-Rothery conditions should be satisfied:

| # | Rule | Quantitative Limit |
|---|------|--------------------|
| 1 | **Atomic Size Factor** | $\vert \dfrac{r_A - r_B}{r_A} \vert \times 100\% \le \mathbf{15\%}$ |
| 2 | **Crystal Structure** | Identical room-temperature structure (FCC, BCC, or HCP) |
| 3 | **Valence** | Same valence (or lower-valence solute dissolves more in higher-valence solvent) |
| 4 | **Electronegativity** | Electronegativity difference $\le \mathbf{0.2}$ to $\mathbf{0.4}$ on Pauling scale |
| 5 | **Electron Concentration (e/a)** | Favorable valence electron-to-atom ratio for the phase stability |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy ‚Äî The "Tea-Sugar" Model

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine making a sugar solution in tea.
> - If the sugar grains (solute atoms) are **roughly the same size** as water molecules ‚Üí they mix smoothly (size rule).
> - If sugar grains are **frozen ice cubes** (different "crystal form") ‚Üí they don't mix well (structure rule).
> - If sugar has **opposite electric charge** to water ‚Üí they repel and form a new compound, not a solution (electronegativity rule).
> - If you add **too much sugar** for the available "electron-to-water ratio" ‚Üí excess sugar remains undissolved (electron concentration rule).
> - **Valence** is like chemical "compatibility" ‚Äî same charge type mixes better.

In metallurgy, "tea" = the solvent metal lattice; "sugar" = solute atoms. The five rules determine how much "sugar" the "tea" can dissolve before saturation.

## 1.4 Why These Rules Matter in Engineering

The Hume-Rothery rules are foundational in:
- **Alloy design** for aerospace (Al-Cu, Ni-Cr, Ti-Al systems)
- **Brass and bronze metallurgy** (Cu-Zn, Cu-Sn)
- **Solder and bearing alloys** (Pb-Sn, Ag-Cd)
- **Semiconductor dopant selection** (analogous principles)
- **Predicting intermetallic compound formation** vs. extended solid solubility
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Discussion of Each Rule

### üîπ Rule 1 ‚Äî Atomic Size Factor (Radius Ratio Rule)

**Physical Reasoning:** When a solute atom of a different size replaces a solvent atom, the surrounding lattice is **elastically strained** (compressed or stretched). If the strain energy exceeds a critical threshold, the lattice becomes unstable and a new phase (intermetallic compound) nucleates instead.

- If size difference $\le \mathbf{15\%}$ ‚Üí extensive solid solubility is possible.
- If size difference $\mathbf{>15\%}$ ‚Üí solubility is severely restricted; intermediate phases form.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** The "15% rule" is empirical, derived from experimental phase diagrams of binary metal systems. Systems like **Cu-Ni (FCC-FCC, $\Delta r \approx 2\%$)** show complete solid solubility in all proportions. In contrast, **Cu-Zn ($\Delta r \approx 7\%$, but valence differs)** shows only limited Œ±-phase solubility.

**Mathematical Statement:**

$$
\Delta r \,(\%) = \frac{\vert r_{\text{solvent}} - r_{\text{solute}} \vert}{r_{\text{solvent}}} \times 100\%
$$

where $r$ denotes the atomic radius for **CN = 12 (coordination number 12)** as the reference state.

### üîπ Rule 2 ‚Äî Crystal Structure Rule

**Physical Reasoning:** Two metals with **identical crystal structures** have matching atomic packing patterns. Substitution is then geometrically simple. Mismatched structures lead to:
- Different slip systems
- Different lattice parameters
- Incompatibility of atomic positions

**Example Pairs with Identical Structure ‚Üí Complete Solubility:**
- **Cu (FCC) ‚Äì Ni (FCC)** ‚Üí isomorphous
- **Mo (BCC) ‚Äì W (BCC)** ‚Üí isomorphous
- **Mg (HCP) ‚Äì Cd (HCP)** ‚Üí partial (limited by size)

### üîπ Rule 3 ‚Äî Valence Rule (Relative Valence Effect)

**Physical Reasoning:** The number of conduction (valence) electrons per atom determines the Fermi surface geometry and Brillouin zone interactions. A **higher-valence solute** added to a **lower-valence solvent** is less soluble than the reverse.

- **Rule of thumb:** *A metal of higher valence can dissolve more of a lower-valence metal than vice versa.*
- Example: In the **Cu-Zn system**, Zn (valence 2) dissolves up to ~38% in Cu (valence 1), but Cu dissolves only ~2% in Zn.

### üîπ Rule 4 ‚Äî Electronegativity Rule

**Physical Reasoning:** If the electronegativity difference is large, electrons transfer from the less electronegative to the more electronegative atom, forming **ionic or covalent character** ‚Üí leading to **intermetallic compounds** rather than metallic solid solutions.

$$
\Delta \chi = \vert \chi_A - \chi_B \vert
$$

- For $\Delta\chi \le \mathbf{0.2}$ ‚Üí solid solution likely
- For $\mathbf{0.2 < \Delta\chi < 0.4}$ ‚Üí partial solubility
- For $\Delta\chi > \mathbf{0.4}$ ‚Üí compound formation favored

**Example:** Mg ($\chi = 1.31$) and Si ($\chi = 1.90$, $\Delta\chi = 0.59$) form **Mg‚ÇÇSi** (an intermetallic), not a solid solution.

### üîπ Rule 5 ‚Äî Electron Concentration Rule (Hume-Rothery Phases)

**Physical Reasoning:** The stability of certain intermetallic phases (called **Hume-Rothery electron compounds**) depends primarily on the **electron-to-atom ratio (e/a)**, not on the specific elements. When the Fermi surface approaches a Brillouin zone boundary, the energy is minimized and the phase becomes stable.

The famous **Hume-Rothery phases** are:

| Phase | Crystal Structure | Critical e/a Ratio | Example Compound |
|-------|-------------------|-------------------|------------------|
| **Œ±** (primary solid solution) | FCC | $\le 1.36$ | Cu in Œ±-brass (Cu-Zn) |
| **Œ≤** | BCC (CsCl-type) | $\mathbf{3/2 = 1.50}$ | CuZn, AgCd, Cu‚ÇÉAl |
| **Œ≥** | Complex cubic (52 atoms/cell) | $\mathbf{21/13 \approx 1.615}$ | Cu‚ÇÖZn‚Çà, Ag‚ÇÖCd‚Çà |
| **Œµ** | HCP (close-packed) | $\mathbf{7/4 = 1.75}$ | CuZn‚ÇÉ, Ag‚ÇÖAl‚ÇÉ |

> [!NOTE]
> The electron concentration (e/a) is calculated as:
> $$\dfrac{e}{a} = \dfrac{x_A \cdot V_A + x_B \cdot V_B}{x_A + x_B}$$
> where $V$ is the valence (number of conduction electrons per atom) and $x$ is the atomic fraction.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units | Application |
|---|-------------------|-----------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | Atomic size mismatch | $\Delta r = \frac{\vert r_A - r_B \vert}{r_A} \times 100$ | % | Predict solubility extent |
| 2 | Size rule limit | $\Delta r \le 15\%$ | % | Rule-of-thumb cutoff |
| 3 | Electron concentration | $\frac{e}{a} = \sum x_i V_i$ | electrons/atom | Hume-Rothery phase ID |
| 4 | Œ±-phase (FCC) limit | $\frac{e}{a} \le 1.36$ | electrons/atom | Maximum solid solubility |
| 5 | Œ≤-phase stability | $\frac{e}{a} = 1.50$ | electrons/atom | BCC electron compound |
| 6 | Œ≥-phase stability | $\frac{e}{a} \approx 1.615$ | electrons/atom | Œ≥-brass structure |
| 7 | Œµ-phase stability | $\frac{e}{a} = 1.75$ | electrons/atom | HCP electron compound |
| 8 | Electronegativity limit | $\Delta\chi \le 0.2$ | Pauling units | SS vs. compound |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Brass (Cu-Zn):** Designing Œ±, Œ≤, Œ≤' phase microstructures for machining vs. cold-working.
- **Aluminum alloys:** Choosing alloying elements (Mg, Si, Cu) to maximize solid solution strengthening.
- **Solder design:** Sn-Pb, Sn-Ag-Cu lead-free solders obey Hume-Rothery constraints.
- **High-entropy alloys (HEAs):** Modern design uses the inverse principle ‚Äî large $\Delta r$ creates distortion-induced strengthening.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Verification of 15% Size Rule ‚Äî Cu-Zn System

**Given Data:**
- $r_{\text{Cu (FCC, CN=12)}} = 1.278$ √Ö
- $r_{\text{Zn (HCP, CN=12)}} = 1.394$ √Ö (CN-12 corrected)
- Solvent = Cu, Solute = Zn

**Step 1:** Compute absolute size difference.

$$
\Delta r_{\text{abs}} = \vert r_{\text{Cu}} - r_{\text{Zn}} \vert = \vert 1.278 - 1.394 \vert = 0.116 \; \text{√Ö}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute percentage size difference (relative to solvent Cu).

$$
\Delta r \,(\%) = \frac{\Delta r_{\text{abs}}}{r_{\text{solvent}}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\Delta r \,(\%) = \frac{0.116}{1.278} \times 100\%
$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate numerically.

$$
\Delta r \,(\%) = 0.0908 \times 100\% = 9.08\%
$$

**Step 4:** Compare to the 15% threshold.

$$
9.08\% \;\le\; 15\% \quad \checkmark
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** The Cu-Zn system satisfies the size rule (Œîr ‚âà 9%), so significant solid solubility is geometrically permitted. The experimentally observed solubility of Zn in Cu (~38 at%) is consistent with this size-rule allowance.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Maximum Solubility of Zn in Cu via Electron Concentration Rule

**Given Data:**
- Valence of Cu: $V_{\text{Cu}} = 1$ (group IB)
- Valence of Zn: $V_{\text{Zn}} = 2$ (group IIB)
- Solvent = Cu (FCC matrix, Œ±-phase)
- Critical e/a for Œ±-phase (FCC) = **1.36**

**Step 1:** Set up the electron concentration equation. Let $x$ = atomic fraction of Zn; $(1-x)$ = atomic fraction of Cu.

$$
\frac{e}{a} = (1 - x) \cdot V_{\text{Cu}} + x \cdot V_{\text{Zn}}
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute valence values.

$$
\frac{e}{a} = (1 - x)(1) + x(2) = 1 - x + 2x = 1 + x
$$

**Step 3:** Apply the Œ±-phase (FCC) limit $\frac{e}{a} = 1.36$ to find the solubility boundary.

$$
1 + x = 1.36
$$

**Step 4:** Solve for $x$.

$$
x = 1.36 - 1.00 = 0.36
$$

**Step 5:** Convert to atomic percent.

$$
x = 0.36 \times 100\% = 36\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** The electron concentration rule predicts a **maximum solid solubility of ~36 at% Zn in Cu**, which closely matches the experimental phase diagram value of ~38.5 at% Zn at 450 ¬∞C and ~32.5 at% at room temperature. The small discrepancy arises from temperature-dependent entropy and strain effects.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Identification of the Œ≤-Phase in Ag-Cd System

**Given Data:**
- Valence of Ag: $V_{\text{Ag}} = 1$
- Valence of Cd: $V_{\text{Cd}} = 2$
- Observed Œ≤-phase stoichiometry: **AgCd (50 at% Cd)**
- Critical e/a for Œ≤-phase (BCC) = **1.50**

**Step 1:** Compute the e/a ratio for 50 at% Cd.

$$
\frac{e}{a} = 0.5 \times V_{\text{Ag}} + 0.5 \times V_{\text{Cd}} = 0.5(1) + 0.5(2) = 0.5 + 1.0 = 1.5
$$

**Step 2:** Compare with Œ≤-phase theoretical value.

$$
\frac{e}{a} = 1.50 \quad \text{(calculated)} \quad \text{vs.} \quad \frac{e}{a} = 1.50 \quad \text{(theoretical)} \quad \checkmark
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** The Œ≤-phase in the Ag-Cd system forms exactly at the Hume-Rothery electron concentration 3/2, confirming the universality of the e/a rule across different alloy systems.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Œ≥-Phase Identification in Cu-Zn System

**Given Data:**
- Œ≥-phase compound: **Cu‚ÇÖZn‚Çà**
- Total atoms per formula unit: $5 + 8 = 13$
- $V_{\text{Cu}} = 1$, $V_{\text{Zn}} = 2$

**Step 1:** Compute total valence electrons per formula unit.

$$
e_{\text{total}} = 5 \times V_{\text{Cu}} + 8 \times V_{\text{Zn}} = 5(1) + 8(2) = 5 + 16 = 21
$$

**Step 2:** Compute electron concentration.

$$
\frac{e}{a} = \frac{e_{\text{total}}}{a_{\text{total}}} = \frac{21}{13} \approx 1.615
$$

**Step 3:** Match with theoretical Œ≥-phase value.

$$
\frac{e}{a} = 1.615 \quad \text{(calculated)} \quad \text{vs.} \quad \frac{e}{a} \approx 1.615 \quad \text{(theoretical Œ≥-phase)} \quad \checkmark
$$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix ‚Äî Compliance of Real Alloy Systems

| Alloy System | Œîr (%) | Crystal Structure (A / B) | Œîœá (Pauling) | Valence Effect | e/a Behavior | Predicted Solubility |
|--------------|--------|----------------------------|--------------|----------------|--------------|----------------------|
| **Cu ‚Äì Ni** | 2.5 | FCC / FCC | 0.04 | Same (1) | ‚Äî | **Complete (100%)** |
| **Cu ‚Äì Zn** | 9.1 | FCC / HCP | 0.07 | Zn higher | Limits Œ± to ~36% | **Limited Œ±** |
| **Ag ‚Äì Au** | 0.2 | FCC / FCC | 0.04 | Same (1) | ‚Äî | **Complete** |
| **Ag ‚Äì Cd** | 5.0 | FCC / HCP | 0.04 | Cd higher | Œ≤ at e/a = 1.5 | **Limited + Œ≤ compound** |
| **Cu ‚Äì Sn** | 14.3 | FCC / BCT | 0.10 | Sn higher | Multiple compounds | **Limited + intermetallics** |
| **Mg ‚Äì Si** | 9.7 | HCP / Diamond-cubic | 0.59 | Different | ‚Äî | **Compound (Mg‚ÇÇSi) only** |
| **Fe ‚Äì Cr** | 0.3 | BCC / BCC | 0.18 | Same-ish | ‚Äî | **Complete at high T** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Observation:** The Cu-Ni system is a textbook example where all five Hume-Rothery conditions are satisfied ‚Üí complete solid solubility (isomorphous). In contrast, Mg-Si violates the electronegativity and crystal structure rules ‚Üí forms an intermetallic, not a solid solution.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram ‚Äî Hume-Rothery Rules Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Start: Two Metals A and B] --> rule1{Rule 1: Size factor less or equal 15 percent}
    rule1 -->|No| failA[Reject: Insignificant solubility]
    rule1 -->|Yes| rule2{Rule 2: Same crystal structure at room temperature}
    rule2 -->|No| limitA[Limited solubility expected]
    rule2 -->|Yes| rule3{Rule 3: Same valence OR favorable relative valence}
    rule3 -->|No| limitB[Limited solubility]
    rule3 -->|Yes| rule4{Rule 4: Electronegativity difference less or equal 0.2}
    rule4 -->|No| compA[Tendency to form intermetallic compound]
    rule4 -->|Yes| rule5{Rule 5: Favorable electron to atom ratio}
    rule5 -->|No| limitC[Secondary phase forms at given e per a]
    rule5 -->|Yes| passA[Pass: Extensive substitutional solid solubility predicted]
    failA --> endA[End]
    limitA --> endA
    limitB --> endA
    compA --> endA
    limitC --> endA
    passA --> endA
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram ‚Äî Electron Concentration and Hume-Rothery Phases

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    eaLow[e per a less or equal 1.36] --> phaseA[Alpha Phase: FCC Primary Solid Solution]
    eaMid1[e per a equals 1.50] --> phaseB[Beta Phase: BCC Electron Compound]
    eaMid2[e per a equals 1.615] --> phaseC[Gamma Phase: Complex Cubic 52 atoms per cell]
    eaHigh[e per a equals 1.75] --> phaseD[Epsilon Phase: HCP Electron Compound]
    phaseA --> exA1[Example: Alpha brass Cu up to 36 at percent Zn]
    phaseB --> exB1[Example: CuZn, AgCd, Cu3Al]
    phaseC --> exC1[Example: Cu5Zn8, Ag5Cd8]
    phaseD --> exD1[Example: CuZn3, Ag5Al3]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram ‚Äî Substitutional Solid Solution Formation Block

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    solventLattice[Solvent Lattice: Crystal of A atoms] --> site1[Lattice Site 1]
    solventLattice --> site2[Lattice Site 2]
    solventLattice --> site3[Lattice Site 3]
    site1 --> solA1[Solute B atom of similar size]
    site2 --> solA2[Solute B atom of similar size]
    site3 --> solA3[Solute B atom of similar size]
    solA1 --> sss[Random Substitutional Solid Solution Phase]
    solA2 --> sss
    solA3 --> sss
    sss --> resultA[Preserves solvent crystal structure]
    sss --> resultB[Solute may induce local lattice strain]
    sss --> resultC[Beyond solubility limit: secondary phase nucleates]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## üìò PART A ‚Äî Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam ‚Äì Dec 2023]`
**State the five Hume-Rothery rules for substitutional solid solubility.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The five Hume-Rothery rules governing substitutional solid solubility are:

1. **Atomic Size Rule:** The atomic radii of solute and solvent must differ by **not more than 15%**.
2. **Crystal Structure Rule:** The two metals must have the **same crystal structure** (both FCC, both BCC, or both HCP) for extensive solubility.
3. **Valence Rule:** The two metals should have the **same valence**; otherwise, a higher-valence metal dissolves more of a lower-valence one than vice versa.
4. **Electronegativity Rule:** The electronegativity difference must be **small (‚â§ 0.2 on Pauling scale)**; otherwise, an intermetallic compound forms.
5. **Electron Concentration Rule:** The **valence electron-to-atom ratio (e/a)** must lie within the stability range of the desired phase (e.g., ‚â§ 1.36 for FCC Œ±-phase).

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam ‚Äì May 2024]`
**Explain the significance of the "15% size difference rule" with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **15% rule** is an empirical threshold stating that if the difference in atomic radii of two metals exceeds 15% of the solvent radius, the lattice strain energy becomes so large that the solid solution becomes unstable and a new phase (intermetallic compound) forms instead.

**Example:** In the **Cu-Zn system** (brass), the atomic radius difference is only about 9%, so zinc dissolves extensively in copper (up to ~38 at%) to form Œ±-brass. In contrast, **Cu-Pb** has a radius mismatch of ~36%, exceeding the 15% limit; hence, lead is virtually insoluble in copper, and the system shows a eutectic with negligible terminal solid solubility.

---

## üìó PART B ‚Äî Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Common pitfalls students commit in Hume-Rothery questions:
> 1. **Confusing absolute and relative radius differences** ‚Äî always express as percentage of solvent radius.
> 2. **Forgetting to apply CN=12 correction** to atomic radii before comparison.
> 3. **Mixing up the e/a ratios for the four Hume-Rothery phases** ‚Äî memorize 1.36, 1.50, 1.615, 1.75.
> 4. **Ignoring the relative valence effect** ‚Äî the *direction* of valence mismatch matters.
> 5. **Not showing the calculation steps** in numerical problems ‚Äî partial marks are awarded per logical step.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam ‚Äì May 2023, Module 4]`

**(a)** [7 marks] Explain the role of the **size factor, crystal structure, and electronegativity** rules in determining the solid solubility of binary metallic alloys, with at least **two examples** for each rule.

**(b)** [7 marks] The atomic radius of Cu is **1.278 √Ö** and that of Zn is **1.394 √Ö** (CN-12 corrected). Calculate the percentage size difference and verify whether Zn can dissolve in Cu based on the 15% rule. Also determine the **maximum solubility of Zn in Cu** (FCC Œ±-phase) using the electron concentration rule, given $V_{\text{Cu}} = 1$ and $V_{\text{Zn}} = 2$.

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) ‚Äî 7 marks:**

**1. Size Factor Rule (2 marks):**
- Solute atom must fit into the solvent lattice with **lattice strain below a critical value**.
- Empirical limit: $\Delta r \le 15\%$.
- *Example 1:* Cu-Ni system ‚Äî $\Delta r \approx 2.5\%$ ‚Üí complete solid solubility.
- *Example 2:* Cu-Pb system ‚Äî $\Delta r \approx 36\%$ ‚Üí negligible solubility.

**2. Crystal Structure Rule (2 marks):**
- Identical crystal structures ensure **geometric compatibility** of atomic sites.
- *Example 1:* Cu (FCC) and Ni (FCC) form continuous solid solutions.
- *Example 2:* Mo (BCC) and W (BCC) are completely miscible.

**3. Electronegativity Rule (3 marks):**
- If $\Delta\chi$ is large, electrons localize, forming **ionic/covalent character** ‚Üí intermetallic compounds, not metallic solid solutions.
- *Example 1:* Mg ($\chi = 1.31$) and Si ($\chi = 1.90$, $\Delta\chi = 0.59$) ‚Üí form compound **Mg‚ÇÇSi**, no SS.
- *Example 2:* Cu ($\chi = 1.90$) and Au ($\chi = 2.54$, $\Delta\chi = 0.64$) ‚Äî wait, this is a special case; instead, **Cu-Ni** with $\Delta\chi = 0.04$ ‚Üí extensive solid solubility.

**Part (b) ‚Äî 7 marks:**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Compute the size difference.

$$
\Delta r = \vert 1.278 - 1.394 \vert = 0.116 \; \text{√Ö}
$$

$$
\Delta r \,(\%) = \frac{0.116}{1.278} \times 100\% = 9.08\%
$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Verify the 15% rule.

$$
9.08\% \le 15\% \quad \checkmark
$$

Hence, **Zn can dissolve in Cu**.

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Set up the electron concentration formula. Let $x$ = atom fraction of Zn.

$$
\frac{e}{a} = (1 - x)(1) + x(2) = 1 + x
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Apply Œ±-phase (FCC) critical value.

$$
\frac{e}{a} = 1.36 \implies 1 + x = 1.36
$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Solve.

$$
x = 0.36 = 36 \; \text{at\% Zn}
$$

**Final Answer:** The maximum solid solubility of Zn in Cu is approximately **36 at%**, in excellent agreement with the experimental value (~38.5 at% at 450 ¬∞C).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam ‚Äì Dec 2022, Module 4]`

**(a)** [7 marks] Discuss the **valence effect** and the **electron concentration rule**. Tabulate the e/a ratios for the four Hume-Rothery electron compounds (Œ±, Œ≤, Œ≥, Œµ) with one example of each.

**(b)** [7 marks] In the **Ag-Cd system**, the Œ≤-phase has the stoichiometry **AgCd (50 at% Cd, 50 at% Ag)** with a BCC (CsCl-type) structure. Verify whether this is a Hume-Rothery Œ≤-phase using the electron concentration rule, given $V_{\text{Ag}} = 1$ and $V_{\text{Cd}} = 2$. Also identify the Œ≥-phase that should form in the same system.

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) ‚Äî 7 marks:**

**Valence Effect (3 marks):**
- The **number of conduction electrons per atom** affects the Fermi energy and Brillouin zone overlap.
- **Relative valence effect:** A metal of *higher* valence can dissolve *more* of a *lower*-valence metal than the reverse.
- Example: In Cu-Zn, Zn (valence 2) dissolves ~38% in Cu (valence 1), but Cu dissolves only ~2% in Zn.

**Electron Concentration Rule (2 marks):**
- Certain intermetallic phases (Œ≤, Œ≥, Œµ) have **fixed e/a ratios** regardless of which elements form them.
- This is because the Brillouin zone is touched by the Fermi surface at a critical electron concentration, lowering total energy.

**Tabulation (2 marks):**

| Phase | Structure | e/a Ratio | Example |
|-------|-----------|-----------|---------|
| Œ± | FCC | ‚â§ 1.36 | Cu-Zn (Œ±-brass) |
| Œ≤ | BCC (CsCl) | 3/2 = 1.50 | CuZn, AgCd, Cu‚ÇÉAl |
| Œ≥ | Complex cubic (52 atoms/cell) | 21/13 ‚âà 1.615 | Cu‚ÇÖZn‚Çà, Ag‚ÇÖCd‚Çà |
| Œµ | HCP | 7/4 = 1.75 | CuZn‚ÇÉ, Ag‚ÇÖAl‚ÇÉ |

**Part (b) ‚Äî 7 marks:**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Compute e/a for 50 at% Cd, 50 at% Ag.

$$
\frac{e}{a} = 0.5 \times 1 + 0.5 \times 2 = 0.5 + 1.0 = 1.50
$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Compare with the theoretical Œ≤-phase value.

$$
\frac{e}{a} = 1.50 \quad \text{(calculated)} = 1.50 \quad \text{(theoretical Œ≤-phase)} \quad \checkmark
$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Conclude.

The compound **AgCd** is confirmed as a **Hume-Rothery Œ≤-phase electron compound**.

**Step 4 (3 marks):** Identify the Œ≥-phase.

For the Œ≥-phase, the critical e/a is 21/13 ‚âà 1.615.

Let $x$ = atomic fraction of Cd.

$$
\frac{e}{a} = (1 - x)(1) + x(2) = 1 + x
$$

Set equal to 1.615:

$$
1 + x = 1.615 \implies x = 0.615 \approx 61.5 \; \text{at\% Cd}
$$

This corresponds to the stoichiometry **Ag‚ÇÖCd‚Çà** (5 Ag : 8 Cd ‚Üí 5/13 = 38.5% Ag, 61.5% Cd), which is the known Œ≥-phase in the Ag-Cd system.

**Verification:**

$$
\frac{e}{a} = \frac{5(1) + 8(2)}{13} = \frac{21}{13} \approx 1.615 \quad \checkmark
$$

---

## üìå Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quick-Fire Revision ‚Äî Read this the night before the exam:**

- ‚úÖ **5 Hume-Rothery rules:** Size ‚â§ 15%, same crystal structure, same valence, Œîœá ‚â§ 0.2, favorable e/a.
- ‚úÖ **Always use CN-12 atomic radii** when comparing sizes.
- ‚úÖ **Relative valence effect:** Higher-valence solute is **less** soluble than lower-valence solute in the same solvent.
- ‚úÖ **Electron concentration ratios (memorize!):** Œ± ‚â§ 1.36, Œ≤ = 1.50, Œ≥ = 21/13 ‚âà 1.615, Œµ = 7/4 = 1.75.
- ‚úÖ **e/a formula:** $\frac{e}{a} = \sum (x_i \cdot V_i)$ where $x_i$ = atomic fraction, $V_i$ = valence.
- ‚úÖ **Cu-Ni is the prototype** of a Hume-Rothery "ideal" system ‚Äî all rules satisfied ‚Üí complete solubility.
- ‚úÖ **Cu-Zn Œ±-phase solubility** = ~36 at% (predicted) vs. ~38.5 at% (experimental) ‚Äî using e/a = 1.36.
- ‚úÖ **Œ≤-brass CuZn** has e/a = 1.5 ‚Üí BCC.
- ‚úÖ **Œ≥-brass Cu‚ÇÖZn‚Çà** has e/a = 21/13 ‚Üí complex cubic with 52 atoms/cell.
- ‚úÖ **Large Œîœá (>0.4)** ‚Üí intermetallic compound favored (e.g., Mg‚ÇÇSi).
- ‚úÖ **Engines of these rules** are strain energy, Fermi surface‚ÄìBrillouin zone interactions, and chemical bonding character.
- ‚úÖ **Valence electrons** = group number for s-block + p-block metals, or more carefully: **conduction electrons per atom** (Cu = 1, Zn = 2, Al = 3, Si = 4, Fe = variable, etc.).
- ‚úÖ **Application tip:** For a numerical problem, always state the **rule being applied**, the **formula used**, the **substitution of values**, and the **conclusion** ‚Äî KTU examiners award step-marks generously.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Equilibrium diagrams of common binary systems (five types)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAUT304 - MATERIAL SCIENCE AND METALLURGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Equilibrium diagrams of common binary systems (five types)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **equilibrium phase diagram** (also called a **constitution diagram** or **binary phase diagram**) is a graphical representation that depicts the stable phases, their compositions, and the relative amounts of each phase present in a metallic alloy system at various temperatures under conditions of **thermodynamic equilibrium** (i.e., very slow cooling rates that allow complete diffusion).

For a binary system consisting of components **A** and **B**, the diagram is plotted on a 2D graph where:
- The **X-axis** represents the **chemical composition** (typically weight percent of component B, ranging from 0% to 100%).
- The **Y-axis** represents the **temperature** (in ¬∞C or K).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mandate:** You must be able to draw, label, and interpret the **five fundamental types** of binary equilibrium diagrams, namely:
> 1. **Isomorphous System** (Complete Solid Solubility) ‚Äì e.g., Cu‚ÄìNi
> 2. **Eutectic System** (Limited Solid Solubility) ‚Äì e.g., Pb‚ÄìSn
> 3. **Peritectic System** ‚Äì e.g., Cu‚ÄìZn, Pt‚ÄìAg
> 4. **Eutectoid System** ‚Äì e.g., Fe‚ÄìC (steel portion), Cu‚ÄìSn
> 5. **Peritectoid System** ‚Äì e.g., Cu‚ÄìSn, Ni‚ÄìZn

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are mixing two colors of paint, **Yellow (A)** and **Blue (B)**, in varying proportions. If you mix them in the **liquid state** (above the melting point), they form a single uniform green liquid ‚Äî this is analogous to a **single liquid phase (L)**. As this liquid cools, at some temperature, it begins to **freeze** into a solid. Depending on how well the two atoms "like" each other, the solid may:
- Stay as a **single uniform solid** (like mixing all colors into one ‚Äî Isomorphous).
- **Reject** one of the colors and split into **two different solids** (Eutectic).
- React in **special proportions** to form a new compound (Peritectic/Eutectoid).

The phase diagram is simply a **"map"** of what solid/liquid mixture exists at each (composition, temperature) coordinate, drawn under perfectly slow, equilibrium cooling.

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Invariant Reactions** form the backbone of these five diagrams:
> - **Eutectic:** Liquid ‚Üí Solid‚ÇÅ + Solid‚ÇÇ (on cooling)
> - **Peritectic:** Liquid + Solid‚ÇÅ ‚Üí Solid‚ÇÇ (on cooling)
> - **Eutectoid:** Solid‚ÇÅ ‚Üí Solid‚ÇÇ + Solid‚ÇÉ (on cooling)
> - **Peritectoid:** Solid‚ÇÅ + Solid‚ÇÇ ‚Üí Solid‚ÇÉ (on cooling)

## 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in Phase Diagrams

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Composition (weight %) | $C$ or $w$ | **wt%** |
| Composition (atomic %) | $X$ or $C_a$ | **at%** |
| Temperature | $T$ | **¬∞C** or **K** |
| Melting point of pure A | $T_m^A$ | ¬∞C |
| Melting point of pure B | $T_m^B$ | ¬∞C |
| Eutectic temperature | $T_E$ | ¬∞C |
| Eutectic composition | $C_E$ | wt% |
| Solubility limit | $C_s$ | wt% |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Types ‚Äî Structural Logic

### **Type 1: Isomorphous System (Complete Solid Solubility)**

**Definition:** A binary system in which the two components are **completely miscible in the solid state** in all proportions, forming a **single solid solution phase (Œ±)** across the entire composition range.

**Classic Example:** **Copper‚ÄìNickel (Cu‚ÄìNi)**

**Key Features (from KTU Board pattern):**
- Two single-phase regions: **Liquid (L)** and **Solid Solution (Œ±)**.
- One **two-phase region (L + Œ±)** shaped like a "lens" or "lobe" bounded by the **liquidus** (upper) and **solidus** (lower) curves.
- The liquidus and solidus meet at the pure component melting points: $T_m^{Cu} = 1085¬∞C$ and $T_m^{Ni} = 1455¬∞C$.
- **No invariant reaction** occurs; solidification occurs over a temperature range.

**Tie-Line / Lever Rule (Crucial for KTU Problems):**

For an alloy of overall composition $C_0$ at temperature $T_1$ lying in the $(L + \alpha)$ region:
- Let $C_L$ = composition of the liquid phase (read from liquidus at $T_1$).
- Let $C_\alpha$ = composition of the solid Œ± phase (read from solidus at $T_1$).

**Fraction of Solid (Œ±):**
$$W_\alpha = \frac{C_0 - C_L}{C_\alpha - C_L}$$

**Fraction of Liquid (L):**
$$W_L = \frac{C_\alpha - C_0}{C_\alpha - C_L}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lever Rule Mnemonic:** The fraction of the phase is proportional to the length of the **opposite arm** of the tie-line. The fulcrum is at the alloy composition $C_0$.

---

### **Type 2: Eutectic System (Limited Solid Solubility)**

**Definition:** A system where the two components have **limited mutual solid solubility**, and the liquid transforms into **two distinct solid phases simultaneously** at a specific temperature and composition.

**Classic Example:** **Lead‚ÄìTin (Pb‚ÄìSn)** ‚Äî the textbook system for solder alloys.

**Key Features:**
- Two terminal solid solutions: **Œ±** (Sn-rich) and **Œ≤** (Pb-rich).
- Maximum solubilities: $C_\alpha^{max}$ at the **eutectic temperature** $T_E$ on the Œ± side, and $C_\beta^{max}$ on the Œ≤ side.
- **Eutectic Point E** at coordinates $(C_E, T_E)$ where: $\text{Liquid}(C_E) \xrightarrow{\text{cooling}} \alpha(C_\alpha^{max}) + \beta(C_\beta^{max})$
- Three two-phase regions: (L + Œ±), (L + Œ≤), and (Œ± + Œ≤).
- Below $T_E$, the solid solubility **decreases** with temperature (solvus lines slope downward).

**Numerical Values for Pb‚ÄìSn (must memorize for KTU):**
- $T_E = 183¬∞C$
- $C_E = 61.9 \text{ wt% Sn}$
- $C_\alpha^{max} = 19.2 \text{ wt% Sn}$ (at $183¬∞C$)
- $C_\beta^{max} = 2.5 \text{ wt% Sn}$ (at $183¬∞C$)

---

### **Type 3: Peritectic System**

**Definition:** A system in which, at a specific **peritectic temperature** $T_P$, a **liquid phase reacts with a previously formed solid phase** to produce a **new solid phase**.

**Classic Example:** **Copper‚ÄìZinc (Cu‚ÄìZn)** in the region near 60‚Äì70 wt% Cu, and **Platinum‚ÄìSilver (Pt‚ÄìAg)**.

**Peritectic Reaction (on cooling):**
$$L(C_P) + \alpha(C_\alpha^P) \xrightarrow{\text{cooling at } T_P} \beta(C_\beta^P)$$

**Key Features:**
- A **peritectic point P** exists at composition $C_P$ and temperature $T_P$.
- The Œ≤ phase typically forms as a **shell** around the primary Œ± phase, and further cooling requires **solid-state diffusion** through the Œ≤ shell ‚Äî making peritectic reactions slow and often incomplete.
- The **liquidus, solidus, and solvus** all converge at point P.

---

### **Type 4: Eutectoid System**

**Definition:** A system in which, on cooling, **one solid phase transforms isothermally into two new solid phases**. (No liquid is involved.)

**Classic Example:** The **Iron‚ÄìCarbon (Fe‚ÄìFe‚ÇÉC)** system, which is the most industrially important ‚Äî governs the heat treatment of steels.

**Eutectoid Reaction (on cooling):**
$$\gamma \text{ (austenite, } C_\gamma^{eut}) \xrightarrow{\text{cooling at } T_{eut}} \alpha \text{ (ferrite)} + Fe_3C \text{ (cementite)}$$

**Key Numerical Values for Fe‚ÄìFe‚ÇÉC:**
- $T_{eut} = 727¬∞C$
- $C_\gamma^{eut} = 0.76 \text{ wt% C}$
- $C_\alpha^{eut} = 0.022 \text{ wt% C}$ (ferrite)
- $C_{Fe_3C}^{eut} = 6.70 \text{ wt% C}$ (cementite)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU frequently asks:** Explain why the eutectoid reaction in Fe‚ÄìC produces pearlite (a lamellar mixture of Œ± + Fe‚ÇÉC). The reason is that the transformation requires carbon diffusion, and lamellar structure minimizes diffusion distance.

---

### **Type 5: Peritectoid System**

**Definition:** A system in which, on cooling, **two solid phases react to form a third solid phase**.

**Peritectoid Reaction (on cooling):**
$$\alpha + \beta \xrightarrow{\text{cooling at } T_{pd}} \gamma$$

**Classic Example:** Found in **Cu‚ÄìSn** (bronze) and **Ni‚ÄìZn** systems at lower temperatures.

**Key Features:**
- Occurs entirely in the **solid state** (no liquid involved).
- Slower than peritectic reactions because solid-state diffusion is sluggish.
- Industrially less critical than eutectoid, but appears as a subtle feature in complex alloy diagrams.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Lever Rule (Solid fraction)** | $W_\alpha = \dfrac{C_0 - C_L}{C_\alpha - C_L}$ | Phase amount in two-phase region |
| 2 | **Lever Rule (Liquid fraction)** | $W_L = \dfrac{C_\alpha - C_0}{C_\alpha - C_L}$ | Phase amount in two-phase region |
| 3 | **Sum of fractions** | $W_\alpha + W_L = 1$ | Validation check |
| 4 | **Eutectic invariant** | $L(C_E) \rightleftharpoons \alpha + \beta$ at $T_E$ | Pb‚ÄìSn, Cu‚ÄìAg |
| 5 | **Peritectic invariant** | $L + \alpha \rightleftharpoons \beta$ at $T_P$ | Cu‚ÄìZn, Pt‚ÄìAg |
| 6 | **Eutectoid invariant** | $\gamma \rightleftharpoons \alpha + Fe_3C$ at $727¬∞C$ | Fe‚ÄìC steels |
| 7 | **Peritectoid invariant** | $\alpha + \beta \rightleftharpoons \gamma$ at $T_{pd}$ | Cu‚ÄìSn, Ni‚ÄìZn |
| 8 | **Gibbs Phase Rule** | $F = C - P + 1$ (at constant pressure) | Degrees of freedom |
| 9 | **At invariant point** | $F = 0$ ‚áí $C = P - 1$ | Three phases coexist |
| 10 | **Eutectic fraction (Pb‚ÄìSn, 40% Sn)** | $W_\alpha = \dfrac{40 - 2.5}{19.2 - 2.5}$ | Solder calculations |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Phase diagrams directly guide the design of **solders** (Pb‚ÄìSn eutectic gives 183¬∞C melting), **brasses** (Cu‚ÄìZn peritectic controls Œ±/Œ≤‚Ä≤ phases), and **steels** (Fe‚ÄìC eutectoid defines hypoeutectoid, eutectoid, and hypereutectoid grades).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Lever Rule (from Mass Balance)

**Statement:** For an alloy of overall composition $C_0$ existing in two phases of compositions $C_\alpha$ and $C_L$ at temperature $T_1$, the mass fraction of each phase is given by the lever rule.

**Proof (Step-by-Step):**

Let the total mass of the alloy be $M$. Let $M_\alpha$ and $M_L$ be the masses of the Œ± and L phases respectively.

**Step 1 ‚Äî Total mass conservation:**
$$M = M_\alpha + M_L$$

**Step 2 ‚Äî Component B mass conservation:**
The total mass of component B in the alloy must equal the sum of component B in each phase.

$$M \cdot C_0 = M_\alpha \cdot C_\alpha + M_L \cdot C_L$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Substitute $M_L = M - M_\alpha$:**
$$M \cdot C_0 = M_\alpha \cdot C_\alpha + (M - M_\alpha) \cdot C_L$$

$$M \cdot C_0 = M_\alpha \cdot C_\alpha + M \cdot C_L - M_\alpha \cdot C_L$$

**Step 4 ‚Äî Rearrange to isolate $M_\alpha$:**
$$M \cdot C_0 - M \cdot C_L = M_\alpha \cdot C_\alpha - M_\alpha \cdot C_L$$

$$M \cdot (C_0 - C_L) = M_\alpha \cdot (C_\alpha - C_L)$$

**Step 5 ‚Äî Define the mass fraction of Œ±:**
$$W_\alpha = \frac{M_\alpha}{M} = \frac{C_0 - C_L}{C_\alpha - C_L}$$

**Step 6 ‚Äî By complementarity:**
$$W_L = 1 - W_\alpha = \frac{C_\alpha - C_0}{C_\alpha - C_L}$$

$$\boxed{\;W_\alpha = \frac{C_0 - C_L}{C_\alpha - C_L} \qquad W_L = \frac{C_\alpha - C_0}{C_\alpha - C_L}\;}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Geometric interpretation:** If you draw a horizontal tie-line from the liquidus (at $C_L$) to the solidus (at $C_\alpha$), the lever rule says the fraction of each phase is proportional to the **length of the opposite arm** of the lever, with the **fulcrum at $C_0$**.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

**Problem:** A **Pb‚Äì40 wt% Sn** alloy is slowly cooled from the liquid state to a temperature just **above** the eutectic temperature (i.e., 184¬∞C).
Given: $C_E = 61.9\% \text{Sn},\; C_\alpha^{max} = 19.2\% \text{Sn},\; C_\beta^{max} = 2.5\% \text{Sn}$.

**Required:** Calculate the fraction of proeutectic Œ± and the fraction of liquid just above 183¬∞C.

**Solution:**

The alloy composition $C_0 = 40\%$ Sn lies between $C_\alpha^{max} = 19.2\%$ and $C_E = 61.9\%$, so at 184¬∞C it is in the **two-phase (L + Œ±)** region.

At $T = 184¬∞C$ (just above eutectic):
- $C_L \approx C_E = 61.9\% \text{ Sn}$
- $C_\alpha \approx C_\alpha^{max} = 19.2\% \text{ Sn}$

**Fraction of Œ± (primary solid):**
$$W_\alpha = \frac{C_0 - C_L}{C_\alpha - C_L} = \frac{40 - 61.9}{19.2 - 61.9} = \frac{-21.9}{-42.7}$$

$$W_\alpha = 0.5128 = 51.28\%$$

**Fraction of Liquid:**
$$W_L = 1 - 0.5128 = 0.4872 = 48.72\%$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Marking Trick:** Always state the **units** of the answer (mass fraction or wt%) and clearly indicate **which phase** the lever arm refers to. Examiners award 1 mark specifically for correct phase identification.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix ‚Äî Comparing the Five Systems

| Property | Isomorphous | Eutectic | Peritectic | Eutectoid | Peritectoid |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Solid solubility** | Complete | Limited | Limited | Limited | Limited |
| **Phases involved** | L, Œ± | L, Œ±, Œ≤ | L, Œ±, Œ≤ | Œ±, Œ≥, Fe‚ÇÉC | Œ±, Œ≤, Œ≥ |
| **Invariant reaction** | None | L ‚Üí Œ± + Œ≤ | L + Œ± ‚Üí Œ≤ | Œ≥ ‚Üí Œ± + Fe‚ÇÉC | Œ± + Œ≤ ‚Üí Œ≥ |
| **Invariant temperature (typical)** | ‚Äî | 183¬∞C (Pb‚ÄìSn) | 598¬∞C (Cu‚ÄìZn) | 727¬∞C (Fe‚ÄìC) | ~580¬∞C (Cu‚ÄìSn) |
| **State during reaction** | ‚Äî | Liquid present | Liquid + Solid | All Solid | All Solid |
| **Diffusion requirement** | Atomic | Atomic | Atomic (slow) | Carbon (slow) | Atomic (very slow) |
| **Microstructure produced** | Uniform grains | Lamellar (in eutectic) | Encapsulated Œ≤ | Pearlite (lamellar) | Layered Œ≥ |
| **Industrial relevance** | Cu‚ÄìNi alloys | Solders | Brasses | Steels | Bronzes |

---

## 3.4 Gibbs Phase Rule Application

For a binary system (C = 2) at constant pressure:

$$F = C - P + 1 = 3 - P$$

| Number of Phases (P) | Degrees of Freedom (F) | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 2 | Area: T and C can vary independently |
| 2 | 1 | Line: For a given C, T is fixed (or vice versa) |
| 3 (invariant) | 0 | Point: Both T and C are uniquely fixed |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Short-Answer Question Pattern:** *"At the eutectic point of the Pb‚ÄìSn system, the degree of freedom is zero. Justify using the Gibbs phase rule."*
> **Answer:** At the eutectic point, three phases (L, Œ±, Œ≤) coexist in equilibrium. Substituting C = 2 and P = 3 into $F = C - P + 1$ gives $F = 2 - 3 + 1 = 0$. Hence, both the temperature ($T_E = 183¬∞C$) and composition ($C_E = 61.9\%$ Sn) are uniquely fixed ‚Äî the eutectic is an **invariant point**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram ‚Äî Architecture of the Five Binary Systems

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph A["TYPE 1: ISOMORPHOUS - Cu-Ni"]
        A1["Pure Cu Tm = 1085 C"] --> A2["Liquid L Region"]
        A2 --> A3["L plus alpha LENS Region"]
        A3 --> A4["Single alpha solid solution"]
        A4 --> A5["Pure Ni Tm = 1455 C"]
    end

    subgraph B["TYPE 2: EUTECTIC - Pb-Sn"]
        B1["Pure Pb Tm = 327 C"] --> B2["L plus alpha Region"]
        B2 --> B3{"EUTECTIC POINT E 183 C, 61.9 percent Sn"}
        B3 --> B4["L plus beta Region"]
        B4 --> B5["Pure Sn Tm = 232 C"]
        B3 --> B6["alpha plus beta Region Below Te"]
    end

    subgraph C["TYPE 3: PERITECTIC - Cu-Zn"]
        C1["Liquid L"] --> C2{"PERITECTIC POINT P L plus alpha gives beta"}
        C2 --> C3["beta phase forms shell on alpha"]
        C3 --> C4["Slow cooling - incomplete reaction"]
    end

    subgraph D["TYPE 4: EUTECTOID - Fe-C"]
        D1["gamma austenite"] --> D2{"EUTECTOID POINT 727 C, 0.76 percent C"}
        D2 --> D3["alpha ferrite"]
        D2 --> D4["Fe3C cementite"]
        D3 --> D5["PEARLITE lamellar microstructure"]
        D4 --> D5
    end

    subgraph E["TYPE 5: PERITECTOID - Cu-Sn"]
        E1["alpha phase"] --> E2{"PERITECTOID POINT alpha plus beta gives gamma"}
        E2 --> E3["gamma phase - all solid state"]
        E3 --> E4["Slow solid state diffusion"]
    end
```

## 4.2 Phase Region Identification Matrix (Schematic Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LEFT["Single Phase Regions"]
        R1["L - Liquid 100 percent L"]
        R2["alpha - Terminal Solid Solution A rich"]
        R3["beta - Terminal Solid Solution B rich"]
        R4["gamma - Intermediate Phase Austenite Eutectoid only"]
    end

    subgraph MID["Two Phase Regions"]
        T1["L plus alpha - Solidification Range"]
        T2["L plus beta - Solidification Range"]
        T3["alpha plus beta - Below Eutectic Line"]
        T4["alpha plus gamma - Eutectoid Decomposition Zone"]
    end

    subgraph RIGHT["Invariant Points"]
        I1["EUTECTIC E - L transforms to alpha and beta"]
        I2["PERITECTIC P - L reacts with alpha to form beta"]
        I3["EUTECTOID - gamma transforms to alpha and Fe3C"]
        I4["PERITECTOID - alpha reacts with beta to form gamma"]
    end

    R1 --> T1 --> R2
    R1 --> T2 --> R3
    R2 --> T3 --> R3
    R2 --> T4 --> R4
    T1 --> I1
    T1 --> I2
    T4 --> I3
    R2 --> I4
    R3 --> I4
```

## 4.3 Decision Flowchart ‚Äî Identifying the Type of Binary Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START["Examine binary phase diagram"] --> Q1{"Is there complete solid solubility across full composition range"}
    Q1 -- YES --> ISO["TYPE 1 ISOMORPHOUS Example Cu Ni"]
    Q1 -- NO --> Q2{"Does a liquid transform into two solids at one temperature"}
    Q2 -- YES --> Q3{"Is one solid phase a terminal solution on the left"}
    Q3 -- YES --> EUT["TYPE 2 EUTECTIC Example Pb Sn"]
    Q2 -- NO --> Q4{"Does liquid react with one solid to form a new solid on cooling"}
    Q4 -- YES --> PER["TYPE 3 PERITECTIC Example Cu Zn"]
    Q4 -- NO --> Q5{"Does one solid transform into two solids on cooling"}
    Q5 -- YES --> EUTD["TYPE 4 EUTECTOID Example Fe C 727 C"]
    Q5 -- NO --> Q6{"Do two solids react to form a third solid"}
    Q6 -- YES --> PERD["TYPE 5 PERITECTOID Example Cu Sn"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Drawing Tip:** When asked to "draw and label the eutectic diagram," examiners expect at least: (1) the **liquidus and solidus** on both sides, (2) the **solvus lines** showing decreasing solubility, (3) clear labeling of all **three single-phase regions** (L, Œ±, Œ≤), (4) the **three two-phase regions**, and (5) the **eutectic point E** with its coordinates.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam ‚Äì May 2023]**
**Define the term "eutectic reaction" with a suitable example. State the eutectic temperature and composition for the chosen example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **eutectic reaction** is an **invariant three-phase reaction** in which, on cooling, a **single liquid phase transforms isothermally and isocompositionally into two distinct solid phases** at a specific temperature.

$$L \xrightarrow{\text{cooling at } T_E} \alpha + \beta$$

**Example:** The **Lead‚ÄìTin (Pb‚ÄìSn)** system.
**Eutectic Temperature:** $T_E = 183¬∞C$
**Eutectic Composition:** $C_E = 61.9 \text{ wt\% Sn}$, with $C_\alpha = 19.2\% \text{Sn}$ and $C_\beta = 2.5\% \text{Sn}$ at the eutectic point.

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for reaction equation, 1 mark for values)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam ‚Äì Dec 2022]**
**Differentiate between a eutectic reaction and a eutectoid reaction.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Eutectic Reaction | Eutectoid Reaction |
|---|---|---|
| **Phases involved** | Liquid + two solids | One solid ‚Üí two solids |
| **Reaction type** | $L \rightarrow \alpha + \beta$ | $\gamma \rightarrow \alpha + \beta$ |
| **State of matter** | Liquid is present | Entirely solid-state |
| **Diffusion required** | Atomic diffusion in liquid | Solid-state diffusion (slow) |
| **Example** | Pb‚ÄìSn at 183¬∞C | Fe‚ÄìC (Œ≥ ‚Üí Œ± + Fe‚ÇÉC) at 727¬∞C |
| **Industrial significance** | Solders | Heat treatment of steels |

*(2 marks for tabular comparison with at least 4 differences, 1 mark for examples)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) ‚Äî Module Internal Choice

> **KTU 2024 Pattern Note:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, split typically as **7 + 7** across two sub-parts. You must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) ‚Äî Focus: Eutectic System + Lever Rule**

**[KTU University Exam ‚Äì July 2023]**

**(a)** Draw a **neat, fully-labeled equilibrium phase diagram of the Lead‚ÄìTin (Pb‚ÄìSn) system**. Mark all single-phase, two-phase, and three-phase regions. Indicate the eutectic point with its temperature and composition. (7 marks)

**(b)** A **Pb‚Äì30 wt% Sn alloy** is slowly cooled from the liquid state to **200¬∞C** and held at that temperature until equilibrium is achieved.
- (i) Identify the phases present at 200¬∞C.
- (ii) Calculate the **mass fraction of each phase** present at 200¬∞C.
- (iii) Determine the **mass fraction of eutectic constituent** in the microstructure when cooled to room temperature.

Given: $T_E = 183¬∞C,\; C_E = 61.9\% \text{ Sn},\; C_\alpha^{max}(183¬∞C) = 19.2\% \text{ Sn},\; C_\alpha(200¬∞C) \approx 12\% \text{ Sn}$ (approx. from solvus), $C_\beta(200¬∞C) \approx 3\% \text{ Sn}$ (approx. liquidus reading). (7 marks)

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A:**

**(a) Pb‚ÄìSn Phase Diagram (7 marks):**

The student must produce a diagram with the following labeled features:

**Single-phase regions (3):**
- **L** (Liquid) ‚Äî above the upper curve
- **Œ±** (Pb-rich solid solution) ‚Äî left dome, below the solvus
- **Œ≤** (Sn-rich solid solution) ‚Äî right dome, below the solvus

**Two-phase regions (3):**
- $(L + \alpha)$ ‚Äî between liquidus and solidus on the left
- $(L + \beta)$ ‚Äî between liquidus and solidus on the right
- $(\alpha + \beta)$ ‚Äî below the eutectic horizontal line

**Three-phase invariant point (1):**
- **Eutectic point E** at $(61.9\% \text{ Sn}, 183¬∞C)$

**Pure component melting points:**
- Pure Pb: $T_m = 327¬∞C$
- Pure Sn: $T_m = 232¬∞C$

> **Mark Distribution:** 2 marks for axes & labels, 2 marks for correct curve shapes, 2 marks for labeling all regions, 1 mark for the eutectic point coordinates.

---

**(b) Pb‚Äì30 wt% Sn Alloy (7 marks):**

**(i) Phases present at 200¬∞C (2 marks):**

Since $C_0 = 30\% \text{ Sn}$ lies between $C_\alpha(200¬∞C) = 12\%$ and $C_L(200¬∞C) \approx 45\%$, the alloy at 200¬∞C is in the **two-phase (L + Œ±) region**.

**Phases present:** Liquid (L) + Solid Œ± (Pb-rich)

---

**(ii) Mass fractions at 200¬∞C (3 marks):**

Using the lever rule with $C_0 = 30\%$, $C_L = 45\%$, $C_\alpha = 12\%$:

**Fraction of Œ±:**
$$W_\alpha = \frac{C_0 - C_L}{C_\alpha - C_L} = \frac{30 - 45}{12 - 45} = \frac{-15}{-33} = 0.4545$$

$$W_\alpha \approx 45.45\%$$

**Fraction of Liquid L:**
$$W_L = 1 - 0.4545 = 0.5455 \approx 54.55\%$$

---

**(iii) Mass fraction of eutectic constituent at room temperature (2 marks):**

At the eutectic temperature (183¬∞C), the remaining liquid of composition $C_L = C_E = 61.9\%$ transforms entirely to the eutectic mixture.

**Mass fraction of eutectic constituent = Mass fraction of liquid just above 183¬∞C:**
$$W_{eutectic} = W_L \text{ at } 183¬∞C = \frac{C_0 - C_\alpha^{max}}{C_E - C_\alpha^{max}} = \frac{30 - 19.2}{61.9 - 19.2} = \frac{10.8}{42.7} = 0.2529$$

$$\boxed{W_{eutectic} \approx 25.3\%}$$

The remaining $74.7\%$ is **proeutectic Œ±** (primary Œ± that solidified before reaching the eutectic temperature).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) ‚Äî Focus: Isomorphous + Eutectoid Systems**

**[KTU University Exam ‚Äì Dec 2023]**

**(a)** With the aid of a **neat sketch**, describe the **isomorphous phase diagram** of the **Cu‚ÄìNi system**. Explain the terms **liquidus, solidus, and tie-line**.
