# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ‚Äî Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Instructions (KTU pattern):** Answer in **80‚Äì120 words**, define/state, may include a one-line derivation or example. Each carries **3 marks**.

---

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam ‚Äî Model Question, May 2024 Pattern]
**State Gibbs' Phase Rule. Explain the significance of each term in the rule with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> Gibbs' Phase Rule is a thermodynamic criterion that relates the **degrees of freedom ($F$)** of a system at equilibrium to the **number of components ($C$)** and the **number of co-existing phases ($P$)**:
> $$F = C - P + 2$$
> The constant **"2"** represents the two external intensive variables ‚Äî **temperature ($T$)** and **pressure ($P$)**. $C$ is the minimum number of independent chemical constituents required to express the composition of every phase, and $P$ is the number of physically distinct, mechanically separable phases. **Example:** Pure water at its triple point has $C=1, P=3$, giving $F=0$ ‚Äî both $T$ and $P$ are uniquely fixed (0.01 ¬∞C, 0.006 atm). **(3 marks)**

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam ‚Äî Model Question, Dec 2023 Pattern]
**What is the Lever Rule? In which region of a binary phase diagram is it applicable?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The **Lever Rule** is a graphical-numerical technique used to determine the **relative mass (or weight) fractions of two co-existing phases** in equilibrium within a binary phase diagram. It is applicable **only in two-phase regions** (e.g., L+Œ±, L+Œ≤, Œ±+Œ≤) of a binary T‚ÄìC diagram. A horizontal **tie-line** is drawn at the temperature of interest; the overall composition acts as the **fulcrum**, and the phase fractions are inversely proportional to the lever-arm lengths:
> $$W_\alpha = \dfrac{C_\beta - C_0}{C_\beta - C_\alpha}, \qquad W_\beta = \dfrac{C_0 - C_\alpha}{C_\beta - C_\alpha}$$
> It is **not** valid in single-phase or three-phase (invariant) regions. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B ‚Äî Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module-Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part-B question carries **14 marks** split into **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Module-internal choice is mandatory.

---

### **Question A** [14 Marks]

#### (a) [7 marks] ‚Äî Derive Gibbs' Phase Rule starting from the equilibrium condition that the chemical potential of each component is equal in all co-existing phases. Hence, calculate the degrees of freedom for the eutectic point of a binary alloy system.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 ‚Äî State the rule and identify variables (2 marks)**

A heterogeneous system at equilibrium has:
- $C$ components
- $P$ co-existing phases
- Two external variables: $T$ and $P$

The number of independent intensive variables to describe each phase is $(C-1) + 2 = C+1$.

**Step 2 ‚Äî Total variables and constraints (2 marks)**

$$
\text{Total variables} = P(C+1)
$$

At equilibrium, $\mu_i^\alpha = \mu_i^\beta = \mu_i^\gamma = \ldots$ for each of the $C$ components. For each component, there are $(P-1)$ independent equalities.

$$
\text{Total constraints} = C(P-1)
$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Compute $F$ (2 marks)**

$$
F = P(C+1) - C(P-1) = PC + P - CP + C = C - P + 2
$$

**Step 4 ‚Äî Apply to eutectic point of a binary alloy (1 mark)**

For a binary eutectic: $C=2$, $P=3$ (two solids + liquid). At constant pressure (1 atm), use the condensed form:

$$
F = 2 - 3 + 1 = 0
$$

**Interpretation:** The eutectic is an **invariant point** ‚Äî both temperature and composition are uniquely fixed at constant pressure.

**(7 marks awarded)**

---

#### (b) [7 marks] ‚Äî A Cu‚Äì40 wt % Ni alloy is slowly cooled from the liquid state to 1300 ¬∞C. Using the Cu‚ÄìNi phase diagram, the solidus composition of the Œ± phase at 1300 ¬∞C is 32 wt % Ni, and the liquidus composition is 55 wt % Ni. Calculate the mass fractions of the solid Œ± and the liquid phases at this temperature using the Lever Rule.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 ‚Äî Identify the data (1 mark)**
- $C_0 = 40$ wt % Ni
- $C_\alpha = 32$ wt % Ni (solid Œ±)
- $C_L = 55$ wt % Ni (liquid)

**Step 2 ‚Äî Apply the Lever Rule (3 marks)**

$$
W_\alpha = \dfrac{C_L - C_0}{C_L - C_\alpha} = \dfrac{55 - 40}{55 - 32} = \dfrac{15}{23}
$$

$$
W_L = \dfrac{C_0 - C_\alpha}{C_L - C_\alpha} = \dfrac{40 - 32}{55 - 32} = \dfrac{8}{23}
$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Convert to percentages and verify (1 mark)**

$$
W_\alpha = \dfrac{15}{23} \approx 0.652 \quad \text{or} \quad 65.2\%
$$

$$
W_L = \dfrac{8}{23} \approx 0.348 \quad \text{or} \quad 34.8\%
$$

**Closure check:** $0.652 + 0.348 = 1.000$ ‚úì

**Step 4 ‚Äî Final statement (2 marks)**
At 1300 ¬∞C, the Cu‚Äì40 wt % Ni alloy consists of approximately **65.2 % solid Œ± phase** and **34.8 % liquid phase** by mass. The solid Œ± is richer in Cu (32 % Ni) and the liquid is richer in Ni (55 % Ni), consistent with solute partitioning during solidification.

**(7 marks awarded)**

---

### **Question B (Alternative Choice)** [14 Marks]

#### (a) [7 marks] ‚Äî State the Lever Rule and derive the expression for the mass fraction of a phase in a binary two-phase region. Discuss the physical significance of the inverse-lever relationship.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 ‚Äî Statement (1 mark)**
The Lever Rule gives the relative mass fractions of two co-existing phases ($\alpha$ and $\beta$) in a binary phase diagram at a chosen temperature, using the compositions at the two ends of a horizontal tie-line.

**Step 2 ‚Äî Derivation (4 marks)**
Using total-mass and solute-mass conservation:

$$
W_\alpha + W_\beta = 1 \quad \text{and} \quad W_\alpha C_\alpha + W_\beta C_\beta = C_0
$$

Eliminating $W_\beta$:

$$
W_\alpha = \dfrac{C_\beta - C_0}{C_\beta - C_\alpha}
$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Inverse-lever interpretation (2 marks)**
The product $W_\alpha (C_0 - C_\alpha) = W_\beta (C_\beta - C_0)$ is a **mechanical equilibrium** of a lever. The phase **compositionally closer** to the alloy has a **shorter arm** and therefore a **larger fraction**. This is the *inverse-lever* principle ‚Äî *near phase, more amount.*

**(7 marks awarded)**

---

#### (b) [7 marks] ‚Äî A binary alloy of lead and tin contains 60 wt % Sn. At 200 ¬∞C, the equilibrium phase compositions are: Œ±-phase (Pb-rich solid) contains 18 wt % Sn, and Œ≤-phase (Sn-rich solid) contains 97 wt % Sn. Determine: (i) the mass fractions of Œ± and Œ≤ phases, and (ii) the number of degrees of freedom for the system using the condensed form of Gibbs' Phase Rule. Comment on the result.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 ‚Äî Identify data (1 mark)**
- $C_0 = 60$ wt % Sn
- $C_\alpha = 18$ wt % Sn
- $C_\beta = 97$ wt % Sn

**Step 2 ‚Äî Mass fractions via Lever Rule (3 marks)**

$$
W_\alpha = \dfrac{C_\beta - C_0}{C_\beta - C_\alpha} = \dfrac{97 - 60}{97 - 18} = \dfrac{37}{79} \approx 0.468 \;\; (46.8\%)
$$

$$
W_\beta = \dfrac{C_0 - C_\alpha}{C_\beta - C_\alpha} = \dfrac{60 - 18}{97 - 18} = \dfrac{42}{79} \approx 0.532 \;\; (53.2\%)
$$

**Closure check:** $0.468 + 0.532 = 1.000$ ‚úì

**Step 3 ‚Äî Degrees of freedom (2 marks)**
For the two-phase Œ± + Œ≤ region of a binary alloy at constant pressure (Pb‚ÄìSn metallurgical system):

$$
C = 2, \quad P = 2, \quad F = C - P + 1 = 2 - 2 + 1 = 1
$$

**Step 4 ‚Äî Comment (1 mark)**
A single degree of freedom means that **at constant atmospheric pressure**, once the temperature is chosen, the compositions of Œ± and Œ≤ are **uniquely fixed by the phase diagram**. Conversely, once the temperature is fixed, the alloy composition can vary (within the two-phase field) and the mass fractions adjust automatically.

**(7 marks awarded)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks on this topic (and how to avoid it):**
>
> 1. **Confusing $C$ (components) with $P$ (phases).** A common error is to write $F = P - C + 2$ ‚Äî the sign of the middle term is **reversed**. The correct form is $F = C - P + 2$.
> 2. **Forgetting the "2" or using "1" by default.** Always check whether the problem allows $P$ to vary. If pressure is held at 1 atm (typical for alloys), use $F = C - P + 1$. For the **general** rule, the answer is $+2$.
> 3. **Swapping $C_\alpha$ and $C_\beta$ in the lever rule.** A small slip gives a negative fraction. Always assign $C_\alpha$ to the **left** (lower) boundary and $C_\beta$ to the **right** (higher) boundary.
> 4. **Applying the lever rule in a single-phase region.** It is valid **only** in two-phase fields. In a single-phase field, the fraction of that phase is trivially 1.
> 5. **Forgetting the closure check.** $W_\alpha + W_\beta = 1$ is a 1-mark sanity check. If your fractions don't sum to 1, you have made an algebra error.
> 6. **Reporting a negative $F$.** A negative value means the assumed phase assemblage is **impossible** at equilibrium ‚Äî don't proceed to "compute" it. The correct response is to state that the configuration is not realizable.
> 7. **Writing `|x|` inside a markdown table.** The vertical pipe character breaks the table syntax. In LaTeX, use `\vert` or `\mid` instead. Examiners in **digital** valuation portals may auto-fail the row.
> 8. **Not specifying the variant of Gibbs' rule.** Always state whether you are using the **general** form ($+2$) or the **condensed** form ($+1$). Marks are deducted for ambiguity.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Module 4 ‚Äî Lever Rule & Gibbs' Phase Rule: High-Density Summary**

- [ ] **Lever Rule ‚Äî applicable region:** Only in **two-phase fields** of a binary T‚ÄìC phase diagram.
- [ ] **Lever Rule ‚Äî formula pair:** $W_\alpha = \dfrac{C_\beta - C_0}{C_\beta - C_\alpha}$, $W_\beta = \dfrac{C_0 - C_\alpha}{C_\beta - C_\alpha}$.
- [ ] **Lever Rule ‚Äî closure check:** $W_\alpha + W_\beta = 1$ (always verify).
- [ ] **Lever Rule ‚Äî inverse-lever principle:** *Phase closer to alloy composition = larger mass fraction.*
- [ ] **Lever Rule ‚Äî graphical interpretation:** Tie-line endpoints are the **phase compositions**; overall composition is the **fulcrum**.
- [ ] **Lever Rule ‚Äî derivation basis:** Two linear equations ‚Äî total mass conservation + solute mass conservation.
- [ ] **Gibbs' Phase Rule ‚Äî general form:** $F = C - P + 2$.
- [ ] **Gibbs' Phase Rule ‚Äî condensed form:** $F = C - P + 1$ (constant pressure, e.g., atmospheric-pressure metallurgical systems).
- [ ] **Gibbs' Phase Rule ‚Äî "2" stands for:** Temperature + Pressure (the two external intensive variables).
- [ ] **Gibbs' Phase Rule ‚Äî physical constraint:** $F \ge 0$ (negative $F$ is forbidden).
- [ ] **Gibbs' Phase Rule ‚Äî max phases at invariant:** $P_{\max} = C + 2$ (when $F = 0$ in general form).
- [ ] **Derivation ingredients:** Variables per phase = $C + 1$; total variables = $P(C+1)$; equilibrium constraints = $C(P-1)$.
- [ ] **Eutectic point (binary, $P = 3$):** $F = 2 - 3 + 1 = 0$ at constant pressure ‚Äî **invariant** (fixed $T$ and fixed composition).
- [ ] **Eutectoid in Fe‚ÄìFe$_3$C:** $\gamma \to \alpha + \text{Fe}_3\text{C}$ at 727 ¬∞C and 0.77 wt % C ‚Äî invariant.
- [ ] **Single-phase region:** $P = 1 \Rightarrow F = C + 1$ (most general, $+2$) or $C$ (condensed, $+1$).
- [ ] **Three-phase invariant in a binary at constant $P$:** $F = 0$ ‚Äî the only variables are $P$ (held fixed) and the system is "locked."
- [ ] **Frequent KTU trap:** Students mix up the **solidus** and **liquidus** compositions when reading the Cu‚ÄìNi diagram ‚Äî always read the **solidus** at the lower curve and the **liquidus** at the upper curve.
- [ ] **Worked-result recall (Cu‚Äì40 wt % Ni at 1300 ¬∞C):** $W_\alpha \approx 65.2\%$, $W_L \approx 34.8\%$.
- [ ] **Worked-result recall (Pb‚Äì60 wt % Sn at 200 ¬∞C):** $W_\alpha \approx 46.8\%$, $W_\beta \approx 53.2\%$.
- [ ] **Frequent KTU trap:** A negative $F$ in your answer **must** be flagged as "physically impossible / phase assemblage not realizable" ‚Äî do not silently drop the sign.
- [ ] **Format tip:** Always **state the variant** of Gibbs' rule you are using in the first line of your answer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Strengthening mechanisms: cold working and hot working
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAUT304 - MATERIAL SCIENCE AND METALLURGY
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Strengthening mechanisms: cold working and hot working

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Strengthening Mechanisms ‚Äì Foundational Definition

**Strengthening mechanisms** in metallurgy refer to the deliberate, controlled processes used to enhance the **mechanical strength** and **hardness** of a metal or alloy by obstructing the motion of **dislocations** through the crystal lattice. Among the various strengthening routes (solid-solution strengthening, precipitation hardening, grain-boundary strengthening, and strain hardening), **cold working** and **hot working** are the two principal **thermomechanical processing** routes classified based on the processing temperature relative to the material's **recrystallization temperature**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Strengthening mechanisms* in metallic materials are those metallurgical techniques that intentionally increase the resistance to plastic deformation. The most industrially significant mechanisms are **strain hardening (cold working)**, **solid-solution strengthening**, **precipitation hardening**, **grain-size refinement (Hall-Petch)**, and **phase-transformation strengthening**.

---

## 1.2 Cold Working (CW) ‚Äì Technical Definition

**Cold working** is the plastic deformation of a metal carried out at a temperature **below its recrystallization temperature**, typically at room temperature. Because no significant recovery or recrystallization occurs during the process, the dislocation density rises sharply (from ~10‚Å∂ to ~10¬π¬≤ lines/cm¬≤), resulting in **strain hardening** (also called **work hardening**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cold Working = Plastic Deformation + Strain Hardening (No Recrystallization)**
> The term "cold" does **not** literally mean freezing temperature; it means *below* the recrystallization temperature $T_R$.

---

## 1.3 Hot Working (HW) ‚Äì Technical Definition

**Hot working** is the plastic deformation of a metal carried out at a temperature **above its recrystallization temperature**, where the rate of softening (dynamic recovery + dynamic recrystallization) equals or exceeds the rate of work hardening. This produces a refined, equiaxed grain structure with **no net strain hardening**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hot Working = Plastic Deformation + Dynamic Recrystallization (Steady-State Softening)**
> The metal remains soft and ductile during deformation, so large reductions in cross-section are possible without fracture.

---

## 1.4 The Conceptual Analogy ‚Äì A Bangle Maker's Craft

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy (Kerala Local Context):**
> Imagine a **traditional brass bell-metal (Kanchu Vellam) maker** in **Mannar, Kerala** hammering a glowing red-hot ingot on an anvil. The metal is **soft, ductile, and easy to shape** into intricate curves because the heat allows the crystals to constantly re-form ‚Äî this is **hot working**.
>
> Now, after the bell cools to room temperature, the craftsman places it on a **lathe** and presses a spinning steel die against its surface to emboss a floral pattern. The metal resists deformation and becomes noticeably **harder and stiffer** with each pass ‚Äî this is **cold working**. The bell now rings with a brighter, sharper tone because the surface has been **strain-hardened**.

This analogy captures the essence:
- **Hot working** ‚Üí easy shaping, large strains, **no strength gain** in the final part.
- **Cold working** ‚Üí difficult shaping, limited strains, but **significant strength gain** in the final part.

---

## 1.5 Why This Topic is High-Yield for KTU 2024

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pattern Note:**
> KTU regularly frames 14-mark questions comparing **cold vs. hot working**, asking for the **recrystallization temperature concept**, the **% cold work formula**, the **effect on properties**, and a **stress‚Äìstrain / yield-strength vs. cold-work curve** sketch. Mastering this topic is mandatory for full marks in Module 4.

---

## 1.6 Physical Constants and Reference Metrics (Bolded)

- **Recrystallization Temperature ($T_R$)** for pure metals $\approx$ **0.4 $T_m$ (in Kelvin)**, and for commercial alloys $\approx$ **0.5 $T_m$** (where $T_m$ is the absolute melting temperature in Kelvin).
- **Typical Cold-Work Range for Steels:** **5% ‚Äì 75%** reduction in area.
- **Typical Hot-Work Temperature for Steels:** **$T_R$ + 100 ¬∞C to 200 ¬∞C** above $T_R$.
- **Dislocation Density (Annealed):** $\sim \mathbf{10^6 \text{ lines/cm}^2}$.
- **Dislocation Density (Severely Cold-Worked):** $\sim \mathbf{10^{12} \text{ lines/cm}^2}$.

---

## 1.7 Cold Working vs. Hot Working ‚Äì A First-Pass Comparison

| Parameter | Cold Working | Hot Working |
|---|---|---|
| Processing Temperature | Below $T_R$ | Above $T_R$ |
| Mechanism Active | Strain Hardening | Dynamic Recrystallization |
| Resulting Strength | **Increases** | Roughly unchanged |
| Ductility | **Decreases** | Retained |
| Surface Finish | Excellent | Rough / scaled |
| Dimensional Accuracy | **High** | Low |
| Force Required | High | Low |
| Grain Refinement | Limited (elongated grains) | **Significant (equiaxed fine grains)** |
| Industrial Examples | Rolling, drawing, extrusion of wires, stamping, cold-rolled sheets | Hot-rolling, forging, hot-extrusion, hot-forging of ingots |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Strengthening Mechanisms ‚Äì Contextual Placement

Before diving into cold and hot working, the **five canonical strengthening mechanisms** in crystalline metals must be placed in proper context. Cold/hot working is fundamentally a subset of **strain hardening (mechanism 1)**, but hot working also enables **grain refinement (mechanism 3)**.

1. **Strain Hardening (Cold Working)** ‚Äì increase in dislocation density.
2. **Solid-Solution Strengthening** ‚Äì solute atoms distort the lattice.
3. **Grain-Boundary Strengthening (Hall‚ÄìPetch)** ‚Äì smaller grains ‚Üí more barriers.
4. **Precipitation Hardening (Age Hardening)** ‚Äì coherent precipitates block dislocations.
5. **Phase-Transformation Strengthening** ‚Äì martensite formation in steels.

---

## 2.2 Mechanism of Cold Working (Step-by-Step Logic)

Cold working operates through **dislocation multiplication and entanglement**. The logical chain is:

- **Step 1 ‚Äî Initial State:** The annealed metal has a low dislocation density ($\sim 10^6$ lines/cm¬≤). Grains are equiaxed.
- **Step 2 ‚Äî Dislocation Multiplication:** Under applied stress, sources such as the **Frank‚ÄìRead source** generate new dislocations. The density rises to $\sim 10^{10}$‚Äì$10^{12}$ lines/cm¬≤.
- **Step 3 ‚Äî Dislocation Interaction:** Moving dislocations encounter obstacles ‚Äî forest dislocations, solute atoms, grain boundaries ‚Äî and either pile-up, tangle, or form sessile locks (e.g., **Lomer‚ÄìCottrell locks**).
- **Step 4 ‚Äî Increased Critical Resolved Shear Stress (CRSS):** A higher stress is required to propagate further dislocations through this forest of obstacles. This is observed macroscopically as a higher **yield strength** and **ultimate tensile strength**.
- **Step 5 ‚Äî Reduced Ductility:** With limited mobile dislocations, the material cannot sustain further plastic flow, so **% elongation** and **% reduction in area** drop sharply.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:**
> This is the same reason a **copper wire becomes stiffer after repeated bending** and finally snaps. Each bend multiplies dislocations until the wire is fully embrittled.

---

## 2.3 Mechanism of Hot Working (Step-by-Step Logic)

- **Step 1 ‚Äî Heat Above $T_R$:** The metal is heated above its recrystallization temperature so that atomic mobility is high.
- **Step 2 ‚Äî Plastic Deformation:** The metal is forged, rolled, or extruded. Dislocations are generated as in cold working.
- **Step 3 ‚Äî Dynamic Recovery:** Dislocations rearrange, annihilate, and form low-energy sub-grain boundaries (cell structures).
- **Step 4 ‚Äî Dynamic Recrystallization:** New, strain-free, equiaxed grains nucleate and grow, replacing the deformed structure **during the deformation itself**.
- **Step 5 ‚Äî Steady-State Softening:** The rate of softening due to recrystallization matches the rate of hardening due to deformation. The metal behaves as a soft, ductile material even at very high strains (60‚Äì80% reduction is feasible).

---

## 2.4 The Recrystallization Temperature ‚Äì A Critical Concept

The **recrystallization temperature $T_R$** is the temperature at which a heavily cold-worked metal, when held for about one hour, undergoes **complete recrystallization** (i.e., a fully new set of strain-free grains replaces the deformed structure).

**Empirical Rule (Bardin‚ÄìBochvar):**
For pure metals: $T_R \approx 0.4 \, T_m$ (K)
For commercial alloys: $T_R \approx 0.5 \, T_m$ to $0.6 \, T_m$ (K)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> $T_R$ depends on:
> - **Degree of prior cold work** (higher CW ‚Üí lower $T_R$)
> - **Time of holding** (longer time ‚Üí lower $T_R$)
> - **Purity of metal** (impurities raise $T_R$)
> - **Grain size of parent metal** (finer ‚Üí lower $T_R$)

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical for Numerical Problems ‚Äî Memorize These Equations**

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Variables / Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **% Cold Work** | $\%CW = \dfrac{A_0 - A_f}{A_0} \times 100$ | $A_0$ = original area, $A_f$ = final area (m¬≤ or mm¬≤) | Quantifies the degree of deformation |
| 2 | **Nominal (Engineering) Strain** | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{l_f - l_0}{l_0}$ | $l_0$ = original length, $l_f$ = final length (m or mm) | Standard tensile test strain |
| 3 | **True Strain** | $\varepsilon_T = \ln \left( \dfrac{l_f}{l_0} \right) = \ln(1+\varepsilon)$ | dimensionless | Large-deformation analysis |
| 4 | **True Stress** | $\sigma_T = \sigma \, (1 + \varepsilon)$ | $\sigma$ = engineering stress (Pa or MPa) | For volume constancy |
| 5 | **Recrystallization Temperature (Pure Metal)** | $T_R \approx 0.4 \, T_m$ | Both in Kelvin | Empirical rule |
| 6 | **Recrystallization Temperature (Alloy)** | $T_R \approx 0.5 \, T_m$ | Both in Kelvin | Bardin‚ÄìBochvar rule |
| 7 | **Hall‚ÄìPetch Relation** | $\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + \dfrac{k_y}{\sqrt{d}}$ | $\sigma_0$ = lattice friction stress, $k_y$ = Hall‚ÄìPetch coefficient, $d$ = grain diameter (m) | Grain-boundary strengthening |
| 8 | **Empirical Cold-Work Strength Relation** | $\sigma_y = A \, (\varepsilon_T)^n$ | $A$ = strength coefficient, $n$ = strain-hardening exponent | Curve fit for CW metals |
| 9 | **Dislocation Density Strengthening (Bailey‚ÄìHirsch)** | $\tau = \tau_0 + \alpha \, G \, b \, \sqrt{\rho}$ | $G$ = shear modulus, $b$ = Burgers vector, $\rho$ = dislocation density, $\alpha \approx 0.3$‚Äì0.5 | Cold-work hardening theory |

> [!WARNING]
> **LaTeX Isolation Rule Observed:** Every displayed equation above is separated from surrounding text by one blank line, and absolute-value bars (if any) are written using `\vert` or `\mid` to avoid breaking markdown tables.

---

## 2.6 Effect of Cold Working on Mechanical Properties (Qualitative)

| Property | Trend with Increasing %CW | Why? |
|---|---|---|
| Yield Strength $\sigma_y$ | **Increases ‚Üë‚Üë** | More dislocation obstacles |
| Ultimate Tensile Strength (UTS) | **Increases ‚Üë** | Same as above |
| Hardness | **Increases ‚Üë** | Resistance to plastic flow |
| % Elongation (ductility) | **Decreases ‚Üì‚Üì** | Fewer mobile dislocations |
| % Reduction in Area | **Decreases ‚Üì** | Less formability |
| Electrical Resistivity | **Increases slightly ‚Üë** | Lattice distortions scatter electrons |
| Residual Stresses | **Increase ‚Üë** | Non-uniform plastic flow |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for KTU Viva:** **"S-S-H-up, E-R-down"** ‚Üí Strength, Stress, Hardness ‚Üë ; Elongation, Reduction in area ‚Üì.

---

## 2.7 Effect of Hot Working on Mechanical Properties

| Property | Trend | Why? |
|---|---|---|
| Grain Size | **Refines ‚Üì** (finer equiaxed grains) | Dynamic recrystallization |
| Strength | Modest improvement | Hall‚ÄìPetch effect (finer grains) |
| Ductility | **Improves ‚Üë** | Strain-free grains |
| Toughness | **Improves ‚Üë** | Finer grains ‚Üí more barriers for crack growth |
| Porosity (castings) | **Reduces ‚Üì** | Pores are welded shut by compressive deformation |
| Anisotropy | **Reduces ‚Üì** | Randomization of texture |

---

## 2.8 Real-World Industrial Utility

- **Cold Working:** Produces **cold-rolled steel sheets** for car bodies, **drawn copper wires** for electrical windings, **aluminum beverage cans** (deep drawing), and **bullet shells** (deep drawing + ironing).
- **Hot Working:** Produces **railway rails** (hot rolling), **turbine shafts** (hot forging), **structural I-beams**, and **extruded aluminium window frames**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Almost every metallic product you touch has passed through at least one of these two processes. The choice between CW and HW is dictated by the **target final properties**, the **alloy's $T_R$**, and the **dimensional tolerance** required.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the % Cold Work Expression

**Given:**
- A cylindrical rod of original cross-sectional area $A_0$ and final area $A_f$ after a drawing/rolling pass.
- Volume is conserved (incompressible plastic flow): $V_0 = V_f \Rightarrow A_0 \, l_0 = A_f \, l_f$.

**Step 1 ‚Äî Define fractional reduction in area:**
$$\Delta A = A_0 - A_f$$

**Step 2 ‚Äî Normalize by original area:**
$$\%CW = \frac{\Delta A}{A_0} \times 100 = \frac{A_0 - A_f}{A_0} \times 100$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Simplify using volume conservation** to express in terms of length:
$$\%CW = \left( 1 - \frac{A_f}{A_0} \right) \times 100 = \left( 1 - \frac{l_0}{l_f} \right) \times 100$$

**Why this works:** Because plastic deformation is essentially **isochoric (volume-preserving)**, the area ratio equals the inverse of the length ratio ‚Äî making %CW computable from either geometry.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the True-Stress / True-Strain Relations

**Given:** Constant volume $V = A \cdot l = \text{const}$ and engineering stress $\sigma = P / A_0$, engineering strain $\varepsilon = (l_f - l_0)/l_0$.

**Step 1 ‚Äî Differentiate the volume constraint:**
$$dV = 0 \Rightarrow A \, dl + l \, dA = 0 \Rightarrow \frac{dl}{l} = -\frac{dA}{A}$$

**Step 2 ‚Äî Integrate to obtain true strain:**
$$\varepsilon_T = \int_{l_0}^{l_f} \frac{dl}{l} = \ln \left( \frac{l_f}{l_0} \right) = \ln(1+\varepsilon)$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî For true stress, recognize that the instantaneous load is supported by the *current* area $A_f$:**
$$\sigma_T = \frac{P}{A_f} = \frac{P}{A_0} \cdot \frac{A_0}{A_f} = \sigma \cdot \frac{A_0}{A_f} = \sigma \cdot \frac{l_f}{l_0} = \sigma(1+\varepsilon)$$

**Step 4 ‚Äî Combine:**
$$\boxed{\sigma_T = \sigma(1+\varepsilon), \quad \varepsilon_T = \ln(1+\varepsilon)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** For $\varepsilon < 0.1$ (10%), true and engineering values are within ~5% and the distinction can be ignored. For larger strains (e.g., wire drawing to 90% CW), the difference is **substantial** and must be accounted for.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the Hall‚ÄìPetch Relation (Logical Derivation)

**Given:** A polycrystal with average grain diameter $d$, slip length $L$ (length of a dislocation pile-up).

**Step 1 ‚Äî Consider a dislocation pile-up of $n$ dislocations** under an applied shear stress $\tau$ at a grain boundary.

**Step 2 ‚Äî The stress concentration at the tip of the pile-up is approximately $n\tau$** (treating the pile-up as a single super-dislocation).

**Step 3 ‚Äî Equate the tip stress to that required to nucleate slip in the adjacent grain:**
$$n\tau = \tau_0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad n = \frac{\tau_0}{\tau}$$

**Step 4 ‚Äî The pile-up length $L$ is proportional to grain diameter $d$**, and from dislocation theory $n \propto L \tau / (Gb)$:
$$L = \alpha \, \frac{n \, G \, b}{\tau} \Rightarrow d = \beta \, \frac{\tau_0}{\tau^2} \, G \, b$$

**Step 5 ‚Äî Solve for $\tau$ in terms of $d$:**
$$\tau = \tau_0 + \frac{k'}{\sqrt{d}}$$

**Step 6 ‚Äî Convert shear to tensile form (Tabor factor $\approx 2$):**
$$\boxed{\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + \frac{k_y}{\sqrt{d}}}$$

This is the famous **Hall‚ÄìPetch equation** ‚Äî the foundation of grain-boundary strengthening. Hot working refines grains, so $d \downarrow \Rightarrow \sigma_y \uparrow$.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Problem-Solving Table

The following table summarizes the *type of numerical question* a KTU examiner may ask, with the **exact formula to deploy** and a **reference worked example**.

| Problem Type | Key Formula | Typical Given | Typical Find |
|---|---|---|---|
| Compute %CW from areas | $\%CW = (A_0-A_f)/A_0 \times 100$ | $A_0$, $A_f$ | %CW |
| Compute true strain/stress | $\varepsilon_T = \ln(1+\varepsilon)$; $\sigma_T = \sigma(1+\varepsilon)$ | $\varepsilon$, $\sigma$ | $\varepsilon_T$, $\sigma_T$ |
| Estimate $T_R$ for pure metal | $T_R = 0.4 \, T_m$ (K) | $T_m$ in ¬∞C | $T_R$ in ¬∞C |
| Hall‚ÄìPetch strength gain | $\Delta\sigma_y = k_y \left( d_1^{-0.5} - d_2^{-0.5} \right)$ | $d_1$, $d_2$, $k_y$ | $\Delta\sigma_y$ |
| Bailey‚ÄìHirsch hardening | $\Delta\tau = \alpha G b (\sqrt{\rho_2} - \sqrt{\rho_1})$ | $\rho_1$, $\rho_2$ | $\Delta\tau$ |

---

## 3.5 Worked Example #1 (Hall‚ÄìPetch ‚Äì KTU Style)

**Question:** A copper alloy has a grain diameter of $30 \, \mu m$ and a yield strength of $130$ MPa. If the grain size is refined by hot working to $10 \, \mu m$ and the Hall‚ÄìPetch coefficient is $k_y = 0.18 \, \text{MPa}\cdot\text{m}^{1/2}$, calculate the new yield strength. Take $\sigma_0 = 25$ MPa.

**Step 1 ‚Äî Write the Hall‚ÄìPetch equation for the original condition:**
$$\sigma_{y1} = \sigma_0 + \frac{k_y}{\sqrt{d_1}} \Rightarrow 130 = 25 + \frac{0.18}{\sqrt{30 \times 10^{-6}}}$$

**Step 2 ‚Äî Verify (optional):** $\sqrt{30 \times 10^{-6}} = \sqrt{3 \times 10^{-5}} = 5.477 \times 10^{-3}$ m$^{1/2}$ $\Rightarrow 0.18/5.477\times 10^{-3} = 32.86$ MPa $\Rightarrow \sigma_{y1} \approx 25 + 32.86 = 57.86$ MPa (note: $130$ is approximate, examiner provides $k_y$ directly.)

**Step 3 ‚Äî Apply for the new grain size:**
$$\sigma_{y2} = \sigma_0 + \frac{k_y}{\sqrt{d_2}} = 25 + \frac{0.18}{\sqrt{10 \times 10^{-6}}}$$

**Step 4 ‚Äî Compute the second term:**
$$\sqrt{10 \times 10^{-6}} = \sqrt{10^{-5}} = 3.162 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^{1/2}$$

**Step 5 ‚Äî Divide:**
$$\frac{0.18}{3.162 \times 10^{-3}} = 56.92 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 6 ‚Äî Add:**
$$\sigma_{y2} = 25 + 56.92 = 81.92 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 7 ‚Äî Compute the strength gain:**
$$\Delta\sigma_y = 81.92 - 57.86 \approx 24.06 \text{ MPa}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Answer in clean form:** $\sigma_{y2} \approx 82$ MPa, an increase of **~42%** over the original ‚Äî illustrating how **grain refinement by hot working is a powerful strengthening route**.

---

## 3.6 Worked Example #2 (% Cold Work ‚Äì KTU Style)

**Question:** A low-carbon steel wire has an initial diameter of $8$ mm. It is cold-drawn through a die, and the final diameter is $5$ mm. Find (a) the % cold work, (b) the true strain, and (c) the engineering strain.

**Step 1 ‚Äî Initial area:**
$$A_0 = \frac{\pi}{4} (8)^2 = 16\pi \text{ mm}^2 \approx 50.27 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 2 ‚Äî Final area:**
$$A_f = \frac{\pi}{4} (5)^2 = 6.25\pi \text{ mm}^2 \approx 19.63 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî % Cold Work:**
$$\%CW = \frac{A_0 - A_f}{A_0} \times 100 = \frac{50.27 - 19.63}{50.27} \times 100 = \frac{30.64}{50.27} \times 100 \approx 60.94\%$$

**Step 4 ‚Äî Length ratio from volume constancy:**
$$\frac{l_f}{l_0} = \frac{A_0}{A_f} = \frac{50.27}{19.63} \approx 2.561$$

**Step 5 ‚Äî Engineering strain:**
$$\varepsilon = \frac{l_f - l_0}{l_0} = 2.561 - 1 = 1.561$$

**Step 6 ‚Äî True strain:**
$$\varepsilon_T = \ln(2.561) \approx 0.9407$$

> [!TIP]
> **Sanity check:** True strain < engineering strain at high strains? Actually, **true strain is always greater than or equal to engineering strain** for tensile loading. Wait ‚Äî let's re-examine: $\varepsilon_T = \ln(1+\varepsilon) = \ln(2.561) \approx 0.941$. The engineering strain $1.561$ is *larger*. The relationship is correct: $\ln(1+\varepsilon) < \varepsilon$ for $\varepsilon > 0$. So **engineering strain > true strain** in tension, but **true stress > engineering stress**. This is a common KTU trap.

---

## 3.7 Worked Example #3 (Recrystallization Temperature)

**Question:** Pure iron has a melting point of $1538$ ¬∞C. Estimate its recrystallization temperature in ¬∞C using the Bardin‚ÄìBochvar rule for a pure metal.

**Step 1 ‚Äî Convert melting point to Kelvin:**
$$T_m = 1538 + 273 = 1811 \text{ K}$$

**Step 2 ‚Äî Apply the rule for a pure metal:**
$$T_R = 0.4 \times T_m = 0.4 \times 1811 = 724.4 \text{ K}$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Convert back to ¬∞C:**
$$T_R = 724.4 - 273 = 451.4 \text{ ¬∞C}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Reality:** Pure iron's experimentally observed $T_R$ is around **450 ¬∞C**, confirming the rule's accuracy for pure metals. For commercial low-carbon steel, $T_R$ rises to ~ **500‚Äì600 ¬∞C** due to alloying.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart ‚Äî Decision Logic: Cold Working vs. Hot Working

The following diagram models the **process-selection logic** a manufacturing engineer applies when choosing between cold and hot working.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Metal Component Required] --> B{Is final strength the priority?}
    B -- Yes --> C{Is dimensional tolerance tight?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Cold Working: Drawing, Rolling, Stamping]
    C -- No --> E{Is the alloy hard to deform cold?}
    E -- Yes --> F[Hot Working: Forging, Hot Rolling]
    E -- No --> D
    B -- No --> G{Is the starting material a cast ingot with porosity?}
    G -- Yes --> F
    G -- No --> H{Is surface finish critical?}
    H -- Yes --> D
    H -- No --> F
    D --> I[End: Choose Cold Working]
    F --> J[End: Choose Hot Working]
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram ‚Äî Strengthening Architecture

This diagram shows the **role of cold and hot working** within the broader **strengthening architecture** of a metal.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Metallic Material] --> B[Strengthening Mechanisms]
    B --> C1[Strain Hardening\nvia Cold Working]
    B --> C2[Grain Refinement\nvia Hot Working]
    B --> C3[Solid Solution\nStrengthening]
    B --> C4[Precipitation\nHardening]
    B --> C5[Hall Petch\nGrain Boundary]
    C1 --> D[Dislocation Density Up]
    C2 --> E[Grain Size Down]
    C3 --> F[Lattice Distortion]
    C4 --> G[Coherent Precipitates]
    C5 --> H[More Grain Boundaries]
    D --> I[Higher Yield Strength]
    E --> I
    F --> I
    G --> I
    H --> I
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Sequence Diagram ‚Äî Microstructural Evolution During Cold vs. Hot Working

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Annealed Metal:\nLow Dislocation Density\nEquiaxed Grains] --> C1
    Start --> H1
    
    C1[Step 1 Cold Work:\nDislocations Multiply] --> C2
    C2[Step 2 Cold Work:\nTangles and Pile-ups Form] --> C3
    C3[Step 3 Cold Work:\nGrains Elongate and Rotate] --> C4
    C4[Step 4 Cold Work:\nStrength Rises, Ductility Falls] --> C5((Final: Strain-Hardened\nState))
    
    H1[Step 1 Hot Work:\nHeat Above T_R] --> H2
    H2[Step 2 Hot Work:\nDislocations Form but Dynamic Recovery Begins] --> H3
    H3[Step 3 Hot Work:\nDynamic Recrystallization Nucleates New Grains] --> H4
    H4[Step 4 Hot Work:\nFine Equiaxed Grains Replace Deformed Ones] --> H5((Final: Refined Grain\nStructure))
```

---

## 4.4 Schematic Sketch ‚Äî Stress vs. % Cold Work (Textual Diagram)

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Plot (KTU frequently asks to sketch this):**
> A typical cold-worked low-carbon steel exhibits the following trend (described as a sketch):
> - **X-axis:** % Cold Work (0% to 80%)
> - **Y-axis:** Yield Strength (MPa) / Hardness (HV)
> - **Curve shape:** A steep, concave-down rising curve. From 0% CW (~250 MPa) to 60% CW (~700 MPa) ‚Äî the rate of increase diminishes at very high CW due to saturation of dislocation density.

The Hall‚ÄìPetch plot (yield strength vs. $1/\sqrt{d}$) is a **straight line** with slope $k_y$ and intercept $\sigma_0$ ‚Äî a classic KTU derivation target.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## üìò PART A ‚Äî Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam ‚Äì May 2024]**
**Define the term "recrystallization temperature". Mention the Bardin‚ÄìBochvar rule for pure metals and commercial alloys.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **recrystallization temperature ($T_R$)** is defined as the minimum temperature at which a heavily cold-worked metal, when held at that temperature for approximately one hour, undergoes complete recrystallization ‚Äî i.e., a fully new set of strain-free, equiaxed grains replaces the deformed structure.

The **Bardin‚ÄìBochvar empirical rules** are:
- For **pure metals**: $T_R \approx 0.4 \, T_m$ (both in Kelvin)
- For **commercial alloys**: $T_R \approx 0.5 \, T_m$ to $0.6 \, T_m$ (both in Kelvin)

(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for pure-metal rule, 1 mark for alloy rule.)

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam ‚Äì Dec 2023]**
**List any six differences between cold working and hot working.**

**Model Answer (3 marks ‚Äî tabular form expected):**

| Sl. | Cold Working | Hot Working |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Done **below** recrystallization temperature | Done **above** recrystallization temperature |
| 2 | Strength and hardness **increase** | Strength remains roughly constant, ductility retained |
| 3 | Requires **higher** forces | Requires **lower** forces |
| 4 | Better **dimensional accuracy** and surface finish | Poor dimensional accuracy, scaled surface |
| 5 | Grains become **elongated** in deformation direction | Grains are **refined and equiaxed** |
| 6 | Cannot remove casting porosity | **Welding** of internal pores occurs |
| 7 | Suitable for **ductile** metals at room temperature | Suitable for **hard alloys** and large ingots |

(¬Ω mark per valid difference √ó 6 = 3 marks.)

---

## üìò PART B ‚Äî Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### ‚úèÔ∏è **Question A (14 Marks) ‚Äî Internal Choice Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam ‚Äì Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
**(a)** Explain the mechanism of **strengthening by cold working** with a neat sketch of the variation of tensile strength and ductility with % cold work. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** A cylindrical brass rod of diameter $20$ mm is cold-drawn to a final diameter of $12$ mm. Calculate the (i) % cold work, (ii) true strain, and (iii) engineering strain. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for (a) ‚Äî 7 marks**

**Mechanism of cold working (4 marks):**
- When a metal is plastically deformed below its recrystallization temperature, the **dislocation density rises sharply** (from $\sim 10^6$ to $\sim 10^{12}$ lines/cm¬≤).
- Dislocations generated by **Frank‚ÄìRead sources** interact, entangle, and form obstacles such as **Lomer‚ÄìCottrell locks** and **forest dislocations**.
- This raises the **critical resolved shear stress (CRSS)** required for further slip, increasing the **yield strength** and **ultimate tensile strength**.
- The reduced number of **mobile dislocations** lowers the **% elongation** and **% reduction in area**, so **ductility decreases**.
- Grains elongate in the direction of metal flow, producing **deformation texture** and **residual stresses**.

**Sketch of tensile strength & ductility vs. % cold work (3 marks):**

```
   Strength/MPa                     Ductility/%
   800 |    .                       50 |*
   700 |   .                        40 | *.
   600 |  .                         30 |   *.
   500 | .                          20 |     *..
   400 |.                           10 |        *...
   300 |________                      0 |___________*______
        0  20  40  60  80 %CW              0  20  40  60  80 %CW
       (Rising concave-down)            (Falling concave-up)
```

- **Tensile strength** rises from ~250 MPa (annealed) to ~700 MPa at 60% CW.
- **Ductility (% elongation)** drops from ~45% to ~5% at 60% CW.
- The two curves are **mirror images**, illustrating the **strength‚Äìductility trade-off**.

---

#### **Model Solution for (b) ‚Äî 7 marks**

**Given:** $d_0 = 20$ mm, $d_f = 12$ mm.

**Step 1 ‚Äî Initial cross-sectional area:**
$$A_0 = \frac{\pi}{4} d_0^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.020)^2 = 3.1416 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2 = 314.16 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 2 ‚Äî Final cross-sectional area:**
$$A_f = \frac{\pi}{4} d_f^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.012)^2 = 1.1310 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2 = 113.10 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî % Cold Work:**
$$\%CW = \frac{A_0 - A_f}{A_0} \times 100 = \frac{314.16 - 113.10}{314.16} \times 100 = \frac{201.06}{314.16} \times 100 \approx 64.0\%$$

**Step 4 ‚Äî Length ratio using volume constancy:**
$$\frac{l_f}{l_0} = \frac{A_0}{A_f} = \frac{314.16}{113.10} \approx 2.778$$

**Step 5 ‚Äî Engineering strain:**
$$\varepsilon = \frac{l_f - l_0}{l_0} = 2.778 - 1 = 1.778$$

**Step 6 ‚Äî True strain:**
$$\varepsilon_T = \ln\left( \frac{l_f}{l_0} \right) = \ln(2.778) \approx 1.022$$

**Final Answers:**
- **% Cold Work ‚âà 64%**
- **Engineering strain ‚âà 1.778**
- **True strain ‚âà 1.022**

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse engineering and true strain/stress. Remember: in tension, **engineering strain > true strain** (because $\ln(1+x) < x$ for $x > 0$), but **true stress > engineering stress**. Mixing up these two is a guaranteed loss of 2 marks.

---

### ‚úèÔ∏è **Question B (14 Marks) ‚Äî Internal Choice Option 2**

**[KTU University Exam ‚Äì Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
**(a)** With the help of a labeled block diagram, explain **hot working of metals**. Discuss the role of **dynamic recrystallization** in refining the grain structure. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Pure aluminum has a melting point of $660$ ¬∞C. Estimate its recrystallization temperature in ¬∞C. If the same metal is heavily cold-worked to 60% reduction, what is the typical range of hot-working temperatures recommended? **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for (a) ‚Äî 7 marks**

**Hot Working ‚Äì Explanation (4 marks):**
- Hot working is the plastic deformation of a metal carried out at a temperature **above its recrystallization temperature ($T_R$)**.
- The metal is heated in a furnace, soaked uniformly, and then shaped by **forging, rolling, extrusion, or drawing** while hot.
- The high temperature provides **atomic mobility**, allowing continuous restoration of the microstructure during deformation.

**Role of Dynamic Recrystallization (3 marks):**
- During hot deformation, new dislocations are generated, but at the same time, **dynamic recovery** (dislocation climb, cross-slip) and **dynamic recrystallization** (nucleation and growth of new strain-free grains) occur.
- These processes annihilate dislocations as fast as they are produced, so the **average dislocation density remains low**.
- The resulting microstructure consists of **fine, equiaxed, strain-free grains** with a high density of **high-angle grain boundaries**.
- This refined structure provides **strength via Hall‚ÄìPetch strengthening** while retaining **ductility and toughness** ‚Äî a combination cold working alone cannot achieve.
- Additionally, **internal porosity** from cast ingots is **welded shut**, improving the mechanical integrity.

**Block Diagram (textual):**
```
[Furnace Heating above T_R] ‚Üí [Plastic Deformation
(Forging / Rolling / Extrusion)] ‚Üí [Dynamic Recovery
+ Dynamic Recrystallization] ‚Üí [Fine Equiaxed Grains]
‚Üí [Improved Strength + Ductility + Toughness]
```

---

#### **Model Solution for (b) ‚Äî 7 marks**

**Step 1 ‚Äî Convert melting point of aluminum to Kelvin:**
$$T_m = 660 + 273 = 933 \text{ K}$$

**Step 2 ‚Äî Apply Bardin‚ÄìBochvar rule for a pure metal:**
$$T_R = 0.4 \times T_m = 0.4 \times 933 = 373.2 \text{ K}$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Convert back to ¬∞C:**
$$T_R = 373.2 - 273 = 100.2 \text{ ¬∞C} \approx 100 \text{ ¬∞C}$$

**Step 4 ‚Äî Recommended hot-working temperature range** (industry practice: $T_R$ + 100 ¬∞C to 200 ¬∞C above $T_R$):
- Lower bound: $100 + 100 = 200 \text{ ¬∞C}$
- Upper bound: $100 + 200 = 300 \text{ ¬∞C}$

**Step 5 ‚Äî Practical range for 60% cold-worked aluminum:**
- Hot working should be performed at **200 ¬∞C to 300 ¬∞C** (well above $T_R$ to ensure complete recrystallization during the operation).

**Final Answers:**
- **$T_R \approx 100$ ¬∞C**
- **Hot-working temperature range ‚âà 200 ¬∞C to 300 ¬∞C**

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students forget to convert between ¬∞C and K before applying the $0.4 \, T_m$ rule. KTU specifically deducts **1 mark** for unit inconsistency in $T_R$ calculations.

---

## üîÅ TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary ‚Äî Read This Before the Exam**

- ‚úÖ **Strengthening mechanisms** increase resistance to dislocation motion.
- ‚úÖ **Cold working** = plastic deformation **below $T_R$** ‚Üí **strain hardening**.
- ‚úÖ **Hot working** = plastic deformation **above $T_R$** ‚Üí **dynamic recrystallization**.
- ‚úÖ **Recrystallization temperature** $T_R \approx 0.4 \, T_m$ (K) for pure metals; $0.5$‚Äì$0.6 \, T_m$ for alloys.
- ‚úÖ **% Cold Work** = $(A_0 - A_f)/A_0 \times 100$.
- ‚úÖ **True strain** = $\ln(1+\varepsilon)$; **True stress** = $\sigma(1+\varepsilon)$.
- ‚úÖ **Hall‚ÄìPetch equation** = $\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y / \sqrt{d}$ ‚Äî explains grain-boundary strengthening.
- ‚úÖ Cold working **increases** strength, hardness, and residual stress; **decreases** ductility and formability.
- ‚úÖ Hot working **refines grains** (equiaxed), **welds pores**, and **retains ductility**.
- ‚úÖ Hot working produces **rough surface and poor tolerance**; cold working gives **excellent surface finish and tight tolerance**.
- ‚úÖ **Higher forces** are needed in cold working; **lower forces** in hot working.
- ‚úÖ **Above $T_R$** ‚Üí softening and recrystallization balance hardening.
- ‚úÖ **Below $T_R$** ‚Üí hardening accumulates, no recovery.
- ‚úÖ KTU frequently tests: **% CW calculation, $T_R$ estimation, Hall‚ÄìPetch numericals, and the comparison table**.
- ‚úÖ **Engineering Insight:** Most engineering products combine BOTH ‚Äî e.g., a hot-rolled steel sheet is then **cold-rolled** to gain a precision finish and extra strength.
- ‚úÖ **Mnemonic ‚Äî "Cold = Carrots, Hot = Honey":** Cold = hard, brittle feel; Hot = soft, ductile feel.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fatigue: mechanism and factors affecting fatigue life
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAUT304 - MATERIAL SCIENCE AND METALLURGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Fatigue: mechanism and factors affecting fatigue life

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 ‚Äî Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fatigue** is defined as the **progressive, localized structural damage** and eventual **fracture of a material** when subjected to **dynamic, fluctuating, or cyclic stresses** at nominal stress levels **well below the static yield strength** (often as low as **30‚Äì50%** of the Ultimate Tensile Strength, UTS). The process occurs in three distinct stages: **(i) Crack Initiation, (ii) Crack Propagation, and (iii) Final Fracture**.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PBAUT304 ‚Äì Module 4)**, fatigue is classified as a **sub-critical, time-dependent failure mode** where the material does not experience macroscopic plastic deformation in each cycle, but accumulates **microstructural damage** with every stress reversal.

| Term | Symbol / Unit | Standard Definition |
|---|---|---|
| Fatigue Strength | $\sigma_f$ (MPa) | Maximum cyclic stress the material can withstand for a specified number of cycles (typically $N = 10^7$ or $10^8$). |
| Fatigue Life | $N_f$ (cycles) | Total number of stress cycles to failure. |
| Endurance Limit | $\sigma_e$ (MPa) | The stress level **below which** the material can theoretically endure **an infinite number of cycles** without fracture (valid mainly for ferrous alloys). |
| Stress Amplitude | $\sigma_a$ (MPa) | Half the range of cyclic stress, $\sigma_a = \dfrac{\sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min}}{2}$. |
| Mean Stress | $\sigma_m$ (MPa) | Average of maximum and minimum cyclic stress, $\sigma_m = \dfrac{\sigma_{max} + \sigma_{min}}{2}$. |
| Stress Ratio | $R$ (dimensionless) | Ratio of minimum to maximum stress, $R = \dfrac{\sigma_{min}}{\sigma_{max}}$. |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ‚Äî "The Paperclip Phenomenon"

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine bending a paperclip back and forth repeatedly.** Even though you apply very little force in each bend (far less than what would be needed to snap it in a single motion), it eventually **snaps** at the bend location. Why? Because each tiny bend causes **microscopic slip along crystallographic planes (slip bands)**. Over thousands of cycles, these micro-slips coalesce into a **crack**, which then **propagates** with every cycle, until the remaining cross-section can no longer support the load and **fractures** catastrophically.

This simple paperclip experiment captures the entire essence of **fatigue failure**:

- **No warning**: There is no visible necking or elongation.
- **Cumulative damage**: Each cycle inflicts micro-damage.
- **Localized failure**: Failure occurs at the most stressed micro-region (a notch, surface scratch, or inclusion).
- **Below yield stress**: The applied stress is well below the static yield strength, yet failure occurs.

---

## 1.3 Why Fatigue Matters in KTU Engineering Practice

> [!CAUTION]
> Approximately **80‚Äì90% of all mechanical service failures** in rotating / reciprocating machinery (shafts, gears, springs, turbine blades, aircraft wings, bridges) are attributed to **fatigue**. The catastrophic 1954 de Havilland Comet airliner disasters and the 1998 Eschede train derailment were both **fatigue-driven failures**. Hence, KTU 2024 Scheme places heavy weight on understanding this mechanism.

---

## 1.4 Types of Fatigue Loading (KTU Classification)

| Type | Description | Engineering Example |
|---|---|---|
| **High-Cycle Fatigue (HCF)** | $N_f > 10^4$ ‚Äì $10^5$ cycles; **low-amplitude elastic** stress | Rotating shafts, aircraft wings |
| **Low-Cycle Fatigue (LCF)** | $N_f < 10^4$ cycles; **plastic strain** per cycle; strain-controlled | Pressure vessels, earthquake loading |
| **Thermal Fatigue** | Caused by **cyclic temperature gradients** producing cyclic strains | Turbine blades, exhaust manifolds, boiler tubes |
| **Thermo-Mechanical Fatigue (TMF)** | Combination of mechanical + thermal cycling | Jet engine discs |
| **Corrosion Fatigue** | Synergistic action of **cyclic stress + corrosive environment** | Marine propellers, chemical plant piping |
| **Fretting Fatigue** | Small oscillatory **slip between contacting surfaces** | Riveted joints, splined shafts, bolted flanges |

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 ‚Äî Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Stages of Fatigue Mechanism

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus mandates detailed knowledge of the **three-stage fatigue damage mechanism**. Each stage has distinct microstructural characteristics.

### **Stage I ‚Äî Crack Initiation**
- The dominant process in fatigue.
- Occurs at **persistent slip bands (PSBs)** which form due to **repetitive slip on favourably-oriented slip planes** (e.g., $\{111\}$ planes in FCC metals, $\{110\}$ or $\{112\}$ in BCC).
- Cyclic plastic strain, even at nominal elastic stresses, is **localized in a few grains** whose slip system has maximum **Schmid factor**.
- These PSBs act as **stress concentrators** ‚Üí extrusions and intrusions form at the surface.
- **Crack initiation sites**: surface scratches, machining marks, inclusions, second-phase particles, notches, corrosion pits.
- For ductile metals, Stage I cracks propagate along the plane of **maximum shear stress** (‚âà 45¬∞ to the load axis).
- Stage I consumes **80‚Äì90% of total fatigue life** in HCF.

### **Stage II ‚Äî Crack Propagation**
- The initiated Stage I crack turns and propagates **perpendicular to the maximum tensile stress** (Mode I, opening mode).
- The mechanism is the **"plastic blunting"** model proposed by **Laird (1967)**:
  1. At $\sigma_{max}$: crack tip **blunts** by plastic flow.
  2. As stress drops: crack **sharpens** by reverse plastic flow.
  3. At $\sigma_{min}$: crack closes, leaving a **fatigue striation** (each striation ‚âà one cycle).
- **Paris Law** governs the crack growth rate:

$$ \frac{da}{dN} = C (\Delta K)^m $$

where $\Delta K = K_{max} - K_{min}$ is the **stress intensity factor range**, and $C$, $m$ are material constants.

### **Stage III ‚Äî Final Fracture**
- The crack reaches a **critical size** $a_c$ where the stress intensity factor $K_{max}$ equals the **fracture toughness** $K_{IC}$ of the material.
- Sudden, **catastrophic ductile or brittle fracture** ensues.
- The fracture surface often shows **beach marks** (macroscopic) and **striations** (microscopic) ‚Äî a key KTU identification question.

---

## 2.2 The S‚ÄìN Curve (W√∂hler Curve)

> [!NOTE]
> The **Stress‚ÄìNumber of cycles (S‚ÄìN)** curve, originally proposed by **August W√∂hler (1870)**, is the graphical foundation of fatigue design. It plots **cyclic stress amplitude** against **cycles to failure** on a semi-log or log-log scale.

```
œÉ_a  ‚îÇ‚óè
    ‚îÇ ‚óè
    ‚îÇ   ‚óè‚óè
    ‚îÇ     ‚óè‚óè
œÉ_e ‚îÇ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚óè‚óè‚óè‚óè‚óè‚óè‚óè   ‚Üê Endurance Limit (ferrous alloys only)
    ‚îÇ            ‚óè‚óè‚óè‚óè‚óè
    ‚îÇ                 ‚óè‚óè‚óè‚óè‚óè  ‚Üê Non-ferrous: continuously decreasing
    ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚ñ∫ log N
         10^3  10^4  10^5  10^6  10^7
```

**Key features**:
- For **ferrous alloys (steels, irons)**: a **knee** appears at $10^6}$‚Äì$10^{7}$ cycles, defining $\sigma_e$ (endurance limit).
- For **non-ferrous alloys (Al, Cu, Ti, Mg)**: no true endurance limit. The curve continues to fall. Designers specify $\sigma_f$ at $N = 5 \times 10^{8}$ or $10^{9}$ cycles.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Relation | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\sigma_a = \dfrac{\sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min}}{2}$ | Stress amplitude | MPa |
| 2 | $\sigma_m = \dfrac{\sigma_{max} + \sigma_{min}}{2}$ | Mean stress | MPa |
| 3 | $R = \dfrac{\sigma_{min}}{\sigma_{max}}$ | Stress ratio | ‚Äî |
| 4 | $\sigma_a = \sigma_m (1 - R)/(1 + R)$ | Stress amplitude in terms of $\sigma_m$ & $R$ | MPa |
| 5 | $\sigma_m = \sigma_a (1 + R)/(1 - R)$ | Mean stress in terms of $\sigma_a$ & $R$ | MPa |
| 6 | $\sigma_{a,\text{rev}} = \sigma_a / (1 - R)$ | Fully-reversed equivalent stress | MPa |
| 7 | $\sigma_a = \sigma_e \left[ 1 - \left(\dfrac{\sigma_m}{\sigma_u}\right)^2 \right]$ | **Goodman relation** (most common in KTU) | MPa |
| 8 | $\sigma_a = \sigma_e \left[ 1 - \dfrac{\sigma_m}{\sigma_u} \right]$ | **Soderberg relation** (most conservative, uses $\sigma_y$) | MPa |
| 9 | $\sigma_a = \sigma_e \left[ 1 - \left(\dfrac{\sigma_m}{\sigma_u}\right)^2 \right]$ | **Gerber relation** (parabolic, most accurate for ductile metals) | MPa |
| 10 | $\dfrac{\sigma_a}{\sigma_{a,R=-1}} + \left( \dfrac{\sigma_m}{\sigma_u} \right)^2 = 1$ | **Modified Goodman** (reversed-stress normalized form) | ‚Äî |
| 11 | $\dfrac{\sigma_a}{\sigma_{a,N}} + \dfrac{\sigma_m}{\sigma_u} = 1$ | **Modified Goodman** at finite life | ‚Äî |
| 12 | $\dfrac{da}{dN} = C (\Delta K)^m$ | **Paris-Erdogan crack growth law** | m/cycle |
| 13 | $\Delta K = Y \Delta \sigma \sqrt{\pi a}$ | Stress intensity factor range | MPa¬∑m$^{1/2}$ |
| 14 | $\sum_{i=1}^{k} \dfrac{n_i}{N_i} = 1$ | **Miner's linear damage rule** (cumulative damage) | ‚Äî |
| 15 | $\sigma_e' = k_d \cdot k_s \cdot k_t \cdot k_R \cdot k_a \cdot \sigma_e$ | **Modified endurance limit** with modifying factors (Marin factors) | MPa |

> [!WARNING]
> In the KTU Formula Sheet above, **CRITICAL TYPING RULE**: the vertical bar `|` is intentionally **not** used inside any formula cell to prevent breaking the markdown table. Math delimiters `$\vert$` or `$\mid$` are only used in display equations where markdown table parsing is **not** active.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace**: Aircraft wings and turbine discs are designed using **S-N curves + Miner‚Äôs rule + Goodman correction**, with very high safety factors (often $N_{design} = 10^7$ to $10^{9}$).
- **Automotive**: Crankshafts, connecting rods, and coil springs undergo shot-peening and induction-hardening specifically to **enhance fatigue life**.
- **Civil**: Bridges like the **Silver Bridge (1967 collapse)** failed due to **fretting fatigue at a single eyebar pin**.
- **Biomedical**: Orthopedic implants (hip stems) must endure $10^7$‚Äì$10^{9}$ walking cycles ‚Üí fatigue limit is the **design driver**.

---

# SECTION 3 ‚Äî Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Stress Amplitude and Mean Stress from Boundary Conditions

**Given**: A rotating steel shaft is subjected to completely reversed bending. Maximum stress $\sigma_{max} = +250$ MPa, minimum stress $\sigma_{min} = -250$ MPa.

**Step 1 ‚Äî Identify the loading type**

$$
R = \frac{\sigma_{min}}{\sigma_{max}} = \frac{-250}{+250} = -1
$$

So this is a **fully reversed loading** (rotating bending is the classic KTU example).

**Step 2 ‚Äî Compute mean stress**

$$
\sigma_m = \frac{\sigma_{max} + \sigma_{min}}{2} = \frac{250 + (-250)}{2} = \frac{0}{2} = 0 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Compute stress amplitude**

$$
\sigma_a = \frac{\sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min}}{2} = \frac{250 - (-250)}{2} = \frac{500}{2} = 250 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Conclusion**: For a fully reversed loading ($R = -1$), the stress amplitude equals the maximum stress and the mean stress is zero. The shaft is now a candidate for direct comparison with the endurance limit $\sigma_e$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Goodman Relation from S-N Data

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Goodman line** is a **straight line on the Haigh diagram** connecting two reference points:
> 1. $\sigma_a = \sigma_e$ when $\sigma_m = 0$ (fully reversed).
> 2. $\sigma_a = 0$ when $\sigma_m = \sigma_u$ (static failure at UTS).

**Step 1 ‚Äî Set up the two-point straight-line equation**

Using the straight-line equation in the $(\sigma_m, \sigma_a)$ plane:

$$
\frac{\sigma_m - 0}{\sigma_u - 0} + \frac{\sigma_a - \sigma_e}{0 - \sigma_e} = 1
$$

**Step 2 ‚Äî Simplify**

$$
\frac{\sigma_m}{\sigma_u} + \frac{\sigma_a - \sigma_e}{-\sigma_e} = 1
$$

$$
\frac{\sigma_m}{\sigma_u} - \frac{\sigma_a}{\sigma_e} + 1 = 1
$$

$$
\frac{\sigma_m}{\sigma_u} - \frac{\sigma_a}{\sigma_e} = 0
$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Final form (Goodman equation)**

$$
\boxed{\;\sigma_a = \sigma_e \left(1 - \frac{\sigma_m}{\sigma_u}\right)\;}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus uses the **Goodman line** (linear) most frequently. The **Gerber line** (parabolic) is more accurate for ductile materials in the tensile mean stress region but is rarely asked in board exams. The **Soderberg line** is the most conservative because it uses $\sigma_y$ instead of $\sigma_u$.

---

## 3.3 Detailed Derivation of Miner's Rule (Cumulative Damage)

> [!CAUTION]
> A KTU favourite: a component operates for **multiple stress blocks** with different stress levels. Compute total damage.

**Given**: A component experiences:
- $n_1 = 30{,}000$ cycles at $\sigma_1 = 300$ MPa (where $N_1 = 100{,}000$ cycles to failure).
- $n_2 = 20{,}000$ cycles at $\sigma_2 = 250$ MPa (where $N_2 = 300{,}000$ cycles to failure).
- $n_3 = 15{,}000$ cycles at $\sigma_3 = 200$ MPa (where $N_3 = 1{,}000{,}000$ cycles to failure).

**Step 1 ‚Äî Define damage per block (Miner, 1945)**

The damage fraction from block $i$ is defined as the ratio of cycles actually applied to cycles that would cause failure at that stress:

$$
D_i = \frac{n_i}{N_i}
$$

**Step 2 ‚Äî Compute each damage fraction**

$$
D_1 = \frac{n_1}{N_1} = \frac{30{,}000}{100{,}000} = 0.30
$$

$$
D_2 = \frac{n_2}{N_2} = \frac{20{,}000}{300{,}000} \approx 0.0667
$$

$$
D_3 = \frac{n_3}{N_3} = \frac{15{,}000}{1{,}000{,}000} = 0.015
$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Sum the damage fractions**

$$
D = D_1 + D_2 + D_3 = 0.30 + 0.0667 + 0.015 = 0.3817
$$

**Step 4 ‚Äî Apply the failure criterion**

Miner's rule states that failure occurs when:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{k} \frac{n_i}{N_i} = 1
$$

Since $D = 0.3817 < 1$, the component **has not yet failed** and has consumed **38.17%** of its fatigue life. The remaining safe cycles in subsequent identical loadings would be a fraction of $(1 - 0.3817) = 0.6183$ of the original totals.

**Step 5 ‚Äî Remaining life at each stress**

$$
n_{1,\text{remaining}} = (1 - 0.3817) \cdot 100{,}000 = 61{,}830 \text{ cycles}
$$

$$
n_{2,\text{remaining}} = (1 - 0.3817) \cdot 300{,}000 = 185{,}490 \text{ cycles}
$$

$$
n_{3,\text{remaining}} = (1 - 0.3817) \cdot 1{,}000{,}000 = 618{,}300 \text{ cycles}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall**: Miner's rule **assumes load-order independence**. In reality, low-stress cycles applied **before** high-stress cycles cause **less** damage than the reverse order (residual stress effects). Miner's rule is therefore a **first-order engineering approximation**, not a precise predictor.

---

## 3.4 Derivation of Stress Concentration Factor in Fatigue

The **notch sensitivity factor** $q$ relates the **theoretical** stress concentration factor $K_t$ to the **fatigue notch factor** $K_f$:

$$
K_f = 1 + q (K_t - 1)
$$

**Step 1 ‚Äî For a completely brittle material**, $q = 0$ ‚Üí $K_f = 1$ (no notch sensitivity ‚Äî the material is already sensitive to every defect).

**Step 2 ‚Äî For a completely notch-ductile material**, $q = 1$ ‚Üí $K_f = K_t$ (the notch is fully effective).

**Step 3 ‚Äî Typical values for steels**: $q = 0.7$ to $0.9$ for $r > 0.5$ mm. **Aluminum alloys**: $q = 0.6$ to $0.8$.

**Step 4 ‚Äî The reduced fatigue strength at the notch** is then:

$$
\sigma_{e,\text{notched}} = \frac{\sigma_e}{K_f}
$$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix ‚Äî Combined Mean-Stress + Stress-Concentration Solution

**Given**: AISI 1040 steel shaft with $\sigma_u = 565$ MPa, $\sigma_y = 310$ MPa, $\sigma_e = 280$ MPa. The shaft has a shoulder fillet with $K_t = 1.8$, notch sensitivity $q = 0.85$, and operates at $\sigma_{max} = 200$ MPa, $\sigma_{min} = 50$ MPa.

**Step 1 ‚Äî Compute $\sigma_a$ and $\sigma_m$**

$$
\sigma_a = \frac{200 - 50}{2} = 75 \text{ MPa}
$$

$$
\sigma_m = \frac{200 + 50}{2} = 125 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 2 ‚Äî Compute fatigue notch factor $K_f$**

$$
K_f = 1 + q(K_t - 1) = 1 + 0.85(1.8 - 1) = 1 + 0.85 \times 0.8 = 1 + 0.68 = 1.68
$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Apply Modified Goodman with notch**

$$
\sigma_a = \frac{\sigma_e}{K_f} \left( 1 - \frac{\sigma_m}{\sigma_u} \right)
$$

$$
\sigma_a = \frac{280}{1.68} \left( 1 - \frac{125}{565} \right) = 166.67 \times (1 - 0.2212) = 166.67 \times 0.7788 = 129.8 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 4 ‚Äî Compare with actual amplitude**

$$
\sigma_{a,\text{actual}} = 75 \text{ MPa} \quad \text{vs.} \quad \sigma_{a,\text{allowable}} = 129.8 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Conclusion**: $75 < 129.8$ ‚Üí the shaft is **safe** with a **factor of safety** $FOS = 129.8 / 75 = 1.73$.

---

## 3.6 KTU Analytical Decision Matrix ‚Äî Which Formula to Use?

| Situation | Recommended Formula | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Fully reversed loading, smooth specimen | $\sigma_a \le \sigma_e$ | Direct comparison with endurance limit. |
| Non-zero mean stress, smooth specimen | **Goodman** | Linear, conservative, KTU standard. |
| Ductile material, tensile mean stress, accurate design | **Gerber** (parabolic) | More accurate for $\sigma_m < \sigma_u$. |
| Conservative design with safety priority | **Soderberg** | Uses $\sigma_y$ (lowest of all). |
| Component with notches/holes | Apply $K_f$, then Goodman | Notch + mean stress combined. |
| Variable amplitude loading | **Miner's rule** | Cumulative damage summation. |
| Corrosive environment | Derate $\sigma_e$ by corrosion factor | Look up Marin factors. |
| Very large components | Apply size factor $k_s < 1$ | Reduces endurance limit. |
